I am on the button with Queen-Four offsuit. FiveTwo, who is in UTG position, has raised to 2.5 big blinds. This hand is significantly below the standard range for calling or 3-betting against an early position open, especially with several aggressive players still to act in the blinds. Conserving stack and waiting for a better spot is the optimal strategy here.

Facing a UTG raise from FiveTwo, who has been selective with opens based on history (e.g., raised in Hand #3 but otherwise folding often). My 4s8d is offsuit, unconnected trash with negligible equity against a strong EP range. Pot odds are ~2:1 (400 pot, 200 to call), but hand realizes no postflop potential and BB yet to act. Deep stack preservation (38BB) favors folding weak hands OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with [Qd, Kh] facing a standard 2.5x raise (to 250) from the Under-The-Gun (UTG) player, FiveTwo. The pot is currently 400. I need to call 150 more to see a flop, offering me pot odds of roughly 3.6:1 (requiring ~27% equity). KQo is a strong hand that dominates many weaker broadways and has sufficient equity against a typical UTG opening range to justify a call. 3-betting out of position against UTG is risky as it isolates me against a stronger range. Calling allows me to realize my equity and keep the pot manageable while playing out of position. I will call the raise.

I am out of position on a paired board [4c, Tc, 4h] with two overcards (King-Queen high). The UTG raiser likely has a range advantage here with potential overpairs or strong Tens. Leading out (donk betting) serves little purpose as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see if they continue. If they check back, I get to see a free turn card which could improve my hand. If they bet, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing.

Heads-up as the UTG raiser on a paired 4-4-T board, range advantage is still with us (we have all big pairs/AT+/overpairs, while BB has many unpaired broadways and middling pairs). With AKo we have two clean overcards and benefit from denying equity to hands like KQ/QJ/AJ, while also picking up the pot often. Use a small c-bet to keep our range wide, reduce check-raise pain, and set up barrels on favorable turns (A/K/Q/J, or some club runouts).

I am holding QdKh on a board of 4c Tc 4h. The pot is 750 and I face a small c-bet of 200 from the UTG player (FiveTwo). The bet size is approximately 36% of the pot, offering me attractive pot odds of roughly 3.75:1 (requiring ~21% equity). While the UTG range is generally strong and likely contains overpairs or strong Tens that beat me, this board texture is dry and paired, often inducing high-frequency small c-bets with a wide portion of the range, including missed overcards like AK/AJ or lower pairs. I have two overcards and backdoor straight potential. Given the price, I can profitably call to see a turn card, hoping to hit a King or Queen or see the action check through. Folding is too weak given the small sizing and my hand's relative strength against bluffs.

The turn card [Th] puts a second pair on the board, making the board [4c Tc 4h Th]. My hand [Qd Kh] is now effectively Two Pair (Tens and Fours) with a King kicker. Against a UTG opening range, I am in very bad shape. Any pocket pair (55+) makes a higher Two Pair (Tens and Pocket Pair). Any Ace plays the board with an Ace kicker, beating my King kicker. Any Ten or Four has me crushed. I am only beating or chopping with other non-pair broadway hands like QJ or KJ. Leading (donk betting) would only get called by better hands. The standard play is to check to the aggressor. If the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold given my hand is practically the bottom of my range relative to the board texture and opponent's likely holdings.

Turn pairs the T, which is a relatively bad barrel card for AKo: it strengthens a big chunk of BB’s flop-calling range (Tx now trips / full houses possible) while our hand stays ace-high and our fold equity versus pairs is low (55–99 often continue, and Tx never folds). Checking back keeps the pot under control, realizes our showdown equity vs missed floats/backdoors, and balances our checking range with stronger hands that also like pot control on this texture.

The board is double-paired [4c Tc 4h Th 8h], meaning I hold Two Pair (Tens and Fours) with a King kicker. Against a UTG opening range, I am losing to any Ace (which makes T-T-4-4-A) and any pocket pair 55 or higher (which makes a higher two pair). I chop with other King-high hands (like KJ) and only beat missed broadways like QJ. The opponent's turn check-back suggests they likely have showdown value like Ace-high or a medium pocket pair, rather than a strong value hand or pure air. It is unlikely I can bluff them off an Ace or a pair on this board, and betting for value is impossible as worse hands won't call. My best option is to check and hope to get to showdown cheaply or fold if they bet, as their value range crushes me.

On this double-paired runout (TT44 with an 8), AKo has real showdown value: our Ace plays as the kicker (TT44A), so we beat all hands that are just “playing the board” (most pocket pairs 22–99, many broadway floats) and we only lose to hands that improved to a ten, a four, 88, or a backdoor heart flush. Betting turns our hand into a bluff/value-thin spot where worse hands often fold and better hands (Tx/full houses/flushes) can call or raise. Take the free showdown and realize equity.
6d 9c from UTG is a clear fold. This is a weak unsuited hand with poor playability. From early position, I need to play tight and this hand doesn't meet that threshold.

5♠2♦ is one of the weakest starting hands — disconnected, offsuit, almost no playability. I’m in middle position with multiple players yet to act (CO, Button, SB, BB), so raising is reckless and calling 100 to play multiway with essentially no equity is a losing long-term play. Pot is small (150) and my stack is healthy (14500), so preserving chips and waiting for a much better spot is the correct strategy. Fold.

I am in the Cutoff position with King-Six offsuit. This hand is generally too weak to open raise from this position in a 6-max game. With active players behind me and a stack of 76 BBs, there's no reason to play such a marginal hand that often gets dominated post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with folds to me (Claude UTG, Minni MP, Flash CO all folded). Unopened pot, standard spot to consider a steal raise from button as we have position on blinds. However, 6s4h is offsuit, poorly connected, and very weak overall (bottom of ranges). Postflop playability is poor; if called or 3-bet, we're often crushed or floating OOP. Blinds are Pro (SB, deep) and FiveTwo (BB, deep but history shows loose calling tendencies, e.g., Hand 2 big calls then fold). My stack is 38BB, not desperate. Button opening range is wide (~40-50%), but 64o is below that threshold (not suited, big gap). Fold equity vs blinds isn't high enough given history of defense. Better to wait for better spot to steal or play from position. Folding preserves stack for premium hands.

I hold Pocket Jacks [Jc Js] in the Small Blind, a premium starting hand. Action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has a stack of 8600. In a Blind vs Blind scenario, I want to build the pot immediately for value and deny free equity to the Big Blind's random range. Limping is an option to trap, but raising is more standard to extract value from lower pairs and broadway hands that might call. Given the standard raise sizes observed in previous hands (often 2.5x to 3x), I will raise to 300 (3BB) to apply pressure and define the range while out of position.

We’re heads-up BB vs SB where ranges are widest and we’re getting a reasonable price: call 200 more to play a ~600 pot (need ~33% equity). 7-5o isn’t strong, but as a one-gapper it has some straight-making potential and we should defend enough to avoid over-folding to small blind opens. Plan postflop is to play relatively straightforward: continue on good textures (pairs, strong draws) and fold to sustained pressure on bad boards.

I hold an overpair (Pocket Jacks) on a Ten-high board [8h 3h Tc]. The texture is somewhat wet with a flush draw and straight connectors present. I want to extract value from top pair hands (Tx), middle pairs (8x), and charge flush/straight draws while denying free equity. Checking risks giving a free card on a board where many turn cards (A, K, Q, hearts) could be bad for my hand or kill the action. A bet of half-pot (300 into 600) is a standard size to get value from worse made hands and draws without isolating myself only against better hands.

We’re BB vs SB in a single-raised pot and Pro is using a small 1/3-pot c-bet (300 into 900), which pressures wide but also gives us a very good price (need ~25% immediate equity). Even though 7c5h is currently just 7-high with no made draw, we do have some backdoor improvement: runner-runner hearts for a flush and some turn cards (4/6) that can give us playable straight equity, plus we should defend enough versus this sizing to avoid being run over. I’ll call once and play a tight turn strategy: continue on hearts/4/6 or if we pair, otherwise fold versus meaningful turn barrels on bricks.

The turn card is the Ten of Hearts (Th), which is a significant scare card. It pairs the board and completes the potential flush draw. My hand (Pocket Jacks) has been downgraded from a strong overpair to a marginal made hand that loses to any random Ten (trips) or any two hearts (flush). The opponent's range for calling the flop bet includes many Tx hands and flush draws. Betting here isolates me against better hands (trips, flushes) that will call or raise, while worse hands (like 8x or lower pairs) will likely fold. Checking allows me to control the pot size, potentially induce bluffs from missed straight draws, and re-evaluate on the river. I want to reach showdown cheaply rather than building a large pot on a wet board where my relative hand strength has diminished.

Turn goes check to us in a heads-up SRP (SB vs BB) on 8h-3h-Tc-Th. With 7c5h we’ve picked up a real 9-out heart flush draw. Pro’s check after a small flop c-bet is often capped (overcards like AQ/KQ, some weak 8x, occasional slowplays, and some missed floats), so we can profitably apply pressure now: (1) we have solid equity when called, (2) we fold out a lot of his air/overcards that have decent river equity vs us, and (3) we can set up a clean river shove or value/semi-bluff on heart rivers depending on runout. I’ll use a medium sizing that attacks his range without overcommitting vs a check-raise.

I hold Pocket Jacks [Jc Js] on a board of [8h 3h Tc Th]. The turn card (Ten of Hearts) is exceptionally dangerous as it both pairs the board (making trips for any Ten) and completes the flush draw. Given the Blind vs Blind dynamic, the Big Blind's calling range is wide and heavily saturated with Ten-x hands (T9, JT, QT, KT, AT) and suited hearts. My opponent, FiveTwo, has shown tendencies to play value-heavy post-flop (raising flop in Hand #3 with strong made hands). His bet of 700 into a 1200 pot after I checked signals strength rather than a bluff. With no heart in my hand to block flushes and blocking some straight draws (J9, QJ) that might bluff, his range is weighted towards hands that crush me (Trips or Flushes). Preserving my stack is superior to bluff-catching in a spot where the opponent's range connects so strongly with the board.

I'm UTG in a 6-max game with Kc Jh — a borderline but playable hand that I want to take the initiative with rather than limp and allow multi-way callers. Table standard open size has been ~2.5bb, so I will open-raise to 250 to deny equity to the blinds, define my range, and give myself postflop options. Plan: c-bet vs a single caller on many textures, play more cautiously multiway or when facing heavy action; fold to large 3-bets from tight, deep-stacked opponents, but consider calling small 3-bets or playing exploitatively vs looser/shorter players.

I am holding AKo, a premium starting hand. Minni raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range, but AKo is ahead of most opening ranges and performs well in 3-bet pots. I will raise to 750 to build value, take control of the hand, and discourage the players behind me from calling with speculative hands.

T3o is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight potential, or suited value. Facing a UTG open from Minni (tight/aggressive range) and a 3-bet from Flash (also strong range from middle position), folding is mandatory. Pot odds are poor (calling 750 into 1150, ~39% equity needed vs ranges we beat rarely), and multiway pot OOP with 3800 stack is disastrous. Preserve stack for premium spots.

I am facing a UTG open from Minni and a 3-bet from Flash in Middle Position. My hand, Ace-Six offsuit (A6o), is significantly dominated by both opponents' ranges in this configuration. An UTG opening range is already strong, and a 3-bet against UTG typically represents a very tight range (TT+, AQ+). A6o plays poorly multi-way and heads-up against this strength, often being dominated by better Aces. Cold calling is not profitable, and 4-bet bluffing is too risky given the positional strength shown by Flash. The correct play is to fold.

We’re in the small blind facing an UTG open and a 3-bet to 750 with 7-3 offsuit. This hand has extremely poor equity and playability, and calling would put us out of position in a bloated pot with a strong range behind (BB can still squeeze/come along). A bluff squeeze is also a bad candidate because we block nothing and will get called/4-bet too often. Best EV is to preserve the 92bb stack and fold.

J9o in the BB facing a 3-bet from Flash after Minni's UTG open. This is a clear fold. J9o is too weak to call a 3-bet - it plays poorly against strong ranges and doesn't have good implied odds. Even getting 1800:650 (about 2.8:1), J9o doesn't have the required equity against a 3-betting range.

I opened UTG to 250 with KcJh and Flash has made a modest-size 3-bet to 750. KJ off is a marginal 4-bet candidate (too weak to isolate strongly and easily dominated), but it has decent equity against a wide 3-bet range and good postflop playability. Pot is 1,150 and I must call 500 more — that’s ~23–24% equity needed (500 to win ~1,650), which KJo can achieve versus a fairly wide 3-bet range. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, see a flop, and proceed cautiously out of position: continue with strong/top-pair or good draws, fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I have little equity, and fold to a large 4-bet/shove preflop. I will not 4-bet here because my hand is not polarized enough for a value 4-bet nor is it a good bluff to fold out stronger hands.

I hold KcJh — only K-high (one meaningful overcard), no diamonds and no real straight draw on Qd‑8d‑7s. Flash 3‑bet preflop so his continuing range is heavily weighted toward Qx, diamonds and strong broadways that connect with this board. Betting OOP with a marginal hand against that range risks a check‑raise or getting called by better hands; I prefer pot control. I will check to see his action, fold to large aggression, and only continue lightly to realize equity against a small probe bet (or if I pick up a king/jack or a safe river).

I am the pre-flop aggressor with AKo, having 3-bet Minni's UTG open. The flop of Q-8-7 with two diamonds is somewhat coordinated. While this board can hit a 3-bet calling range (AQ, KQs, or pocket pairs), I still hold two overcards and have a range advantage as the 3-better. Minni has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 36% of the pot (600 chips). This sizing is designed to fold out her non-pair hands like AJ or smaller pocket pairs that might feel pressured, while keeping the pot manageable if she decides to continue with a Queen or a draw.

Flash 3‑bet preflop so his continuing range contains a mix of strong broadways/pairs (QQ+, AK, AQ) and some bluffs/diamond combos. On Q♦‑8♦‑7♠ he fires a very small c‑bet (600 into 2,250, ~25% pot). I hold K♣J♥ which is two overcards (K is a meaningful overcard to the Q) but I have no diamond backdoor. Calling 600 to win ~2,850 gives me ~21% pot‑odds — KJ has enough equity versus a wide 3‑bet/c‑bet range to justify a call and keep the pot controllable out of position. Plan: call to see the turn and re‑evaluate — fold to significant turn aggression if I still miss, but if I pair a K or J (or the action checks down) I can extract value or bluff‑catch appropriately. I will not check‑raise or get aggressive here with just overcards.

I have K♣J♥ — two overcards to the Q but no pair or diamond draw and I’m out of position. Flash is a preflop 3‑better who fired a very small flop c‑bet and then checked the turn, which looks like weakness or pot‑control with medium hands; it also means many hands that called the flop (Qx, mid pairs, some missed draws) will still call a thin turn bet. Betting OOP with nothing is a thin bluff with limited fold equity and high reverse‑implied odds if he calls or raises. Pot is 2,850 and my stack is healthy — I should control the pot and preserve chips for better spots or a clean river opportunity. Plan: check and re‑evaluate on the river; fold to significant aggression on this street, consider a river bluff or a value raise only if I pair or the price/line is clearly favorable.

The turn card is a 5 of hearts, which is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Minni has checked to me for a second time. My continuation bet on the flop was called, suggesting Minni likely has a piece of the board (a Queen, a pair, or a draw like diamonds). While AKo has some showdown value against her draws, it is currently behind any pair. Since she called the flop, it is unlikely she will fold a Queen or a pocket pair like JJ/TT to a second barrel on this board. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and hope to hit an Ace or King on the river or reach a cheap showdown.

I opened UTG with KcJh and called Flash’s 3‑bet. He made a small flop c‑bet which I called, then we both checked the turn. The river pairs the board (5h/5d) and it checks to me. I have no pair, no diamond, only K‑high — essentially no showdown value. Flash is a preflop 3‑bettor whose continuing range contains many hands that beat K‑high (Qx, overpairs, sets, A/K type hands) plus some missed draws/bluffs. Given the line (small flop bet then check‑check), he’s likely pot‑controlling a decent hand or giving up with a missed draw, but I don’t have enough fold equity in a 3‑bet pot to expect a bluff to work reliably. Pot is ~2850 and my stack is healthy; a bluff here is thin and often called by worse hands that still beat me. Best, lowest‑variance line is to check and show down — if he bets, I’ll fold to preserve chips.

The river 5d completes the flush draw and pairs the board. Minni has checked to me for the third time. While AK has some showdown value, it is likely behind Minni's range of pocket pairs (99-JJ) or Queens (AQ, KQ) that called the flop. Betting here is unlikely to force a better hand to fold, nor will a worse hand call. Therefore, checking back to reach showdown and see if my Ace-high is good against her missed straight draws or lower pocket pairs is the most prudent action.

I am in the UTG (Under the Gun) position with Jack-Nine offsuit. In a 6-max game, this is too weak to open from early position, as there are five players left to act who could have much stronger holdings. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

Pocket fives is a strong speculative hand from middle position in a 6-handed game. With 38BB effective stack, raising builds the pot for set-mining value, takes initiative, and thins the field against strong hands behind. Standard open-raise size of 2.5x the big blind (250) matches observed opening sizes in prior hands and my aggressive style. Folding or limping is suboptimal as it misses value and allows multi-way pots without initiative.

I am holding 9d 2h in the Cutoff position. This is an extremely weak unsuited hand (trash). Elon in Middle Position has opened with a raise to 250. Facing an open raise, 92o has zero playability and is dominated by the opener's entire range. With 89 BBs, I need to conserve chips and wait for better spots. Folding is the only correct play.

Elon’s 2.5bb open comes from a relatively short 35.5bb stack, which increases the chance he responds to aggression by jamming. J♠6♥ offsuit is too dominated to profitably call in position (poor playability, reverse implied odds) and it’s not a good 3-bet bluff candidate versus a potential shove. With two deep blinds behind who have shown willingness to 3-bet/squeeze, calling also carries extra risk. Best is to release and preserve stack for better spots.

Holding A4o in the SB facing a min-raise from Elon who has a short stack (35bb). A4o is marginal but playable. Elon has been active with raises. I'm getting good pot odds (200 to win 400). Calling is reasonable to see a flop - folding Ax is too tight here, but 3-betting invites a jam from Elon's short stack where I'd be in bad shape against his value range. Flat call to see a flop cheaply.

3♥8♠ is a very weak offsuit hand and is frequently dominated by a raiser’s range. Elon opened to 250 and the small blind called, so I’d be out of position in a multiway pot. The pot is 600 and it costs 150 to call (150/(600+150) ≈ 20% equity required) — 3‑8o does not have that equity versus a raiser+caller and has poor post‑flop playability and reverse implied odds. With a healthy ~130BB stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Flopped top pair on A-Q-2 rainbow board. Against Elon's preflop raising range, I'm ahead of many hands (Qx, pocket pairs, weaker aces). Board is dry with no draws. Betting for value makes sense - around 1/3 pot to get called by worse. Elon is short-stacked so I want to extract value while keeping him in with second pair and speculative hands.

Pocket 5s is still a strong hand post-flop on this dry A♦ Q♣ 2♠ board with no flush or straight draws. As the pre-flop raiser with position on Claude (SB caller), facing a small 1/3 pot bet of 200 into ~600, the pot odds are excellent (calling 200 to win 800, ~5:1). Folding is too tight given my range advantage and Claude's potentially wide SB calling range including Ax, Qx, suited connectors, or bluffs. Raising risks bloating the pot OOP for Claude while he can have AK/AQ sets; instead, calling keeps the pot small, realizes equity against his range (~35-45% equity vs typical caller), controls pot size, and allows turn plays like check-raise or bet if check. Consistent with aggressive pre-flop style but balanced post-flop discipline to maximize EV long-term in tournament.

Turn gave me two pair (Aces and Fours) on 2s-Ad-Qc-4s. This is a strong hand against Elon's range. He called my flop bet, likely with Qx, weaker Ax, or a pocket pair. Now there's a spade draw on board. I should bet for value and protection - around 65-70% pot to extract value from queens and charge draws. Elon is short-stacked with ~3350, so I want to build the pot while he's committed.

Facing Claude's turn bet of 650 into 1650 from the SB OOP, pocket 5s retains strong equity (~35-45% vs his wide SB calling range including weak Ax, Qx, bluffs, and incomplete draws). The 4s changes little fundamentally—blocks low sets/straights, and spade draw completion is possible but his flop sizing was small for pure draw value. With position, excellent pot odds (650 into 1650 needs ~28% equity), and my shorter effective stack (33BB vs his deep), calling realizes equity while controlling pot size OOP for him. Raising commits too much (~40% stack) against possible AK/AQ/sets and invites stacks from worse; folding too tight given range advantage and prior flop call balance. Allows river flexibility: check-call/raise weak Ax, fold to bombs. Maximizes EV in multi-hand tournament.

River brought the 6s completing the spade flush draw. I have A♠ giving me the nut flush! This is the absolute best hand possible. Elon has ~2700 behind and the pot is 2300. He's called both streets - likely has Qx, two pair, or possibly a smaller flush. I should bet for value - sizing around 1200-1400 to get called by worse hands like AQ, Q-high flush, or smaller flushes. Going too big might fold out his bluff-catchers. A medium value bet extracts from his calling range.

Pocket 5s on this river board (2s Ad Qc 4s 6s) is just a weak one-pair hand with no improvement, vulnerable to the completed spade flush (three spades on board), any Ace (board Ad blocks nuts but weak Ax common), Queen pairs/two-pair combos, sets, and straights like 3-5 (23456). Claude's SB calling range preflop was wide, but his small flop donk (200 into ~550), larger turn bet (650 into ~950), and now sizable river bet (1300 into ~2300, half-pot) after flush completes indicates strong value polarization—heavy on Ax (A2/A4/A6/AQ), flushes (e.g., KTs, JTs, 75s), or bluffs that missed but unlikely post-completion as bluffs thin out. Pot odds ~3.8:1 (21% equity needed) are decent, but equity vs. his multi-street donk-bet range is <20% (beats pure air/very weak Qx ~10-15% of range, loses to 85%+). Calling commits 48% of stack (1300/2700) into a pot where we rarely have best hand and face potential further action; raising to 2600 (min-raise) overcommits short stack (96%) with no fold equity vs. value. Folding preserves 27BB stack for better spots in 6-handed tournament (hand 21/150), consistent with prior disciplined calls keeping pot controlled but folding to this river bomb as planned for flexibility against strong ranges. Long-term EV-maximizing decision.

UTG with 98o (offsuit connectors) in a 6-handed game. This hand has poor playability, low equity against ranges, and no fold equity for a raise. Stacks are around 90-170BB except mine at 27BB, but still, folding weak hands from early position preserves stack for premium spots. Pot is just blinds (150), no limpers; standard fold.

I am in Middle Position with 7s 3c, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in Texas Hold'em (7-3 offsuit). With four players left to act behind me, including the button and blinds, entering the pot would be significantly negative EV. I have no equity to justify a raise or a call. The correct play is a trivial fold.

Folds to me in the cutoff with 9♠6♠, a hand that plays well with initiative and position (can make strong draws and some disguised two-pair/straights). Limping invites the button/SB/BB to realize equity cheaply and puts me in more multiway spots; raising lets me attack the blinds and keeps my range strong. With stacks ~90–170bb behind, I’m fine opening and folding to a big 3-bet if needed. Standard sizing in this game has been ~2.5x, so I’ll use that to pressure blinds without overinvesting.

Q5o on the button facing a cutoff raise to 250. This is a marginal hand - while I have position, Q5 offsuit doesn't play well postflop and isn't strong enough to 3-bet. The hand is too weak to profitably call or raise here. Easy fold.

I have J♥3♣ in the small blind facing a CO open to 250. The pot is 400 and calling 200 would require roughly 33% equity to break even, and this hand performs poorly vs a typical cutoff opening range and has very little post‑flop playability out of position. A min 3‑bet to 400 as a bluff is unattractive — I lack useful blockers and risk getting 4‑bet or played around postflop. With a 130bb stack and plenty of better spots, I conserve chips and fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Jack-Eight offsuit. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from the Cutoff. I have already posted 100, so it is 150 more to call into a pot that will be 550. This gives me pot odds of approximately 27%. Given that FiveTwo's opening range from the Cutoff is likely wide and I am getting a good price, I will call to see a flop, despite being out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I have Jack-high on a board of T-5-4 rainbow. This flop does not connect well with my hand, and I am out of position against FiveTwo. I will check to see how the pre-flop aggressor acts. If FiveTwo bets, I will likely fold as I have no real equity or draws on this board.

Heads-up in position as the preflop raiser on Ts-5h-4d, I can apply a small, range-based c-bet to fold out a lot of BB’s overcard/air holdings and deny equity. My 9s6s has some backdoor potential (spade runouts; 7/8 turns give me real equity), and betting keeps initiative so I can barrel selectively on good turns. I’ll use a ~1/3 pot sizing to keep my bluff inexpensive and avoid overexposing myself to a check-raise on a somewhat connected board.

I am holding Jack-high on a dry board (T-5-4 rainbow) and have no significant equity or draws. FiveTwo, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550. While the pot odds are roughly 21%, calling here with Jack-high out of position is a losing play as I have no clear way to win the hand unless I bluff later, which is risky against a player who has shown aggression. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold [Kh, Ad] (AK offsuit) in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is a premium starting hand that should be opened for a raise to build the pot and define ranges. The standard opening size at this table has been consistently 2.5x (250 chips), observed in multiple previous hands. Raising to 250 achieves the goal of entering the pot aggressively without over-committing from early position. I have a healthy stack of 89 BBs, so I am comfortable playing this hand deep.

Pro is opening UTG in a 6-max game, which is typically a relatively tight/strong range. QTo offsuit is a marginal defend here: it’s frequently dominated (AQ/KQ/QJ, AT/KT) and doesn’t realize equity well multiway, plus there’s squeeze risk from Claude/Minni behind. With ~95bb and no need to take thin spots, folding preserves EV and avoids tough postflop decisions.

J2 suited is a weak hand. Facing a UTG raise (which typically represents strength), with players behind me who could squeeze, this is a clear fold. J2s doesn't have enough playability to profitably call or 3-bet here.

Facing an UTG open to 250 with 2♦9♥ offsuit on the button. This is a very weak, poorly connected hand that fares badly versus an UTG opening range and is unlikely to play well multiway if the SB/BB peel or shove. Elon in the big blind is a short stack (≈26bb) and could jam, and a 3-bet bluff here is too risky and thin — I’d rather preserve my 13k stack and wait for a better spot in position. Standard, straightforward decision is to fold and avoid a marginal, dominated spot early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with K6s. Pro has raised to 250 from UTG. While K6s has some potential as a suited hand, calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand against a range that is likely much stronger than mine. Additionally, Elon in the Big Blind is short-stacked (2600 chips) and might choose to shove, putting me in a difficult spot. Given the tournament context and my position, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in BB facing UTG raise to 250 (150 to call into 400 pot, ~23% pot odds). Held T4d suited, marginal hand with some playability (flush/straight potential) but poor equity vs Pro's strong UTG range (likely 22+, ATs+, AJo+, KQs+). Pro has been aggressive but UTG suggests strength. My 26BB stack is short, but calling risks domination OOP; better to preserve stack for better spots. Folding is disciplined EV+ play.

K7o is too weak to open UTG in a 6-handed game, and limping invites multiway pots where we’re dominated by better Kx and broadways. With ~95 BB effective versus most stacks, the EV of folding is higher than entering a pot out of position with a hand that rarely makes strong, clean top pairs. Preserve stack for better UTG opens.

Pocket 2s in middle position. Small pairs can be profitable for set-mining with deep stacks (170bb effective). A standard 2.5x open to 250 is appropriate - if I get 3-bet I can fold, and if I get callers I'm looking to hit a set on the flop. Limping is weak, folding is too tight for this hand.

7♦2♦ is the worst starting hand — very weak equity and highly likely dominated. Claude opened from middle to 250 and there are three players (button, SB, BB) left to act; calling out of position into potential multiway action with a raggy suited connector is a losing long-term play. A light 3-bet/squeeze with 7♦2♦ is too thin (and Elon’s short stack can easily shove over it), and the min-raise size offers little fold equity. Stack is healthy so preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is correct. Fold.

6-4 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand. Even with the advantage of position on the button, calling a raise from middle position with this hand is unprofitable in the long run. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Preflop, in the small blind with AhJh (suited premium broadway), facing a middle position raise to 250 from Claude (loose-aggressive based on history). Pot 400, to call 200. My stack ~25BB effective. This hand has excellent equity, playability, and blockers. Standard play is to 3-bet for value/isolation rather than calling OOP or flatting. Min raise 400 too small; sizing to ~3x the open (750) builds pot while denying equity to worse hands and pressures BB. If shoved on, AhJh is crush vs calling ranges. Deep stacks behind but Pro BB likely wide.

I am facing a Middle Position open to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 750. My hand, Queen-Nine offsuit, is too weak to call a 3-bet, especially with the original raiser still left to act behind me. Cold calling here would put me out of position with a dominated hand in a potentially multi-way or 4-bet pot. The pot odds of roughly 37% are not sufficient for this hand strength against tight ranges. Folding is the standard and correct play.

Elon 3-bet to 750 and only has 1100 behind. With pocket 2s, I'm set-mining, but the implied odds are terrible here. I need ~7.5:1 to profitably set-mine, but I'm only getting about 4.4:1 (calling 500 to win ~2200 max). Elon's short stack severely limits my potential winnings when I hit. Easy fold.
7h9c is a weak offsuit hand from UTG position. This is not a profitable open from early position against 5 players yet to act. Standard fold.

I’m in middle position with A♠Q♥ — a strong, value hand to open. There’s no prior raise (pot is 150, 100 to call), so limp/calling is too passive and invites multiway action. I want to isolate the blinds and build the pot while keeping room to navigate a 3‑bet with deep stacks. A standard 3x open to 300 is appropriate: it discourages callers/limpers, extracts value from worse A‑x and broadway hands, and keeps postflop playability. If I face a 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate based on size and opponent tendencies (likely call or 4‑bet vs certain opponents).

A2o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially against an open raise from middle position. While it has an Ace, it is frequently dominated by better Aces and doesn't offer much in terms of straight or flush potential. Folding is the most prudent play here to preserve stack size.

On the button with Kc3c (suited King), facing a standard 3x raise from Minni in middle position after UTG and CO folds. Pot is 450, to call 300 (pot odds ~1.5:1). My stack is 29.5BB, others deeper. K3s has good playability: suited for flush potential, King blocker, decent equity multiway (~35-40% vs typical MP range). Button position allows postflop control. SB and BB act behind but I close the action preflop effectively if they fold/call. Folding is too tight from BTN; raising risks OOP stacks calling/4betting with marginal hand. Calling to realize equity and outplay postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Three offsuit (K3o). Facing a 3x raise from Minni in middle position and a flat call from Elon on the button, my hand is significantly weaker than their ranges. Additionally, I would be playing out of position against two players if I called. The reverse implied odds are high if I hit a King, as I am likely dominated. Folding is the only prudent option here to preserve my stack.

Ts4o is too weak to defend out of position versus a 3x open from Minni plus a cold call from Elon. Even though the direct price (200 into 750) looks tempting, our hand is frequently dominated when we make a pair and has poor playability multiway, leading to reverse implied odds. No good squeeze candidates here either given stack sizes and positions. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

Heads-up on a very dry flop (2c-6d-9s). I have A♠Q♥ — no pair but the best high-card and a hand that fares well vs a wide calling range. I'm first to act and out of position, so I want to take the initiative with a small c-bet to (1) get value from worse Ax and broadway hands that will call, (2) fold out pure air and some overcards, and (3) keep the pot controllable against made pairs. Elon called preflop with a likely wide range but only a minority of that range connected with this board (9/6/2 or a small set). A ~1/3 pot sized bet gives good fold equity while avoiding bloating the pot where I have only Ace-high. If Elon raises very large or shoves, I will give credit to a strong made hand and fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate the turn (value-bet A/Q turns, check/control otherwise). Betting 250 (≈1/3 pot) is the right balance here.

Heads-up on flop with Kc3c on 2c6d9s board. I have King-high, backdoor club flush draw (one club on board), and excellent pot odds (4:1 to call 250 into 1000). Minni's min-raise preflop from MP and small c-bet (1/3 pot) suggest a wide range including overpairs, Ax, suited broadways, and bluffs. This board is dry, missing most draws, so her betting range is polarized. Position allows me to float profitably, realize equity, and take control on turn if she checks or bet small. Raising risks commitment with marginal hand and shallower stack (~26BB); folding is too tight given odds and playability from preflop call. Plan to c/c or fold turn unless improves; bluff raise turn if good card/check.

Board is 2c-6d-9s-5d and I have A♠Q♥ — still only Ace‑high. I c‑bet the flop and Elon called, which looks like a fairly strong calling range (pairs like 9x/6x/5x, pocket pairs, some broadways and occasional draws). The 5♦ on the turn helps lots of those hands (and gives some new 5x hands) and does not improve me. Betting again here is a pure double‑barrel bluff and risks an immediate shove from Elon (he’s short, ~1.8k behind) putting me to a painful call with poor equity. I’ll control the pot and check to see the river — if he checks back I can show down A‑high vs air; if he bets small I can call or fold depending on size; if he jams I’ll fold. Checking preserves stack and lets me re‑evaluate with more information on the river.

Heads-up on turn with Kc3c on 2c6d9s5d board. I have King-high with a backdoor club flush draw and gutshot straight draw potential (3 for 3-4-5-6-7 but incomplete). The board is draw-heavy now with diamond flush draw open and straight possibilities (34,47,78). Minni bet flop small (250 into ~1050, ~1/4 pot) as OOP aggressor, which I floated profitably from position with good pot odds and playability. Her turn check suggests weakness: possible missed draw, middle pair (66,99? unlikely), or air giving up after c-bet. My perceived range from BTN call pre and flop call includes Ax, broadway, sets, draws; King blocks her TP. Position allows me to bet for thin value (vs J-high, Q-high, weak pairs), protection (vs draws), or pure bluff. Checking risks giving free card to draws or her improving; pot control not ideal with 24BB stack. Betting ~half-pot (700 into 1250) pressures her wide check range, gets folds from worse showdown hands, and sets up river jam if called. High fold equity given history (Minni folds to aggression when checked to, e.g., Hand 13). Plan: if called, bet river or check depending on card; builds stack long-term by exploiting position.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the dry 2c-6d-9s flop, then checked the turn. Elon called preflop and on the flop and now leads the turn for 700 into a 1,950 pot. His line polarizes toward a lot of made hands (9x/6x/sets, some 5x that the turn completed) with only a minority being pure floats or rare two‑diamond semi‑bluffs. Calling 700 to win ~3,350 (pot if I call) requires ~21% equity; A♠Q♥ has only ~6 clean outs to pair by the river (~13% raw). Raising as a bluff is too risky — his short stack can shove and I can be forced to fold or call a big extra amount with very poor equity. Given the math and range reading, this is a -EV spot to continue. I fold and preserve my stack for better situations.

Ten♣? (Ten of Hearts) and Deuce♣? (2♣) offsuit is a very weak UTG holding. Acting first out of position into 5 opponents, this hand is likely dominated and has very poor playability and blocker value (minimal straight/flush potential). Limping/calling 100 invites multiway pots and post‑flop trouble; raising with junk is exploitable. With a healthy 12,450 stack it's optimal to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

T3s is a very weak hand to open from middle position in a 6-max game. It lacks sufficient high-card strength and connectivity to justify a raise, and folding is the standard play to avoid being out-of-position with a dominated hand.

Folds from UTG (Minni) and MP (Flash), so unopened pot to me in cutoff with 3650 stack (~36BB). 9h5h is a suited gapped connector with good playability in position, especially against blinds who may defend wide based on history (Claude and FiveTwo often call stations). Steal blinds or build pot OOP for them, realize equity multiway or isolate if called. Standard open-raise size 2.5x BB to 250 to deny equity and take initiative.

I am holding J5 offsuit on the Button, which is a statistically weak hand with poor playability. Facing a standard open raise to 2.5BB from the Cutoff (Elon), I am likely dominated. Elon has a shorter stack (34BB), making him less susceptible to being bullied off a hand if I were to 3-bet bluff, and calling would be a losing play in the long run. The disciplined and correct move is to fold.

Pocket 7s is well ahead of a CO opening range and plays much better with initiative than as a small-blind flat (where we’re OOP and invite the big blind in / squeezes). With Elon only ~34bb, a 3-bet also pressures him to make a clear decision and often lets us play a straightforward pot (or even get it in with decent equity if he jams a reasonable range). I’ll use a standard SB 3-bet size to reduce BB’s incentive to come along.
I have 5s9c - a very weak hand. Facing a raise from Elon and a 3-bet from FiveTwo, I need to call 800 more. 95o has poor playability and no significant equity against 3-betting ranges. Easy fold.

Preflop, facing a 3-bet to 900 total (650 to call) from FiveTwo in the SB after my CO open-raise to 250 into an unopened pot. My 9h5h is a suited gapped connector with excellent playability, live cards, flush potential, and straight potential. Heads-up postflop, I have position on the SB (they act first every street). Pot odds are favorable: calling 650 into 1250 gives ~3.2:1 implied odds, and even direct pot odds are nearly 2:1, requiring only ~33% equity, which 95s achieves against a typical SB 3-bet range (often 8-12% including pairs, broadways, suited aces/broadways). FiveTwo's history shows wide defending, check-calling tendencies (e.g., Hand 2, Hand 3, Hand 18), suggesting they may overplay medium strength postflop. Folding is too tight given position and playability; 4-betting 95s risks 34BB stack with poor post-OOP equity if shoved on. Calling realizes equity best while controlling pot size OOP for them. Plan: call, see flop in position, c-bet flops with initiative or check back floats.

3-bet pot vs Elon with a low SPR (~1.5) and a monotone flop (3s-8s-9s). With 77 holding no spade, we have some showdown value but are in rough shape versus any spade and we can’t profitably continue versus an all-in. If we c-bet, Elon’s short stack incentivizes him to jam a lot of his continuing range (any spade, some made hands), forcing us to fold and burning chips. Checking keeps the pot controlled, realizes our equity, and may induce bluffs from his non-spade broadways that we can reassess against on later action.

Flop of 3s8s9s hits me perfectly with top pair (9h) and backdoor heart flush draw. Board is extremely draw-heavy (flush draws, gutshots like J10), so betting protects my hand and denies equity to draws while extracting value from weaker pairs (e.g., 87, A-high, lower pairs), overcards, and bluffs in FiveTwo's SB 3-bet/call range. FiveTwo checked, which is standard with monster (trap/slowplay flush/set) or medium strength/draws, but their history shows frequent check-calls with marginal hands (Hands 2,3,18,19). I have position, initiative from preflop range, and favorable pot odds/SPR (~1.4 after a pot-sized bet). Folding equity against draws/bluffs. Checking back risks giving free cards to improve (spade flush, straights) while my hand is vulnerable to overpairs/sets. Bet 2/3 pot (1300 into 1900) balances value/protection, leaves SPR ~1.3 for controlled aggression on later streets if check-raised (can fold to huge raise but call smaller). Avoids overbetting thin value early.

We’re getting a very good price (1300 to win 4500), and Elon is an active player who can stab this flop with a wide range in position—especially given the low SPR where he may simply bet-small/commit with lots of hands, not just made flushes. While 77 with no spade is in bad shape versus any made flush and many value hands (8x/9x/overpairs), the number of actual two-spade combos that call a 3-bet is limited, and he can easily have unpaired broadways / one-spade hands that we currently beat. Raising doesn’t generate meaningful fold equity against a stack that’s already close to committed and just bloats the pot versus the part of his range that crushes us. Calling keeps bluffs in and lets us reassess on the turn (especially on spade vs non-spade runouts).

Turn Qh doesn’t improve our 77 and we still have no spade, so we’re mostly in bluff-catcher mode. With Elon having only 1450 behind into a 4500 pot, any bet from us effectively puts him all-in and will mainly get action from hands that already beat us (made flushes/straights/two pair/sets) while folding out the bluffs and worse hands we want to keep in. Checking keeps his bluffs and semi-bluffs alive and lets him make the mistake of jamming too wide into a pot where we’ll be getting excellent odds to continue.

Turn Qh brings a heart, giving me a flush draw (5h9h + Qh) alongside top pair (9s) with a weak 5 kicker. Board remains draw-heavy (spade flush possible since three spades on flop, straights like 10-J for 8-9-10-J-Q, or 5-6-7-8-9, 10-J-Q possible). FiveTwo's second check after check-calling flop suggests medium strength (overpairs, weaker pairs like 88/TT/JJ, unfinished draws like spade combo draws, or traps with monsters), consistent with their history of passive check-calls (Hands 2,3,18). My stack is 1450 (~14BB) into 4500 pot (SPR ~0.32), making pot control irrelevant—we're getting it in soon anyway. Checking risks giving a free river card to improve draws (spade flush, straight fills) or facing a river bet without position advantage. Shoving 1450 extracts max value from their worse hands (8x two-pair, lower pairs, ace-high, naked draws) that can't fold profitably (~25% equity needed to call into 5950 pot), while having ~35-45% equity vs typical SB 3-bet/call range even if behind (blocks 99, live cards for equity realization). Fold equity against bluffs/marginal floats. Position + initiative + low SPR favors jamming over smaller bet (they can fold worse). Maximizes EV in high-roller tourney.

Elon’s turn bet is effectively an all-in for 1450 into a pot that’s already 5950, so I’m getting about 7400:1450 (~5.1:1) and only need ~20% equity. While 77 with no spade is drawing dead vs any spade (made flush), his line can still contain a meaningful chunk of no-spade hands that stabbed the monotone flop after my check (broadway air, some underpairs/overpairs without a spade, occasional floats) and now just rip the rest to maximize fold equity against exactly my “pocket pair, no spade” holdings. Raising makes no sense (he’s all-in), and folding would be too tight given the price and his demonstrated aggression. I’ll take the pot-odds bluff-catch.

I am holding 3-2 offsuit, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Additionally, I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position, which requires a very strong range to open. There is no reason to play this hand; it is a standard fold.

T8o is a marginal offsuit hand from middle position preflop, lacking playability and equity multiway. Facing a call of 100 into a 150 pot gives poor implied odds given the likely multiway pot with aggressive opponents like Claude and Minni behind. Standard play is to fold and preserve stack for better opportunities in this deepstack tournament phase.

I hold 5d 2h in the Cutoff, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em (unconnected, offsuit, low cards). With two players acting before me folding, I have an opportunity to open, but doing so with 52o is a losing play long-term, especially with the chip leaders (Claude and Minni) in the blinds who can easily defend. The prudent action is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

Folds to me on the Button: this is the best spot to apply pressure and pick up the blinds. Even though 5♠4♦ is weak and offsuit, position compensates, and opening keeps my range wide and hard to play against. I’ll use a small-but-standard steal size to risk little while still generating folds and keeping SPR manageable if called. Versus a 3-bet from either blind (especially deep stacks), I’m generally folding this hand rather than defending too wide with dominated equity.
7-3 offsuit is a trash hand. Facing a button raise, this is an easy fold from the small blind. No playability, no equity, kicker problems even if I hit.

6♦3♥ is a very weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and few blockers. Facing a button open to 250 (2.5x) while I’m the big blind means I’ll be out of position postflop — calling 150 to see a flop with this hand is negative EV. There’s also limited implied odds given the BTN’s 5.55k stack and I have no reason to 3-bet bluff here. Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

Qh7s is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated potential, and low equity multiway from UTG position in a 6-handed game. Standard fold to preserve stack early in the tournament (hand 28/150) with deep stacks. No opponent tendencies from history justify playing this speculative hand out of position.

I am in Middle Position with 8h 4h, a weak suited two-gapper. With 4 players left to act behind me, including the Button and Blinds, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to justify an open raise. It is easily dominated and will often be difficult to play post-flop out of position against the Button or Cutoff. The standard play is to fold.

Action is on us in the cutoff with no raise in front (150 in pot from blinds). 7-2 offsuit is the weakest possible holding, has very poor playability, and realizes equity badly when called. With three players left to act (BTN/SB/BB) who can defend or 3-bet, opening this hand is unnecessary risk for a small blind pickup. Limping is also negative EV and invites isolation. Best decision is to fold and preserve stack for better steal spots.
On the button with T6o after 3 folds. This is a prime stealing spot. T6o isn't premium but has decent playability, and I have position on both blinds. Standard button open to steal the blinds.

I'm in the small blind with 9♠4♠ facing a button open to 250 (pot 400, I must call 200). This hand is weak and frequently dominated, and I'll be out of position post‑flop to both the button and the big blind. 3‑betting with 9♠4♠ is thin (poor blockers, high variance) and calling 200 sets up difficult OOP decisions or a multiway pot where my equity is low. With deep stacks and plenty of hands to pick spots with, I preserve chips and fold, surrendering the small blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6c. Claude, who has been active and aggressive from the button, has raised to 250. While I am getting 2.6:1 odds to call (150 to win a total pot of 400), 86-offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position against a deep-stacked opponent. I prefer to wait for stronger holdings or better positional opportunities rather than defending with a marginal hand that often gets dominated or faces difficult decisions post-flop.

I am in UTG position, which is the earliest and most disadvantageous position pre-flop. My hand, Jack-Four suited (Js 4s), is very weak. While suited hands have some playability, J4 is too disconnected and weak to open profitably from early position against 5 opponents. The likelihood of being dominated or facing a 3-bet is high. Given my stack size of 8900 (89 BB) and the early stage of the tournament, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. The standard and correct play is to fold.

We’re first in from middle position with 9♠6♣ offsuit (actually 9h6c), 6-max, 59.5bb effective. This hand has poor playability, is frequently dominated when we make a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well out of position versus the cutoff/button and the blinds, who have shown willingness to raise/3-bet. With only the blinds in and no special incentives, this is a standard disciplined fold.

K9o in cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a solid hand in late position with only 3 players to act behind me. With a healthy stack of 16750, I should open-raise to steal blinds or isolate. Standard raise to 250.

I'm on the button with K♦7♦ facing a cutoff open to 250 (2.5bb). With ~123bb effective and position, K7s is a clear caller — it has good playability and nut-flush potential and realizes equity well in position against a reasonably wide cutoff opening range. A tiny 3-bet to 400 is too small to be a credible isolation (I’d size much larger if I wanted to isolate), so calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves post‑flop flexibility. Plan: see the flop in position, c-bet or float on favorable boards, and fold to heavy action if I miss and face strong resistance. Call 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten of Diamonds and Five of Clubs (T5o), which is a very weak starting hand. Claude opened from the Cutoff with a raise to 250, and Minni called from the Button. Being out of position against two active players with a hand that has poor post-flop playability makes this an easy fold. I need to preserve my stack for more favorable situations rather than defending with marginal garbage.

87h suited is a strong speculative hand with excellent playability for straights and flushes. Position is BB (out of position), but facing a cutoff raise from Claude (loose-aggressive raiser per history) and a button call from Minni (frequent caller/raiser). Pot odds are excellent: 150 to call into 650 (~4.3:1) plus strong implied odds with deep stacks (70BB effective). Folding is too tight; 3-betting is unnecessary as range isn't strong enough for value/semi-bluff squeeze given villain stacks and tendencies. Calling keeps pot multi-way, realizes equity, and allows postflop maneuvering.

Middle pair (8s) with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9 for 7-8-9-T) on a Ts-2d-8s flop. Decent but vulnerable hand OOP in a multiway pot against Claude (loose-aggressive c-bettor) and Minni (calling station). Pot is 800, acting first. Betting risks folding out better equity realization or getting raised OOP; thin value unlikely to get called by worse. Checking allows pot control, induces bluffs from Claude's wide range, realizes equity multiway vs Minni's passive style, and sets up check-raise opportunities if overbet. Superior to leading given position and board texture (spade flush draw, straight draws possible).

I have K9o on Ts 2d 8s - no pair, no draws (I don't have spades). Two opponents called my preflop raise. This is a board that connects well with calling ranges (tens, eights, spade draws). With no made hand and two players to act behind me, a c-bet into two opponents is risky and likely unprofitable. Better to check and control the pot with my showdown value.

Flop Ts‑2d‑8s; my hand K♦7♦ has no pair — just one overcard (K) and only a backdoor diamond route (need both turn and river). Both opponents checked the flop, so with two players in the pot my fold equity is low and a c‑bet is unlikely to pick up the pot consistently. Given deep stacks and a fairly dry board, I prefer pot control and to realize equity in position: check and see the turn. Plan: call a small probe bet to see a free card (or fold to heavy pressure), and become aggressive if I hit a King or a diamond or if opponents show continued weakness.

Turn 7c perfectly improves me to two pair (eights and sevens with 8h7h on Ts-2d-8s-7c board), a very strong hand in this multiway pot. Board texture supports draws (spade flush draw, open-enders like J9/T9/96), and villains' flop checks suggest wide ranges including overcards, mid-pairs, and draws rather than monsters. OOP first to act, betting extracts value from worse pairs/draws (e.g., T9, 99, Ax suited spades), protects against villainous draws, and denies free cards. Half-pot size (500 into 800) balances thin value, fold equity vs bluffs, and pot control while keeping stacks deep (70BB effective). Checking risks free river cards to draws or check-raise spots OOP; betting is superior GTO/exploitative play vs Claude's LAG style and Minni's passive calling.

I have K9o on T-8-7-2 board. Let me assess my equity: I have a gutshot straight draw (need a J for J-T-9-8-7). That's only 4 outs, roughly 8% to hit. Elon bet 500 into 800 (now 1300), so I need to call 500 to win 1300 - getting about 2.6:1 odds. I need roughly 11:1 for a gutshot. Even with implied odds, this is too thin. No pair, weak draw, and Minni still to act behind me. Clear fold.

Turn gives me 7♣ — I’ve picked up a pair of 7s with a king kicker (middle pair). Elon’s turn bet of 500 into ~1300 is small (a probe/denial size) and can represent a wide range: hands that improved (T’s, 8’s, 2’s), missed spade draws, or thin value. I’m in position with a live hand that has decent showdown value against bluffs and many backdoor holdings but is behind any Ten and vulnerable to better two-pair/trips. Raising to 1000 would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and risk getting 3-bet or committed when I’m often behind; folding would be too nitty given my hand’s equity and position. I’ll call 500 to keep the pot manageable, realize my equity, and re-evaluate on the river — fold to large river aggression if I don’t improve, or extract/value-size if the river improves me or Elon shows weakness.

River 2c pairs the low deuce on Ts-2d-8s-7c-2c board, giving me two pair (eights and sevens with 8h7h), a strong made hand heads-up. Minni's button call preflop (loose range) and flop check/check-through, then turn call of 500 into ~1300 suggests a wide range: top pair (Tx, Ax), mid-pairs (99-66), weaker pairs, or missed draws (spades, gutshots like 9x). The paired river doesn't drastically improve many villain hands (trips 2s rare from her range, few full house combos like T2/82/72 unlikely), but it may slow down overpairs or naked aces from raising. As first to act OOP, checking risks her checking behind worse (e.g., T-high, small pairs) or value-betting stronger (e.g., trips if holding 2x). Betting extracts thin-max value from her calling-station tendencies (history shows frequent check/calls multiway), denies equity to any lingering draws (club flush unlikely), and protects against bluffs. Sizing 2/3 pot (~1200 into 1800) is standard for value with fold equity vs air/improved draws, leaving deep stacks for post-bet play while committing <20% stack.

I have K♦7♦ on a board T♠ 2♦ 8♠ 7♣ 2♣ — that’s two pair (7s and 2s) with a K kicker. Pot is 3,000 and Elon bets 1,200, so calling 1,200 gives me 1,200/(3,000+1,200) ≈ 28.6% equity needed to break even. Elon’s line (called preflop, checked flop, small turn probe 500, small river 1,200) is consistent with a mixed range: value hands that beat me (T‑ and 8‑hands, any 2 for trips/full house, possibly slow‑played pocket pairs) plus a fair number of missed spade draws and marginal K/Qx hands. My two‑pair beats most bluffs and weaker Kx/Qx hands but loses to the main value combos. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and risk getting raised off by better hands; calling keeps the pot controlled, extracts value from bluffs/weaker pairs, and uses my position. I’ll call 1,200 and go to showdown (and fold to a large raise).

6-handed UTG, Q8 suited is near the bottom of an opening range but has good playability and can realize equity well in position against the blinds if we avoid going multiway. This table has shown relatively low 3-bet frequency and a lot of calling/checking postflop, so opening is preferable to limping to take initiative and deny equity. I’ll use a slightly larger than min open to reduce callers and simplify postflop lines.
I have AQo, a premium hand, facing a min-raise from UTG. With position on the raiser and a strong hand, I should 3-bet for value. Standard 3-bet sizing would be around 3x the raise, so 750-800.

FiveTwo opened UTG (250) — a relatively tight opener — and Claude has 3-bet to 750 from middle. My 6c7s is a weak, unsuited connector with poor playability against two strong ranges. Calling 750 into a 1,150 pot would commit a large chunk of stack-risk (pot would be ~1,900) and I’d need ~28% equity to break even; against an UTG open and a middle 3-bet my actual equity with 7-6 offsuit is well under that, especially if the pot goes multiway. A squeeze/raise here would be a pure bluff and risk committing chips without the equity or fold equity I want. Prefer to conserve chips for better spots in position with hands that have real equity.

I am on the button with Queen-Seven offsuit. There has been an UTG open followed by a 3-bet from the player in middle position. Q7o is far too weak to call or 4-bet in this situation, as it is likely dominated by both active players' ranges. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

2d Jh is a trash hand with no high card strength, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo and a 3-bet from Claude (who has shown aggression in prior hands), the range is strong. In SB, calling 700 into 1150 offers poor implied odds and terrible equity against their continuing range (~15-20% at best). Folding preserves stack for better spots. BB acts after, but no steal incentive here.

I am holding Ten-Eight suited (8d Td) in the Big Blind. The action began with an UTG raise to 250 by FiveTwo, followed by a 3-bet to 750 from Claude in Middle Position. This action represents significant strength. UTG opening ranges are typically strong, and a cold 3-bet against UTG usually indicates a premium range (JJ+, AK, AQs). 

I need to call 650 chips to see a flop. The pot odds are roughly 36%, but there are several negatives: 1) I would be playing out of position against the aggressor. 2) The original raiser (FiveTwo) is still left to act and could 4-bet, forcing me to fold after putting money in. 3) My hand is easily dominated by hands like AT, TT, 88+ or better flush draws. 

Given the strength shown by the opponents and my poor position, T8s is not a profitable defend here. The standard play is to fold and preserve chips.

We opened Q8s from UTG and now face a 3-bet to 750 from Claude with us out of position and ~57bb effective. Q8s is near the bottom of an UTG opening range and performs poorly continuing OOP versus a 3-bet: it’s dominated by Claude’s value range (AQ/KQ/QJ, higher suited broadways) and doesn’t have the right properties for a 4-bet bluff (no ace/blockers, poor playability when called). Calling would put us in a low-EV, tough postflop spot and risk bleeding chips. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack for better spots.

K9o from UTG is a marginal hand that's typically outside a standard UTG opening range. From early position, I need to play tighter as there are 5 players left to act behind me. K9o plays poorly against calling/3-betting ranges and is easily dominated by AK, KQ, KJ, KT. With a healthy 169 BB stack, no need to take unnecessary risks with speculative holdings from UTG.

10-2 offsuit is a very weak hand in middle position with four players yet to act (CO, BTN, SB, BB). Calling 100 or opening risks facing raises and playing a poor hand out of position in multiway pots where T2o has almost no equity. With ~103bb I want to preserve the stack for better spots and late-position steals. Fold and wait for a stronger holding or a better position to attack.

I am in the Cutoff with 9d 3c, which is a very weak hand that does not warrant an opening raise in this position. Since the action has folded to me and there are several players left to act behind me who could have stronger hands, the most prudent and standard play is to fold.

On the button with three folds ahead (UTG, MP, CO), Ks8s is an excellent hand to open: king-high suited connector with great playability, position, and flush potential. Blinds are short-handed behind (SB and BB only), and my stack is deep. Standard aggressive open to 2.5x BB steals blinds frequently or builds pot OOP with strong hand. Raise sizing is typical preflop.

I hold 8h 2c in the Small Blind, which is a statistically very weak hand (often considered one of the worst starting hands). Facing a raise from the Button (Elon) to 250, I am out of position and would be investing chips with a hand that has poor playability and low equity against an opening range. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

Button open is wide and we’re heads-up in the BB, so this is a good spot to mix in some 3-bet bluffs. A4o plays poorly as a flat (dominated by better Ax and has weak playability OOP), but the Ace blocker reduces the frequency Elon has strong Ax/AA, increasing fold equity. By 3-betting we put immediate pressure on his wide steal range and can often win the pot preflop; if called, we can still represent strong value hands on many boards.

Facing a 3-bet from the BB after my button open with Ks8s. FiveTwo's stack is 4850 (~48BB), mine 9350 deep. BB 3-bets can be polarized: value (TT+, AQo+) or bluffs (suited aces, suited connectors). Ks8s is well-positioned: suited, king blocks strong hands like AK/KQ, good equity (~35-40% vs calling range), excellent postflop playability in position (flush/straight potential). Pot odds: calling 600 more into 1150 (~1.92:1) is profitable even if equity is marginal. Folding too tight; 4-betting risks getting jammed on or called by better, losing fold equity edge OOP for opponent postflop. Calling keeps pot manageable, realizes equity IP, and allows future bluffs/floats. Consistent with aggressive button strategy and history where FiveTwo calls speculatively.

We’re the 3-bettor OOP in a heads-up pot, and this flop (T-J-4 rainbow-ish) still favors our perceived range (overpairs, AK/AQ, some AJ/KJ) enough to justify a high-frequency, small c-bet. With A4 we have a weak made hand (bottom pair) that benefits from denying equity to his two-overcard floats and random suited hands, and we also have some backdoor potential. A small sizing keeps our range wide and puts pressure on his misses without overinflating the pot when we’re likely to go into pot-control mode on many turns if called.

Flop: Tc Jd 4s. Heads-up IP vs BB after calling 3bet pre with Ks8s. Board is low, uncoordinated with one spade matching my suit. Ks8s has king-high (blocks strong Ax/Kx), backdoor spade flush draw, and decent showdown value. FiveTwo's BB 3bet range is polarized (QQ+/AK/AQs for value, suited Ax/connectors for bluffs). This flop misses much of their value range but hits bluffs/draws. My equity is solid (~35-45% vs range), pot odds excellent (450 into 2200, ~6:1, need 14%). Calling keeps pot manageable (~3100 post-call), realizes equity in position, allows control OOP for opponent on later streets. Raising risks jam from their 4400 stack (pot commit), folding too tight given playability/history. FiveTwo bets frequently but shows down marginal hands; plan to call turns or float/bluff if whiffed. Consistent with deep IP speculative calling strategy to maximize stack long-term.

Turn is a relative brick (7h) in a 3-bet pot, but our actual hand is just bottom pair with a weak kicker in a low SPR spot (pot 2650, we have 4400). If we bet, most better hands (Jx/Tx/PPs) continue and most worse hands (A-high floats) fold, so we bloat the pot without clear value while exposing ourselves to a raise that puts us in a rough stack-commit decision. Checking keeps the pot manageable, allows Elon to stab with missed overcards/gutters, and lets us realize our showdown value; we can then respond based on sizing (more willing to bluff-catch small/medium, folding to big pressure).

Heads-up on turn IP vs BB with Ks8s on TcJd4s7h board. Pot 2650, opponent checks after leading flop into my calling range. My hand: king-high with nut spade flush draw (9 outs, ~19% equity standalone), blocks strong Kx/AK, strong playability. Board somewhat drawy (straight draws like 89, Q9, flush draws unlikely yet), turn 7h changes little, adds weak heart draws. FiveTwo's BB 3bet-call flop range polarized: sets (TT/JJ rare, 44 unlikely), two-pair (TJ/J4 rare), top pair (AJ/KJ/QJ/T9), draws (spade/club? board one spade/club). Checking turn likely weak top pair, missed draw, or trap with strong hand. Equity vs range ~45%+, excellent pot control IP but betting optimal: semi-bluff value from K-high/draw vs floats/weak pairs, deny equity to their draws (89 for str, clubs if any), build pot for river shove if spade hits or thin value. Check-back risks free river card completing their draws. Sizing: ~55% pot (1400 into 2650) balances value/protection, leaves them pot-committed if called (4400 behind), fold equity on air. Consistent with deep-stack IP aggression, prior exploitative calls realizing equity profitably. Long-term stack maximization via applying pressure post-oop check.

Elon’s turn bet is only ~1/2 pot after we check, giving us ~26% immediate pot odds. In this 3-bet pot, our turn check doesn’t cap us (we can still have overpairs/AJ/slowplays), so his stab can reasonably include plenty of one-and-done bluffs and draw/overcard hands (KQ, AQ, Q9, etc.) in addition to value (Jx/Tx/overpairs). With bottom pair + ace kicker we have some showdown value versus those bluffs, plus a few meaningful improvement outs (A or 4) that can let us continue more confidently on many rivers. Folding would likely overfold this node given the sizing and allow him to profitably stab too often. Plan: call and reassess river—more willing to continue on A/4 rivers and versus small bets; more willing to fold versus a big river jam on bricks where his value is dense.

River is a brick (3h) that doesn’t meaningfully improve our A4 beyond the weak showdown we already have (pair of 4s). As the 3-bettor OOP, our range can still contain strong hands, so checking keeps our line credible and lets Elon continue bluffing missed overcards/gutters that called flop and bet turn. Betting here is awkward: worse hands (A-high/KQ-type misses) usually fold, while better hands (Tx/Jx/overpairs/77) call or raise, so a lead mostly turns our hand into a thin/negative block-bet and denies us the chance to pick off bluffs. Best is to check and evaluate facing a bet based on sizing (more willing to bluff-catch small/medium, folding to heavy pressure).

River board Tc Jd 4s 7h 3h completes heart flushes and some wheel straight draws (e.g., 5-6), but my Ks8s holds king-high with blockers to Kx/AK/AQ, excellent showdown value against BB 3-bet ranges that include AJ/KJ/QJ/Tx, overpairs that missed, or missed draws now checking. FiveTwo's line: 3-bet pre (polarized), flop bet (probe/strong), check-call turn 1400 (~half pot) into growing pot suggests mid-strength (TP weak kicker, pocket pairs like 88/55/22, or stubborn draws), unlikely monsters (would raise turn) or pure air (folds turn). Their remaining stack 3000 into 5450 pot (~35% pot odds if shoved), we are deep but they shorter. IP on checked river, betting extracts value from worse pairs/draws while denying cheap showdown, fold equity vs air/improved draws fearing flush rep. Check-behind too passive, risks free SDV but misses thin value from frequent J/T/8/5/2 holdings in BB defense. Shove 3000 pot-commits them, balances my range (flushes, two-pair+, str8s), consistent with turn aggression repping strength, exploits history of speculative calls while folding marginals river. Maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

Facing a ~55% pot river bet that effectively puts us all-in, our hand (4s with ace kicker) is near the very bottom of the range we arrive here with after 3-betting, c-betting, then check-calling turn. Elon’s line (call flop, bet turn, big river barrel) is heavily weighted toward value in a 3-bet pot: Jx/Tx, occasional slowplayed overpairs, and some sets (77/TT/JJ). The natural bluff candidates are missed straight draws like KQ/Q9/98, but our Ace of spades blocks a meaningful portion of his Ax floats (AK/AQ) that would also bluff, reducing his bluff density. With our stack-to-pot situation, we don’t need to “look him up” with the very bottom of range; we should preserve chips and fold this spot unless we have a strong read that he overbluffs big rivers.

UTG with A♣9♣ (A9s) and ~103 BB — a clear open-raise candidate in 6-max. I want initiative and to thin the field while building a pot with a playable suited ace, so I’ll raise rather than limp. A 3xBB open to 300 is a standard size here (slightly larger than the min) to discourage multiway pots and give good fold equity. Postflop plan: continuation-bet favorable A-high or club/connected flops; on dry, uncoordinated boards control pot size. Versus a big 3-bet from a tight player I’ll often fold and preserve chips; I’ll call smaller/wide 3-bets for implied odds and will commit to calls vs short-stack shoves when pot odds justify it.

I am in the middle position holding T7o. Minni has opened for 300 from UTG. Ten-Seven offsuit is a very weak hand, especially against an early position raise. The risk of being dominated or facing further action from players behind me is high. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to conserve my stack.

Preflop in cutoff facing a UTG raise to 300 (3x BB) from Minni, who has been aggressive but UTG opens suggest a strong range (TT+, AQ+, etc.). My hand K8o is offsuit, dominated by typical calling/raising hands like KQ, KJ, AK, and has poor playability postflop without position advantage over UTG. Pot odds to call are about 2.5:1 (300 to win 750), but implied odds are limited due to hand strength. Folding preserves my deep stack (123BB) for better spots in position later. No history shows Minni weak UTG. Standard fold.

I hold 7h 4d on the Button. This is a very weak hand (7-high offsuit). Minni has opened the pot with a raise to 300 from UTG, indicating a potentially strong range. Facing a 3bb open from early position with a trash hand, my equity is very poor, and I do not have the odds or the hand strength to continue. 3-betting as a bluff against UTG is not profitable here. The correct play is to fold.

Q5o is too weak to defend the small blind versus a UTG open: we’ll be out of position, often dominated when we make a queen, and we don’t realize equity well with this stack depth (~30bb). A 3-bet bluff is also poor here because UTG’s range is tighter and we’d frequently get called and be forced to play a bloated pot with a dominated hand. Best is to cut losses and wait for a better spot.

3-5 offsuit is a weak hand. While I'm getting decent pot odds (2.25:1), this hand plays poorly postflop and is easily dominated. Against Minni's open raise, folding is the disciplined play rather than defending with such a weak holding.

I am in the Under the Gun (UTG) position with Eight-Three offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the poor hand quality and the fact that there are five players still to act behind me, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot. Folding is the only viable play here to conserve my stack.

AKo is a premium hand, one of the best starting hands. In middle position after UTG fold in a 6-handed game, this is a standard raise spot to build the pot, deny equity to worse hands, and isolate against blinds. Deep stacks (123BB) favor aggressive play. Standard raise size around 3x the BB given the dynamics from history where opens are 250-300. Raising to 300 takes initiative and has good fold equity against recreational opponents like FiveTwo (short stack on button) and blinds.

I hold Td 6c in the Cutoff. This is a very weak offsuit hand (T6o) with little playability. Facing a raise to 300 (3BB) from Elon in Middle Position, I am well behind his opening range. My hand does not justify a call or a bluff 3-bet. The standard and most profitable play is to fold immediately to preserve chips.

We’re on the button with A8 suited versus a middle-position open. This is a hand that plays well in position (nut-flush potential, decent top-pair value) and benefits from realizing equity rather than inflating the pot. With ~30bb, a 3-bet to ~900 would commit a big chunk of our stack and risks running into a 4-bet where we’d have to fold and burn too much equity; plus the blinds are deep and can complicate things if they come along. Calling keeps the SPR reasonable, lets us outplay postflop in position, and preserves stack flexibility.
68o is a weak hand out of position. Even with 3:1 pot odds (250 to win 750), playing this hand OOP against a raiser and caller isn't profitable. Minni is still behind me and could squeeze. Easy fold.

I have Q♠J♣ in the BB facing Elon’s raise to 300 and the Button’s call. Pot is 750 and I need 200 more (total 300) to see a flop. QJo is worth defending here — the call is cheap (200 to win ~950, ~4.75:1) and preserves stack depth. I don’t want to 3-bet because the Button is in, I’ll be out of position postflop and Elon barrels a lot; isolating with QJo in a multiway pot is suboptimal. Plan: call to see the flop, continue with top pair/good draw, and otherwise keep the pot under control or fold to heavy action on dry boards.

I hold Q♠J♣ and completely missed the flop. Board Ks‑5d‑Kh is paired and very hostile to my hand: no pair, no meaningful draws, and my equity is very low vs any king or even medium pocket pairs. Both opponents checked to me, but this is a multiway spot with thin fold equity — a bluff here is too risky and a bet just bloats the pot out of position. I’ll keep the pot under control and check to see a turn card; fold to any sizeable bet and only continue if I pick up real equity or both opponents show clear weakness on later streets.

Monster hand with trip kings (Kc pairs the board's Ks and Kh), Ace-high kicker is the nuts among possible holdings. Board is paired kings with a low 5d, minimal straight draws, possible weak flush draw or weaker pairs/draws.
