Preflop: I raised AKo, got called by loose BTN FiveTwo (short stack ~26BB, station tendencies from history) and BB Minni (defensive caller). Minni checked flop, indicating weakness. OOP first to act postflop vs two opponents, must bet for value/protection against draws/improvements. Pot 950, bet ~2/3 pot (650) to get value from worse hands like Kx weaker kicker, two-pair (K5 rare), or bluffs. Builds pot while keeping stacks in (FiveTwo covered). Avoid check to prevent free cards or multiway cheap river.

Flop is K♠ 5♦ K♥ in a 3-way pot and Elon c-bets 650. With A♥8♥ we have only ace-high plus a very thin backdoor heart draw (need two hearts) and no meaningful straight potential. Any A/8 pair is rarely good versus a multiway c-bet on a paired-high board, and we don’t have the stack depth (~26.5bb) to profitably float and apply pressure later without risking a large chunk of our stack. Raising would be close to committing and gets called frequently by any Kx and some 5x/pairs. Pot odds don’t justify continuing with this equity, so we preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

Flop: K♠‑5♦‑K♥. I have Q♠J♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Elon opened pre and now bets 650 into the 1600 pot; FiveTwo folded so it’s heads‑up. Calling 650 to win a 2250 pot requires ~29% equity. Elon's range after an open+continuation bet is heavily weighted to Kx, big pairs and overpairs that dominate my hand; even hitting a Q or J usually won’t beat a King. I have virtually no realistic outs or fold equity, and a raise as a pure bluff is too risky with deep stacks and an aggressive opponent who can barrel. Following my preflop plan to check and fold to significant action, I fold this flop and conserve my stack for a better spot.

UTG with 2c8s offsuit is one of the weakest possible starting hands. From early position, it has no playability post-flop, poor equity against any calling range, and limping or raising would be a mistake as it invites multiway pots where this hand performs terribly. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots. My deep stack (130BB) allows patience in a long tournament.

I hold 8h 7s in middle position. This is an off-suit connector with low equity and poor playability, especially from early/middle position where there are several players left to act, including a short stack in the cutoff and a deep stack on the button. The standard play is to fold this hand pre-flop.

Folded to us in the cutoff with 26.5bb, J5o is near the bottom of a profitable open range: it’s dominated when called and plays poorly postflop. With a deep-stacked, capable opponent on the button (Claude) who can apply frequent 3-bet pressure, opening this hand risks bleeding chips we can’t comfortably defend with at this stack depth. Better to preserve and steal with hands that realize equity and defend vs 3-bets more cleanly.

On the button with J6 suited after 3 folds. This is a standard steal spot - I have position and only the blinds to get through. J6s plays reasonably well postflop if called due to suited potential. Raising to 250 (2.5x) to steal the blinds.

Claude opened to 250 from the button — likely a fairly wide steal. I have Kc7c: suited king with some backdoor flush/straight potential and a blocker to some Kx hands. Calling 200 (cheap relative to a ~100bb stack) gives me ~3:1 immediate pot odds to see a flop and is the +EV defensive play from the small blind. A min 3-bet here would be thin and leaves me OOP vs both opponents; folding is too tight versus a button open. Plan: call and proceed cautiously postflop — continue with club/king/strong connected flops, pot-control or fold on dry boards if facing significant pressure, and re-evaluate if the BB cold-calls or over-bets.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Trey of Hearts. Claude raised from the Button to 250, and Minni called from the Small Blind. I only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 750. While the pot odds are roughly 4:1, T3o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. It is easily dominated and lacks connectivity or suit value to navigate post-flop effectively. I will fold and wait for a better spot to preserve my stack.

I have Kc7c — a medium pair (sevens) with a very strong king kicker on a 5d-2s-7d flop. Claude opened from the button, so his range is wide and contains many missed overcards and some diamond combos; that makes my hand ahead of a lot of his continuation-bet range. The board is fairly dry but has two diamonds, so there are meaningful 4-card diamond draws that I want to charge/protect against. Out of position, I don’t want to give free cards and I want to extract value from worse hands (Kx, weaker pairs, some turned draws) while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll make a half-pot probe to both get value and deny equity — 300 into the 600 pot. If Claude raises substantially I will strongly consider folding (I’m OOP with a marginal made hand); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn, folding to big aggression or proceeding cautiously if the turn is non-diamond and non-pairing.

I completely whiffed this flop - J6 clubs on a 5d 2s 7d board gives me jack high with no pair, no draw (wrong suit for the diamond draw). Minni's donk bet of 300 into 600 represents some kind of made hand or draw. With zero equity and no backdoor draws, continuing here is lighting money on fire. Clean fold.

I am in UTG position with 5h 7d, which is a very weak off-suit one-gapper. From early position, I need a strong hand to open the pot because there are 5 players left to act behind me. This hand has poor playability and equity. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

We’re effectively MP (UTG already folded) with ~26.5bb and Ts6s. This hand is too weak to open from this seat: it performs poorly when called, and it can’t profitably continue versus the likely 3-bets from the big stacks behind (Claude/Minni) plus the blinds. With our stack size, avoiding marginal opens that get us pressured is higher EV than trying to steal here.
Q9o in cutoff with two folds ahead. This is a decent spot to open-raise as a steal attempt with only button and blinds remaining. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

Facing a cutoff open to 250 on the button with 9♦3♦. This is a very weak, often-dominated suited hand with poor postflop playability. Calling 250 into a 400 pot requires ~38% equity and the small blind and big blind still have to act — likely creating a multiway pot that further reduces my equity. 3-betting as a bluff with 9‑3s is marginal (poor fold equity and bad playability if called or 4-bet). I have ~105bb and can wait for much better spots on the button. Fold and preserve my stack for a higher-ROI situation.

I am holding 3h 5d in the small blind. Claude, the chip leader in the cutoff, has raised to 250. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability, especially out of position against an active chip leader. The risk of calling or three-betting with such low equity does not justify the potential reward. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play in this situation.

Preflop in BB facing a CO raise to 250 after two folds pre. Pot is 400, to call 150 (already posted 100), offering ~2.67:1 odds requiring ~27% equity. 5s6h is offsuit connectors with straight potential and decent multiway playability, though OOP. Claude has been aggressive in history (frequent raises from late pos, some c-bets but folds to resistance like Hand 34). Vs likely wide CO range (20-30% hands: broadways, suited Ax, connectors, pairs), 56o realizes ~35%+ equity and benefits from deep stacks (130BB effective). Folding too tight in BB defense; calling is +EV to realize equity and play postflop speculatively. No 3bet as too weak. Avoid all-in risks early in tournament.

Flop of 7s Tc Ac completely misses my 5s6h. No pair, no straight draw (gutshot absent), only backdoor spade flush draw (one spade in hand). Equity against Claude's CO continuing range (Ax, broadways, sets, bluffs) is low ~15-20%, especially OOP heads-up. Pot 550 deep stacks (~130BB effective). First to act OOP with air: standard check-fold to likely wide cbet range (Claude aggressive historically, cbets flops like Hand 34 but folds to 3bets). Donk betting too thin, no blockers, poor playability. Check preserves fold equity post-turn if checked behind (unlikely) or induces fold to check-raise (overkill here). Maximize stack by avoiding spew.

I raised preflop with Q9o and Elon called from BB. Flop is 7s Tc Ac - I missed completely but have a gutshot to a straight (need a J). This is a good c-bet spot: the Ace-high board favors my perceived range as the preflop raiser, and Elon checked to me. Standard continuation bet around 1/3 pot to apply pressure.

Facing a 200 bet into 750 pot (~3.75:1 odds post-call), requiring ~21% equity to call. 5s6h has no pair, no straight draw (board 7sTcAc blocks common straights), only weak backdoor spade draw. Equity vs Claude's CO c-bet range (Ax, broadways, sets, some bluffs) is ~12-18% max, insufficient even with pot odds, especially OOP with poor playability and reverse implied odds if I brick turn. Claude's history shows aggression (frequent raises/cbets, e.g., Hand 34 folded to resistance, Hand 33 value bet trips), but folding weak hands preserves stack in deep tournament (127BB eff). No fold equity from check-raise without blockers; check-call spews. Standard fold to avoid -EV spot early.

Qs7h is too weak and dominated to open from UTG in a 6-handed game, especially with several aggressive opponents behind who can 3-bet and put pressure on our ~26 BB stack. It has poor playability and reverse implied odds when we make a marginal queen. Best is to preserve chips for stronger opens/spots.

Q9o in middle position is a marginal hand. With a healthy stack of 16800 and only FiveTwo folded so far, I can make a standard open raise to try to steal the blinds. This is a borderline playable hand that I can raise with from MP, folding to significant resistance.

Cutoff with A♥8♣ facing a standard middle-seat open to 250. This is a playable, non‑polar hand — I prefer to keep the raiser and possible callers in my range and play post‑flop in position rather than 3‑bet or fold. Calling preserves my positional advantage vs Claude, keeps the pot size manageable (SPR will be ~15) and lets me realize equity against his opening range while extracting value from worse A‑x and speculative hands. A tiny 3‑bet to 400 won’t accomplish much and a larger 3‑bet risks getting 4‑bet by deeper stacks (Elon/Pro). Plan: call, evaluate the flop — barrel strong/advantageous textures and top pair/top kicker or strong draws; proceed cautiously on dry, uncoordinated boards.

I am on the button with Ace-Ten offsuit. This is a strong starting hand, particularly in position. Claude raised from middle position, and Minni called from the cutoff. ATo is ahead of a lot of Claude's opening range and plays well against a caller like Minni. While I could 3-bet here to try and take the pot down pre-flop, Claude has been very active and possesses a large stack, making a 4-bet possible. By calling, I maintain pot control, keep my options open, and ensure I play the rest of the hand with the advantage of position on both opponents.

Preflop in the small blind with K6o offsuit facing a middle position raise from Claude to 250 and calls from Minni (cutoff) and Flash (button), creating a multiway pot of 900 with only 200 to call. K6o has mediocre high card strength but poor playability multiway postflop, dominated by likely stronger king ranges from raiser and callers. Position is poor in SB, and Pro still to act in BB. Pot odds are decent (~4.5:1), but equity realization is low against three opponents. Folding preserves stack for better spots; calling leads to difficult postflop decisions with marginal hand.

I hold 7d 6d in the Big Blind. Facing a raise to 250 and two callers, the pot odds are extremely favorable. I need to call 150 chips into a pot that will be 1050 after my call, offering 7:1 odds. Suited connectors like 76s play well in multi-way pots due to their potential to make straights and flushes. Although being out of position against three opponents is difficult, the price is too good to fold a hand with this playability. A 3-bet squeeze is too risky against three opponents including an MP opener. I will simply call and try to hit the flop.

I am out of position in a multi-way pot (4 players) on a board of [Tc Ad 9s]. This texture hits the pre-flop raiser's range (Ax, broadways) and the callers' ranges very hard. My hand (7d 6d) has only 7-high and a weak gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) plus a backdoor flush draw. Leading into three opponents on an Ace-high board is not profitable. My plan is to check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as my draw is to the low end of the straight and my immediate showdown value is nil.

I flopped bottom pair (9s) with Q kicker on A-T-9 board. Against 3 opponents on an Ace-high board, someone likely has an Ace or better. My hand has some showdown value but is too weak to bet for value - I'd only get called by hands that beat me. Checking to pot control and hopefully reaching showdown cheaply is the best play here.

I have A♥8♣ on T♣ A♦ 9♠ — top pair but an unimpressive kicker. Both Claude (preflop raiser) and Pro checked the flop, so I should take the initiative in position to get value from worse A‑x, Tx, and to deny equity to open‑ender draws (QJ, J8, 78-type combos). A half‑pot bet protects my hand without bloating the pot when my kicker is vulnerable and keeps weaker hands/callers in that I can extract from on later streets. If I get called, I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check/call small bets; fold to heavy aggression if my kicker looks dominated). If I face a sizable raise, I will heavily consider folding given my marginal kicker.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Tens) on a somewhat coordinated board of Ad-Tc-9s. After the pre-flop action where four players saw the flop, the pot is around 1000 chips. Minni has led out for 525, which is roughly half the pot. While calling is an option to keep her range wide and maintain position, this board has several straight draws (QJ, KJ, 87) that I'd rather not give a free or cheap card to. Additionally, I can get significant value from weaker one-pair hands like AQ, AJ, or even AK if Minni is playing them this way. A raise here allows me to take control of the hand, protect my equity, and build a pot while I likely have the best hand. If I encounter a 3-bet from Claude or Pro behind me, I'll have to consider the possibility of a set of 99s, but for now, value-raising is the priority.

On the flop of [Tc Ad 9s], I hold a weak gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) with [7d 6d]. The action has escalated significantly: Minni bet 525 into a multi-way pot, and Flash raised to 1500. Flash's raise into three opponents signifies a very strong hand, likely two pair, a set, or a made straight. My gutshot draw has at most 4 outs (roughly 16% equity over two streets), but I am getting only about 2:1 pot odds on a call, requiring ~33% equity. Furthermore, the 8 could give someone else a higher straight (QJ). Calling 1500 chips (a large portion of my stack) with such low equity and terrible reverse implied odds is a clear mistake. I must fold.

Facing a bet from Minni (525) and a raise from Flash (1500) with just bottom pair (9s) and Q kicker on A-T-9 board. This is a multiway pot where I'm facing significant aggression. At least one opponent likely has an Ace, and Flash's raise over Minni's bet shows real strength. My bottom pair is crushed by any Ace, AT, A9, TT, 99, or even T9. I have only a gutshot to a straight (J gives me QJT98). Getting about 2:1 but I'm way behind here and will face more action on turn/river. Clear fold.

Flop: A♦ T♣ 9♠ — I have A♥8♣: top pair but a vulnerable kicker. I led with a small bet to get value and deny some equity; Flash on the button responded with a large raise to 1,500 which polarizes his range (value: sets, two‑pair, A9/AT, strong tens; bluffs/semi‑bluffs: KQ/QJ type combos). Folding here throws away value against bluffs and some worse Ax; raising would bloat the pot and commit me with a marginal kicker against a value‑heavy range. Calling (I must put in +975 more, totaling 1,500) keeps me in position, gives correct pot odds (~24% to call into the ~4,050 pot after my call) to realize equity vs a polarized range, and lets me re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: if I improve (8 → two pair) or the button checks back, press for value; if the button fires a large turn bet or jams, strongly consider folding unless I improve.

I have top pair (A♥8♣) with a vulnerable kicker on T♣ A♦ 9♠ 2♥. Flash polarised his flop raise to 1,500 and then checked the turn — this looks like pot control with a medium/strong holding or a timed‑check with some bluffs. In position I want to extract thin value from worse A‑x/T‑x and charge any turn floaters while keeping the pot manageable against a stronger range. I’ll make a small/medium value bet (~1,500 into 4,050, ~35–40% pot) to accomplish that: it gets called by worse aces and medium pairs, folds some air, and leaves room to fold to a large raise (my 8‑kicker is too weak to commit to a big confrontation). If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate the river and proceed cautiously (check/call or small value bet on a safe river; fold to polarized jams unless I improve).

I have top two pair (Aces and Tens) on a board of Ad-Tc-9s-2h. Pre-flop, I called a raise in position. On the flop, I raised Minni's lead to 1500 to protect my hand and gain value from weaker Aces (AJ, AQ, AK) and draws. Minni called the raise and has now 'donk' led into me on the turn for 1500. While a lead into the raiser often indicates strength (like a set of 9s or a straight with QJ), my hand is too strong to fold given the pot odds and the low Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR). I have 5000 chips remaining in my stack, and the pot is already over 5500. I am effectively committed to this hand. By raising all-in now, I maximize value from Ace-X hands that Minni might be overvaluing and I deny equity to any remaining draws like QJ or 87. If she does have me beat, I still have four outs to a full house.

Fold. Flash’s line — raising the flop and then making a very large turn raise to 5,000 — is highly polarised and very value‑heavy in a heads‑up spot (sets: AA/TT/99/22, two‑pair combos like AT/A9/T9, and strong A‑x). I have top pair with a very weak 8‑kicker; I’m dominated by the majority of that value range and my equity vs a value‑heavy polarization is well below the ~25% pot equity required to call 3,500 into a 10,550 pot. Raising to 6,750 would needlessly commit almost my entire stack against a range that crushes me. Preserve chips and wait for a cleaner spot — fold.

A6o from UTG is a borderline hand. It's playable in 6-max but has reverse implied odds if I hit an ace and face resistance. However, I've been maintaining an active image and have a healthy stack. I'll open raise to 250 as standard, consistent with my prior play.

Claude opened UTG to 250 — an early-position raise that represents a fairly strong range. I have 8♦5♦, a low suited connector with poor playability out of position. Calling 250 into a 400 pot gives only marginal pot odds (~2.6:1) and will likely turn into a multiway pot against players yet to act (Flash, Elon, the blinds), which drastically reduces my hand’s equity and implied odds. A 3-bet bluff is unattractive here given the players and stack depths (risk of a 4-bet or getting isolated out of position). With 73BB and plenty of hands to press with in better spots, the correct play is to fold and conserve chips.

Claude has been opening quite frequently from early positions. King-Nine suited is a strong enough hand to continue from the cutoff, offering both flush and straight potential along with high card value. While a 3-bet is an option, calling allows me to play a pot in position against an active opener without over-committing pre-flop.

49dd is a strong suited connector on the button with excellent position. Facing an UTG raise from Claude (who raises frequently but has a strong range) and a cutoff call from Flash (looser tendencies shown in history), calling 250 into 650 offers great pot odds (over 2.6:1 direct, plus strong implied odds multiway). Postflop playability is high with flush and straight potential, and button position allows controlling the pot effectively. Folding is too tight for this spot; 3-betting is unnecessary and risks bloating the pot OOP to Claude. Blinds may join, increasing dead money. Optimal to call and realize equity.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Threes (33). UTG raised to 2.5bb and there are two callers (Cutoff and Button). The pot odds are very attractive: I need to call 200 chips into a pot that will be 1100 before the BB acts, giving me better than 5:1 odds. My stack is deep enough (84bb) to set-mine profitably, as the implied odds are high in a multi-way pot if I hit a set. I will be out of position, but the strategy is simple: play fit-or-fold on the flop. The only major risk is a squeeze from the short-stacked BB, to which I would fold, but the price is too good to pass up for the potential reward.

We’re getting a very favorable price: 150 to win a pot of 1100 (and closing the action), needing roughly ~12% equity. Even though 62o is a very weak hand with poor realization multiway and out of position, the pot is already inflated and we can proceed with a strict fit-or-fold plan postflop (mostly check/fold when we miss). With ~25bb, calling doesn’t commit us and we preserve the option to win a big pot when we spike two pair/trips.

I am in the Small Blind with Pocket Threes on a flop of Ts-Kc-Jh. This is an extremely wet board that hits the ranges of the pre-flop raiser (Claude) and the multiple callers very hard (broadway straights, two pairs, sets). My underpair is essentially worthless here, and I have no draws. With four other players in the pot, the probability that someone has a strong hand is near 100%. My plan was to set-mine; I missed my set, so I must play 'fit or fold'. I will check and fold to any betting action.

We completely whiff this KJT flop (no pair, no meaningful straight draw). Multiway in an inflated pot, this texture smashes the preflop raiser and callers’ ranges, so a lead/bluff has very low fold equity and risks us getting floated/raised. With only a thin backdoor club possibility, the highest-EV line is to check and play fit-or-fold versus bets.

I have Ah6s and completely missed this Ts-Kc-Jh board. No pair, no draw (only a gutshot with Q needed). This is a very coordinated board with 4 opponents who called preflop. C-betting here with pure air into a multiway pot on a connected board would be -EV. Easy check.

I have top pair with a nine kicker on a very coordinated board (K-J-T). There are four other players in the pot, making it highly likely that someone has connected strongly with this texture, such as two-pair, a set, or a straight (AQ or Q9). Claude, the pre-flop aggressor, checked, which could indicate a trap or a marginal hand. By checking behind, I exercise pot control, avoid a potential check-raise on this dangerous board, and look to see a turn card for free while retaining the ability to evaluate the strength of my hand relative to the action on the next street.

Flop of TsKcJh is highly coordinated with broadway straight draws (Q9, AQ, etc.), pairs, and overpairs possible in multiway pot from wide preflop ranges (UTG raise + calls/limps). My 94dd completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (not even gutshot), only backdoor flush and straight draws. Everyone checking to button suggests weakness, but multiway with sticky opponents (history shows frequent check-calls), betting air risks calls from draws/pairs and commits chips OOP to Claude's range if raised. Position allows free turn card to improve or control pot cheaply. Checking realizes equity on backdoors while keeping pot small; can bluff missed turns or value bet improvements later. Betting too thin here reduces fold equity multiway and bloats pot with nothing.

I hold Pocket Threes on a board of Ts-Kc-Jh-Js. The board is now paired with Jacks and contains three broadway cards. My hand has virtually zero showdown value (counterfeited by the board and losing to any kicker higher than a 3). With four other players remaining in a 5-way pot, it is statistically certain that I am beaten, likely by trips, a straight, or even just high cards playing a better kicker. I have no reason to bet and will fold immediately to any aggression. Checking is the only viable option.

We have complete air (no pair, no straight/flush draw) on a very connected board that already hit a lot of ranges, and it’s still 5-way. After everyone checked the flop, the turn pairing the J reduces some two-pair but also increases the likelihood someone has Jx/Kx/Tx or a straight draw that won’t fold. With our 24bb stack, a stab risks too much for very low fold equity, and if we get called we’re essentially dead. Best EV is to check and take the free river, only considering a bluff later if action stays passive and we get a very favorable scare card/runout.

Board is Ts-Kc-Jh-Js. I have Ah6s - complete air with just ace high. The board is extremely coordinated with straights possible (AQ is the nuts), trips, two pairs. With 4 opponents who all checked, someone likely has a piece and is trapping, or we all have nothing. No reason to bet into this multiway pot with zero equity besides a lucky river ace. Check and see free river.

On the turn, the Jack pairs the board (Ts-Kc-Jh-Js). I currently have Kings and Jacks with a Ten kicker. While this is a decent hand, the board is extremely coordinated. Anyone with a Jack now has trips, and the straights (AQ and Q9) are still very possible. Given that the flop was checked around by five players and the turn has been checked to me again, I'm choosing to check behind for pot control. I also picked up a spade flush draw, giving me additional equity to see a free river card. Betting into four opponents on this texture with only two pair (Kings up) is risky, as I could easily be walking into a trap or a superior hand like a Jack or a slow-played straight.

Multiway pot on a highly coordinated flop (TsKcJh) that hit many broadway ranges, followed by a turn Js that pairs the board, potentially completing straights (e.g., QT, KQ) and trips (JJ, Jx). My 94dd has zero equity: no pair, no flush draw (backdoor diamonds only), no straight draw (94 doesn't connect). Universal checks pre-turn indicate weakness across the field, especially from aggressive raiser Claude and sticky callers like FiveTwo/Pro/Flash (per history of frequent check-calls even light). On button, checking behind realizes minimal showdown value cheaply, induces bluffs on river from missed draws, and allows selective value/bluff on river improvements or whiffs. Betting pure air multiway risks calls from pairs/top pair/draws (opponents rarely fold), bloating pot OOP to Claude if raised, and commits chips without fold equity against stationy lines seen (e.g., Hand #36 Minni/Flash aggression but prior check-calls). Pot control prioritizes survival and stack maximization in early tournament; better spots await with deep effective stacks.

I hold Pocket Threes on a board of Ts-Kc-Jh-Js-4c. My hand is Two Pair (Jacks and Threes) with a King kicker. While the action has been completely passive with checks on the flop and turn, this is a 5-way pot. It is highly likely that at least one of my four opponents holds a Ten, a King, a Four, a Jack, or a pocket pair higher than Threes (e.g., 55-99). Betting would likely only get called by better hands and fold out worse ones (missed straight draws like Q9 or AQ if they didn't have the straight). There is no value in betting. Checking allows me to get to showdown cheaply or fold if someone bets, as I cannot withstand any aggression.

We have pure air (6-high) on a paired, high-card, very connected board (K-T-J-J-4) in a 5-way pot. After Pro checks, any river bluff from us has to get through three players still to act (Claude/Flash/Elon), and in a multiway checked-down line it’s very likely someone holds at least a Tx/Kx/Jx that will call even a small stab. Our fold equity is too low and the downside (getting called and torching chips with ~24bb) outweighs the upside of stealing 1250. Best EV is to take the free showdown and check.

River with Ah6s on Ts-Kc-Jh-Js-4c. I have ace-high which has some showdown value. Everyone has checked through all streets, indicating weak holdings across the board. Betting as a bluff would require folding out 4 players which is very unlikely - anyone with a T, K, J, or pocket pair calls. My ace-high might actually be good at showdown against missed draws. Check for free showdown.

The board is highly coordinated (Ts Kc Jh Js 4c), and with five players in the pot, there is a significant risk that someone holds a Jack for trips, a Queen for a straight, or a King with a better kicker (like AK or KQ). Since the flop and turn were checked around by everyone, it's likely that the hand strengths are marginal across the board. My hand (Kings and Jacks with a Ten kicker) is relatively strong, but betting into three opponents on this board texture is unlikely to result in a call from a worse hand. I will check behind to take the hand to showdown and minimize risk in a pot that hasn't seen much action.

River 4c pairs my 4d for bottom pair (4s with 9 kicker), improving from pure air. However, on this highly coordinated board (Ts Kc Jh Js 4c) with broadway straights (e.g., QT, AQ for TJQKJ), trips (Jx), top pairs (Kx, Qx possible), and pairs improved, bottom pair remains very marginal multiway against UTG raiser Claude's strong range and sticky limpers/callers (history of frequent check-calls, e.g., Hands #2,3,14,15). Universal checks flop/turn/river indicate weakness/capped hands, likely missed draws or ace-high. On button, betting thin (low fold equity vs stations like FiveTwo/Pro/Flash) risks bloating pot OOP if raised by Claude or called by better; checking realizes equity cheaply at showdown (possibly best vs bluffs/ace-high), preserves deep stack (12450) for tournament progression (hand 37/150), and maintains pot control theme from prior streets. Superior to marginal value bet with poor EV against ranges.

Under the gun with A♦3♣ in 6‑handed play is a marginal spot. The hand is frequently dominated by other A‑x and is difficult to play out of position against several deep, capable players behind (Elon, Claude, Flash). I could limp or open‑raise to 250–300 to try to steal, but that invites 3‑bets or tough postflop decisions with a weak kicker. With 73BB I’ll preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

I am holding 9-8 offsuit in middle position. While 9-8 can be a decent speculative hand when suited, the offsuit version is significantly weaker and generally considered a fold in a 6-max game from this position. With several active and aggressive players left to act behind me, including a short-stacked FiveTwo in the small blind who might look to shove, there is no reason to enter the pot with a sub-par hand. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop, in cutoff with TsJh suited connectors, which is a strong hand for opening from late position. Folds from UTG and MP, blinds are short SB (2350) and deep BB. Standard open-raise size around 2.5BB (250 total) to build pot and take initiative. Position advantage postflop, good playability. Min-raise is 200 but larger for isolation/value. Button (Pro) may defend wide, but still profitable spot.

I hold A8o on the Button facing a raise from the Cutoff (Elon). While A8o has some equity, it is generally dominated by a Cutoff opening range (which includes AT+, pairs, better suited connectors). Elon has been active, but calling risks a squeeze from the aggressive Big Blind (Claude) or the short-stacked Small Blind (FiveTwo). 3-betting is an option but slightly loose. Folding is the most standard, low-variance play to preserve my stack of 82.5 BB for better spots.

Facing a CO open to 2.5bb from the small blind with 23.5bb effective, 9h2h is too weak to defend: it’s dominated when we make a pair, has poor playability out of position, and we still have the BB behind who can squeeze. The pot odds aren’t enough to justify a loose call here, and it’s not a good 3-bet candidate at this stack depth. Best is to conserve chips and fold.

Pocket 66 in BB facing a standard open from Elon in the cutoff. Getting 400:150 pot odds with deep stacks (both have 12K+). This is a classic set-mining spot with excellent implied odds. Calling to see a flop.

I have pocket 6s on an 8s-3d-5d board. I didn't hit my set but I have an underpair to the 8. This is a dry board with a diamond draw. As the preflop caller out of position, checking to the preflop raiser is standard. If Elon c-bets small, I can consider calling with my pocket pair given the low board texture. If he bets large, I should fold as his value range beats me.

Preflop, I raised from cutoff with JTo offsuit (decent speculative hand with position). BB (Claude) defended, which is wide given history of calling stations. Flop 8s-3d-5d is low, disconnected, with a diamond flush draw. My hand has no pair, no strong draw (backdoor spade and straight possibilities), but as preflop aggressor with position, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. The board favors my wide CO opening range (overpairs, overs, some pairs), and Claude's BB calling range hits this flop poorly (few strong hands). Betting applies fold equity, denies equity to draws/improvers, and builds pot if called with worse. Bet sizing at ~2/3 pot (375) balances value/thin value against floats/draws while keeping pot manageable OOP stacks deep.

I have pocket 6s on an 8-3-5 two-diamond board. Elon c-bet 375 into ~550, a standard sizing. My 6s are an underpair to the 8 but beat all his overcard bluffs (AK, AQ, KQ, etc.). Getting ~2.5:1 odds (375 to win 925). Board texture favors my range as the BB caller. I'll call and re-evaluate on the turn - if a scare card comes or he barrels big, I can fold. If he checks, I can potentially get to showdown.

Turn brought the 9c - a blank that doesn't complete the diamond draw but adds another overcard to my 66. I now have an underpair to both the 8 and 9 on board. With pot at 1300 and check to me, standard line is to check. If Elon checks back, I can potentially win at showdown against his AK/AQ type hands. If he barrels again, I'll likely have to fold as my pair is weak on this runout.

On the turn, the 9c completes some straight draws (6-7, 10-J) and adds a backdoor club draw, but my JTo picks up an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to 7 or Q for the nuts or near-nuts Broadway). As preflop raiser with position, my range is perceived as strong (overs, pairs, sets, flush draws). Claude has defended wide preflop/BB, called flop cbet on a draw-heavy board, and checked again, consistent with his calling station tendencies from history (frequent check-calls with marginal holdings). Betting continues to apply pressure, denies equity to his floats/worse draws/pairs, gets value from weaker made hands (pairs, weaker draws), and builds the pot OOP for him while my equity is decent (~35-40% if called). Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and misses value. Size at 2/3 pot (~850) balances thin value/protection/fold equity without overbetting into potential monsters.

I have pocket 6s on 8-3-5-9 board - now an underpair to both the 8 and 9. Elon has bet flop and now fires 850 into 1300 on turn (~65% pot). My 66 only beats pure bluffs at this point. His continuing aggression suggests real strength - overpairs, top pair (A8, 98, T9), or sets. Getting 2.5:1 odds needing ~28% equity, but against his value-heavy turn barrel range I'm crushed. Time to fold and save chips.

I am in the UTG position with Ten-Six offsuit (Ts, 6d). This is an extremely weak hand and should not be played from early position in a 6-max game. With a healthy stack of 133 big blinds, there is no reason to enter the pot with trash cards. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in middle position with 4h5c, an offsuit low connector. This hand has poor playability, low equity multiway, and doesn't warrant entering the pot voluntarily from this spot. Multiple players behind, including tight players like Claude and others with deep stacks. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better opportunities in a deepstack tournament.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Four offsuit (K4o). This is a weak hand, even for a late-position steal. The player on the Button (FiveTwo) is short-stacked (approx 23 BBs) and might shove over a raise. The Small Blind (Claude) is the chip leader and has been active, and the Big Blind (Minni) is also capable of defending. Given the potential for re-raises and the poor playability of K4o post-flop, folding is the standard and most profitable decision here.

Folded to us on the Button with JTo and ~23.5bb. This is comfortably within a standard button opening range and we have strong positional advantage versus the blinds. Raising rather than limping maximizes fold equity (steal the blinds), denies equity to the blinds’ random holdings, and keeps initiative for postflop. With our stack depth, a small-but-clean open sizing is ideal to avoid overcommitting while still pressuring wide defenses.

T9o in the SB facing a button raise of 250. This is a decent defending hand against a button steal - good playability with straight potential. Getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400). FiveTwo has a short stack (2100), so 3-betting could induce a shove I don't want to call. Calling is the right play here to see a flop in position relative to the BB.

I’m on the big blind with 2♠8♦ — a very weak offsuit hand. Button opened to 250 and the small blind has already called, so this is likely to be a multiway pot where my hand is commonly dominated and has almost no postflop playability out of position. Although the call is only 150 (pot odds ~20%), multiway equity with 2‑8o is well below that threshold and raising to isolate would be reckless with this holding. Preserving my 7200 stack for better spots is the best option here.

Flopped top pair with T9o on 8s5dTd. I have top pair decent kicker plus a gutshot straight draw (need J). FiveTwo has only ~1850 behind after the preflop raise. I should bet for value - this is a strong hand against their button raising range. Betting around 250-300 to build the pot and get value from overcards, draws, and worse pairs.

Claude’s small donk (275 into 875) from the SB after flatting pre is very often a capped/merged range: Tx worse kickers, 8x/5x, diamond draws, and some stabs. With TPTK-ish (top pair, strong-ish kicker) and only ~1.9k effective behind, I want to take control of the hand, charge the flush/straight draws, and build a pot where we can comfortably jam most turns. Raising rather than calling also denies equity and prevents tough multi-street decisions versus random overcards/diamonds.

I have top pair (tens) with a 9 kicker plus a gutshot straight draw. FiveTwo raised my c-bet to 850, leaving them with only 1250 behind. Pot is 1725 and I need 575 to call. Getting ~3:1 odds. Their range includes better tens (JT, QT, KT, AT), sets, two pair, flush draws, and bluffs. With their short stack, they're essentially committed. I could shove but calling keeps the pot controlled - if I'm behind to a set or two pair, I don't want to bloat it further. If they have a draw or worse ten, the money goes in on later streets anyway. Call and reevaluate.

Turn paired the 5s, giving me tens and fives with a 9 kicker. FiveTwo has only 1250 behind with 2300 in the pot - they're essentially pot committed. The paired board reduces flush draw combos slightly. I should bet to get value from worse tens and draws while putting them to a decision. Betting around 700 puts them in an awkward spot where calling commits most of their stack. If they have a better ten or set, I'm losing anyway, but I can't check-fold top pair here.

We have two pair (TT and 55) on a very bloated pot (3000) with only 1250 total. Facing Claude’s small turn lead (700) after donk/calling the flop raise, his range is still wide: lots of Tx, 8x, diamond draws, and some 5x/boats. With this SPR, if we just call we’ll have 550 left into ~3700 and will be effectively forced to put it in on most rivers anyway—while also letting his draws realize for a cheap price when they miss (they may not pay the extra 550). Shoving maximizes value versus Tx/8x that feel priced in, charges diamond draws, and prevents him from seeing a river for only 700. We’re getting called very often due to his pot odds, and the protection/value outweighs the downside of running into the top of his range (5x/boats).

FiveTwo is all-in (0 chips remaining). Pot is 4250 and I need 550 to call - getting ~7.7:1 odds. I have top pair with decent kicker on a paired board. Even if they have a better ten or made two pair, the pot odds make this an easy call. I only need ~12% equity to break even, and my hand is way stronger than that against their range.

UTG in a 6-handed tournament with AcJd offsuit. This is a standard opening hand from early position: strong ace, good playability, blockers to top pairs. Standard raise size of 2.5x the BB (250) to build the pot, apply pressure, and narrow the field. Short stack in cutoff (FiveTwo 24BB) might shove, but AJ has good equity against shoving ranges. Effective stacks are deep except FiveTwo, so fold equity is decent. Folding would be too tight pre-flop.

I am in Middle Position facing a standard 2.5x raise from Elon in UTG. My hand is A8o. Elon has been opening frequently from early position, which might suggest a slightly wider range, but A8o performs poorly against a typical UTG opening range (often dominated by AT+, AJ, AQ, AK and pairs 88+). Additionally, calling leaves me vulnerable to squeezes from the players behind me, including the short-stacked FiveTwo or the deep-stacked Claude and Flash. Playing A8o out of position or sandwiched in a multi-way pot is a losing strategy long-term. The disciplined play here is to fold and wait for a better spot.

KJs is too strong to fold in the cutoff versus a 2.5bb UTG open, especially with position and decent playability. With ~24bb, a non-all-in 3-bet would risk committing a large chunk of my stack and plays poorly versus a 4-bet jam, while an outright shove is likely to get called by a strong range (AQ+/TT+) where KJs is dominated too often. Flatting keeps the pot controlled, realizes equity in position, and lets me proceed with straightforward postflop decisions.
75o is a weak holding. Even on the button with position, this hand doesn't play well enough to call facing a raise and a caller. Not suited, poor connectivity. Easy fold.

I’m in the small blind with KcTd facing an UTG raise to 250 and a caller — a 3-way pot is likely. KTo is marginal but defendable from the SB: I only need to put in 200 more (250 total) into a 650 pot, which gives ~23.5% break-even equity to continue. 3-betting/squeezing here with KTo OOP against an aggressive UTG raiser and a caller is too loose and can commit too many chips without a strong hand or initiative. Call 250 keeps the pot controllable, preserves stack depth to play postflop, and lets me fold to heavy action if I miss or continue when I hit top pair/strong draws. I’ll re-evaluate on the flop and will fold to significant pressure if the board is unfavorable.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9-5 offsuit. The UTG player (Elon) opened to 2.5bb, and both the Cutoff (FiveTwo) and Small Blind (Minni) have called. I am being offered pot odds of approximately 5.6 to 1 (150 to call into a pot of 850). While these odds are mathematically enticing, 9-5 offsuit is a bottom-tier hand that realizes its equity very poorly in a four-way pot, especially out of position. My hand is easily dominated by the ranges of three other players, and I will likely be forced to fold on most flops unless I hit a miracle two-pair or better. Given the strength of the UTG range and the risk of being trapped against the short-stacked Cutoff, folding is the most disciplined and professional play here.

Check. I defended KTo preflop but the flop 8♥ Q♠ 2♣ completely misses — I only have a lone K overcard and no meaningful draws. Pot is 850 with two opponents and SPR is high (~8), so I want to keep the pot small OOP and see what the preflop aggressor (and the short stack) do. Betting here as a bluff in a multiway spot is too risky and invites raises; checking lets me realize equity for free and re-evaluate on the turn. Plan: if faced with a small c‑bet I’ll consider a call to control the pot and fold to heavy pressure; if checked to me again I’ll continue to the turn and look to value-bet if I pair up (K or T) or bluff-shove only on clear signs of weakness.

Preflop raiser with AJ on a Q-8-2 rainbow flop, a dry board that favors my perceived range (overpairs, Qx, sets). Minni checks as first to act, showing weakness. AJ has two overcards for equity realization if called, plus fold equity against calling ranges that may have dominated broadways, suited connectors, or pocket pairs below queens. FiveTwo is short-stacked (21BB effective) and may fold or shove, but my hand has decent equity vs shoves. C-bet 60% pot to take it down often or build pot when ahead, maintaining aggression from UTG raise. Checking risks giving free cards to draws and loses value from worse hands.

We have KJ-high on a very dry Q-8-2 rainbow board facing a large ~60% pot c-bet from UTG into two players. In this configuration UTG’s betting range is heavily weighted toward strong one-pair+ hands (AQ/KQ/QJ, overpairs, sets) and our hand has no direct draw—only a few “pairing” outs (K or J) that are often dominated and won’t realize well with the low SPR (if we call, SPR will be < 1 and we’ll be forced into tough/losing turn decisions). A bluff-raise/jam is unlikely to generate enough folds versus an UTG multiway sizing, and Minni can still continue behind. Best play is to preserve the remaining ~21bb for a better spot.

Fold. I checked the flop with K♣T♦ and Elon (UTG) fires 500 into a 850 pot — calling 500 to win 1350 requires ~37% equity. I have no realistic draws and only ~6 clean outs to pair K or T (~24% to hit by river), so calling is a losing long‑term play. Raising as a bluff OOP is too thin against an aggressive UTG raiser who barrels often. I’ll preserve my 6.95k stack and wait for a better spot rather than pay off with a marginal hand.

I am in UTG position with J8 offsuit. This is a statistically weak hand that does not warrant an opening raise or a limp from early position, especially with 5 other players left to act. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better hand or position.

We’re effectively first-in from middle position with Kh6s offsuit and ~21.5BB. K6o is dominated by the Kx that continue (and by a lot of suited Broadways/Ax), plays poorly postflop, and isn’t a good candidate to open/raise in this seat—especially with strong, deeper stacks behind (Claude/Minni/Flash/Elon) who can call/3-bet and put us in tough stack-to-pot spots. Limping is also -EV with this stack depth. Best is to preserve chips for better opens/shoves.

J3o is a weak offsuit hand that's not profitable to open from the cutoff. Even with only 3 players behind, this hand doesn't have good playability post-flop and will often be dominated when called. Folding is the standard play here.

I’m on the button with 8h9h — a suited connector with strong post‑flop playability and the positional advantage. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; this is a good spot to take the initiative against the blinds rather than limp and create a multiway pot. I raise to 300 (≈3x BB): big enough to pick up folds and avoid too many callers, but small enough to keep the pot playable heads‑up. If I get called I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable heart/connected boards and control the size of the pot; if I face a large 3‑bet I’ll re‑evaluate (fold to very large 3‑bets, call or flat vs moderate ones).

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 2c, which is one of the weakest possible hands in Hold'em. Minni, on the Button, has opened with a 3x raise. Given my extremely poor hand strength and the fact that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if I play, folding is the only viable strategic option to preserve my stack.

Preflop in BB facing a button raise to 300 after multiple folds. Minni likely has a wide stealing range from the button. My 6h8d is offsuit with moderate playability (gutshot potential, live cards), but positionally disadvantaged OOP postflop. Pot odds are excellent: calling 200 into 450 (pot becomes 650), getting over 3:1 immediate odds plus deep stack implied odds (my 13625, Minni 6650). Equity vs a wide button range (40-50% hands) is around 35-40%, sufficient to defend profitably in multiway potential or heads-up. Folding too tight early in tournament; calling to realize equity and control pot OOP with speculative hand. No raise value, fold too weak.

Preflop called a wide button steal range OOP with speculative 68o for multiway potential and implied odds, but flop QcKs3s completely misses: no pair, no straight draw (gutshot unlikely), no flush draw (off two spades). Board favors Minni's button range heavily (KQ, Kx, Qx, suited broadways, Ax suited spades, connectors). As OOP heads-up, betting has no value and bluff equity poor vs polarized button continuation range. Checking to induce bluffs or check-behind but plan to fold to bet. Realizing preflop equity was speculative; no showdown value here. Pot 650 small relative to stacks (effective 6650), no need to commit without hit. Check-fold maximizes EV long-term.

I’m the preflop aggressor (raised to 300) and the flop is dry (Qc Ks 3s) — a great spot to take the initiative. I have no pair or meaningful draw but many of the Big Blind’s defend/call hands (small pairs, missed broadways, Ax) will fold to a compact continuation bet. Bet sizing: the pot is 650, so a ~1/3–40% pot probe (~250) buys the pot a lot of folds while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: if he folds we take it down; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (pot-control with no pair, only continue aggressively on heart/connected runs or obvious bluffs); if he check‑raises large I will fold this hand without investing more (no pair/no clean equity).

Facing a small continuation bet from Minni on the button after I defended wide preflop OOP with 68o. The flop QcKs3s is a complete whiff for my hand: no pair, no straight draw, no flush draw (heart and diamond). Board heavily favors her wide button range (KQ, Kx, Qx, suited Ax/Kx/Qx, broadway, spade draws). Her 250 bet into ~650 pot is ~38% pot, standard for value or as a probe. Calling 250 gets ~4.6:1 odds (pot 1150), requiring ~18% equity, but vs her range I have <10% (pure bluff catcher or dominated). Raising has poor fold equity OOP against a player who has shown aggression (e.g., Hand 14,15 wins; Hand 36 big bets); stacks deep (effective 6400), but no story for 68o as strength. Preflop plan was speculative defend with implied odds/multiway potential, but heads-up miss means check-fold maximizes EV long-term, preserving 13425 stack for 110 hands left in tournament.

5♣2♣ is far below a profitable UTG open/limp range 6-handed, especially at ~21.5bb effective where we can’t realize equity well and will often face isolation/3-bets. Best EV is to preserve stack for better steal/reshove spots rather than enter multiway out of position with a dominated suited wheel.

Q7 suited in middle position is a marginal hand. With 4 players still to act behind me, this is typically a fold. While suited, the hand lacks connectivity and the queen is easily dominated. Standard fold from this position.

5♠3♣ is a very weak, unplayable hand — especially out of position against a button and the blinds still to act. Calling 100 into a 150 pot just to see a flop invites multiway action and domination; raising would be reckless. With a healthy 73bb stack I should preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position. Fold.

I am on the button with 8h 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Although it's folded to me and I have the opportunity to steal the blinds, this hand has very little post-flop equity or playability if called. Given my current stack size and the stage of the tournament, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with garbage hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folded to me in the small blind with KTo offsuit against the big blind only. This is a standard stealing spot in a tournament. KTo has good equity heads-up and decent postflop playability. Pro in BB has folded to raises before and their stack is playable but not short. Raising to 2.5x the BB (250 total) applies pressure, builds the pot with position postflop if called, and frequently steals the dead money. Folding is too tight, limping is suboptimal as it invites multiway pots or check-raises.

In a Blind vs Blind scenario, KJs is a premium hand. Facing a standard open raise to 2.5bb from the Small Blind, I have excellent equity and position. While 3-betting is a valid option to build the pot immediately, calling allows me to keep the opponent's range wide, including his bluffs, and play a pot in position with a strong hand. Given the stack depth of over 80BB, calling minimizes variance and maximizes playability post-flop.

Preflop raise with KTo from SB was a standard steal attempt against a single defender in the BB, as it has decent equity and playability heads-up. However, the flop of 3h-4d-4h completely misses my range: no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (T-high doesn't connect). The board is paired low cards with two hearts, which favors Pro's BB calling range including small pairs, suited connectors like 54s/43s/65s, heart draws, or even Ax suited. As the preflop aggressor OOP, betting air here risks folding out better hands or getting check-raised/floated; checking allows me to realize equity when behind, pot control, and fold to aggression without bloating the pot with a polarized range. Pro may check weak holdings behind, giving a free card, or bet and I can fold profitably. Tournament stage: deep stacks relative to blinds (13175 effective), no need to bluff recklessly early in hand 42/150. Check-fold is optimal.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc Jc on a 3h 4d 4h board. My opponent (SB) raised pre-flop and has now checked to me. The board is paired and low, which connects reasonably well with a BB defending range, but I currently only hold King-high with overcards. Betting here doesn't accomplish much; it unlikely folds out Ace-high or small pocket pairs, and I risk getting check-raised off my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity (6 outs to top pair), keeps the pot controllable, and lets me re-evaluate on the turn. I have sufficient showdown value against total air to justify a check.

Turn board 9s is a relatively blank card that doesn't significantly improve my specific hand (KhTs remains king-high air with no draws) and may complete some straight draws like 56 or 78 that could be in Pro's BB defending range. As preflop raiser OOP who checked the flop (correctly pot-controlling air on a board favoring defender's range), leading now would represent a polarized range (sets/two-pair or bluffs/draws), but Pro's flop check-back suggests weakness or trapping, and his range (small pairs, suited connectors/hearts, Ax) crushes this board texture better than mine. Bluffing OOP reduces fold equity due to position disadvantage and my capped perceived range post-flop check. Effective stacks are deep (my 13175, his 8000 vs pot 500), so realizing equity when behind by checking is superior to risking chips on a low-equity bluff (~20-25% vs calling range). If Pro checks again, free river to potentially bluff or showdown K-high; if he bets, easy profitable fold. Preserves stack for better spots in tournament.

I hold Kc Jc on a turn board of 3h 4d 4h 9s. The pot is 500. My opponent (SB) has checked to me twice. I have King-high, which has some showdown value against missed straight draws or lower high-card hands, and I have 6 outs (Kings and Jacks) to likely make the best hand on the river. Betting here turns my hand into a bluff and might isolate me against better hands like Ace-high or small pairs that will call, while folding out hands I already beat. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting top pair on the river or winning at showdown with King-high if the opponent is on a pure bluff/give-up. This is a low-variance line suitable for a small pot.

River 4c completes a monstrous board with three 4s (4d/4h/4c), alongside 3h/9s, making trips standard and quads possible with pocket 4s. My KhTs is pure king-high bluff-catcher air, with zero showdown equity against Pro's BB defending range (low pairs like 22/55/66, suited connectors 23s-56s, heart draws, A4s/43s, or Ax suited), which connects massively here - he likely holds at least a 4, 3x, 9x, or paired low card for showdown value or better. Preflop I raised as a standard SB steal, but checking flop/turn correctly pot-controlled and realized equity on a defender-favorable texture; now OOP on river, betting any amount polarizes my capped range (no sets/two-pair likely after checks) into bluffs, but fold equity is minimal as Pro hero-calls light or traps with monsters on this dry runout. Pot 500 is tiny vs my 13k stack and 150-hand tournament (hand 42/150, deep effective), so checking preserves chips, induces bluffs from worse nothing (unlikely), shows down king-high potentially winning vs pure air, or folds profitably to bets (his range bets trips+ aggressively). No value to bet, low EV bluff. Check is optimal pot control/extraction denial.

The board has run out [3h, 4d, 4h, 9s, 4c], putting three fours on the board. My hand is K-J, giving me Trip Fours with a King-Jack kicker. On this texture, any pocket pair makes a Full House (4-4-4-P-P), and any 3 or 9 also makes a Full House. Additionally, any Ace beats me (Trip Fours with Ace kicker). Given the opponent (SB) raised pre-flop and then checked all three streets, his range is capped but likely contains plenty of Ace-highs or small pocket pairs attempting to get to showdown cheaply. It is unlikely I can fold out a Full House (even a small one like 22-66) or an Ace-high with a bet in this small pot. My hand beats his pure bluffs (Q-high, J-high, etc.). Therefore, turning my hand into a bluff has low expected value as better hands calling frequency is likely high given the board texture. Checking back realizes my equity against his absolute air and loses the minimum against his showdown value.
T2o from UTG is a clear fold. This is one of the weakest starting hands and I'm in early position where I need to play a tight range. With 157BB stack, I can afford to wait for better spots.

7♠9♦ is a weak, unsuited, marginal hand in middle position. I’m facing a preflop commitment (to call 100) with several players yet to act and likely facing a raiser with a much stronger opening range. There’s not enough pot or implied odds to justify calling or turning this into a bluff/3-bet — raising would be high variance and I don’t have position or fold equity against aggressive opponents (Elon/Flash). Preserve my 7.3k stack and wait for a better spot in late position or with a stronger/suited connector hand.

I am in the Cutoff with King-Eight offsuit (K8o). While it's folded to me and there's an opportunity to steal the blinds, K8o is a marginal hand. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) is sitting on a short stack of about 20 big blinds, which makes them very likely to shove over an opening raise. If they do shove, I would be in a difficult spot mathematically and likely behind their range. Additionally, Elon on the button has a large stack and has been playing aggressively. Folding is the safest and most disciplined play here to wait for a better opening candidate.

Preflop on the button with 2c8h offsuit, one of the worst possible hands. No high cards, no suitedness, poor connectivity, and terrible postflop playability. Even though folded to me and button is a good stealing spot with dead blinds money (pot 150 to call 100 effectively), 28o is outside any reasonable opening range. Typical BTN open is ~40-50% of hands, but starts from better low offsuit like A2o+, K5o+, and suited connectors; 28o is bottom ~5% and dominated by any defense. BB FiveTwo has only 20BB and likely defends wide/shoves, SB Pro solid stack. Risk equity loss postflop not worth 2.5:1 immediate odds. Preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with J4s, and action has folded to me. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) has a relatively short stack (20.5 BB), which increases the likelihood of a shove if I show weakness or raise. J4s is a below-average hand, often considered a fold in SB vs BB charts, or occasionally a mix. Being out of position with a weak hand against a short stack who might be looking for spots to double up or shove is risky. Preserving my stack of ~84 BB for better spots is prudent. I will fold.

Holding 3♣2♦ offsuit UTG in a 6-max game is the bottom of my range. To call 100 into a 150 pot and play out of position against five players (many of whom are deep-stacked and likely to raise) is a negative EV line — the hand has almost no showdown value and poor postflop playability. With 73bb I can wait for a much better spot. Fold to preserve my stack and pick a better opportunity later.

I am holding Ace-Jack suited in middle position. This is a very strong starting hand in a 6-max game. Following the table's standard opening size of 2.5x the big blind, I will raise to 250 to take the initiative and thin the field. My goal is to extract value or take the pot down pre-flop while maintaining a solid range from this position.

9d5d is a suited connector with some potential for straights, flushes, and two-pair hands. In cutoff position facing a standard raise from middle position (Flash, who has shown aggression but also vulnerability post-flop in history), calling is optimal. Deep stacks provide good implied odds if I connect. Raising risks too much with a speculative hand, and folding wastes position and playability. Will see flops in position and control pot.

I am on the Button with 3d 8d facing a raise from Flash (Middle Position) and a call from Elon (Cutoff). The pot is 650 and it costs 250 to call. Although I have position and the hand is suited, 8-3 suited is a very weak hand with poor playability and reverse implied odds, especially in a multi-way pot against an opener and a caller. The pot odds of roughly 28% are not sufficient for a hand this weak. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

We’re in the small blind with Q6o facing a 2.5x open plus a cold call, with the big blind still behind. Even getting ~3.25:1 immediate odds (200 to win 650), Q6o performs poorly multiway and plays terribly out of position, and we’re shallow enough (21.5bb) that postflop mistakes are costly. A squeeze/jam is far too loose here because we’ll get called often enough by better queens, pairs, and broadways. Best stack-preserving decision is to let it go.

J8 suited in the big blind with 150 to call into a pot of 650. I'm getting over 4:1 odds with a suited hand that has decent playability - potential for flushes, straights, and pairs. Easy call in this spot.

I flopped an open-ended straight draw with J8 (Q or 7 completes it). Board is 2c-Tc-9s. I'm first to act with two opponents behind me. With ~31% equity to hit by the river, I could semi-bluff, but checking seems prudent here - see a free card and avoid getting raised off my draw. If I hit, I can extract value; if I miss, I can reassess.

I started the hand with a strong semi-premium hand (Ac Jc) and raised pre-flop. The flop (2c Tc 9s) provides me with two overcards and a backdoor nut flush draw. Since Claude (the Big Blind) checked, I will lead out with a continuation bet of 450 (approximately 56% of the pot). This bet allows me to maintain the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately if my opponents missed, and builds the pot for when I improve on later streets.

Flop board is 2c Tc 9s, giving me middle pair (9s) with a weak 5 kicker and minimal backdoor draws. Flash's preflop raise and c-bet sizing of 450 into a ~800 pot (now 1250) suggests a standard continuation range: overpairs, top pair (Tx), sets, or draws (clubs, gutshots like J8). My hand has decent blocker to 98 and position for free cards or realizing equity later. Pot odds are favorable (~3.7:1), requiring ~21% equity, and heads-up vs Flash's polarized range, I have around 40% equity (blocks strong 9x, pairs well vs bluffs). Claude's check suggests weakness, unlikely to continue strongly multiway. Calling keeps pot controlled, realizes equity in position, and avoids overcommitting with a marginal one-pair hand. Raising risks isolation vs better or getting check-raised off equity; folding wastes playability given implied odds deep-stacked (stacks ~12k+). Will re-evaluate turn.

I have J8h with board 2c-Tc-9s - giving me an open-ended straight draw (Q or 7 completes). Pot is 1700, need 450 to call, getting ~3.8:1 odds. With 8 outs (~17% on turn), I'm slightly short on direct odds but implied odds are great if I hit. Both opponents are deep-stacked. Easy call to chase this draw.

I have J8h with an open-ended straight draw (Q or 7 completes). The turn brought 5c, putting 3 clubs on board - a dangerous texture since I don't have any clubs. I'm first to act with 0 to call. Best play is to check - I want to see a free card if possible with my ~17% equity to hit the straight. If someone bets, I'll need to evaluate pot odds. Betting here as a semi-bluff is risky with two opponents and a flush-possible board.

I have successfully made the nut flush on the turn with the Ace of Clubs. This is currently the best possible hand. Since Claude has checked, I will lead out with a value bet of 1300 into the 2150 pot. This sizing is intended to extract value from sets, two-pair combinations, or smaller flushes that my opponents might be holding, while building a pot that allows for a potential all-in on the river.

The turn 5c perfectly pairs my 5d for two pair (nines and fives), significantly improving my hand from middle pair on the flop. This board now features a completed club flush draw (2c-Tc-5c), an open-ended straight draw potential (67, 78, J8 for gutshots), but my two pair is strong against most of Flash's preflop raising range that continued with a flop c-bet and now a 1300 bet into 3450 (~38% pot). Likely Flash range: strong made hands like sets (22/TT/99/55), top pair Tx top kicker or better, T9 two pair, or made flushes (AcXc, KcQc, etc.); semi-bluffs with club draws (e.g., Ac4c, Jc8c); or polarized bluffs (missed overcards like AK, AQ). I block 99 and 9d reduces straight draw combos. Equity vs this range is strong (~60-70% vs non-nutted hands, 0% vs flushes/sets), and pot odds are excellent (3450 to 1300 = ~2.65:1, needing only ~23% equity, plus multiway implied odds with deep stacks). Claude's check after check-calling flop suggests weakness (possible weak pair/draw), unlikely to have nuts. Calling maintains pot control, realizes equity in position (acting after Flash, before Claude), induces bluffs from Flash, keeps worse hands like Tx, 9x, draws, or overpairs in, and avoids overcommitting to potential monsters while preserving stack for better spots. Raising could extract value from draws but risks isolating vs nuts or folding out equity; shoving too aggressive deep. Plan: call, then bet river for value if checked to or call down reasonably.

I have J8h with an open-ended straight draw (Q or 7 completes). The turn 5c put 3 clubs on board. Flash bet 1300, Elon called. I need 1300 to call into 4750 pot = ~21.5% equity needed. I have 8 straight outs but 2 of them (Qc, 7c) would complete potential flushes for opponents. So really 6 clean outs = ~13% equity. Both opponents showed strength on this flush-heavy board. Pot odds don't support the call with contaminated outs and two opponents who could have flushes or flush draws. Time to fold.

I hold the absolute nuts with the Ace-high flush on a board that is not paired, meaning no full houses or higher hands are possible. Elon has been 'sticky' throughout the hand, calling my pre-flop raise, my flop continuation bet, and my large turn bet. This suggests he likely has a strong hand such as a set of Tens, Nines, or Fives, or perhaps a lower flush. Since I have the best possible hand, my goal is to maximize value. A bet of 3000 into the 4750 pot is large enough to extract significant value from his calling range while still being a price he is likely to pay with a set or a smaller flush.

River Qd is a blank that doesn't complete new draws and pairs nothing relevant. Board: 2c-Tc-9s-5c-Qd features a completed club flush, possible two pairs (T9 best), sets. My two pair (9s and 5s) with 9d/5d blockers is strong against Flash's three-barrel range: value (flushes AcXc/KcXc, sets 22/TT/99/55 though blocked, T9/TQ), but also thinner value like Tx/9x top pairs or bluffs (missed clubs pre-turn, overpairs, gutshots like J8/67 that bricked). Flash's turn bet after flush completion and now large 3000 bet (39% into 7750) polarizes: nuts/value or bluff. Pot odds excellent (7750:3000 = 2.58:1, need ~28% equity). Vs full range ~45-55% equity (beats Tx/9x/overpairs/bluffs, loses to nuts); even vs nuts-only 0%, but unlikely pure given sizing/aggression history (Flash overbets value but bluffs e.g., Hand 13 fold, Hand 38). Blockers reduce 99/55/T9 combos. Calling extracts max value from worse (induces calls), pot-controlled heads-up deep (~11k stack post-call), position irrelevant river. Raising risks isolation vs nuts (shove 11175 commits ~80% stack thin); folding too tight given odds/blockers/history. Call, showdown if hero-called.

I am currently the chip leader with a significant stack. While Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o) is on the lower end of an opening range from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 6-handed game, I have consistently opened for 2.5x BB (250) in previous hands to exert pressure and maintain an aggressive table image. Given my stack size and the relative stack sizes of the opponents (particularly the short stack on the button), a standard raise is appropriate to attempt to pick up the blinds or lead the action on the flop.

Facing an UTG raise to 250 from Flash, who has been aggressive but whose early position range is strong (top ~15-20% of hands). My holding of 6s4d is offsuit, uncoordinated trash with no high card value, straight potential, or suitedness for speculative play. Position is middle, so out of position postflop if I call. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 400, ~38% equity needed vs a strong range, but 64o has <30% vs typical UTG). Stacks are deep for most, but no fold equity for a 3bet bluff here with this hand. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots in a tournament with 105 hands left.

I hold 9s 8s in the Cutoff. Flash (UTG) opened to 250. This is a strong range position, but 98s is a premium speculative hand that plays well in position with deep stacks (84.5 BB effective). Folding is too tight given the implied odds and playability. 3-betting is risky against an UTG open range. Calling allows me to see a flop relatively cheaply and capitalize on board textures favorable to my range while avoiding bloating the pot pre-flop against a strong range. The short stack on the button is a concern for a squeeze, but calling remains the standard +EV play here.

We’re on the button with 7♠2♥ facing an UTG open (250) and a cutoff cold-call. Even with position, this hand has extremely poor playability and equity, and our stack is only ~21.5bb, so calling creates too many spots where we’ll bleed chips or be forced into marginal all-ins. With two players already showing interest and blinds still behind, folding preserves stack and avoids -EV situations.

Q3 suited is a weak hand. Facing an UTG raise from Flash and a call from Pro, I'm out of position with a marginal holding. Even getting 3.25:1 pot odds, Q3s plays poorly multiway and out of position. The queen is dominated by many hands in their ranges, and the 3 provides little backup. Folding is the disciplined play here.

2♦4♠ is a very weak, unplayable hand. I'm facing an UTG raise to 250 with a caller (pot = 650) and need to call 150 more. Calling requires ~18.8% equity (150/800) to break even, but 2-4o has almost no equity multiway against an UTG opening range plus a caller and plays horribly out of position with no backdoor prospects. A 3-bet bluff into two players is too risky and likely to be expensive if I face a reraise. With a 7,200 stack and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the correct strategy. Fold.

On a paired board like Kd-Ks-4h, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage as I am more likely to hold strong Kings (AK, KQ) or high pocket pairs compared to the caller. A small continuation bet is a standard and effective play here to capitalize on this advantage and force folds from hands that missed the flop, such as middle pairs or suited connectors. Given the dry nature of the board, I will bet roughly 35% of the pot.

I hold 9s8s on a board of 4h Kd Ks. The board texture heavily favors the UTG opener's range, which contains all strong Kings and high pocket pairs. While I am in position, I have only 9-high with backdoor straight and flush possibilities, which is very weak equity. The opponent's continuation bet of roughly 1/3 pot is standard and difficult to defend against without a piece of the board or a stronger draw. Floating with the intention to bluff later is risky against the chip leader on a board that hits their range so hard. Folding is the disciplined and correct play.

K5o is a marginal offsuit hand at best, completely unplayable from UTG in a 6-handed tournament. No position advantage, easily dominated by better Kx, poor playability postflop. Opponents have varying stacks but no action yet; folding preserves chips for premium spots to maximize long-term stack growth over 150 hands.

I hold King-Jack offsuit in Middle Position with 82 BBs. Following a fold from UTG, this hand is strong enough to open-raise in a 6-max game to steal the blinds or play for value. A standard open size of 2.5x (250 chips) puts pressure on the opponents while keeping the pot manageable.

We’re 21.5bb effective in the cutoff facing a single MP open to 2.5bb. A5o isn’t a great flatting hand here (dominated often, invites BTN/blinds to come along, and we’ll be playing a low SPR pot with a marginal ace). The best way to realize value is to leverage fold equity: an all-in 3-bet applies maximum pressure, and our ace blocks many of Pro’s strongest continues (AK/AQ/AA), while still having reasonable equity when called (wheel/ace-high potential). Given the sizing, Pro would need ~43% equity to call, so his calling range should be fairly tight, making the shove +EV in a 6-handed, late-position spot.

T3o is a very weak hand. FiveTwo made a large 3-bet to 2150 (almost 9x the original raise), indicating significant strength. With T3o, I have no playability worth 2150 chips even with position. Clear fold.

Pro opened to 250 and FiveTwo shoved/3-bet to 2,150 (now all-in). Pot is 2,550 and I must call 2,100 more — pot odds are roughly 1.21:1, so I need ≈45.5% equity. 8♦9♣ offsuit is a speculative, marginal hand and is well below that equity against a likely shove range (pockets, broadways, strong suited Aces). I’d also be out of position and risking a large portion of my 7,150 stack. Min‑raising to 4,050 or shoving would commit too much with a dominated, speculative hand and gains little vs an all‑in. Preserve my stack and fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Pro opened to 250 from Middle Position and FiveTwo shoved all-in for 2150. While I have a large stack, A5o is a weak hand to call a 20BB+ shove, especially with the original raiser (Pro) still left to act. If I call, I risk being dominated by FiveTwo's shoving range (which likely includes better Aces and pocket pairs) and I provide Pro with an opportunity to re-shove or call with a premium hand. Given the stack sizes and the strength of my hand, folding is the most disciplined and mathematically sound play.

I opened to 2.5bb with KJo in MP. FiveTwo shoved for 21.5bb from the Cutoff. The pot is currently 2550 and it costs me 1900 to call. The pot odds are approximately 1900/4450 = 42.7%. Against a typical 21bb shoving range (likely 77+, AJs+, AQo+), KJo performs poorly, often being dominated by AJ/AQ/AK/KQ or flipping against pairs. Even against a wider range, my equity rarely exceeds the required 43%. Given I still have a very healthy stack of nearly 80bb, there is no need to take a marginal or negative EV spot here. Folding is the prudent long-term play.

I am in UTG position with King-Nine offsuit (K9o). In a 6-handed game, this hand is too weak to open from early position. It is easily dominated by better Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) and plays poorly out of position post-flop against multiple opponents. With 5 players left to act behind me, the standard play is to fold.

We’re first-in after UTG folds with QJo, a strong enough 6-max opening hand that benefits from initiative and fold equity. Limping gives up aggression and invites multiway pots where QJ is dominated more often. With ~25.5bb, I’ll use a standard-but-not-too-large open to apply pressure to the blinds and isolate, while keeping my risk controlled if a big 3-bet comes behind.
2-7 offsuit is one of the worst hands in poker. No reason to call a raise with this garbage hand. Easy fold.

7♠3♦ offsuit is a extremely weak hand with almost no playability post‑flop. FiveTwo opened from middle position to 250 — even though he's short (2300) his open range from MP will still dominate most junk hands. Calling 250 (≈3.5% of my 7,150 stack) buys a marginal spot and invites blinds (Flash/Elon) to defend or squeeze; 3‑bet bluffing with 7‑3o is too thin. Better to preserve my stack on the button and wait for a cleaner opportunity to steal or apply pressure. I fold.

I am currently the chip leader with over 19,000 chips. FiveTwo, who has a relatively short stack of 2,300 (23 BB), has opened for 2.5x. My hand, 5s 7d, is very weak and does not play well out of position. Given the stack sizes, calling would likely lead to an awkward post-flop situation where I am committed against a short stack's range. There is no reason to risk chips with a sub-par hand when I can wait for better spots to utilize my chip lead.

3c4d is an extremely weak offsuit hand with minimal straight potential and no high card strength. FiveTwo's raise from middle position suggests a strong range including pairs, broadways, and suited connectors. Equity is poor (~25-30% vs typical range), postflop playability is bad OOP, and while pot odds are decent (150 into 550, ~3:1), implied odds are low given the hand's nature. FiveTwo has 23BB and shown aggression but folding trash preserves stack for better spots in this multi-table tournament simulation.

UTG 6-handed with 27bb, Q2o is far below a profitable opening range and plays poorly postflop (dominated by any Qx, weak kicker, little connectivity).
