Folds to us in the small blind, so this is effectively a blind-vs-blind spot where we should raise very wide to pick up the dead money and deny the BB’s equity realization. A5o has good properties as an open: it blocks strong Ax continues, can make nut wheel straights, and plays reasonably well as the aggressor even when called. With deep stacks and being out of position, I prefer a slightly larger SB open than min-raising to pressure the BB’s defend range and simplify postflop.

Facing a SB raise to 300 with A5o in the BB. This is a standard defend - A5o has good equity against a typical SB opening range. I'm getting decent odds (200 to win 400) and have an ace blocker. Calling is the straightforward play here rather than folding or 3-betting.

Blind-vs-blind SRP after we opened larger: ranges are very wide, and as the preflop aggressor we can apply a high-frequency small c-bet on this Q-high texture. Ac5s is mostly a no-showdown hand, but it benefits from fold equity versus BB’s air/underpairs and denies equity from hands like K-high/J-high. We also retain some backdoor potential (A as an overcard, runner-runner clubs/straight possibilities) to continue on favorable turns. I’ll use a small sizing to keep it efficient and difficult to exploit; if check-raised, this hand is an easy fold.

I have A5o on Qc8s6c board. Completely missed - just ace high with no real draws. The small 1/3 pot bet from FiveTwo could be thin value or a probe, but I'm out of position with no pair, no flush draw, and only a weak backdoor gutshot. Floating OOP with ace high against a small bet is a losing play long-term. Better to fold and save chips.

I am dealt 6s 2c in the Under the Gun (UTG) position. This is one of the weakest possible starting hands in poker. Playing it from the earliest position would be a significant mistake, as there are five opponents still to act behind me. Standard strategy dictates an immediate fold.

Preflop, unopened pot after UTG fold. KTo offsuit is playable from middle position: decent high card value, straight potential, blockers to strong kings. Raising builds pot with position on blinds, steals dead money (150 in pot), and has fold equity against short-stacked Minni (20BB) who might fold or shove into our calling range. Standard sizing around 2.5x BB consistent with aggressive history. Avoid limping or folding a stealing spot in tournament to maximize stack growth.

I hold 8c 3c in the Cutoff, facing a raise to 250 from Elon in Middle Position. My hand is very weak (8-high, large gap) despite being suited. Calling is unprofitable due to reverse implied odds and the likelihood of domination, plus the risk of players behind squeezing. 3-betting as a bluff is not optimal with this specific hand. The standard play is to fold.

Elon’s 2.5x open looks like his standard sizing and he’s shown willingness to apply pressure but also to give up postflop. On the Button, A4o is not a premium defend (dominated by better Ax), but position plus the ace blocker makes a flat reasonable versus a likely wide opening range, especially given we can realize equity well in position and win pots with initiative-taking lines on favorable boards (A-low, paired, or wheel/low-card textures). I’m not 3-betting because it bloats the pot with a dominated offsuit ace and risks getting shoved on by Minni’s short 20bb stack or squeezed by the deep SB, forcing us to fold and burn more chips. Flat keeps the SPR manageable and lets us play postflop in position.

I have A9s in the small blind. Elon raised to 250 and FiveTwo called. I'm getting good pot odds (200 to win 650+, about 3.25:1). A9 suited has decent playability with flush potential and top pair possibilities. While I'm out of position, the price is right to see a flop.

I have K♣7♠ in the big blind facing Elon’s open to 250 with two callers (Button and SB). Calling 150 to see a flop gets me ~1,000 in a pot (≈15% break‑even equity), so the price is good to defend one of my better broadway/king hands. I’m short‑stacked (~20BB) so I don’t want to create a big multi‑way pot with a marginal hand by raising or shoving — K7o is easily dominated by raiser’s and callers’ ranges. Plan: call, see the flop cheaply; proceed only if I hit or get a favorable, low‑commitment opportunity — fold to heavy action otherwise.

I have A9o on a 6d-Jd-4s board with no pair, no flush draw (my cards are spade/club), and just ace high. With 3 opponents still in the pot and being out of position, I have very little equity here. Betting would be a mistake - I should check and likely fold to any significant action. No reason to put chips in with this weak holding.

I hold Kc7s on a 6d Jd 4s flop with no pair and no real draw. I defended pre‑flop because the price was right, but I’m short‑stacked (~19BB) and this is a multiway spot where my king is frequently dominated. There’s no reason to build the pot or bluff into multiple opponents — pot‑control is optimal. I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the turn: fold to sizable aggression unless I pair the King or pick up a very favorable, low‑risk spot to double up.

As preflop raiser in a multiway pot, facing checks from SB and BB (and assuming BTN checked), this is a standard continuation bet spot. The flop 6d Jd 4s is low and drawy (diamond flush draw, open-enders like 53 or 75), which my KTo blocks some overpairs and top-of-range like KJ while having decent fold equity against the calling ranges of blinds and a speculative BTN call. No direct equity but king-high has playability, and c-betting ~50% pot applies pressure on short-stacked Minni (19BB, likely to fold or shove pairs/draws we beat or can fold to), while deeper stacks will fold weak hands. Builds pot or takes it down now; plan to check turn if called. Consistent with aggressive image from history to maximize stack.

Elon’s 1/3-pot c-bet into 3 players is a range-heavy stab on a board that favors a lot of callers (Jx, diamond draws, 6x/4x). With A4o we have bottom pair + ace kicker, and we’re last to act so we realize our equity cleanly and can re-evaluate turns (often folding to bigger pressure on bad runouts like diamonds/overcards, continuing on improving cards like A/4, or against continued small sizing). Folding is too tight given price (need ~25%) and position; raising would be spewy since we mainly fold out bluffs and isolate ourselves versus better hands/draws that can continue or 3-bet jam.

I have A9o with no pair, no flush draw (my cards are spades/clubs on a diamond board), and no straight draw. Elon c-bet 500 and FiveTwo called, showing strength from two opponents. Even getting 4:1 pot odds, my ace-high has minimal equity - I'd need to hit an ace or 9 and have it be good. With two opponents showing interest on this J-high board, folding is correct.

I checked the flop to keep the pot small with a marginal holding and re-evaluate. Facing Elon’s 500 c-bet into ~2000 with FiveTwo already calling, I’m in a multiway pot and Kc7s has no pair and no realistic draw (only a couple of king outs and very thin backdoor possibilities). Calling 500 (~5BB) would leave me ~14BB and likely commit me to further action with poor equity; a raise would either overcommit my short stack or be dominated. Pot odds require ~15–17% equity to justify a call, which I don’t have multiway. Preserve my stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the turn against FiveTwo on the button after c-betting flop and getting called. Board is 6d Jd 4s 3d, which is extremely draw-heavy: three diamonds (flush draws completed for anyone with two diamonds), low coordinated cards (straights like 5x hit gutshots or open-enders improved). My KhTc is pure king-high showdown value with no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (ten doesn't connect well). Preflop raise was standard with KTo, flop c-bet had decent blockers and fold equity multiway, but FiveTwo's call indicates strength: likely Jx, pairs like 66/44/55/77 (overpairs or sets), combo draws, or made flushes already. Turn 3d is a disaster card for my range, improving many calling hands and villain's floats/bluffs. Betting here would be a bluff without much equity (king-high rarely best) and poor fold equity vs a sticky button caller like FiveTwo (history shows calls big pots, e.g., Hand 2, 39). Thin value bet too thin as king-high crushed by Jx+. Pot 2000 into my 4825 stack (SPR ~2.4), but checking maintains control, induces bluffs from missed draws (which I beat), extracts value if villain has showdown-only, and allows hero call/fold on river. Consistent with disciplined play: don't bloat pot with marginal king-high on scary board. Plan: check, call small bets on river if any, fold to large ones.

Elon c-bet small multiway then checked this diamond turn heads-up. That line is very often range-capped/inducing (A-high/KQ type give-ups, some medium pairs, and some diamond draws taking a free card). With A4 we have a weak made hand that benefits a lot from denying equity to two-overcard hands and charging flush draws, and we can do it in position with a sizing that doesn’t commit us. I’ll fire a small-to-medium probe that can fold out his air, get value/protection vs overcards, and put his draws to a decision. If he check-raises big, I’m comfortably folding; if he calls, I’ll mostly take showdown on many rivers and avoid paying off large bets on bad runouts (diamonds/overcards).

Facing a turn bet of 800 into 2800 after checking the scary 3d turn card on a board of 6d Jd 4s 3d, which completes flushes and low straights (e.g., 5x makes straight), my KhTc is pure king-high bluff-catcher with zero equity against villain's range. FiveTwo called flop c-bet multiway from button (loose positionally), then donk-bets turn after my check, representing Jx, two diamonds (flush), 55/77/88 sets/overpairs, or 53/75 straights. Preflop raise + flop c-bet polarizes my range to value or bluffs, but king-high is crushed by calls and villain rarely bluffs into preflop raiser here (history shows FiveTwo calls large pots like Hand 2/39/61 but doesn't overbluff). Pot odds ~3:1 but reverse implied odds terrible (river bricks rarely make K best, villain bombs with value); SPR post-call ~1.1 too low for thin play. Folding preserves stack for better spots (4825 effective, tournament phase favors discipline over spew). Consistent with prior plan: check for control, fold to aggression on disaster card.

Preflop from UTG in a 6-handed game with A5o (offsuit). This is a standard fold as it's a weak ace with poor kicker, low playability, and vulnerable postflop. No steals or limpers to exploit yet; blinds posted standard pot of 150. Positionally disadvantaged, better to wait for stronger hands to build stack long-term. Minni short at ~18BB but doesn't change UTG range.

I am in middle position with King-Four offsuit (K4o), which is a statistically weak hand. With four opponents left to act, including the button and blinds, the probability of running into a stronger hand is significant. K4o has poor playability post-flop and is easily dominated. With a healthy stack of 75 BB, there is no pressure to make loose opens. The correct and standard play is to fold.

We’re first in from the cutoff after two folds with K9o. In 6-max this is a clear open: we have position on both blinds, a king blocker helps vs 3-bets, and we can win the 150 dead chips often. SB is short (~18bb) so we also apply pressure and can comfortably fold if faced with a tough reshove/3-bet range. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb size to reduce multiway play while still keeping steals efficient.

Holding 94o on the button facing a cutoff raise. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly even with position. 94o is not in my calling or 3-betting range here - it lacks connectivity, suited potential, and high card value. Easy fold.

I have 2♥6♠ — essentially the worst playable hand. Facing a cutoff open to 250 from the SB with the big blind still to act, I’d be out of position and dominated by the opener’s range. Calling 200 (250 total) is a marginal investment of ~13% of my stack for very poor post‑flop playability and little fold equity. A small reraise or shove isn’t attractive either — it won’t reliably fold better hands and would commit my tournament life with almost no equity. Preserve chips and fold preflop.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Deuce suited (Kc 2c). FiveTwo has opened to 250 from the Cutoff. Given that I have already posted the 100 big blind, I only need to call 150 more to see a flop in a pot that currently totals 400. This gives me excellent pot odds of 2.67:1. K2s is a hand with decent post-flop playability due to its flush potential and the possibility of hitting a top pair that can occasionally be the best hand against a late-position opening range. While out of position, the price is too good to fold. I will call and see a flop.

I have flopped a King-high flush draw on a board of Qc 4c 3s. As the Big Blind, I am first to act against the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo. I will check here with the intention of calling a continuation bet to realize my equity or potentially check-raising if I feel I can apply pressure. Checking is the standard play to see how the aggressor proceeds on this texture.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser in position, this flop is a standard small c-bet spot. The board (Qc-4c-3s) is relatively static and favors my opening range (I have more Qx and strong overpairs than BB), while Flash’s defend range contains a lot of unpaired hands that will fold to a stab. My hand (Ks9c) has little immediate showdown value, but benefits from fold equity and can pick up turns (K/Q/club turns) to continue barreling/selectively realize. I’ll use a small size to pressure his air and keep my risk low versus a check-raise.

I have flopped a King-high flush draw (Kc 2c) on a board of Qc 4c 3s. FiveTwo has made a continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 550. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to win 750 total, approximately 3.75:1). With 9 outs to the flush and a potential overcard with the King, my equity justifies a call. Raising is an option to apply pressure, but calling allows me to see the turn cheaply and realize my equity while keeping FiveTwo's range wide.

I am out of position with a King-high flush draw on a board of 4c Qc 3s Jd. On the turn, the Jack of Diamonds doesn't significantly change the board texture for my hand. Since I am first to act, checking is the most strategic move. It allows me to see how the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, reacts. I have 9 outs to a flush and potentially 3 outs to a King, giving me approximately 25-30% equity. Checking allows me to potentially see a free river card or call a reasonably sized bet to realize my equity.

Flash’s flop call on Qc-4c-3s keeps in a lot of Qx, pocket pairs, Jx floats, and especially club draws. The Jd turn is a card that continues to connect with his peel range, and my hand (Ks9c) doesn’t pick up meaningful equity (just a weak gutshot to T plus thin overcard equity). Given Flash’s demonstrated tendency to continue/call with medium strength and draws, a second barrel is likely to get called too often and puts me in an awkward spot on many rivers. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes what equity I have, and lets me take selective river stabs on good cards (A/T/K) if he checks again, while comfortably folding versus a river lead on bricks.

I have King-high after missing my flush draw on the river. The board is 4c Qc 3s Jd 6d. I checked the flop and called a small bet from FiveTwo, then we both checked the turn. FiveTwo's turn check suggests either a marginal pair (like 77-TT or a weak Jack) taking a free showdown, or a complete air hand. While a bet might occasionally force a fold from a weak pair or Ace-high, it's more likely I'm called by any pair given the pot odds. Since King-high has very little showdown value but the pot is small, I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or fold to any significant bet.

River is checked to us after BB called a small c-bet on Qc-4c-3s and we checked back the Jd turn. With Ks9c we have essentially no showdown value, but this is also a poor bluff spot: Flash’s flop call range contains a lot of Qx, Jx floats that improved on the turn, and pocket pairs that tend to be sticky. The 6d doesn’t meaningfully change ranges, and our turn check makes a river stab less credible, so we’re unlikely to generate enough folds to justify risking chips. Take the free showdown and move on.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o). In a 6-handed game, this hand is significantly below the standard opening range for Under The Gun, which typically consists of pairs, stronger broadways, and suited aces/connectors. With a healthy stack of 75 BB, there is no pressure to make loose moves from early position. Opening here risks getting 3-bet or dominated by the five players yet to act, including a short stack on the button who might shove. The disciplined play is to fold.

We’re effectively first-in (UTG folded) with 52o in middle position. This hand has terrible playability, is easily dominated when it makes a pair, and doesn’t realize equity well multiway. With a short stack on the button (Minni ~18.5bb), there’s extra value in avoiding thin, negative-EV opens/limps that can bloat pots and create awkward spots versus aggressive blinds. Best play is to fold and preserve stack for higher-quality steal/iso opportunities.

9s7h offsuit is a weak hand even in late position. While I'm in the cutoff with only 3 players behind, 97o doesn't have good playability or equity against calling ranges. Minni is short-stacked and may look to get it in, while Flash has a big stack to defend. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I’m on the button with Tc7s and a shortish stack (1,850 ≈ 18.5bb). Action folded to me, pot is 150 and I only need to call 100 to see a flop in position. Tc7s is marginal — playable in position but not worth inflating the pot out of line against a deep, sticky SB (Flash) and an often-defending BB (Elon). A min-raise or larger open will likely induce calls or a 3‑bet and create multiway trouble for this hand; an all‑in is too weak. Calling keeps my range wide, uses positional advantage, and costs very little, allowing me to c‑bet or fold depending on the flop texture and opponents’ reactions. I’ll call 100 and re-evaluate on the flop.

I hold Ace-Queen Offsuit in the Small Blind, which is a premium hand in a 6-max game. The action folded to Minni on the Button, who limped for 100. Limping from the button generally indicates a weak or speculative range. With Minni being short-stacked (1750 chips), I want to raise for value and to take initiative in the hand. Raising to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard size to discourage the Big Blind from defending cheaply and to put pressure on the limper. This size also sets up a potential commit if Minni decides to shove with her remaining 17.5 big blinds.

In the big blind with Ac7d, facing a raise to 400 from the small blind Flash after three folds and a button limp from Minni (checked/called 100). Pot is 600, calling 300 more for pot odds of roughly 2:1 (600:300), requiring about 33% equity, which A7o can achieve against a wide SB stealing range that includes many hands worse than ace-high. Flash has a massive stack (16k) and has shown aggression in past hands (e.g., frequent raises and value bets), but SB raises after a limp are often light to isolate or steal. Minni's limp on button is loose, but her stack is short (1750), and she's already in cheaply. Ac7o blocks AA/AK, has decent playability (straight potential with 7, ace blocker), and position OOP but multiway pot favors seeing a flop cheaply. Folding is too tight in BB defense spot; raising is overplaying marginal ace. Plan: call, check flop and evaluate board texture, c/f most non-ace boards, proceed cautiously OOP vs likely wide ranges.

I already limped for 100 and Flash (SB) isolated to 400; Elon (BB) called. Pot is 900 and I must call 300 more to make my total 400. Tc7s is a marginal hand but playing it in position is profitable here — calling costs 300 into 1200 (pot+call) so my break-even equity is ~25%. A 3-bet or shove with this hand is too ambitious: Flash’s isolation raise likely contains many hands that dominate me and Elon’s call range is wide, so my fold equity on a shove is limited and I’d be committing with a weak holding. I’ll call the 300 (400 total) to see a flop in position, then proceed based on texture (continue with pairs/strong draws, fold to heavy action on dry boards).

On a King-high dry board (8c-Kd-2h) after raising pre-flop with Ace-Queen, I am in a multi-way pot against the Big Blind and the Button. While I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, the board is likely to have hit the calling ranges of my opponents (especially Kx hands like K-J or K-10). With Minni being very short-stacked, any bet I make will likely commit her or force a decision for her entire stack. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how the other two players react before committing more chips with only Ace-high. If it checks around, I get a free turn card with two overcards to improve.

Flop is K d 8c 2h, a dry king-high board with no flush draws and limited straight draws. I have Ace-high with no pair, no draws, and the Ace doesn't block many king ranges effectively here. Pre-flop, I defended wide in BB vs SB isolation raise after BTN limp, but this flop misses me completely. Flash (SB, huge stack, aggressive history) checks first, which could represent weakness (missed K, air, trap), but he often value bets kings. Betting would be a pure bluff into two opponents, poor with my wide range and reverse position. Minni (short stack BTN) limped and called raise, could have speculative hands but unlikely to have K often. Plan from pre-flop: check flop OOP on non-ace boards and c/f to aggression. Checking induces bluffs or keeps pot small; calling station tendencies in field suggest value betting is better when I have it. Check to realize equity and control pot.

Board is Kd-8c-2h — a very dry, king-high rainbow. I hold Tc7s: no pair, no meaningful draws (with one club in hand and one on the flop a flush is impossible; only tiny backdoor straight chance that needs two cards). Flash isolated preflop and both opponents checked the flop, so my fold equity as a bluff is low against two players (Flash can comfortably call with Kx or pairs; Elon defends wide). Pot is 1200 and my stack is 1450 (~14.5bb) — shoving or barreling here is high-variance and likely negative EV. I’ll check to use position, keep the pot manageable, and fold to significant aggression. I can look for a better spot to apply pressure on a favorable turn or concede cheaply if they show strength.

I have improved to a pair of Queens with an Ace kicker on the turn. Since the flop was checked around by all three players, it is less likely that my opponents are holding a King. I will lead out with a half-pot bet of 600 to extract value from weaker pairs (like Q-J, Q-T, or an 8), pocket pairs, and to protect against potential draws like J-10.

On the turn Qc, the board is now K-Q-8-2 rainbow with two clubs, bringing possible flush draws and adding Q to create more straight possibilities (e.g., J10, JT9). I have Ac7d: pure ace-high with no pair, no draw, and the club ace blocks some flush draws but doesn't improve me. Pot is 1800, Flash bets 600 (~1/3 pot), which is a standard value or probe bet. Flash checked flop (dry board) and now bets turn, representing strength like Kx, Qx, sets, or two-pair, or bluffing semi-bluffs with draws/clubs. My pre-flop wide BB defense range misses this board often, and I have no equity or showdown value OOP. Minni short-stacked behind might call with speculative hands, but folding air here preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 85 hands left. Calling risks further aggression on river where I fold anyway; raising is a pure bluff with bad blockers and reverse position vs aggressive big stack. Consistent with pre-flop/flop plan to c/f non-improving boards. Fold to control risk and wait for equity.

I have Tc7s with no pair and no meaningful draws on K♦-8♣-2♥-Q♣. Flash was the preflop isolator and now barreled the turn; Elon folded, so this is heads-up against an aggressive, deep stack — his line looks value-heavy (Kx/Qx/8x or a set). Pot is 1,800 and a call costs 600, so I’d need ~25% equity to break even. My only clean outs are three tens (~3/46 ≈ 6.5%), far below the required equity. Raising/shoving is too ambitious (little fold equity and would commit most of my 1,450 stack). Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

We’re 6-handed but still first to act, and 9♠4♠ (actually 9h4s) offsuit has very poor playability and domination issues. Limping UTG just invites isolation from the button/cutoff and puts us in tough multiway spots with a hand that rarely makes strong, clean pairs or good draws. With solid stack depth (127bb), we don’t need to take thin, negative-EV opens here—especially with aggressive players behind and a couple short stacks who can reshove over loose opens.
2h5h is a very weak hand, even if suited. In middle position with 4 players still to act behind me, this is an easy fold. No reason to get involved with such marginal holdings.

I’m short-stacked (1,450 ≈ 14.5 BB) in the cutoff with J♥7♥. Pot is 150 and only 100 to call. With ~15 BB the correct framework is push-or-fold — small raises (to 200) leave me shallow and create awkward postflop decisions; calling 100 is passive and gives the button and blinds cheap spots to isolate. J7s has enough suit/blocker/steal equity to make an all‑in profitable often: it maximizes fold equity vs the button/SB/BB and, if called, still has reasonable equity against a calling range. I want to simplify and pressure the bigger stacks rather than play postflop from a short stack. So shove all-in.

Minni has shoved all-in for 1,450 chips, which is 14.5 big blinds. While I am on the button with a decent hand (QJo), this hand is often dominated by a standard 15BB shoving range from the cutoff (which usually includes better Queens, Jacks, Aces, and pocket pairs). To call 1,450 to win a pot that will be 3,050 (assuming the blinds fold), I need approximately 47.5% equity. QJo typically has around 43-46% equity against a reasonable shove range. Furthermore, calling invites the Small Blind (Elon, 4375) or the Big Blind (Pro, 7400) to shove over the top, which would put me in a difficult position. Given my healthy stack of 17,300, there is no need to take a high-variance, likely -EV risk here. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Q<8o is a marginal offsuit hand with poor playability out of position. Minni's shove from cutoff for 1450 (all-in) after two folds indicates a wide range but still likely ahead of my hand. Calling 1400 OOP into a 1600 pot offers insufficient equity against their shoving range (dominated by better Qx, Ax, pairs, etc.). My stack of 4375 is playable; folding preserves chips for better spots. No raise value with this hand. Standard fold to avoid spew.

Opponent Minni has shoved all-in for 1450 chips (14.5 BB) from the Cutoff. The pot is currently 1600, and I need to call 1350 to see a showdown. This offers me pot odds of approximately 1350/2950 = 45.7%. My hand, Tc 3c, is a weak speculative hand that performs poorly against a standard short-stack shoving range (likely pairs, broadways, and Aces). Against a generous top 30% range, my equity is only around 34%, which is well below the required ~46% equity to make a profitable call. Preserving my stack of 7400 is more important than taking a negative EV spot.
T5 suited is too weak to open from UTG. Even though it's suited, T5s is not in a standard UTG opening range. With 5 players behind me, I should fold and wait for a better spot.

Open-raise from middle with T♥8♠ (T8o). UTG has folded in a 6-max game and I want to take the initiative rather than limp into a multiway pot with a marginal hand. I have ~16BB, so a standard 2.5–3x BB open (300 total) buys fold equity vs the blinds and keeps the pot manageable. If I face a large 3-bet I will fold this hand OOP; if called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop and c-bet appropriate textures. Limping/calling is too passive here.

I am in the Cutoff with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Minni, who is extremely short-stacked with only 1,300 chips, has raised to 300. Even though she is short, this hand has no value against a raise, and there are still three players left to act behind me who could easily wake up with a stronger hand or shove over the top. The most disciplined and correct play here is to fold.

Pocket 3s on the button vs a single 3x raise from short-stacked Minni (13BB effective). Excellent position, pot odds 450:300 (~1.5:1), strong implied odds to stack her 1000 chips behind if set hits. Standard set-mining spot in position against one opponent. Deeper SB/BB behind unlikely to squeeze wide given action. Calling maximizes EV.

I am in the Small Blind with K5o. Minni, who is short-stacked (13bb), opened to 3bb from Middle Position, and Elon called on the Button. K5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly multi-way and out of position. Minni's raise size relative to their stack indicates strength or commitment, meaning I have little fold equity if I were to 3-bet, and my hand is likely dominated by their range (better Kings, Aces, Pairs). Calling 2.5bb to play a dominated hand OOP against two opponents is losing strategy. Folding is the only prudent play.

We’re getting a tempting price (200 to win 750) and we close the action, but 23o is at the very bottom of defendable hands: it makes dominated pairs/weak two-pairs, has very poor playability multiway, and equity realization will be bad versus a 3x open plus a caller. With Minni short, there’s also less implied upside when we do connect. Best stack-preserving, EV-focused play is to pass and wait for a spot with better realizable equity.

I continuation-bet to seize the initiative but keep the pot manageable: half-pot (375). Board is Q♠ J♠ 4♦ and my T♥8♠ has almost no showdown value, but it does have a gutshot to a 9 (4 outs) and a tiny backdoor spade possibility. Heads‑up vs the button caller I can credibly represent Q/J or a spade draw and charge his marginal holdings and draws with a 1/2-pot c‑bet. Size is deliberately medium — large enough to fold out overcards and small pairs, small enough not to commit my short stack with a marginal hand. Plan: if he raises hard I’ll fold; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only with meaningful improvement or a clear read.

Pocket threes completely whiffed the flop, leaving us with a weak underpair on a Qs Js 4d board that connects strongly with Minni's middle-position raising range (Qx, Jx, QJ, overpairs QQ/JJ/TT, spade draws like AsXs). Her half-pot c-bet polarizes towards value or draws, against which our ~18-25% equity (mainly runner-runner set/straight outs) is insufficient vs the 375 into 1125 pot (~4:1 odds requiring ~20% equity). Minni's short stack (~925 remaining pre-bet, ~550 post-bet) means she'll likely jam any further action, denying fold equity on a raise and limiting implied odds. Preflop set-mining was +EV in position, but folding postflop preserves our 40BB stack for superior spots in a 6-handed tournament.

I'm UTG in a 6-max game with A♠T♥ and ~2050 (~20.5 BB). Limping is too passive here — I want initiative and fold equity with a playable Ace. Open to 3xBB (300 total) which is large enough to fold out marginal hands from the blinds and narrow the field, but small enough to leave ~1,750 (≈17.5 BB) behind for post‑flop maneuvering. If I face a large, committed 3‑bet (big portion of my stack) I will fold ATo; against a small 3‑bet from a wide range I may shove or call depending on the sizing. If called I'll C‑bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously when I face heavy action or an Ace on the board.

Minni, who is playing on a very short stack of 1,750 chips (17.5 BB), has raised to 300 from UTG. Queen-Six offsuit is a very weak hand in this position, especially facing an open from a short-stacked player whose range is likely quite strong or who is looking to commit. There is no reason to play this hand with several players left to act behind me. Folding is the only sensible play.

Preflop in cutoff facing a UTG raise to 300 from Minni, who has a short stack of 1750 (about 17.5BB). T6s is a speculative suited connector with some playability in position, but an UTG open suggests a strong range (pairs, broadway, suited aces). Pot is 450, calling 300 gives about 2.5:1 odds, requiring roughly 28% equity, which T6s likely doesn't have against a tight UTG range (estimated 30-35% at best, but playability reduced multiway). Deep stacks behind (Pro on button, blinds) increase risk of squeezes or 3-bets. Minni's short stack means she may shove postflop if checked to. Folding preserves our 4075 stack (40BB) for better spots in a long tournament (hand 68/150). No history indicates Minni is overly loose from UTG.

I hold A4o on the Button facing a 3bb open raise from Minni (UTG). Minni is short-stacked (17.5bb), and an UTG open typically indicates a strong range (better Aces, pairs) that dominates A4o. While I have position, the hand plays poorly post-flop against a strong range, and Minni's stack size makes 3-bet bluffing risky as they may easily shove. The most disciplined and profitable play here is to fold.

We’re in the small blind facing a 3bb UTG open from a very short stack (~17.5bb). That sizing/range is typically strong and, being out of position with 84o, we’ll realize equity very poorly even with decent immediate pot odds. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive because Minni is short and likely to continue/jam often, forcing us to either fold and burn chips or call off very bad. Best play is to cut losses and preserve stack.

Facing a 3x raise from Minni in UTG with 97o. Getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450). 97o is too weak to defend against an UTG raise, even from a short-stacked player. While Minni is short-stacked and might be loosening up, 97o plays poorly out of position and has little equity against a typical UTG opening range. Easy fold.

I am in the UTG position with Ten-Seven offsuit. This is a very weak hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening range from early position in a 6-max game. Furthermore, several players behind me have relatively short stacks, increasing the likelihood of a shove that I would be forced to fold to. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

K3o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability, dominated by any other king, and little to no straight or flush potential. In middle position with 40BB stack, facing a standard BB to call of 100 into 150 pot, folding is optimal to preserve stack and wait for better spots. No reads or history suggest otherwise this hand.

I hold Jack-Four suited (Jh 4h) in the Cutoff. This is a weak speculative hand that falls below the standard opening range for this position, especially with a deep-stacked aggressive player on the Button (FiveTwo) and a short-stacked Big Blind (Minni) who might shove over a raise. My stack is healthy (73.5 BB), so I do not need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. Folding is the disciplined decision.

Folds to us on the button with KQc (strong suited broadway). This is a standard open-raise for value and as a steal: we have position, good playability, and can pressure the blinds—especially the short-stacked BB who will often be forced into shove-or-fold decisions with a capped defending range. Raising also avoids giving a cheap multiway flop where our hand can be dominated or outflopped. I’ll use a simple 2.5bb sizing to 250 to generate folds while keeping my risk controlled versus the SB’s deeper stack.

K4o in the SB facing a button raise. This is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, especially with the BB still to act. K4o is easily dominated by better kings in villain's range and doesn't have good post-flop playability. Standard fold here.

I have Q2o in the big blind facing a button open to 250. Calling 150 into a 400 pot requires ~27% equity (150/(400+150)=150/550≈27%). Q2 offsuit is frequently dominated by a button's opening range and performs poorly out of position — especially against a deep stack who can apply pressure. With only ~21bb (2100) I don't want to invest in marginal, dominated spots or be forced off later streets. A 3-bet or shove with Q2o is unjustifiable. I fold to preserve my stack for better spots.

UTG in a 6-handed game with K9o offsuit is a clear fold. This hand lacks the strength and playability required for early position opens, especially with deep stacks behind and aggressive opponents like Claude, FiveTwo, and Flash who frequently raise and defend. My stack of 4075 (~41BB) doesn't necessitate spew; preserving chips for better spots maximizes long-term EV in this tournament phase (hand 70/150). Folding avoids multiway pots OOP where K9o struggles post-flop.

I hold Ten of Diamonds and Eight of Hearts (T8o) in Middle Position. This is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, as it is offsuit and gapped. Opening from Middle Position with T8o is too loose, especially with four players remaining to act, including the button and blinds. Standard strategy dictates folding this hand to conserve chips for better opportunities.

Folded to us in the CO, but 52o is too weak to profitably enter: it flops poorly, is dominated often, and performs badly vs 3-bets. With an aggressive-looking Button behind and a short SB who can jam over opens, the steal EV isn’t reliable enough for this exact hand. Preserve stack and keep the opening range slightly stronger.

On the button with 64o after it folds to me. While this is a decent steal spot, 64o is at the bottom of playable hands. Minni is short-stacked (2050) and might shove, and Flash has a big stack to defend. With my comfortable stack of 16475, I don't need to fight for the blinds with such a weak hand - better to wait for a better spot.

Heads-up against the big blind with 3♠8♦ — a weak holding but openable in the small blind. With a 2,050 stack (~20.5 BB) I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds rather than limp and play a dominated hand postflop. I’ll make a standard small open to the minimum raise (200 total) to maximize fold equity while keeping my commitment low. If Flash 3-bets big I will fold this hand; if he calls I’ll play postflop for pot control and fold to heavy aggression given my poor equity.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Small Blind (Minni) has min-raised to 200. I only need to call 100 more chips to see the flop, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Additionally, I will have position on Minni for the rest of the hand. While J6o is a weak starting hand, the combination of favorable pot odds and positional advantage makes this a standard call in a Blind-vs-Blind situation, especially since Minni's stack is quite short (19 BBs) and her opening range from the SB is likely wide.

I opened preflop and was called; on Jh-Tc-3h I’ve only got bottom pair (3♠8♦) with a weak kicker and two overcards on board plus two hearts (backdoor draw). Against a BB calling range there will be lots of J/T combos, pairs and overcards — my hand is marginal and easily dominated. I want to keep the pot small and avoid committing chips with thin value, so I’ll check and play for pot control. If Flash makes a small probe bet (cheap), I may call to realize equity, but I will fold to significant aggression or a check-raise and re-evaluate on the turn if checked to.

I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker on a somewhat connected board with a flush draw. Minni, the small blind, checked to me. Given that the board has several draws (hearts, straight possibilities with KQ, Q9, 98), I should bet for value and protection. Minni's stack is quite short (19 BBs), so a standard 50% pot bet of 200 chips is appropriate to extract value from draws or weaker hands like a Ten, while also allowing me to control the pot if she plays back at me.

I have 3♠8♦ on J♥ T♣ 3♥ — a small, vulnerable pair with a weak kicker. Flash's 200 into a 600 pot is a modest probe (≈1/3 pot) so the call only requires ~20% equity. Versus a BB calling range there are many overcards and missed hands where my pair has reasonable showdown value and some chance to improve. I want to keep the pot small and realize equity rather than folding a spot or bloating the pot with a marginal hand by raising. I’ll call the 200 to see the turn and re-evaluate — folding to significant turn aggression unless I improve to trips or pick up a strong redraw.

Board: Jh Tc 3h 2d — I have 3♠8♦: bottom pair with a weak kicker and no backdoor flush. I called a modest flop c-bet to see one more card but didn’t improve on the turn. There are many hands that beat me (Jx, Tx, two-pair, sets, and many turn double-barrel combos) and betting now would bloat the pot with a marginal holding while offering little fold equity against an opponent who called the flop. With a 1,700 stack and an 800 pot, pot-control is important — I want to preserve stack flexibility and force my opponent to take the lead. I’ll check and evaluate the river: call small-to-medium river bets that give correct pot odds, but fold to large aggression unless I improve. Checking maximizes my options and avoids committing chips with a thin-value hand.

I have flopped top pair with Jacks, and the Deuce of Diamonds on the turn is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Minni has checked for the second time, which often indicates a weak Jack, a Ten, or a draw. Since I have top pair, I want to continue betting for value against her draws (hearts and straight draws like KQ or Q9) and middle pairs. A bet of 400 (half the pot) is a standard size that extracts value and sets up a potential shove or call on the river, especially considering Minni's short stack of 1,700 chips.

Call 400. I have a marginal bottom pair (3♠) with a weak kicker on Jh-Tc-3h-2d. Flash’s line — calling preflop, a small flop c-bet (200) that was called, then a small turn barrel (400 into a 1200 pot) — is consistent with a wide range (Jx, Tx, some 2x/3x, and bluffs). The turn bet is only ~400 to win ~1600, so I’m getting ~25% pot odds to continue. Raising with this hand would bloat the pot with very little fold equity and poor playability; folding is too tight against such a small bet. I’ll call to realize my equity, see the river, and then fold to large river aggression unless I improve (trips or a lucky 8), while calling any small river bets that give correct pot odds.

I have only a single 3 (bottom pair) with a weak 8 kicker on J♥ T♣ 3♥ 2♦ 5♣. Flash’s line — calling pre, small flop c‑bet, small turn barrel — is wide and includes many J/T value hands but also lots of missed overcards and occasional bluffs. The river 5♣ is a blank for me and doesn’t meaningfully improve my hand. Pot is 1,600 while my stack is 1,300 (SPR ≈ 0.8), so any river bet/raise can easily commit my stack — I need a stronger value hand to charge opponents or to polarize profitably. A river bet here would rarely fold better hands (Jx/Tx) and won’t extract reliable calls from worse hands, so betting is thin and high risk. I’ll check and take the showdown or call a small river probe if offered; fold to large river aggression.

I have top pair with a weak kicker (J6). The board has run out [Jh Tc 3h 2d 5c], which is relatively safe as the main draws (KQ and hearts) have missed. Minni has check-called both the flop and turn, which typically indicates a medium-strength hand like a Ten, a weak Jack, or a draw she was unwilling to fold. Since she checked a third time on the river, she is likely in showdown mode. While I could potentially extract thin value from a Ten, her check-calling range also includes several Jacks that have me out-kicked (J7 through JQ). Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value against her missed draws and avoids the risk of being value-cut by a better Jack.
I am in UTG position with 9h 6s, which is a very weak unsuited hand with a 3-gap. Playing this hand from early position is not profitable. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

A3o from middle position is a marginal offsuit ace that’s frequently dominated when we get called (Ax, bigger aces) and doesn’t realize equity well if we face pressure/3-bets from CO/Button or the blinds. With a very short-stacked Button behind (likely to reshove) and big stacks in the blinds who can apply leverage, opening this hand increases variance for a thin steal. I’ll pass and wait for a better spot.

72 suited is one of the weakest hands in poker. Even from the cutoff with two folds already, this hand lacks the playability and strength to open profitably. If I raise and get 3-bet, I'm in a terrible spot. Easy fold.

I'm on the button with J♥8♣ and a short stack (1300 = 13 BB). Facing only the blinds (pot 150, 100 to call), this is a classic push/fold situation. J8o is marginal postflop and with 13 BB I want to maximize fold equity and avoid difficult postflop decisions. A min-raise leaves me vulnerable to calls and 3-bets from the BB; calling gives up my best chance to steal. Flash (SB) is very deep but will still fold a lot; Elon (BB) has ~39 BB and will call/3-bet some hands, but overall shoving from the button here is the +EV play. All-in for my entire stack (1300).

Minni has shoved all-in from the button for 1300. I am in the small blind with 9-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Even against a wide button-shoving range, 9-5o is a significant underdog and not worth calling 1250 chips for, especially with the big blind (Elon) still to act behind me. I will fold to preserve my stack.

32o is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, low, gapped, no straight or flush potential worth playing. Minni's raise to 1300 (13x BB) from button after folds suggests a strong range or strong isolation play; calling 1200 into 1450 pot offers insufficient odds (~1.2:1) for this trash hand. No fold equity postflop, and my 40BB stack doesn't warrant a speculative call. Folding preserves chips for better spots.

From UTG in 6-max, Q7s is near/below the bottom of a solid opening range and is frequently dominated when called (Qx, 7x) while also performing poorly versus 3-bets. With active opponents behind (Flash/Claude) and a very short stack (Minni) who can reshove, opening creates a lot of negative-EV outcomes where we either fold to pressure or take dominated postflop lines. Best EV is to pass and preserve stack for better spots.

Q4o is a weak hand in middle position. With players still to act behind me including Minni who has a short stack and has been aggressive, this is an easy fold. No reason to get involved with such a marginal holding.

Cutoff with A♠K♥ and a 1,450 stack (~14.5bb). Two folded into me; pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call with a min-raise to 200. At this stack depth AKo belongs in the shove/push range — it’s a top-tier hand that wants to realize equity now rather than allow multiway or post‑flop spots where I’m effectively committing anyway. Shoving maximizes fold equity against the button and the blinds, avoids facing larger 3‑bets post-flop, and gives the best chance to pick up the pot or double up if called. A small raise (200–400) just invites action from deeper stacks and complicates decisions. I will shove my full stack.

I am on the Button holding Jc 4h. Minni has moved all-in for 1450 from the Cutoff. While Minni's range for shoving might be wide given her short stack, J4o is a very weak hand and does not perform well against any reasonable shove range. Furthermore, there are still two players (Elon and Pro) left to act in the blinds. I see no reason to commit chips with such a marginal holding when I am in a healthy stack position.

Preflop in small blind facing a cutoff shove for 1450 into a 150 dead money pot (pot now 1600, call 1400). My hand Q5o is very weak, offsuit with poor playability and dominated by typical shoving ranges (which likely include Ax, Kx, QJ+, pairs). Pot odds are roughly 2.14:1 (calling 1400 to win 3000), requiring ~32% equity, but Q5o has far less against a loose short-stack shove range (~20-25% at best). Minni has been aggressive historically (e.g., large raises in hands 66, 71), but this is still a fold to preserve my 39BB stack for better spots in a 6-handed tournament with 78 hands left. BB still to act, but no incentive to commit with trash. Fold to wait for premium hands.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd Qd. The player Minni has raised to 1450, which appears to be an all-in given her stack is listed as 0. This is 14.5 big blinds. Minni has been very active recently, making large raises in Hand #66 and Hand #71, suggesting a high frequency of stealing with a wide range. Against a top 30-40% range, QJs has roughly 46-50% equity. The pot odds are approximately 1350 to call to win a pot of 2950 (approx 46% equity required). Given her high aggression frequency, her range is likely wider than the break-even point, making this a +EV call. I have a healthy stack of 7250 and can afford the variance to potentially eliminate a dangerous short-stacked player.
UTG with 98o - this is a weak offsuit hand from the earliest position. With 4 players behind me, I need a stronger range to open. Folding is the correct play here.

I am holding Ace-Jack of hearts, which is a premium starting hand in a five-handed game, particularly when suited. Since Claude (UTG) has folded, I am the first to act. Raising is the standard play here to build the pot and define the range of the remaining three players. I will open for 300, which is 3x the big blind, a standard opening size to maintain control of the hand.

2s3h is one of the worst hands in poker, offsuit and disconnected low cards with poor playability postflop. Facing an UTG+1 raise to 300 (3x BB) from Flash, who has shown aggression in spots but from a strong position. Pot odds are ~2.5:1 (calling 300 into 750), requiring ~28% equity, but 23o has <25% vs typical UTG+1 range and worse multiway if others call. Deep tournament (hand 73/150), button position doesn't justify speculative call with trash. Preserve 39BB stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c 2d, which is a very weak hand (trash). Facing a 3bb raise from UTG+1 (Flash), who has a large stack, and being out of position, I have no reason to enter this pot. The hand has very poor playability and equity. Folding is the only standard and correct play here.

Flash is opening to 3x from UTG+1 (5-handed), and while the pot odds are tempting (200 to win 450 now / 650 after call), T6o plays poorly out of position: it’s frequently dominated by better Tx and has weak playability postflop. Against an aggressive opponent who will c-bet and barrel, defending this offsuit combo will realize equity badly and create expensive reverse-implied-odds spots. I’ll keep my BB defense tighter here and avoid marginal OOP calls.

I am UTG in a 5-handed game with Kc 7d. While I have the chip lead, K7o is a weak hand to open with from early position, as it is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop. Given the stack sizes and the presence of players willing to call or 3-bet, folding is the most disciplined play to maintain my stack for better opportunities.

Pocket Aces (Ad Ac) is the strongest starting hand in No-Limit Hold'em. In UTG+1 position with no action before me except blinds posted and Flash folding UTG, a standard open raise is optimal to build the pot, get value from worse hands, and potentially isolate against the blinds. Raise size of 250 is approximately 2.5x the big blind, consistent with common opening sizes observed in previous hands (e.g., many 250 raises preflop). Pot is 150, my stack is 3925 (39BB), sufficient for postflop flexibility. Opponents have deeper stacks, but AA performs well heads-up or multiway while thin value extracting. Folding or limping is -EV; raising maximizes long-term EV.

I hold Ks2h on the Button. The player in UTG+1 (Elon) has opened to 250 (2.5bb). While I have position, K2o is a very weak hand that is often dominated by an opener's range (KQ, KJ, AK, etc.). Elon has a shorter stack (37bb) and might be opening lighter, but K2o plays poorly post-flop and doesn't have enough equity to justify a call or a 3-bet bluff in this spot. Preserving my healthy stack of 88bb is preferable to getting involved with trash hands.

With AKo in the small blind versus a late-position open from a short stack (Elon ~36.8bb), I want to 3-bet for clear value and to isolate. Flat-calling out of position invites the big blind to come along/squeeze and realizes equity poorly. A raise also sets up a natural stack-off versus Elon’s likely reshove range (we’re ahead of many dominated Ax/Kx and flipping versus pairs), while giving us fold equity if he’s opening wide. Sizing: SB 3-bet to ~4x to deny equity and simplify SPR.

Facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo (1000) after Elon opened (250). I have Q9o which is a marginal hand that plays poorly against a 3-bet range. I'm out of position and would need to call 900 into a 1350 pot. Q9o doesn't have great equity against a typical 3-betting range, and Elon could still squeeze behind. Clear fold.

Pocket Aces remain the nuts pre-flop facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo in the SB. FiveTwo has shown aggression in previous hands, including large 3-bets (e.g., Hand 46 to 2150, Hand 31 calling large), but SB 3-bets can be wide for squeeze value or speculative hands. With 36BB effective stack (my 3675), calling 750 into 1350 pot leaves deep stacks post-flop where AA plays well but risks multiway or tough spots. 4-betting extracts value from worse pairs, AK/AQ, suited broadways that FiveTwo might flat or 3-bet light with. Sizing to 2800 (about 2.8x the 3-bet) builds a big pot while leaving fold equity against bluffs and committing if shoved on (which I'd call). Shoving all-in is too strong and folds out too much value; flat risks overcards peeling off. This maximizes EV in a tournament spot with 76 hands left.

Elon’s 4-bet to 2800 leaves him with only 1125 behind, so the SPR is effectively tiny and he’s already pot-committed. With AKo we’re well ahead of any wide 4-bet/value-jam range (dominating many Ax/Kx and only flipping versus pairs), and even versus a tighter range (QQ+/AK) we have enough equity to stack off. Calling lets him realize equity by seeing a flop; raising now to the minimum (3925) effectively puts his remaining stack in preflop, maximizes fold equity against any non-premium 4-bet bluffs, and simplifies the hand.

Pocket Aces remain massively ahead of any 5-bet jamming range from FiveTwo in the SB. Historical aggression from FiveTwo (e.g., large 3-bets like Hand 46, 5-betting spots) suggests a polarized range: QQ+/AK for value or bluffs. Even against QQ+, AA has ~82% equity heads-up. Pot odds are exceptional: calling 1125 into 6825 (plus call) for ~7:1, requiring only ~12% equity, far exceeded. Effective stack 11BB (1125), min raise N/A so only call or fold; no raise possible. Tournament stage (74/150 hands) favors variance absorption for max EV with premium. Folding AA here is a massive mistake; call commits the stack profitably.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with K8o offsuit. This hand is marginal at best; it's not suited, has a weak kicker for top pair potential, and is easily dominated by better kings (KQ, KJ, KQ suited). Standard opening range UTG even short-handed doesn't include K8o. Raising risks playing OOP post-flop with a weak hand, limping is poor strategy pre-flop, so folding preserves stack for better opportunities. Deep stacks (80BB) allow waiting for premium hands.

I am in UTG+1 (effectively middle position in 5-handed play) with Q9 offsuit. This is a weak hand that is easily dominated by hands like KQ, AQ, QJ, or A9 which opponents might 3-bet or flat call with. Without antes and with 88 big blinds deep, there is no need to loosen my opening range this much from early position. The standard play is to fold.

Folds to the button in a 5-handed game: this is a prime steal spot. A5o has an ace blocker, decent playability in position, and benefits a lot from picking up the blinds uncontested. I’ll open-raise rather than limp/call to apply pressure to the blinds and keep initiative; if I face a sizable 3-bet from either blind (especially Flash), I can comfortably fold this offsuit hand.

T8 suited is a playable hand against a button raise. Suited connectors have good playability with straight and flush potential. While I'm out of position, calling 200 into a 400 pot is reasonable. Too strong to fold, but not strong enough to 3-bet OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5s. The Button (FiveTwo) opened for 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to call 150 more into a pot of 600. This gives me 4:1 pot odds, requiring about 20% equity. While 95o might technically have enough raw equity against wide ranges, it is a very poor hand to play out of position in a multi-way pot. Realizing equity with this hand will be difficult, and as the tournament chip leader, I prefer to avoid marginal spots with weak holdings. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I flopped top pair with Tc on a Th-4d-6h board. There's a heart flush draw present. I'm out of position against the preflop aggressor. With top pair medium kicker, I should lead for value and protection against the flush draw. A small bet around 1/3 pot should accomplish this while not bloating the pot with a vulnerable hand.

Claude’s small lead (200 into 800) looks like a low-risk stab/probe from the SB calling range (small pairs, 6x/4x, some Tx, occasional draws). With A5o I don’t have a real draw here (only weak backdoors), so raising would be a fairly naked bluff on a board that hits his range reasonably well; I’d rather not bloat the pot and get played back at. Calling is cheap, keeps his range wide, and gives me position to either take it away on turns when he checks or improve to top pair on an Ace (or occasionally a 5) while also realizing my small backdoor equities. If he continues with meaningful turn pressure on bad runouts, I can comfortably release.

The turn 4s paired the board, improving my hand to two pair (tens and fours). My T8 now beats any single pair and ties with other tens. FiveTwo just called my flop bet - likely a draw or weak made hand. I should continue betting for value against flush draws and worse made hands. Around 65% pot sizing is appropriate.

Claude’s line (donk small on the flop, then a much bigger barrel on the paired turn) is consistent with him moving from a probe to real pressure with made hands (Tx/6x, trips 4x, occasional boats) and some heart-draw semi-bluffs. With As5d we have essentially no clean draw: only river A’s meaningfully improve us, and even those aren’t always clean versus A4/boats. Needing ~28% to continue, this is too far down in my range to defend profitably, and raising would require an unrealistic fold frequency while risking getting played back at by his 4x/strong holdings. I’ll preserve stack and fold.

Current hand is JTo (Jack of Hearts, Ten of Spades) in the UTG position at a 5-handed table. With 88 big blinds, I have a healthy stack, but JTo is a marginal hand to open from early position. It is easily dominated by hands like AJ, KJ, QJ, AT, and KT, which opponents often call or 3-bet with. Additionally, playing out of position against deep-stacked opponents like Claude and Flash is disadvantageous. The standard play for JTo from UTG in a 5-handed game is to fold to avoid reverse implied odds and difficult post-flop situations.

5-handed with Pro folding UTG, I’m effectively in early/mid position but still have Button + blinds to act behind me. 7♦3♦ is too weak to profitably open from here: it’s dominated when we make a 7, makes weak straights, and plays poorly out of position versus competent 3-bets and floats. Limping invites isolation and puts me in multiway spots with a capped range. With plenty of stack depth (82bb), I’d rather preserve chips for higher-EV opens and 3-bet/steal spots.
Q7s on the button with two folds in front. This is a good stealing spot - suited broadway with a gap is playable from the button. I'll raise to pick up the blinds or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-8 offsuit. Claude, the button, has raised to 250. While 9-8 is a decent connector, it is quite weak when played out of position, especially against another large stack like Claude (16,675). The pot odds are roughly 3:1, but the disadvantage of being out of position for the rest of the hand makes this a marginal call at best. Furthermore, Elon is still to act in the Big Blind and could potentially squeeze or shove, putting me in a difficult spot. Given my current chip lead and the need to preserve my stack over the 150 hands, folding here is the most disciplined and lower-variance play.

Preflop in the big blind facing an early position fold, another fold, small blind fold, and a button raise to 250 (2.5x). My hand is 7c8d offsuit, a speculative hand with straight potential and decent playability postflop. Claude on the button has a wide opening range (likely 35-45% of hands based on history of frequent raises). Pot is 400, calling 150 more gives pot odds of about 2.67:1, which is favorable for a multi-street hand deepstacked (~78BB). Folding is too tight here as BB defending range should include this vs button open. No strong 3-bet value or bluff candidate. Calling keeps pot small, realizes equity, and positions me well heads-up out of position with implied odds.

On this Jd Tc 5h flop, I hold 7c8d, giving me a weak gutshot straight draw (4 outs to the 9 for a straight) and no pair or flush potential. Equity is marginal (~16% vs a tight range, less vs polarized), and I'm OOP vs Claude's button range, which includes many Jx, Tx, broadways, Ax suited, and bluffs. Pot is 550, acting first postflop. Betting risks raising his range or folding out better; no strong value or semi-bluff (only gutshot, no overcards or blockers). Checking realizes equity by controlling pot size, invites a c-bet from his wide continuation range (he cbets frequently per history), allowing me to fold cheaply to small bets or call/fold to larger ones while preserving stack. If he checks back (possible with missed draws or weak Jx), I get a free turn card to chase or showdown. Optimal check given playability and deep stacks (~77BB).

I have Q7o on a J-T-5 board with a gutshot to a straight (need a 9 for K-Q-J-T-9). Elon checked to me. This is a good c-bet spot - I have position, overcards with a queen, and a gutshot. The board is somewhat coordinated but I should bet to take down the pot or charge draws. I'll bet around 50% pot.

On this Jd Tc 5h flop, holding 7c8d for only a weak gutshot straight draw (4 outs to a 9), with no pair, overcards, or flush potential. Equity against Claude's button c-bet range (Jx, Tx, broadways, Ax, sets, draws, bluffs) is around 15-20%, insufficient for a profitable call OOP given pot odds of ~3:1 (275 into 825, pot becomes 1100). Raising as a semi-bluff is thin without blockers or fold equity justification vs his frequent aggression. Calling risks difficult turn decisions OOP with minimal implied odds (9-high straight vulnerable to Jx/Tx/A-broadways). Folding preserves stack (~77BB deep) for better spots, standard vs 50% pot c-bet from wide button range. Consistent with preflop BB defense realizing marginal equity selectively.

5-handed and fairly deep (~80bb), T9s is comfortably in an UTG open range due to strong playability (can make strong straights/flushes and semi-bluff well) and benefits from having initiative. Limping invites multiway and lowers our ability to realize equity; opening also pressures the blinds and isolates weaker holdings. I’ll use a standard small raise size to keep my range consistent and not bloat the pot unnecessarily this deep.

Holding Ac2c (suited ace) facing a UTG open to 250. Suited aces have value with nut flush potential. While A2s is on the weaker end, I have position on the raiser and good stack depth. Calling to see a flop is reasonable - I can continue if I hit a flush draw or top pair, fold to aggression on bad boards.

I am on the button with 6d 3c, which is one of the weakest hands in the deck. FiveTwo has raised from under-the-gun (UTG), and Claude has called from UTG+1. Even though I am in position, calling a raise from early positions with this junk is a losing play. I have a significant chip lead and do not need to play speculative, low-equity hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

73o is an extremely weak hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (a competent player based on history) and a call from Claude, the range is strong. In SB out of position, calling commits chips with reverse implied odds and poor post-flop prospects. Folding preserves stack for better spots in a tournament with 73 hands left.

I am in the Big Blind facing a raise to 250 from UTG and a call from UTG+1. The pot is 650, and I need to call 150 more, giving me excellent pot odds of roughly 4.3 to 1. My hand, 9d Td, is a strong suited connector that plays well in multi-way pots and has good playability post-flop. Being deep-stacked (87 BBs) allows for significant implied odds if I hit a strong draw or made hand. A squeeze (3-bet) from out of position against an UTG range is too risky and unnecessary given the great price to call and close the action.

I hold 9dTd on a flop of 8sQc7c. I have flopped an open-ended straight draw (OESD) with outs to a Jack or a Six. Being out of position in a multi-way pot against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and a caller (Claude), the standard line is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal here as it denies me information and might fold out bluffs or isolate me against stronger ranges. I will check and evaluate based on the action behind me; I intend to continue if facing a reasonable bet due to my strong draw.

We have a massive combo draw on Qc-8s-7c: open-ended straight draw (6/J) plus a strong flush draw (clubs). In a multiway pot, checking back gives two opponents a free card on a very dynamic board where many turn cards change equities and action. With initiative, the best way to realize our equity is to c-bet for fold equity vs hands like A-high, underpairs, weak 8x/7x, and to start building a pot for when we hit, while also being comfortable continuing versus a raise due to our very high equity.

I’ll use a fairly strong sizing to pressure one-pair ranges and charge dominated draws, but not so large that we unnecessarily bloat when called by strong Qx/sets: about 75% pot.

Flopped the nut flush draw with Ac2c on Qc7c8s board. I have 9 clean outs to the nuts plus backdoor straight potential and an ace overcard. Pot odds are 600 to win 1400 (~2.3:1), needing about 30% equity. With ~35% equity on the flop (flush draw + overcard), calling is mathematically justified. Raising as a semi-bluff is tempting but Pro is still behind me. Calling maintains flexibility and good implied odds if I hit.

I hold an Open-Ended Straight Draw (9d Td) on an 8s Qc 7c board. The pot is 2000 after FiveTwo bet 600 and Claude called. I need to call 600, offering me pot odds of approximately 3.3:1 or 23% required equity. An OESD typically has about 32% equity by the river (8 outs). Although the presence of two clubs on the board slightly discounts my outs (flush risk), the price is good enough to continue, especially since I am closing the action and won't be raised again on this street. I have good implied odds if I hit my straight against opponents showing strength (likely top pair or better). Folding is too tight, and raising is reckless against two opponents showing strength. I call.

On the turn, the board pairs with the 7s (8s Qc 7c 7s). I still hold an Open-Ended Straight Draw with 9d Td. Being out of position against the pre-flop raiser (FiveTwo) and a caller (Claude) who both showed interest on the flop, my best option is to check. Leading out (donking) into two opponents with just a draw is unprofitable as I have little fold equity against a Queen or trips, and I risk facing a raise that would force me to fold my equity. I will check and decide based on the sizing if faced with a bet, hoping to see a river card cheaply.

Turn pairs the 7, which slightly dampens fold equity versus Qx/slowplayed trips, but we still have a huge 15-out combo draw (9 clubs + 6/J straights minus the 2 club overlaps). Checking here is awkward because we’re sandwiched (Claude can bet and put us in a tougher spot), and we give two players a free river on a very dynamic board. I want to keep applying pressure with initiative, deny equity to hands like pocket pairs/8x/A-high with a club, and start setting up a river pot where we can bet big when we hit. Because it’s multiway and a check-raise will be strong, I’ll use a controlled sizing rather than bombing—betting around half pot keeps our risk manageable while still generating folds.

Still have nut flush draw with Ac2c. Board paired 7s which is concerning for full houses. Pot odds: 1200 to win 5000 = ~24% needed. With 9 flush outs I have ~19.5% raw equity - slightly below odds. However, implied odds are strong if I hit the nuts, and I can still catch an ace. The board pairing reduces my effective equity somewhat since 7x or Q7 now has full house potential. Pro is still behind but already showed weakness. Calling to see the river with the nut flush draw is the play - folding would be too tight given pot size and draw strength.

I hold an Open-Ended Straight Draw (9d Td) on a board of 8s Qc 7c 7s. Facing a bet of 1200 from FiveTwo and a call from Claude, the pot has grown to 7400. I need to call 1200, offering me excellent pot odds of approximately 7.1 to 1 (requiring ~14% equity). With 8 outs to a straight (Jacks and Sixes), my raw equity to hit on the river is roughly 17%. Although the club outs are slightly devalued due to the flush draw on board, the price is too good to fold, and I have implied odds if I hit my straight against opponents showing strength. Raising is not viable with just a draw in a multi-way pot, so calling to realize my equity is the best decision.

I hold 9d Td on a board of 8s Qc 7c 7s Ks. I had an open-ended straight draw on the flop and turn, but the river King did not complete my straight (needed a Jack or Six). I currently have 9-high (playing the board essentially) with absolutely no showdown value. The pot is large (6200) and I have 6750 behind. Two opponents are involved: FiveTwo (UTG) has been aggressive, betting flop and turn, and is now pot-committed with a stack of 6175. Claude (UTG+1) has called two streets, indicating a made hand or a very strong draw. Bluffing into two opponents who have shown significant strength, one of whom is effectively all-in, is extremely low EV. I have missed my draw and must give up the hand to preserve my remaining stack. My only option is to check and fold to any bet.

River comes Ks on Qc-8s-7c-7s-Ks and we completely brick: no straight (need J/6) and no flush (only 4 clubs total), so we’re sitting on T-high.

After we bet flop and turn and got called by BOTH Claude and Pro, their ranges are heavily weighted toward made hands (Qx, some Kx that picked up, and a meaningful amount of 7x/traps). In a multiway pot, a bluff has to get two folds, and our line (double-barrel with a very plausible club/straight draw) is exactly the type that opponents will look up with a queen or better—especially since the river K is a card they can convince themselves doesn’t change much versus a missed draw.

With our stack (~6.2k) being ~pot-sized, an all-in bluff would be high variance with low fold equity, while smaller sizing doesn’t apply enough pressure. Best play is to take the free showdown and preserve chips.

Missed my flush draw completely - just holding ace high. Both Pro and FiveTwo checked the river, showing weakness. However, bluffing here is risky: I'm bluffing into two players who both called 1200 on the turn, showing they have something. The pot is large (6200) but betting would require significant sizing to fold out hands that called twice. Ace high has virtually no showdown value but checking is still better than a multi-way bluff that's unlikely to succeed.
4d9s is a weak offsuit hand with no real playability. UTG is the tightest position - need premium hands here. Easy fold.

I am holding Jack-Seven offsuit in UTG+1. This is a weak starting hand, especially for an early position open. With a comfortable stack of over 18,000 chips and plenty of hands remaining in the tournament, there is no strategic reason to play such a marginal hand and risk chips against the remaining aggressive players. I will fold and wait for a more favorable spot.

Preflop on the button with 6c2h offsuit, which is one of the worst possible hands with terrible connectivity, no high cards, and poor playability multiway. Action has folded to me (Claude UTG and Flash UTG+1 folded), pot is 150 with blinds posted (to call 100). While button is a good spot to steal, 62o has zero showdown value and minimal fold equity if contested by SB Pro or BB FiveTwo, who have solid stacks (~60-67BB) and position. Raising risks doubling blinds or worse, limping creates dead money but invites multiway pots where this hand is crushed. Optimal play is to fold and wait for a playable hand to maximize long-term EV in tournament with 150 hands remaining.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit (Qc 4s). The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has shown aggression in previous hands. Q4o is a very weak hand, even in a blind-versus-blind scenario. While getting 3:1 odds to complete the blind is tempting, playing this hand out of position against an aggressive opponent is likely to lead to a negative expectation due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. Folding preserves my stack and avoids a marginal spot.

I am sitting in the UTG position in a 5-handed game with Ace-Trey offsuit. While 5-handed play requires a wider opening range than full-ring, A3o remains a marginal hand to open from the earliest position, especially with a large stack that I want to preserve. Opening here often leads to difficult post-flop situations out of position against the Big Blind (Claude, who also has a large stack) or facing 3-bets from the medium stacks (Elon, Pro, FiveTwo) who are in the 60-80 BB range. Given the tournament stage and my current lead, folding this hand is the most disciplined and professional play.

Preflop, UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with J7o offsuit. This is a weak hand with poor playability, dominated by better jacks, and unlikely to make strong hands. Early position requires a strong range; limping or raising is -EV. Pot 150, blinds 50/100, standard fold to preserve stack for better spots. Table history shows aggression, but no need to get involved here.

I am on the Button with a very weak hand [3c Th] (Ten-Three offsuit). Although the action has folded to me, this hand has extremely poor playability and equity. Attempting to steal the blinds against the chip leader in the Big Blind with such a weak holding is not a profitable long-term strategy. The standard play is to fold.

Folded to me in the SB; with 6♣2♦ I’m near the bottom of any SB opening range and raising is unlikely to show immediate profit against Claude’s big stack/likely defend frequency. Completing is very cheap (50 more to win 150, needing only ~25% equity) and keeps the pot small out of position. Plan: see a flop and proceed cautiously—mostly check/fold on misses, continue only with decent equity (pair/strong draw) or good textures to stab.

K7s in the BB facing a limp from SB. This is a spot where I should raise to either take down the pot or play with initiative. K7s is strong enough to raise here for value/protection against a limper. Raising to 350 is standard - about 3.5x.

We completed extremely wide getting a great price, but once Claude iso-raises to 350 we’re now facing a much worse situation: 6♣2♦ is dominated, plays terribly out of position, and Claude’s likely continuing range (and postflop pressure) creates big reverse-implied-odds. Even though the immediate pot odds (~250 to win 700 after calling) aren’t awful, we won’t realize equity well from the SB and will bleed on most runouts. Best is to cut losses and preserve stack.

65o is a speculative offsuit hand with minimal playability. In UTG position with 5 active players, it's too weak to open limp or raise preflop as it performs poorly multiway and out of position postflop. Folding preserves my 76BB stack for better spots in this tournament stage.

I am in the Hijack (UTG+1) in a 5-handed game with Queen-Seven offsuit. This is a very weak hand that falls well outside a standard opening range from this position, even 5-handed. With three players left to act, including two large stacks in the blinds who can apply pressure, there is no strategic merit in entering the pot. My stack is healthy at 67 BBs, so I should preserve chips and fold this weak hand.

Folds to us on the Button in a 5-handed game, so we should be opening very wide to pressure the blinds and pick up the dead money. Ks2s is comfortably inside a BTN open range: it has good blocker value (removes strong Kx from opponents’ holdings), suited playability, and position lets us realize equity and control pot size when called. Raising also denies the blinds a free realization versus a limp-heavy line.

A5o in the SB against a button open. Decent Ace-high hand with some playability. Getting 2:1 pot odds to call. While OOP, this is a standard defend against a button steal. I'll call and play postflop - could hit an ace or improve.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket eights, a strong hand in a 5-handed game. FiveTwo (Button) opened for 2.5x and Claude (Small Blind) flat-called. This is an ideal spot for a squeeze play. By raising, I can either take the pot down immediately or narrow the field and take the initiative into the post-flop play. Flat-calling would lead to a three-way pot out of position, which is suboptimal for a medium pair like 88. A raise to 1100 puts pressure on FiveTwo's wide button range and discourages Claude from seeing a cheap flop with speculative hands.

Flash’s 3-bet to 1100 is a large squeeze over the SB flat, which is typically a tighter/value-heavy range. Ks2s is dominated by his continuing Kx/Ax and realizes equity poorly even with position in a bloated pot. The price (850 to win 1600) isn’t enough given reverse implied odds and the likelihood of facing multiple barrels; 4-bet bluffing here is also unattractive with shallow-ish effective stack and weak blockers. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

Facing a 3-bet from the BB with A5o. This is a weak hand to continue with OOP against a 3-bet. Getting ~1.9:1 pot odds, but A5o plays poorly out of position - when I hit my ace I'm often dominated, and it lacks the suited connectivity needed for implied odds. Clear fold.

I am in UTG position with Jd 8c, which is a very weak off-suit hand (Jack-Eight offsuit). With four players left to act behind me, opening the pot with this hand is statistically a losing play. My stack is healthy at 67 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The standard and correct strategic decision is to fold.

5-handed and unopened (Pro folded), A9o is comfortably inside my UTG+1 opening range.
