Limping invites multiway pots where A9o realizes poorly and gets dominated; raising leverages fold equity, can win blinds immediately, and lets me represent strong Ax on many boards. I’ll use a standard sizing consistent with the table (bigger than the 2bb minimum) to reduce call frequency and keep initiative.

K5 suited on the button facing a min-raise from UTG+1. Suited king with position is a reasonable calling hand here. I have the stack to see a flop and can play position postflop. Not strong enough to 3-bet for value, but too good to fold on the button.

I am holding King-Jack suited in the Small Blind, which is a very strong hand in a 5-handed game. FiveTwo opened from UTG+1 and Claude called on the button. Since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, I prefer to 3-bet to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, or isolate one opponent. A raise to 1,100 is a standard sizing to apply pressure on the opener and the caller while representing a strong range.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash (SB) to 1100 after FiveTwo's open-raise to 250 from early position and a call from Claude on the button. My hand is 8d3c, complete trash with no high card value, no suitedness, no straight potential. Pot is 1700, calling 1000 more gives poor implied odds in a likely multiway pot out of position in the BB. Stacks are decent but no need to speculate with this hand. Folding preserves my 7550 stack for better spots in this tournament with 70 hands left.

Flash’s 3-bet is a classic squeeze after my open and Claude’s cold-call. With A9o I’m dominated by the Ax that continue, and calling creates an awkward SPR (~2) where top pair becomes a costly bluff-catcher, plus Claude still has position and can come along. A 4-bet bluff is too high-variance at ~56bb effective and burns a big chunk when we get jammed/called by tighter continuing ranges. Best chip-EV and risk control here is to let it go and wait for a better continue candidate (suited wheel Ax, stronger broadways/pairs).

Flash 3-bet to 1100 after I flatted FiveTwo's open. K5s is a marginal hand facing a squeeze. While I have position, K5s doesn't play well against a 3-bet range - I'll often be dominated by better kings and the flush draw alone isn't enough equity. With a healthy 21k stack, no need to call in marginal spots. Fold and wait for a better opportunity.

5-handed UTG we can open wider, but 6♣4♠ is too weak and dominated, with poor playability and reverse-implied-odds. Given the aggressive reshove/3-bet tendencies shown (especially from Flash/Claude) and our solid 56bb stack, there’s no need to take a marginal spot—best is to preserve stack and wait for better hands/positions.
98o in UTG+1 is too weak to open. Offsuit connectors play poorly from early position - not enough equity and playability to justify opening. Easy fold.

I am on the Button with King-Ten offsuit (Kc Ts). After the first two players folded, it is standard to open for a raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. KTo is well within the opening range for the Button in a 5-handed game. I will raise to 250, which is my standard opening size.

Pocket 77 is a premium hand for 3-betting from the SB facing an isolation raise from the button after two early folds. Button ranges are wide and loose based on history (frequent raises and aggression). 3-betting takes initiative, builds the pot when ahead, isolates against worse pairs/draws, and has great playability postflop with set-mining potential and overpair equity. Calling OOP is viable for set-mining (good implied odds with deep stacks), but 3-bet maximizes EV by folding out junk and extracting value from floats/calls. BB (Pro) may defend wide but our sizing pressures. Standard sizing ~3.5x the raise OOP: 250 x 3.5 = ~875, round to 900. Effective stack vs Flash deep, vs BB 66bb. Strong play to grow stack in tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 3h. Facing a button raise to 250 and a Small Blind 3-bet to 900, the action is heavy. K3s is a marginal hand that plays poorly in 3-bet pots, especially when calling cold. Investing 800 chips (over 12% of my stack) to see a flop with a dominated hand is not profitable. The standard and correct play is to fold.

I opened King-Ten offsuit (Kc Ts) from the Button for 250. Elon, in the Small Blind, has 3-bet to 900. While I have position for the rest of the hand, KTo is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by a standard 3-betting range (like AK, AQ, AJ, KQ, or medium-to-high pocket pairs). Elon's 3-bet size is quite large (3.6x my open), and with a stack of roughly 66-70 big blinds, his range is likely to be relatively strong. Given that I have a significant stack and want to avoid high-variance spots with dominated hands against the shorter stacks, folding is the most disciplined and professional play here.
4s6d is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is an easy fold - I should only be opening strong hands from early position.

I am holding Jack-Seven offsuit in UTG+1 (hijack position in this 5-handed game). This is a weak starting hand that does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against several opponents. With a healthy stack and many hands remaining, there is no reason to risk chips on such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop on the button with As6o after two folds (UTG and CO). Excellent position against two blinds. A6o is a profitable raising hand here due to ace blocker, potential for top pair top kicker, and live cards for straights/flushes. Isolation raise vs blinds who may defend wide but I have positional advantage and deep stacks (~55-79BB). Standard open size of 2.5x BB. Min raise is 200, but 250 is appropriate pot-building size without overcommitting.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit (Ts 7c). The player on the Button (Elon) has raised to 250 (2.5 BB). T7o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. While the pot odds are reasonable, the difficulty of realizing equity with this hand OOP against an aggressive opponent makes calling unprofitable. 3-betting is also not attractive as this hand blocks little of the opponent's continuing range and has poor playability if called. The standard and most prudent play is to fold.

Button open is small and we’re getting a very good price in the big blind (150 to win 400 ~ 27% needed). Even though 9♠5♦ is weak and plays mostly as a bluff-catcher, it has enough raw equity versus a Button opening range to defend, and calling keeps the pot manageable compared to 3-betting a hand with poor top-pair prospects. Plan postflop: proceed cautiously on high/connected boards, continue more on 9xx/5xx or favorable low textures, and be ready to check-fold versus sustained aggression on bad runouts.

We defended BB vs a small BTN open and hit an A-2-3 two-diamond board with 9-high and a gutshot to the wheel (any 4). This is a texture where we can represent a lot (2x/3x/straight/sets), but our specific hand has limited showdown value and no flush draw, so leading risks getting floated or raised by a range that contains plenty of Ax. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets the preflop aggressor c-bet his range, and preserves our ability to either take a free card when he checks back or respond appropriately (often fold vs large pressure, sometimes continue vs small sizing given the gutter).

Post-flop on a 2d Ac 3d board, I have top pair top kicker (As) with the nut flush blocker (spade, but board has two diamonds). FiveTwo limp-called pre from BB and checked, representing a wide range including draws (diamond flush draws, wheel straight draws like 4x, 5x), weaker aces (A2-A5), pairs, etc. This board is highly coordinated and draw-heavy, so I need to bet for value against worse (weaker Aces, pairs, draws) and protection against implied odds on turns/rivers that complete draws. Position on button is excellent for controlling pot. Pot is 550; a 2/3 pot bet of ~370 builds pot, denies equity, and sizes well against calling ranges. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs. Standard c-bet here with strong hand on wet board.

We’re getting a good price versus a likely range c-bet (375 to win 1300 after we call), and while 9♠5♦ has almost no showdown value, we do have a live gutshot to the wheel (any 4) plus a useful diamond blocker (5♦) that slightly reduces his strongest continuing semi-bluffs and helps our ability to represent strength on some runouts.

Out of position I don’t want to inflate the pot with a check-raise that gets continued on heavily by Ax/strong draws, so the best line is to peel once and play turns honestly: continue aggressively/value when a 4 hits; otherwise mostly check-fold versus meaningful turn pressure, and only consider continuing if he uses very small sizing or gives up.

Turn pairs the deuce, which is a strong range card for the BB defender (we have far more 2x, A2, and 32-type combos than the Button). With 9♠5♦ we still have near-zero showdown value, so checking is mostly just setting up a check-fold vs another barrel. Instead, I’ll use a small probe/lead to leverage the range advantage and fold out his high-card air and some marginal floats, while risking a modest amount. If raised, we can comfortably fold; if called, we can continue barreling on good rivers (4 for the straight, many diamonds to represent flushes/strong 2x).

Turn board pairs the deuces (2d Ac 3d 2h), which is somewhat good for my TPTK (As6c) as it reduces some straight draw equity and doesn't complete the nut flush draw directly. FiveTwo's limp-call pre, check-call flop c-bet, and now small turn lead of 450 into 1300 (~1/3 pot) suggests a polarized range: strong value like two pair (A2, 32), trips (22), or sets; weaker Ax continuing; pairs that improved; or bluffs/draws betting for protection/thin value. His small sizing invites calls and doesn't scream monster. I have position, strong showdown value with top pair top kicker (ace blocker), and excellent pot odds (450 into 1750 for ~3.9:1, needing ~20% equity, which I have against his range). Calling keeps the pot controlled (~2200 pot post-call), allows me to realize equity in position, and I can bluff-catch or value bet river if checked to. Raising risks bloating pot OOP against possible monsters and folds out worse; folding is too tight given history and sizing. FiveTwo plays loose passively often (limps BB wide, calls multi-street), so calling exploits that. Stacks deep enough (~45-70BB) for river play.

River is A♣-2♦-3♦-2♥-3♥, so our 9♠5♦ has no pair and at showdown we mostly just play the board (A2233) and chop versus the bulk of Elon’s range (all the random broadways/Ax that don’t contain a 2/3, plus missed diamonds). That creates a great “scoop” bluff spot: by betting we can often fold out his chop-hands, which is a big EV gain, while if he calls with a chop hand our EV is essentially the same as checking (no rake). 

Our turn lead also credibly represents exactly the hands that now beat the board (2x/3x/boats), so a medium sizing should get disciplined folds from his air/missed draws and many indifferent chops. If he raises, we can comfortably fold since that’s heavily weighted to boats.

I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to maximize fold equity without risking a huge amount versus the small portion of his range that improved to a full house.

River board 2d Ac 3d 2h 3h pairs both low cards, turning my TPTK As6c into two pair (aces and threes using board pairs). Villain's BB calling range vs my button raise (Ax, pairs, suited broadways/connectors) check-calls flop on draw-heavy board, leads small turn (~1/3 pot) after deuce pairs (possibly thin value/protection with Ax, two pair A2, or sets), calls my call. River pairs trey, he bets 1/2 pot (1100 into 2200). This polarizes somewhat but loose-passive history (wide limp-calls pre/post, multi-street calls) suggests frequent Ax like A4-K (same two pair), overpairs now two pair, some missed draws turning into bluffs unlikely but possible. FH/quads (22,33,AA,A2,A3) beat me (~20-30% of range: ~6+6+6+~8=26 combos vs ~100 Ax), but majority showdown better or equal. Excellent odds (1100 to win 4400, ~20% equity needed, have ~40-50% vs betting range). Calling exploits his passive tendencies, realizes equity in position, deep stacks post-call (him ~2.3k left, me ~5.7k). Raising risks vs monsters, folding too tight for pot odds/history.

I have been dealt pocket Aces (Ah Ad), the strongest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. My objective is to build a significant pot and maximize value. I am acting first (UTG) in this 5-handed game. I will open with a standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and entice action from my opponents, several of whom have shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands. This sizing is consistent with my opening range and helps disguise the strength of my hand while protecting against speculative holdings.

2c8d is one of the worst possible starting hands in Hold'em, offsuit with no high cards, poor connectivity, and terrible post-flop playability. Facing an UTG raise from Flash to 300 into a 450 pot, this is a clear fold regardless of position or stacks. We have 101bb effective and no reason to speculate with trash. Preserve stack for better spots in this tournament with 66 hands left.

I am on the Button facing a 3bb raise from the UTG player (Flash). My hand, King-Ten offsuit, is a marginal broadway hand that is frequently dominated by a UTG opening range (which typically includes AK, KQ, KJ, TT+, etc.). Calling would leave me vulnerable to domination and difficult post-flop decisions, even in position. 3-betting is not ideal as a bluff here. Given my stack depth (65bb) and the strength of an early position open, folding is the most disciplined and +EV decision.

Flash is opening from UTG into 5-handed action; his range is relatively strong. K7o from the small blind plays poorly (dominated king, weak kicker, bad realization OOP) and calling invites the big blind to come along, worsening our equity. It’s also not a good 3-bet candidate here at ~34bb effective since we don’t have suitedness/connectivity and will be forced into tough postflop spots. Best is to conserve stack and fold.

Holding 3d6d in the BB facing a 300 UTG raise from Flash. This is a very weak hand - suited connectors at the bottom of the range. UTG raises typically represent strong holdings. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (200 to win 450), 36s plays poorly post-flop against a UTG range, especially out of position. Not worth defending here.

UTG position with 5c8h, a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity and no high card strength. Standard tight-aggressive strategy dictates folding trash hands from early position to avoid playing out of position post-flop with marginal equity. Pot is small (150), but risking chips pre-flop with this hand has negative EV against a field of 5 players, many with deep stacks. Preserve stack for premium spots to maximize long-term growth in tournament.

I am in UTG+1 with Jd 3s after the UTG player folded. This is a very weak hand (J3 offsuit) with poor playability and equity. There are still three players left to act behind me, including two large stacks in the blinds and a short stack on the button. There is no reason to get involved with this hand. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Folded to us on the Button 5-handed with 34bb. J8o is not premium but it’s comfortably inside a late-position opening range in a short-handed game, and the blinds are deep enough that we realize positional edge well when called. Raising also pressures the blinds and picks up 150 uncontested often, which is valuable with our stack size. Plan versus resistance: continue versus flats; fold to larger 3-bets from the big stacks unless sizing/reads make it very favorable.

I have Ah2s in the SB facing a button raise to 250 from FiveTwo (short stack with 3150). A2 offsuit is a reasonable hand to defend here - I have an ace and decent pot odds (200 to win 400). Could 3-bet to pressure the short stack, but calling is also fine to see a flop in position relative to the BB.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Queen of spades, a premium hand. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 250 and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. With a strong suited broadway hand, this is a prime opportunity for a squeeze play. FiveTwo has a relatively short stack (3,150), and Claude has been playing a wide range of hands. By raising now, I can potentially take the pot down immediately or build value against weaker calling ranges while maintaining initiative. A raise to 1,100 (approximately 4.5x the initial raise) is a standard squeeze size from the blinds.

Flash’s 3-bet to 1100 is a large sizing versus our 2.5bb open, and with ~31.5bb effective it puts us in an awkward stack-to-pot ratio if we continue (calling leaves ~23bb with a pot already ~2.45k heads-up, and Claude can still come along). J8o is too easily dominated by the broadway-heavy range that 3-bets and then continues versus a short stack, and we don’t have the suitedness/connectivity to realize equity well. A 4-bet shove would need very high fold equity and performs poorly when called. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

Flash 3-bet to 1100 after I flatted FiveTwo's open. A2o is a marginal hand that doesn't play well facing a 3-bet. I need to call 850 into a pot of ~2450, requiring ~35% equity. Against a typical 3-bet range, A2o doesn't have enough equity and plays poorly postflop even when I hit. This is a clear fold - not strong enough to call or 4-bet.

I am in UTG position with 8c 9s, which is an unsuited connector. In a 5-handed game, while ranges are wider than a full ring, 98o is still too weak to open profitably from early position. It plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position, and is often dominated. With a stack of 65.5 BB, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal hands. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

Qs4s is too weak to play from UTG+1 in a 5-handed lineup, especially with ~31.5bb where an open/fold costs a meaningful chunk of our stack and we’re likely to face aggressive 3-bets from the big stacks behind (Button/SB/BB). Limping creates tough multiway, out-of-position spots. Preserve stack and wait for stronger opens/reshove opportunities.

Button position with T7o. Two players folded in front of me, only blinds remain. This is a standard steal spot with position. T7o isn't a premium hand but it's playable on the button against just the blinds. I'll raise to steal or play in position if called.

I am in the Small Blind with 9d 6d. Claude, who is the chip leader and sitting on the button, has raised to 2.5bb. While 96s is a speculative suited connector, calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand against a very active and deep-stacked opponent. Given the pot odds are roughly 3:1, it is tempting, but the risk of being squeezed by Elon in the Big Blind or simply playing a weak hand out of position makes folding the most prudent long-term strategy to preserve my stack for higher-equity opportunities.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 250 after two early folds. My hand is 5h7d, a weak offsuit gapped hand with poor playability, low equity against Claude's button raising range which is wide but still favors folding marginal hands like this. Pot odds are 150 to call into 550 (27% needed), but equity is insufficient, and out of position post-flop is disastrous. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots with 100BB effective depth.

5-handed UTG, A6o is strong enough to open in a short-handed game: it blocks premium Ax, can make top pair vs blinds, and has some wheel potential. With a 31.5bb stack I want to keep initiative and pick up blinds/antes without bloating too much; a standard 2.5x open also avoids giving the blinds overly good immediate odds. Plan is raise/fold versus large 3-bets from the big stacks and play positionally postflop when called.

KJs is a strong hand facing a min-raise from UTG. FiveTwo has a short stack (2900 chips) and has been active. I should 3-bet here for value and to isolate. A raise to 750 is standard sizing - about 3x the open.

I am holding 9h 5h on the button. While the hand is suited, it is extremely weak to consider calling a 3-bet. FiveTwo raised from UTG and Claude has 3-bet from UTG+1. This sequence typically indicates very strong ranges from both players. My hand is easily dominated, and calling here would be a waste of chips. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position and stack size.

AK suited is one of the best hands in poker, especially suited for flush potential. Facing an UTG open from FiveTwo (short stack ~2900 total) and a 3-bet from Claude (deep stack, aggressive history). Calling is loose OOP with two opponents; folding is too tight for this premium. 4-betting applies max pressure: FiveTwo likely folds or commits light, Claude may continue with worse (AQ, JJ-QQ) or fold monsters. Sizing to ~3x the 3-bet (2250 total) builds pot for value while denying equity. Postflop OOP is playable if called, with domination edge and blockers. Maximizes stack EV in tournament.

I am holding a low pair (44) in the Big Blind facing significant strength: a UTG raise, a UTG+1 3-bet, and a cold 4-bet from the Small Blind. The price to call is 2150 chips, which is roughly one-third of my remaining stack. Given the heavy action, it is highly likely I am dominated by higher pairs or facing strong broadway cards. Set mining is not profitable here due to the lack of implied odds (stack-to-call ratio is too low). Preserving my stack of ~64BB is the only viable play.

Facing an UTG+1 3-bet and then a cold 4-bet squeeze from the SB, A6o is in very rough shape (dominated by the value-heavy ranges here like QQ+/AK, and even vs wider it rarely reaches the ~37% equity needed to call). Calling also leaves an SPR near zero out of position, effectively committing without the equity. Shoving doesn’t create fold equity because Elon only has to add a tiny amount more to call, so we’d get snapped (often multiway vs Claude) and burn our tournament stack. Best play is to cut losses and preserve the 29bb stack.

Facing a 4-bet from Elon after my 3-bet. KJs is a decent hand but typically crushed against a 4-bet range (AA, KK, QQ, JJ, AK, sometimes AQs). I'm getting about 31% pot odds needing 1500 to win 4850, but playing OOP against a strong range with a dominated hand is -EV. Elon has shown aggression before but a 4-bet is typically genuine strength. With 20k stack, I don't need to take marginal spots. Disciplined fold.
K3o is a weak hand from UTG position. Even 5-handed, this is too weak to open - I'd need much stronger hands like broadway cards, pairs, or suited aces to open from early position. Easy fold.

I am in UTG+1 with Ace-Six offsuit. While it is an Ace, my position is early and the hand is relatively weak for a full table. However, since the table has been somewhat active and I have a healthy stack, I will enter with a standard opening raise to 250 to represent strength and potentially take the blinds or see a flop in position against some players.

9-7 suited is a strong speculative hand on the button with position on all remaining players. Flash's raise from early position (UTG+1) after Claude folded suggests a wide but not premium range based on history of frequent opens to 250. Calling gets good pot odds (calling 250 into ~650 pot post-call, plus blinds to act), preserves stack, and maximizes implied odds with suited connector potential for straights/flushes/draws postflop. Raising risks bloating pot OOP vs blinds or isolation without fold equity justification (not a polarized 3-bet spot). Folding too weak in position. BB FiveTwo short at 2800 may get involved, but position dominates. Aim to realize equity multiway or steal postflop.

I am in the Small Blind holding Queen-Deuce offsuit (Qd 2h), which is a very weak hand. The action includes a raise from Flash in early position and a call from Elon on the Button. Playing this hand out of position against two opponents is unprofitable despite the pot odds. The risk of domination and the difficulty of realizing equity from the worst position makes folding the only viable option.

We’re getting a very good price (150 more to win a pot of 650, and we close the action preflop) in a multiway pot. 6♠3♠ has decent realizable equity with suited/connected potential, and raising would bloat the pot with a weak hand while we’re the effective stack versus Flash. Take the pot odds and see a flop, then play fit-or-fold / draw-heavy lines postflop.

Flop is extremely dynamic (T♣9♣J♠) and multiway vs the preflop raiser (Flash) plus Elon. With 6♠3♠ we have essentially no made hand and no real draw (only weak backdoor spades/runner-runner), so leading would just burn chips and get raised/called by better ranges. Best is to check and proceed fit-or-fold versus pressure, occasionally continuing only versus very small bets with strong backdoor/implied odds (not really present here).

The flop of Tc-9c-Js is extremely coordinated and hits the calling ranges of both the Button (Elon) and the Big Blind (FiveTwo) very hard. I currently have Ace-high with no draws or backdoors. Given the texture of the board and the fact that I am facing two opponents, a continuation bet is unlikely to succeed and could be costly. I will check and look to fold if there is any betting action, as my equity in this pot is very low.

Middle pair (9s) with a suited diamond 7 kicker on a highly coordinated flop (Tc9cJs): open-ended straight draws (78, Q8), flush draws (any two clubs), wheel draws possible. Flash's preflop raise from UTG+1 and check suggests possible AK/AQ overpairs, missed draws, or traps, but history shows aggression; FiveTwo limp-call with short stack (2650) likely wide/speculative. Pot 800, position on button allows betting for thin value from worse (87o, flush draws), protection against draws realizing equity, and fold equity vs air/missed hands. Checking risks free cards to strong draws/overcards. Bet 2/3 pot (550) balances value/protection/steal while preserving stack (10875 deep), targets FiveTwo's short stack for fold/push decisions. Avoids overbetting into potential monsters given board texture.

Board (T♣9♣J♠) is very dynamic and hits both Elon’s and Flash’s ranges hard (broadways, strong draws, sets). With 6♠3♠ we have no made hand, no straight draw, and only a very thin runner-runner spade backdoor (plus weak pairs that are rarely good). Even though the price is ~29% (550 to win 1900), our realizable equity is far below that, and calling also reopens the action with Flash able to raise. Best play is to conserve chips and fold.

I have Ace-high on a very coordinated board (Tc-9c-Js) that hits the calling ranges of both opponents significantly. Elon, betting from the button into two players, likely has at least a piece of this board or a strong draw. My hand (As-6h) has no draws and effectively zero equity on this texture. Folding is the only correct play here.

I am in the UTG position with 6-4 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Given the early position and the low value of the hand, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

A2o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with poor playability and weak kicker. From UTG+1 (early/mid position) in a 5-handed game, facing blinds and potential callers behind including short-stacked FiveTwo and deep Claude. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor for speculative hand, better to fold and wait for stronger holdings to maximize stack long-term. History shows aggression works with premium hands, not trash.

Current hand is 98o on the Button. The pot contains 150 chips (SB 50 + BB 100) with no apparent antes. To steal the blinds, a standard raise to 200-250 would require a high fold equity (around 60%). The Big Blind (Claude) has a massive chip lead (nearly 200BB) and has shown tendencies to defend or play aggressively, while the Small Blind (FiveTwo) has a reshove stack size of 26BB. Given the lack of antes to sweeten the pot and the likelihood of resistance from the big stack, opening with a marginal off-suit connector is likely slightly negative EV. Folding is the prudent play to preserve the 64BB stack for better opportunities.

Folded to us in the SB, this is a prime steal spot. Ah5s has good playability heads-up, blocks strong Ax continues from BB, and can make nut flushes/strong wheel draws. With ~26bb, I want to apply pressure without bloating the pot too much out of position, so I’ll use a smaller SB open size that risks less while still generating folds and building a pot when called.
I have 2s7h, one of the worst hands in poker. FiveTwo raised to 250 from SB. Even getting decent pot odds (150 to win 350), 72o has very little playability and I'll be out of position. Easy fold.

K3o (King-Three offsuit) is a marginal hand at best, and extremely weak from UTG position in a 5-handed game. It has poor playability postflop, dominated by better kings, and low equity against ranges. With deep stacks (my 11675, others deep except FiveTwo short), folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize long-term EV over remaining 60 hands. No steals viable UTG.

I am in the Cutoff position (UTG+1 in 5-handed) with 9c 8c, a suited connector. With 64 BBs, this is a standard spot to open-raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. The Button is short-stacked (27.5 BB) and likely to play push-or-fold, while the blinds are deep. A raise to 2.5x (250) balances my range and gives me a good price on a steal attempt with a hand that plays well post-flop if called.

Pro’s UTG+1 open is likely fairly tight, and ATo (offsuit) can be dominated if we bloat the pot with a 3-bet and then face a 4-bet jam. With 27.5bb, a 3-bet to ~800 would create a low SPR and effectively commit us, which isn’t ideal versus a strong early-position range. On the button we get maximum realization: we can take flops in position, control pot size, and still have strong top-pair potential plus an ace-blocker for postflop bluffing on favorable runouts. Calling keeps our stack flexible and avoids turning a decent-but-marginal spot into a high-variance preflop commitment.

Q4s in SB facing a raise and a call. Pot is 650, I need 200 to call - getting about 3.25:1 pot odds. Q4s is marginal but has suited potential. Being out of position is a concern, but the pot odds and multiway nature make this a reasonable call to see a flop. If I miss, I can easily fold.

I am in the Big Blind with 4h 6c. The action reached me after a raise from Pro (UTG+1) to 250 and calls from FiveTwo (Button) and Claude (Small Blind). While I am getting excellent pot odds (150 to call into a pot of 850, approximately 5.6:1), the hand 4h 6c is extremely weak and plays poorly multi-way out of position. I risk significant reverse implied odds if I hit a marginal piece of the board. Given the strength of an UTG+1 raise and two callers, it is more disciplined to fold this garbage hand and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I flopped a queen-high flush draw with Qs4s on Ks6sJd. I have 9 outs to the second-nut flush. Being first to act OOP with a drawing hand, checking is better than leading - I can call if someone bets, or take a free card if it checks through. No need to bloat the pot when I'm drawing.

I raised pre-flop with 9c8c and got two callers. The flop comes Ks 6s Jd, which is a very wet board that connects well with the opponents' calling ranges (Kx, Jx, flush draws, straight draws like QT/T9). I have 9-high with no flush draw (I have clubs, board has spades) and no immediate straight draw (only backdoor possibilities). Betting into two opponents on this texture with zero equity is highly negative EV, especially with the Button being short-stacked and likely to commit with any piece. I will check and fold to any aggression.

Both blinds/Pro checking gives us a good opportunity to take the betting lead in position. On K♠ J♦ 6♠ our A♠T♥ has a gutshot to the Q plus strong backdoor potential (nut spade backdoor) and good blockers (A blocks a lot of continues like AK/AJ and the nut-flush component). A small stab pressures Claude’s wide limp/call range and Pro’s check range (often pocket pairs/weak Kx/Qx), denies equity, and sets up profitable turn barrels on Q/A/T/spades without committing our 25bb stack in a marginal spot.

I have a queen-high flush draw with 9 outs. FiveTwo bet 300 into 850, pot is now 1150. I need to call 300 getting about 3.8:1 pot odds. With roughly 19% equity to hit the flush (9 outs), I'm not quite getting direct odds but implied odds make this an easy call - if I hit, I can likely extract significant value. Raising as a semi-bluff is possible but risky OOP with Pro still in the hand.

I opened pre-flop with 9c8c and checked the flop Ks 6s Jd as it completely missed my hand (no pair, no flush draw, only very weak backdoor straight potential). FiveTwo bet 300 and Claude called. With two opponents showing interest and one calling a bet, I have zero equity and am drawing nearly dead against ranges that likely include Kings, Jacks, or flush draws. Despite the attractive pot odds offered by the small bet and call, continuing with 9-high is losing strategy. I must fold.

I hit my queen-high flush on the turn with Qs4s. The board is Ks6sJd8s - I have the second-nut flush (only As beats me). FiveTwo has about 2200 behind and was the aggressor on the flop. Checking to let them continue betting is optimal here - if they have Kx, Jx, or a lower flush they'll likely bet again. Then I can check-raise for value. If they check, I'll bet river. No need to lead out and potentially scare them off when I have such a strong hand.

Turn brings the 8♠ giving us a very strong combo draw: nut flush draw (A♠) plus a gutshot to Q for Broadway (12 clean outs). However, after Claude check-calls the flop in a 3-way pot and we’re now heads-up, his range is heavily weighted to made hands (Kx/Jx/6x/88/66, plus some spade draws) that won’t fold often enough to a turn shove. With our short stack relative to the pot, a bet effectively commits us, and if we get called we’re usually relying on ~24–26% river equity, which isn’t great without meaningful fold equity.

Checking back maximizes realization: we take the free river to hit spades/Q, and when we improve we can comfortably jam for value into a pot that’s already big. If we miss and face a bet, we can make a disciplined fold without torching chips on a low-fold-equity semi-bluff.

I have the queen-high flush (Ks-Qs-8s-6s-4s). Only As beats me. FiveTwo checked the turn after betting flop, and now checks to me on the river. The board has Ks6sJd8sTd - lots of straights possible (Q9, 97), plus Kx/Jx hands. FiveTwo has 2200 behind with 1450 pot. I should bet for value - around 800-900 to get called by straights, two pair, trips, or lower flushes while still looking like a reasonable value bet.

We’re getting a good price: calling 850 to win 3150 (≈27% needed). Our line (small flop stab, then check back turn) caps us and incentivizes Claude to probe river with a fairly wide range, including missed one-spade floats that picked up a flush draw on the 8♠ turn and bricked, plus some thin/blocker value with worse one-pair hands (8x/6x). 

While Claude certainly has plenty of value (Kx/Jx, two-pair like K8/J8/KT, straights like Q9, and some made flushes), holding the A♠ is important: it reduces the density of his strongest flush value combos, and we don’t block his natural bluffs. Given the sizing and required equity, folding would be too tight, and raising would just isolate ourselves versus his strong hands. Best play is to bluff-catch.

Call.

I am in UTG position with [2h, 3d], which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With a stack of roughly 61 big blinds, there is absolutely no reason to play this hand from early position. Opening here would be throwing chips away. The only correct play is to fold immediately.

We’re very short (13.5bb) but hold a top-tier premium (QQ) in an unopened pot (Pro folded). Limping invites multiway, gives up initiative, and makes it easier for the blinds to realize equity against us. I want to build a pot for value while keeping ranges wide enough that worse hands (smaller pairs, broadways, suited aces) can continue. I’ll open-raise and be fully prepared to call off versus any 3-bet/shove given stack depth and hand strength.

I have pocket 5s on the button - a solid hand. FiveTwo raised to 300 from UTG+1 but is very short-stacked with only 1050 total (750 behind). I should 3-bet to isolate them. A raise to 800 puts pressure on them - they'll either fold or shove their remaining stack. With 55, I'm ahead of their shoving range often enough, and I have position. If the blinds come along, I can still flop a set.

I am holding Qh 2d in the Small Blind, which is a very weak hand. The action in front of me includes an open-raise from FiveTwo (who is extremely short-stacked) and a 3-bet from Claude on the button. There is no strategic reason to commit chips to this pot out of position with a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

98o is a marginal speculative hand offsuit. Facing a 3-bet from Claude on the button after FiveTwo's early position open to 3bb. Claude's button 3-bet range is likely wide but still strong enough postflop. FiveTwo is short-stacked (1050 total, 300 already in, 750 left) and may shove or call, making multiway pots likely messy. Pot odds are decent (1250 to win for 700 call), but 98o plays poorly in 3-bet pots with low SPR, no strong equity vs calling ranges. Folding preserves stack for better spots in late tournament phase (hand 91/150). Deep effective stacks but no need for heroics here.

We’re only 10.5bb effective with QQ facing a 3-bet to 800. Calling leaves just 250 behind, creates an awkward SPR, and lets villain realize equity cheaply with overcards/AX while we’re almost always committed anyway. The best play is to jam now: we get maximum value versus Claude’s likely 3-bet/call range (JJ+, AK, sometimes TT/AQ), deny equity, and occasionally pick up the pot immediately if he’s light. Folding QQ here at this stack depth is far too tight.

FiveTwo 4-bet to 1300 but only has 50 chips behind - they're essentially all-in. I need to call 500 more into a pot of 2250. Getting 4.5:1 odds with pocket 5s against a short stack's range is an easy call. Even if they have overcards, I'm roughly a coin flip, and against smaller pairs I'm dominating.

We’re effectively all-in already (50 chips left into a 2,750 pot), so there’s no meaningful decision tree left. On K-T-3 with QQ we’re sometimes behind a Kx/TT, but we still have equity versus hands like JJ/99/AQ and the small jam denies any free turn card. Betting also captures the rare fold from total air that took a preflop stab and now misses. Checking gains nothing because any future bet commits us anyway.
FiveTwo bet their last 50 chips. Pot is 2800, I need to call 50 - that's 56:1 odds. This is an automatic call regardless of my hand. With pocket 5s I could still improve to a set on turn/river.

Q2 offsuit is well below a profitable UTG opening range even 5-handed, especially with my 28.5bb stack and aggressive opponents behind who have shown willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure. Limping creates a dominated, low-realization spot out of position with poor playability. Best EV is to preserve chips and wait for stronger hands/positions.
J3o is a weak hand from UTG+1. Poor connectivity and bad kicker. Standard fold from early position.

I am on the button with 8s 6h. The action has folded to me. While this is a potential stealing position, 86 offsuit is a weak hand. My stack is very healthy (191 BB), and there is no immediate pressure to force a play with a sub-optimal hand. Elon in the Small Blind has shown a willingness to defend or 3-bet in previous hands, and Pro in the Big Blind has a medium stack that might defend. I will choose the conservative route and fold.

Preflop in SB with Ts4s after folds to us. Pot 150, need to add 50 to call (total bet 100). Ts4s is a suited hand with flush and straight potential (gutshots, wheel draws). Completing SB is standard with playable suited hands due to excellent pot odds (pot will be 200 for 50 investment). OOP vs BB who defends wide, but hand has good implied odds if we hit. Raising risks getting 3-bet OOP with marginal hand; completing keeps pot small and realizes equity. Folding dead SB money is -EV long-term in tournament. Goal to maximize stack favors playing speculative hands cheaply.

I am in the Big Blind with [4h Tc], a very weak hand (Ten-Four offsuit). The action folded to the Small Blind (Elon), who limped in by calling the extra 50 chips. Since there is no raise to call, I have the option to check or raise. Given the poor quality of my hand, I have no interest in building a larger pot or risking chips on a bluff pre-flop. Although I will have position on Elon post-flop, T4o plays poorly. The most prudent play is to take the free flop and see if I improve, preserving my stack of 60.5 BBs.

Flop As Jh 4c gives me bottom pair (4s pairing 4c) plus the nut spade flush draw (9 outs, ~35% equity standalone, plus pair value and backdoor straight potential with T). Board is dryish with Ace high, possible Ax, Jx, draws like KQ, spade draws. I act first OOP vs BB who checked preflop (weak range: low pairs, suited connectors, Ax that didn't raise). Checking risks giving free card to draws or check-raise from strong Ax, but betting builds pot with massive equity, gets value from worse pairs/draws (e.g., Jx, 55-33, flush draws), protection, and denies equity. Pot 200, BB 60BB effective. Bet 3/4 pot (150) balances value/thin value while keeping stack committed reasonable (~1.5% stack). Avoids overbetting OOP early street. Tournament stage favors realizing equity aggressively with premium draw+pair.

I hold bottom pair (4s) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-Jack-Four board. The Small Blind limped pre-flop and has now led out for 150 into a pot of 200 (75% pot). This large lead on an Ace-high texture usually indicates strength (Ax or Jx) that dominates my bottom pair. I am drawing very thin against any Ace or Jack. While I have position, calling with bottom pair no kicker on this board is likely a losing play in the long run. I will fold to preserve my stack.
A4s in UTG is a playable hand - suited ace with flush potential and some showdown value. Standard open raise to 250.

I am holding pocket Jacks, which is a premium starting hand. Claude (UTG) has opened for 250. Claude is a large stack and relatively active, so his UTG range may be slightly wider than a traditional tight range, but it still commands respect. I want to 3-bet here for value and to isolate Claude, preventing the players behind me (especially the short stack FiveTwo) from seeing a cheap flop. A standard 3-bet to 750 (3x the open) is appropriate to define the hand and take control of the pot.

My hand is 2h8c, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing a raise from UTG (Claude) and a 3-bet from UTG+1 (Flash), both early positions with strong likely ranges. I'm on the button but stacks are deep, and calling 750 into a 1150 pot commits too much equity with zero showdown value or playability. Even squeezing would require a premium hand here. Best to fold and wait for better spots to build the stack, especially with 58 hands remaining.

I am holding King-Seven offsuit in the Small Blind. Facing a raise from UTG and a 3-bet from UTG+1, my hand is significantly dominated by both opponents' ranges. Cold calling a 3-bet out of position with such a weak hand is not profitable. The best and only standard play here is to fold.

We’re in the BB with 8♠4♣ facing an UTG open and an UTG+1 3-bet. Their ranges are strong and we’ll be out of position with a short, awkward stack (27.5bb). The direct price (650 to win 1150) still requires too much equity versus two strong ranges, and calling creates tough postflop spots with a dominated, low-playability hand. A jam also has poor fold equity into a 3-bet pot and gets called by hands that crush us. Best is to preserve the stack and wait for a better spot.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with As4s. Getting ~2.3:1 pot odds (500 to win 1150). A4s has nut flush potential which gives good implied odds if we hit. We're OOP which is suboptimal, but the suited ace playability justifies a call rather than folding. Not strong enough to 4-bet.

Missed the flop completely - no spades on board, just ace high. Flash 3-bet preflop so likely has strong range (big pairs, AK, AQ). This Qc7c8h board favors their range. I have no pair, no draw. Best to check and likely fold to aggression.

I am holding pocket Jacks in a 3-bet pot. The flop came Queen-high, which is an overcard to my pair. Claude, who called my 3-bet out of position, has checked to me. While I have an overpair to the 7 and 8, the Queen is very much in Claude's calling range (AQ, KQ). By checking back, I control the size of the pot with a medium-strength hand and avoid a potential check-raise. This also allows me to see how Claude reacts on the turn and prevents me from over-committing against a better hand while still maintaining showdown value.

Turn brought Tc completing a three-flush in clubs. I have As4s - no clubs, no pair, no draw. Flash checked the flop which could indicate weakness (missed AK) or slowplay. With ace-high only and no equity against Flash's 3-bet range, I should check. If Flash bets, I'll fold - can't profitably continue with no draw and no made hand on this wet board.

The turn card is the Ten of Clubs (Tc), which is a very dynamic card. It completes some straight possibilities (9x) and brings a third club to the board. I hold the Jack of Clubs (Jc), giving me a flush draw and a straight draw (any 9). Claude has checked for the second time. While my pocket Jacks are still a decent hand, the board is becoming increasingly dangerous with a Queen, a Ten, and three clubs. If Claude is slow-playing a Queen or a made flush, a bet here doesn't accomplish much other than building a pot I might lose. By checking back again, I maintain pot control, realize my equity for free, and keep his range wide. If he has a marginal hand like 8x or 99, I'm ahead, and if he's drawing, I'm not giving him a chance to check-raise me off my equity.

River 2s completes a blank. Board: Qc7c8hTc2s. Flash 3-bet pre but checked both flop and turn, showing significant weakness - likely AK/AJ that missed. I have ace-high which actually has showdown value against their missed broadway hands. Bluffing here is unnecessary since: (1) Flash might call with any pair or club, (2) my ace-high could be best against their AK/AJ that whiffed. Check and see if ace-high wins.

I hold pocket Jacks on a board of Qc-7c-8h-Tc-2s. Pre-flop, I 3-bet Claude's UTG open and he called. Post-flop, the action has been checked through on every street. Claude's triple-check suggests he likely has a marginal hand, missed overcards (like AK or AJ), or a medium pair (like 99). While I have the Jc (blocking some flushes and straights), the Queen is an overcard and the board is somewhat coordinated. My Jacks have significant showdown value against his range of missed draws and smaller pairs. Betting now is unlikely to get called by worse hands, and it risks a check-raise from a slow-played Queen or a trapped hand. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown without further risk.

I am in the UTG position with 6s 3c, which is an extremely weak hand. There is no strategic justification for entering the pot with such poor hole cards, especially from early position. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

A3o is a marginal ace offsuit hand with poor postflop playability, especially from early position (UTG+1) where I'll be OOP against multiple players. It's dominated by better aces and has low equity against typical calling ranges. With 11675 stack (~117BB deep), FiveTwo short at 2700 (~27BB in SB), and others deep, no need to limp or raise speculative hands preflop. Folding preserves stack for premium spots in late tournament (hand 94/150). Standard tight play maximizes EV.

I am on the Button with J4o, which is a very weak hand. Action has folded to me. My stack is 60BB (6000), which is healthy. Although being on the Button allows for a wide opening range to steal the blinds, J4o is too weak and disconnected to be profitable, especially with a large stack in the Big Blind who can defend or apply pressure. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Folds to me in the SB. 9♠2♥ is near-bottom of range and plays very poorly out of position. Claude has a big stack and has shown willingness to pressure limps/enter pots, so completing risks getting raised off my equity or playing a tough postflop spot. With 27bb I don’t need to defend this 50 chips; I’ll conserve stack for better steal spots and hands.

Pocket 99 is a premium hand UTG in 5-handed play, strong enough to open raise for value and to build the pot. The table has been aggressive based on history, with frequent preflop raises and multiway pots, but 99 plays well postflop and wants initiative. Standard open size around 3x BB (300 total) to isolate while keeping it affordable given short stacks like FiveTwo (27BB). Folding is too tight, limping too passive and invites multiway pots where 99 is vulnerable. Raising builds stack by winning blinds often or getting value from worse.

I hold T3o in UTG+1, facing a raise from UTG. This is a very weak hand that is dominated by almost all of the UTG opening range. With players left to act behind me and no potential for this hand to play well post-flop, the correct and trivial decision is to fold.

Button with 88 versus a UTG 3x open and only ~27bb effective for us, I don’t want to flat and invite the deep blinds to squeeze or force us into tough multiway spots. 88 is strong enough to play for stacks at this depth, and a 3-bet creates fold equity while also setting up a low SPR pot in position when called. If UTG responds with a jam, we’re generally priced in versus a reasonable reshove range given the dead money.

A6o facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo after Elon's open. FiveTwo is short-stacked (1800 total, 900 already committed). A6o plays poorly out of position in a 3-bet pot, and Flash could still squeeze behind. Even though FiveTwo might be 3-betting light due to stack pressure, my hand isn't strong enough to cold call or 4-bet here. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the Big Blind with 2s 3h, which is the weakest possible hand in poker. Elon raised to 300 and FiveTwo 3-bet to 900. FiveTwo has a very short stack remaining (only 900 left behind). Regardless of the pot odds, my hand is unplayable against two opponents who are showing significant strength, especially with a 3-bet involved. Folding is the only viable option.

Pocket 99 is a very strong hand UTG and too good to fold to a button 3-bet from a short-stacked aggressive player like FiveTwo (18BB remaining). His 3-bet range is likely wide and polarized: strong hands like JJ+, AKo/AQs and bluffs/light Ax suited connectors. By 4-betting to 2700 (sizing to put him all-in for his 1800 remaining), we apply maximum pressure, isolate him, avoid difficult OOP postflop spots, and get value from his worse hands while making his bluffs fold. We have ~40-50% equity vs his calling range, plus fold equity. Calling risks multiway if blinds wake up (unlikely but possible) and postflop play OOP vs shove-happy shorty; folding is too tight late in tournament when building stack. Deep stack risk (adding ~2400) is low EV compared to gaining his 2700 total.

We’re effectively all-in for our last 1,800 to call the 2,700 4-bet (we’ve already put in 900). With the pot at 3,750, calling 1,800 wins 5,550 total, so we need only ~32% equity. Pocket 88 has that versus a reasonable UTG 4-bet range that includes big aces (AK/AQ) and some pairs; even against fairly tight ranges we’re often close and the dead money makes the call profitable. Since folding gives up too much equity and we can’t maneuver postflop, we take the priced-in stack-off.

I am in UTG position with [7h 4s], which is a very weak unsuited hand (off-suit one-gapper). With 60 big blinds, I have plenty of chips and no reason to get involved with such poor holdings from early position. There are 4 players left to act behind me, increasing the likelihood someone has a better hand. The only standard play here is to fold.

5-handed UTG just folded so we’re effectively in the cutoff, but 7c5h offsuit is still a low-equity, dominated hand that plays poorly vs 3-bets and multiway pots. With aggressive opponents behind (Claude/Flash/Elon have shown frequent raising and 3-betting), opening or limping invites pressure and puts us in tough spots with weak realizable equity. Preserving 55bb and waiting for better steal candidates is higher EV here.

On the button with Q6o after two folds. This is a standard steal spot - Q6o is playable from the button position. I have a healthy stack and only the blinds to get through. Opening to 2.5x is standard.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. The Button (Claude) has opened with a 2.5x raise. Ten-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position, even against a standard button opening range. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, there is no reason to defend with such a sub-optimal hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

9h3s is a trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Defending BB with this against a button raise would be a mistake as it has low equity, plays terribly out of position post-flop, and risks chips unnecessarily. Claude's button raise is likely wide, but folding weak hands preserves my 88BB stack for premium spots in this late tournament stage (hand 96/150). Standard fold.

5-handed UTG, J5o is too weak to open profitably from first position and plays poorly postflop (dominated when we make a jack, low straight potential, no suit). With multiple aggressive stacks behind who have shown a willingness to 3-bet, opening or limping risks getting squeezed off equity or forced into tough spots. Best EV is to preserve the 55bb stack and wait for stronger opens/steal spots from later position.

Q9o in UTG+1 is a marginal hand at best. From early position, this is typically a fold - it's easily dominated by better queens and doesn't play well multiway. With 4 players left to act including the button, I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 9d 5c, which is a very weak starting hand. While I have position on the blinds, 9-5 offsuit has poor playability and is not a strong candidate for a steal, especially since the Big Blind (Pro) has a relatively short stack and might defend or shove over a raise. Given my comfortable stack size, there is no need to play this hand.

Hand 97/150, SB with 4s7d, offsuit low cards with poor playability. Everyone folded pre-flop (FiveTwo UTG, Claude UTG+1, Flash Button), so heads-up vs BB Pro (59bb stack). Pot 150, to call 50. 47o has very low equity (~30-35% vs random BB range) and bad post-flop potential. Standard play is to fold from SB; no need to speculate with trash this late in tournament when better spots exist to build stack. Preserve 88bb stack.
J4 suited from UTG is too weak to open. Even though it's suited, the hand lacks connectivity and strength for an early position open. Standard UTG range should be tighter - folding here.

I am in early-to-mid position with A2o. While it is an Ace, it's a very weak kicker and generally plays poorly against multiple opponents. However, with the current table dynamics and my stack size, I have the option to either fold or open. Given Claude folded, I am essentially under the gun. A2o is generally a fold in this position in a full ring game, but we are playing 5-handed. In a 5-handed game, standard opening ranges are wider, but A2o remains on the cusp of being a marginal open or a fold. Looking at the tournament status (hand 98 of 150), I have a healthy stack. I will fold this to maintain my stack for better spots where I have more post-flop equity or positional advantage.

Preflop on the button with 4s6d, a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges. Blinds are posted (pot 150, to call 100), early position players folded, but 46o is below standard button stealing range given the multiway potential and my deep effective stack. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in the tournament, avoiding speculative hands with dominated equity.

I am in the Small Blind with 5c 3d, a very weak unconnected offsuit hand. The action has folded to me. My opponent in the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has a similar stack size. While I am getting 3:1 odds to complete the blind, playing 5-3 offsuit out of position is generally a losing proposition due to poor playability and reverse implied odds. Attempting to steal with a raise is also risky with such low equity if called. Preserving my 60BB stack for better spots is the prudent play here.

I am UTG with 8-high offsuit (4d 8h), which is one of the weakest possible hands. Given my position and the lack of any connectivity or high-card value, there is no reason to enter the pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

2d8s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity. In UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game, this is an automatic fold. No stealing incentive with pot only 150 and to-call 100, as multiple players behind including deep-stacked Claude in BB. Preserve stack for better spots to maximize long-term EV.

I hold Ace-King suited (Ad Kd) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand. Action has folded to me. With 60 big blinds, my standard play is to open-raise for value to build the pot and capitalize on my position. A raise to 250 (2.5x big blind) is consistent with the standard opening size in this tournament and puts pressure on the blinds while extracting value from worse hands that call.

Button open to 2.5x is likely fairly wide 5-handed, and Pro has shown willingness to fold to 3-bets. From the small blind, flat-calling invites the big blind in and leaves us playing out of position without initiative. 9c8c is a good semi-bluff 3-bet candidate (playable when called, can make strong draws), and taking the betting lead lets us win preflop or c-bet favorable boards.

Sizing up to 1000 pressures the opener and reduces BB’s immediate price to come along while still keeping our stack flexible to fold vs a 4-bet.
J5o is a weak hand facing a 3-bet from FiveTwo. I'd be calling 900 into a 1350 pot out of position with a hand that doesn't play well postflop. Clear fold.

I hold Ace-King suited (Ad Kd) on the Button, a premium hand. After my standard open to 250, the Small Blind (FiveTwo) 3-bet to 1000. FiveTwo started the hand with 4600 chips (46 big blinds), meaning they have 3600 behind. The 3-bet size is large (4x), creating a pot of 1350. If I call, the pot will be 2100 with 3600 effective stacks behind, resulting in an awkward SPR of roughly 1.7. While calling is a valid option to keep the opponent's range wide and use position, the low SPR makes post-flop play difficult if I miss. With AKs, I benefit significantly from seeing all five cards to realize my equity against pocket pairs (TT-QQ) that likely make up a large part of his range. Shoving (4-bet jamming) puts maximum pressure on the opponent, generates fold equity against hands like AQ/AJ or smaller pairs, and ensures I realize my equity in a high-variance but positive EV spot. Given the effective stack depth of ~46BB, getting it all-in pre-flop with AKs is the standard optimal play.

Pro’s 4-bet to 5750 over our SB 3-bet is effectively committing vs our stack and is heavily weighted to value (big pairs/AK, maybe some AQ). We’re being asked to call off for our tournament life with 9c8c. Even with the all-in being slightly short (uncalled chips returned), we still need ~41% equity, and 98s is typically only ~30–36% versus a realistic value-heavy 4-bet range. Given the large sizing and the risk of busting 5-handed, this is a clear fold and we preserve ~35bb to find better spots.

4c6s is a trash hand offsuit with poor connectivity and low cards. UTG position requires a tight range; this hand has no business entering the pot voluntarily. Multiway pots would be disastrous post-flop, and raising is out of the question. Folding preserves my healthy 88BB stack for premium spots late in the tournament (hand 100/150). Opponents have shown loose tendencies historically, but doesn't change fundamentals here.

I am in the Cutoff (CO) position with Ace-Four suited (Ah 4h) after the UTG player folded. With 5 players remaining, A4s is a standard opening hand from the Cutoff due to its blocker value and playability (flush and straight potential). My stack is 71 big blinds, which allows for a standard open. The players in the blinds are deep-stacked, which increases the risk of a 3-bet, but A4s is a good candidate to open-fold if faced with significant aggression, or play in position if called. I will open to 2.5x the big blind.

5-handed, Pro’s UTG+1 open is effectively a middle-position range and should be wider than full-ring UTG. On the button, Kh5h is too weak to flat profitably very often (domination when we make a king, plus high squeeze risk from the deep-stacked blinds), but it works well as a selective 3-bet bluff: we have position, a king blocker to reduce KK/AK/KQ, and decent playability when called. I’ll apply pressure with a standard sizing and comfortably fold to a 4-bet.

Holding 2s4s in the SB facing a raise to 250 from Pro and a 3-bet to 850 from FiveTwo. This is an extremely weak hand - suited but with terrible high card value and connectivity. Calling 800 out of position against a 3-bet with this hand would be burning chips. Easy fold.

I am holding Ace-Five offsuit in the Big Blind. The action began with an early position raise from Pro to 250, followed by a 3-bet to 850 from FiveTwo on the button. Ace-Five offsuit is a weak hand to play in a 3-bet pot out of position, especially against two opponents who are showing significant strength from early and late positions respectively. My hand is likely dominated by better Aces (AJ-AK) or facing pocket pairs. Given my large stack size of 19,750, there is no strategic reason to risk 750 more chips in a marginal spot where I will be playing out of position post-flop with a weak kicker. Folding is the most disciplined and correct play here.

I opened with A4s from the Cutoff/UTG+1 and faced a 3-bet from the Button (FiveTwo). The opponent started the hand with ~37.5 BB and has put in ~22% of their stack, creating a low SPR (~1.5) scenario if I call. A4s is a marginal hand to play out of position against a 3-bet, as it is frequently dominated by the opponent's value range (Ax) and lacks the implied odds to chase flushes/straights at this stack depth. Although I successfully 4-bet shoved against this opponent in the previous hand, repeating this aggression immediately is risky as they may be adjusting or waking up with a strong hand. Folding is the disciplined, standard play here.

I am in UTG position with 3h 6h, a very weak suited gapped hand. With 68.5 BB, I have plenty of chips and no reason to get involved with such marginal holdings from early position. My opening range from UTG should be much stronger (e.g., pairs 55+, suited connectors 76s+, broadways). Folding is the only standard and profitable play here.

5-handed with Pro folding UTG, KQo is comfortably in our UTG+1 opening range. Limping (calling 100) invites multiway play from the deep-stacked button/SB and puts us in tougher postflop spots with a hand that prefers initiative. A standard open around 2.5x pressures the blinds, keeps our range strong, and lets us realize equity with position on the blinds if called while not bloating the pot unnecessarily versus the big stacks.

T4 suited is a weak hand against an UTG+1 open. While I have position on the button, this hand doesn't have enough equity or playability to justify calling 250 against a UTG+1 raise. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am holding 2s 5c in the Small Blind. FiveTwo has raised from early position to 250. This is an extremely weak starting hand and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if I call. Given the strength of the hand and my position, the optimal play is to fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Preflop in BB facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG+1 after two folds. My hand J5s is suited but gapped and jack-high, marginal at best against an early position opening range which likely includes strong pairs, broadways, and suited aces/broadways. Pot odds are decent (150 to call into 400, ~2.7:1), but out of position postflop reduces playability significantly. FiveTwo has been aggressive historically but shorter stack (4750). Folding preserves stack for better spots with 50 hands left in tournament; defending wide OOP with this hand risks tough postflop decisions and poor equity realization.

5-handed (effectively HJ), J8o is just inside a reasonable short-handed opening range, and limping UTG is likely to get punished by aggressive players behind (Flash/Claude/Pro). I’ll take the initiative and try to win the blinds/antes-equivalent now; if I face a sizable 3-bet from the big stacks, I can comfortably fold this dominated hand and preserve my 50bb stack.
Q5o is a weak hand facing a raise from UTG. Not worth defending - easy fold.

I am on the button with 2s-6c, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised from UTG. Given my hand strength and the fact that there's a raise in front of me, there is no reason to enter this pot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

K5o is a very weak hand, offsuit with poor kicker and no playability. Facing an UTG raise from FiveTwo (49bb stack), who likely has a strong range. SB defending range vs UTG open should be tight: pairs 66+, AJo+, suited aces/broadways, suited connectors. K5o doesn't make the cut. Pot odds are mediocre (~3:1 effective after rake/position), but hand is too weak, dominated by better kings, and position is poor OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots with 87bb deep.

I am holding Ah 4s (Ace-Four offsuit) in the Big Blind facing a standard 2.5x raise from FiveTwo in UTG. While the price to call is attractive (getting about 3.6 to 1 pot odds), A4o plays poorly out of position against a strong UTG opening range. It suffers from reverse implied odds, often dominated by stronger Aces. My previous play history (Hand 7 and Hand 68) shows me folding this exact hand configuration against early position raises, adhering to a disciplined strategy to preserve my stack and avoid marginal spots OOP.
4d3d from UTG is too weak to open. This is a bottom-tier suited connector that doesn't play well from early position. Easy fold.

I am in UTG+1 with Ten of Diamonds and Six of Spades. This is a weak starting hand with very little potential for straights or flushes, and the high card value is mediocre. In a five-handed game, playing T6o from early position is generally unprofitable. I have a healthy stack and can afford to wait for a better hand.

Folded to me on the button with pocket fours (44), a solid hand for multiway or heads-up pots in position against blinds. Position is excellent, and 44 has good equity against typical blind calling ranges (overplays worse pairs/hands, dominates suited connectors). Raising builds the pot for value, applies pressure, and denies equity to speculative hands. Standard sizing is 3x the BB (300) given 50/100 blinds and no prior action. My stack (87BB) supports this without overcommitting. Folding too tight, limping passive and exploitable.

The player is in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight offsuit (T8o). The Button (Elon) has raised to 300 (3bb). T8o is a weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. While the raw equity against a wide button range might be borderline close to the pot odds, the realizability of that equity is low due to positional disadvantage. Additionally, the Big Blind is left to act and could squeeze. Investing 2.5bb to play a weak hand out of position is a losing play in the long run. The correct strategic decision is to fold.

Facing a 3bb button open, we’re out of position with 8♠5♦ offsuit (poor playability, dominated when we make a pair, and limited ability to realize equity). While the pot odds are tempting, this hand performs much better suited and we don’t have good 3-bet bluff properties (no blockers, awkward postflop). Against an active, likely barreling opener, folding is the highest-EV, lowest-variance option.

I am currently the chip leader with 19,700 chips. Holding 6c 2h UTG in a 5-handed game is an easy fold. This hand has no equity or playability, especially from early position, and I want to preserve my stack for better opportunities as the tournament progresses.

94o is a complete trash hand from UTG+1 position. No connectivity, no suitedness, low cards. Folding is mandatory to preserve stack and avoid difficult postflop situations with multiple players behind, including deep-stacked opponents like Claude and Flash. Tournament stage requires tight play from early position to maximize stack long-term.

I am on the Button with Jc 5c. The action has folded to me. While the Button is a strong position to steal the blinds, J5s is a fairly weak hand with large gaps and low playability compared to other suited connectors or gappers. The Big Blind (Claude) has a massive chip lead (194 BB) and can easily defend or 3-bet, making a steal attempt less effective. Given the hand strength and the risk of playing a weak hand against a deep stack, folding is the most prudent long-term play.

Blind-vs-blind after everyone folds, we’re getting great immediate odds to complete (50 to win 150) and 9-5o has enough equity vs a wide BB range. Raising would inflate an out-of-position pot against a much deeper stack who can defend/3-bet aggressively, so I prefer to keep the SPR high and realize equity cheaply, then look to take stabs on favorable textures.
FiveTwo limped from SB and I'm in the BB with Q2o. This is a weak hand, but I'm getting a free flop. No need to raise with such a marginal holding - I'll check and see if the flop connects.

Limped blind-vs-blind pot with a very high SPR; on 8h-3s-5h we have a marginal made hand (bottom pair, weak kicker, no heart) that doesn’t want to inflate the pot OOP versus a wide range that can apply a lot of pressure. Checking keeps the pot small, induces bluffs from missed overcards, and lets us comfortably continue versus reasonable sizing while avoiding getting forced into ugly bet/call or bet/fold lines if Claude raises.

I have Q2o on an 8h 3s 5h board - complete miss. No pair, no real draw. FiveTwo checked, and I should check behind to see a free turn. No reason to bluff here with Q-high against a limped pot when villain could have any piece of this board.

Turn bricks off and Claude’s flop check leaves him with a lot of unpaired overcards and some passive draws. With a weak one-pair hand (5x) and no heart, I don’t want to face a big delayed stab from a deep stack, but I do want to deny equity to hands like A-high/K-high and make heart/straight draws pay. A small probe also lets me define the hand cheaply: if he raises, my hand is rarely good enough to continue. If he calls, I can often check rivers and take showdown on safe runouts.

I hit bottom pair with the deuce on the turn. FiveTwo is betting small (100 into 200). Getting 3:1 odds with a pair, this is an easy call. My Q2 now has showdown value against bluffs and draws. FiveTwo's small bet size doesn't indicate much strength.

We river two pair (9s and 5s) in a limped blind-vs-blind pot after probing small on the turn and getting called. Claude’s range after check/call flop and call tiny turn is still very wide and contains many worse hands that can call a reasonable value bet (8x, 5x, 3x, some sticky A-high/9x), while the main concern is hearts completing on the river. Because flushes/9-8 two pair/sets are certainly possible, I don’t want to polarize with a big bet; instead I’ll go for a small-to-medium thin value bet and comfortably fold if raised.

River completed the heart flush (9h). I have bottom pair with Q2 on 8h3s5h2c9h. FiveTwo bets 200 into 600, giving me 4:1 odds. My pair of deuces beats all missed draws. The small bet sizing doesn't indicate a strong flush - more likely a thin value bet or blocking bet. With these odds and showdown value, calling is correct.

Qh3h is a weak suited hand from UTG in a 5-handed game. It has some playability due to suitedness for flush potential and queen high, but from early position, it's not strong enough to open raise or even limp. Folding preserves stack and avoids difficult post-flop spots where I'm likely dominated or out of position. With 88BB effective stack, no need to speculate with marginal hands early. Standard fold to maximize EV long-term.

I am in UTG+1 with 8d 5c, which is a very weak hand (unsuited, unconnected, low cards). With three players left to act behind me, including the blinds, opening this hand has negative expected value. I have no reason to bluff or get involved in this pot. The correct play is to fold immediately.

Button vs blinds in a 5-handed game is a prime steal spot. A♠6♦ has solid playability, blocks strong Ax holdings, and performs well as an open against typical blind defending ranges. With 56bb we don’t want to limp and invite multiway pots; we prefer to apply pressure and take down the dead money. I’ll use a standard, slightly larger than min-raise sizing to reduce the big blind’s immediate price while keeping my range flexible versus a 3-bet.

I have KK - a premium hand. FiveTwo opened to 250 from the button. This is an easy 3-bet for value. I should size to around 3x the open, approximately 750-850, to build the pot while still getting called by worse hands.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten of Clubs and Deuce of Clubs. The Button (FiveTwo) opened for 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) 3-bet to 800. While my hand is suited, T2c is extremely weak and frequently dominated by both the opening range of the Button and the 3-betting range of the Small Blind. I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand against a strong range. With a healthy stack of 19,600, there is no strategic reason to commit chips to a pot where I am a significant underdog. Folding is the most prudent play.

Claude’s small blind 3-bet to 800 creates a low SPR spot (calling leaves ~4.6k behind into ~1.7k), where top-pair decisions get forced quickly. A♠6♦ is an offsuit wheel ace that is heavily dominated by the Ax region of a typical SB 3-bet range (AK/AQ/AJ/AT), and it lacks suitedness/connectivity to realize equity well in a 3-bet pot. Turning it into a 4-bet bluff would require very high fold equity (and plays poorly when called), which I can’t assume here versus a capable opponent with position disadvantage but deep stack. Best EV is to preserve my 53bb stack and continue stealing in better spots.

I am in UTG position (equivalent to Hijack in 6-max) at a 5-handed table with a stack of 6700 (67 BB). My hand is 8s Ts. While suited one-gappers have playability, opening T8s from early position is generally considered too loose, especially with deep-stacked aggressive opponents behind (Claude and Flash) and a shorter stack (FiveTwo) immediately to my left who might shove. The Expected Value (EV) of opening this hand from UTG is typically neutral or negative. I will fold to preserve chips for better spots or better position.

5-handed UTG+1 with J2o is well below opening/limping range. It plays poorly postflop (dominated jack, weak kicker, low connectivity) and there’s no need to take marginal spots with ~53bb. Folding preserves stack and avoids getting isolated by the button/blinds.

I have 2h 3d on the button - one of the worst hands in poker. While button is a good stealing position, 23o has almost no equity when called and doesn't play well postflop. With a healthy stack of 19525, no need to get creative with trash. Folding.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s5s, a speculative suited hand that plays well in heads-up situations. Since everyone has folded to me, I am only up against Elon in the Big Blind. Raising here is a standard play to exert pressure and potentially win the blinds immediately. If Elon calls, I have a hand with good straight and flush potential. Given my deep stack, I can afford to play this aggressively. I will raise to 300 chips.

Tc2s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against a SB steal raise. Folding from BB is standard as it lacks domination resistance, straight potential, or flush draws. Pot odds not compelling enough to defend OOP with trash. Preserve stack for better spots late in tournament.

5-handed we can open a bit wider, but Jh6h is still a dominated suited jack that performs poorly out of position versus multiple players yet to act. With Claude/Flash showing willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure, opening this hand risks either getting pushed off equity or playing a tough pot OOP with marginal showdown value. Preserving stack and waiting for better UTG opens is higher EV here.
J2s is too weak to open from UTG+1. Even with the suited aspect providing some playability, this hand has poor equity and reverse implied odds from early position. Folding is the clear play.

I am on the button with King-Ten offsuit. After two players have folded, I am in a prime position to open the pot. K-To is a strong hand to open from the button, as it dominates many of the hands the blinds might defend with. I'll stick to my standard open raise size of 2.5x the big blind to apply pressure and potentially take the blinds down or play a pot in position.

32o is one of the worst possible hands: offsuit, unconnected low cards, no high card value, poor post-flop playability. Facing a button raise from Flash, who has position and a wide stealing range, but 32o is not in any reasonable defending range from SB. Pot odds are about 2:1 (400:200), but equity against a button open range is abysmal (~20-25% at best, often less vs value). Folding preserves my 8675 stack (86.75BB) for better spots in late tournament (hand 107/150). No history suggests Flash is super weak; standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit (ATo). The action folded to the Button (Flash), who raised to 250. The Small Blind folded. ATo is a strong hand to defend against a Button open, as Button ranges are typically wide. I am getting excellent pot odds (calling 150 to win a pot of 400), requiring roughly 27% equity, which ATo easily has against a stealing range. While a 3-bet is an option to seize initiative, calling is the standard and lower-variance play to realize equity out of position without bloating the pot with a marginal showdown hand.

I hold Top Pair with a Ten kicker on an A-7-7 paired board. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop raiser (Flash). This board texture (Ace high, paired) often favors the aggressor's range, so Flash is likely to make a continuation bet with many hands, including bluffs and worse value hands (lower Aces, pocket pairs).
