Checking allows me to keep their bluffing range in the hand and realize my equity. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only get action from better hands or fold out worse ones.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on a very dry, paired board (As-7c-7d). This board heavily favors my range as the button opener, as I have all the strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) and potentially pocket pairs that are now effectively overpairs. The Big Blind's calling range is wider and contains many hands that missed this flop entirely. Pro has checked to me, which is expected. A small continuation bet of around 40% of the pot should be enough to fold out his air and marginal hands that cannot withstand pressure on an Ace-high board. Even though I only have King-high, the board texture allows me to represent a strong hand effectively.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a decent kicker (Ten) on a paired board [As 7c 7d]. The opponent, opening from the Button, has a wide range and is expected to continuation bet this texture frequently with both value hands and bluffs. Raising here would likely isolate me against better Aces or Trips (7x) while folding out the bluffs I beat. Folding is out of the question given my hand strength. Calling is the optimal play to keep the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands on future streets. The bet size is standard (about 40% pot), and I have excellent odds to continue.

On the Turn, the board is [As 7c 7d 7s]. I hold [Td Ac]. This gives me a full house, Sevens full of Aces (7-7-7-A-A). Given the board texture, any other Ace in the opponent's hand results in a split pot (both make 7-7-7-A-A). I lose to quad Sevens (any 7x) and pocket Aces (which makes Aces full of Sevens). I beat all other pocket pairs (e.g., KK makes Sevens full of Kings). Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Button), checking is the standard play. It allows the opponent to bluff with air or value bet an Ace (resulting in a chop) or a worse pocket pair. Leading out would likely only fold out hands I beat or get action from chops/better hands.

The turn brings a third seven to the board (As-7c-7d-7s), making my current best hand trip sevens with an Ace-King kicker (7-7-7-As-Kd). Pro checked to me, and I need to evaluate where I stand. On this board, any pocket pair or any Ace now makes a full house, both of which beat my trip sevens. Given that Pro called my flop continuation bet on an Ace-high paired board, it is highly likely they have an Ace, a pocket pair, or potentially the case seven (which would be quads). If I bet, I am unlikely to fold out better hands, and I will only be called by hands that have me beat. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, preserve my stack, and potentially reach showdown with some value if Pro is also holding air or a weaker high-card hand.

I hold a Full House (Sevens full of Aces), which is a very strong hand on this board (A-7-7-7-6). I only lose to Quad Sevens (a 7 in the hole) or Pocket Aces. I chop with any other Ace (Ax) because our kickers don't play (best hand is 7-7-7-A-A). Given the opponent checked back the Turn, their range is likely capped to weak Aces, pocket pairs, or air. Betting here yields little value: worse hands (pocket pairs) fold, equal hands (Ax) call for a chop, and better hands (7x) raise. Checking is the superior option to induce bluffs from air or missed draws, and I will happily call a bet to win against bluffs or chop with Ax.

The board has tripled the sevens, and there is an Ace on the flop. Any player with an Ace, a six, or a pocket pair now has a Full House, while anyone with the fourth seven has Quads. Pro called my continuation bet on the flop (As-7-7), which indicates they likely have a piece of the board or a pair. Since all of those hands (Aces, sevens, or pocket pairs) currently beat my hand of trip sevens with an Ace-King kicker (7-7-7-As-Kd), betting serves no purpose. I won't fold out better hands, and I won't be called by worse. Checking back is the most prudent play to reach showdown and see if my King kicker is sufficient to win against a missed draw or air.
K4o is too weak to open from UTG. It's outside a standard opening range for early position - the kicker is too weak and we'll be out of position against most callers. Easy fold.

I am holding Ace-King of hearts, which is one of the strongest starting hands in poker. Claude, the only player before me, has folded. I will open with a standard raise of 3x the big blind (300 chips) to build value and narrow the field while maintaining a consistent betting pattern from previous hands.

On the button with 86bb effective, facing an early position raise to 300 (3bb) from Flash into a 4.5bb pot, getting ~1.5:1 direct odds to call. A2 suited is a speculative hand with excellent playability: ace-blocker, suited for flush/straight potential, and live cards post-flop. Position allows controlling pot size and realizing equity against a potentially wide EP range from Flash, who has raised frequently with marginal holdings in history (e.g., light opens and calls). Folding is too tight in late tournament stage with 42 hands left; 3-betting not ideal as A2s lacks equity denial or fold equity vs deep-stacked EP raiser. Calling to play multiway or isolate post-flop if blinds fold, focusing on implied odds with suited connector-like properties.

I hold 9s5d in the Small Blind. Flash opened to 3bb from early position and Elon called on the Button. I am out of position against two players with a very weak hand (9-high offsuit). Even though I am getting decent pot odds (calling 250 into a pot that will be 1000), 95o does not have enough equity or playability to justify a call, especially multi-way and out of position. The risk of domination and difficulty in realizing equity make this a clear fold.

We’re getting a good price in the big blind: call 200 to win a pot that will be 950 (750+200), needing ~21% immediate equity. 8h5h is too weak to 3-bet versus an UTG+1 open and a button flat (ranges are relatively strong), but it plays well multiway with suitedness and connectedness (can make strong disguised straights/flushes and some good semi-bluff opportunities). With ~50bb effective, defending by calling is the highest-EV line.

Multiway flop versus the UTG+1 preflop raiser and the button caller, this Jc-3c-4s board is likely to connect with their ranges far more than ours. With 8h5h we have no pair, no meaningful straight draw, and only a backdoor heart flush, so there’s not enough equity to justify building the pot with a donk lead. Best is to check and realize whatever equity we can; we’ll generally fold to any normal-sized continuation bet.

I have Ace-King of hearts on a Jack-high board. I was the pre-flop aggressor. FiveTwo has checked to me. I will place a continuation bet of 450 chips (approx. half the pot) to take control of the hand, represent a strong range (like a Jack or an overpair), and try to win the pot immediately against hands that missed the flop or to charge any club draws.

Preflop call with A2dd was speculative for its playability, ace blocker, suitedness, and position in a multiway pot against Flash's aggressive EP open. However, the flop Jc3c4s completely misses: no pair, only a weak gutshot straight draw to the 5 for a wheel (4 outs, ~8% equity standalone), ace-high with poor showdown value, and mere backdoor diamond flush draw. Flash's 450 (~half-pot) c-bet into a 3-way pot fits his aggressive history of frequent EP raises and cbets (e.g., Hands 13,15,44,45,73,84), representing a wide range including overpairs, Jx, flush/straight draws (clubs like AcXc, 5cXc, 2c5c; str8 draws like 25o,56o, Q5s), and bluffs. My equity is crushed multiway if FiveTwo (who limped BB wide per history) continues with draw-heavy hands. Calling 450 into 1400 offers ~3.1:1 odds but requires ~22% equity to break even immediate (actual ~15-20% vs Flash's polarized range, worse if FiveTwo calls); implied odds minimal given reverse blockers and board texture favoring villains' draws/hits. Raising lacks fold equity (Flash calls wide) and thin value. Position allows folding now to realize equity only when ahead later, preserving 83BB stack for better spots with 42 hands left in high-roller tourney. Clear fold to avoid spew.

Flash’s 450 c-bet into 1400 is small, but with 8h5h on Jc-3c-4s we have essentially no current equity: no pair, no real straight draw (only very thin runner-runner possibilities), and only a backdoor heart flush. We’d need ~24% equity to call, and we’re nowhere near that versus an UTG+1 opening range that can c-bet this board with many better hands and strong club combos. A check-raise bluff would risk a large chunk of our ~50bb effective stack with minimal equity and poor turn playability. Best is to preserve chips and fold.

I am dealt pocket Kings (Ks, Kc) in UTG position. This is an premium hand. My objective is to build a large pot and thin the field. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind is appropriate here to extract value and gain control of the hand.

Qh3h is a marginal suited hand with poor high-card strength and low connectivity. Facing an UTG raise from Flash (deep stack, aggressive history), my position is UTG+1, out of position post-flop. Calling risks difficult spots with dominated hands likely, while fold preserves stack for better opportunities in late tournament stage (hand 109/150). Standard fold vs strong early position range.

I hold 8h 7d on the Button facing a UTG raise to 300 from Flash (active stack ~195bb). My hand is an unsuited connector which is generally too weak to cold call an Early Position open, as I am likely dominated by stronger ranges and vulnerable to squeezes from the blinds. Despite having position, the hand strength does not justify the investment of 300 chips. Folding is the standard and most prudent play.

Pocket 44 plays best as a call here. Versus a 5-handed UTG open to 3bb, 3-betting turns our hand into a low-equity bluff that will get called by better pairs/broadways and forces us to play an awkward, low-SRP pot out of position. Calling keeps in dominated overcards, can invite the BB to come along (improving set-mining/implied odds), and our postflop plan is straightforward: continue mainly when we flop a set or get a favorable low board texture; otherwise we can check-fold efficiently versus pressure.

Ah4h in the BB facing a UTG raise to 300 and a SB call. Getting 3.5:1 odds to call 200 into a 700 pot. A4 suited has decent playability - nut flush potential, wheel straight possibilities. Good price to see a flop multiway.

Flop is J♣ J♥ 4♠ and we hold 4♦4♣ for a very strong full house (fours full of jacks). It’s not the absolute nuts (JJ makes quads), but against both players’ ranges we’re far ahead and want to win a big pot. This texture is also fairly dry, so leading risks folding out a lot of their air and medium pairs; checking keeps their ranges wide and lets Flash (preflop raiser) c-bet or Claude stab. Plan: check with the intention to mostly check-raise for value versus a bet (sizing up to start getting stacks in by the river), while noting the small risk of JJ/quads.

I flopped bottom pair (fours) with a backdoor nut flush draw on a paired board (Jh Jc 4s). FiveTwo checked, and I have the option to act. With a paired board and two opponents, my small pair has minimal showdown value - anyone with a jack has me crushed. Betting here accomplishes little; I'd only fold out hands I beat and get called/raised by better. Check to control pot size and see a cheap turn.

I have an overpair (Kings) on a paired board (4s Jh Jc). Both opponents in the blinds have checked to me. While the board pair of Jacks is slightly concerning, it's more likely they have smaller pairs, Ace-high hands, or are simply folding. A continuation bet of 50% pot is appropriate here to extract value from pocket pairs (55-TT) and to protect my hand against overcards like an Ace on the turn. If I get raised, I will have to evaluate the strength of the opponent, but for now, betting for value is the standard play.

We flopped a monster: 4♦4♣ on J♣ J♥ 4♠ is a full house (fours full), only losing to the very rare JJ. Flash’s 1/3-pot c-bet into two players is exactly the spot to start building a stack-off pot while his range is still wide (overpairs, AJ/KJ/QJ, bluffs) and before the action can check through on later streets. I want a raise size that (1) keeps Jx/overpairs continuing, (2) pressures Claude to continue with any Jx/4x/curious pairs, and (3) sets up a clean turn shove with our remaining stack. Raising to 1700 accomplishes that with an SPR that lets us get all-in by the turn/river.

FiveTwo's check-raise to 1700 on a JJ4 board is a very strong line representing trips or a full house. My pair of fours is crushed by any jack (which is very likely given the action). Flash still has to act behind me and could also have a jack. I'm getting ~2.8:1 but my equity against a check-raising range here is terrible - I'm drawing to 2 outs at best if they have a jack, and drawing dead if they have 44. Clear fold.

I hold pocket Kings, which is an extremely strong overpair on a 4-J-J flop. After my continuation bet of 450, FiveTwo has check-raised to 1700. While a check-raise on a paired board can indicate a Jack, FiveTwo has shown significant aggression and willingness to play wide in previous hands (like Hand #91 and Hand #95). Kings are too strong to fold here against one opponent, especially one who may be overplaying a smaller pocket pair or bluffing. Calling is better than raising at this point to keep his bluffs in the pot and avoid over-committing if he does indeed have the Jack, while allowing me to evaluate the turn.

Turn is 4♠ J♥ J♣ 8♦ and we still have a monster (4♦4♣ = fours full). Pot is 4300 and we have only 3050 behind (SPR ~0.7), so the cleanest way to maximize EV is to put the rest in now rather than risk a check-back from hands like AQ/AK, TT/99, or even cautious overpairs. Flash’s flop c-bet/call range contains plenty of Jx that will be forced to continue, and some overpairs will feel priced-in. Yes, the 8♦ slightly increases the number of boats that beat us (notably 88, occasional J8), but those combos are limited compared to all the dominated continues, so jamming is still best for value and to deny any free river.

I am holding pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand, on a board of 4s-Jh-Jc-8d. While the presence of two Jacks on the board is a concern, I am only losing to a Jack, pocket fours, or pocket eights. Pre-flop, I raised and was called by FiveTwo in the small blind and Claude in the big blind. On the flop, after checking, FiveTwo check-raised my continuation bet, and has now shoved all-in on the turn. FiveTwo has demonstrated a pattern of high aggression in previous hands. Given the pot odds (calling 3,050 to win a total pot of approximately 10,400) and FiveTwo's aggressive tendencies, folding pocket Kings here would be too exploitable. He could easily be overplaying a smaller pocket pair (like 99, TT, or QQ) or even a semi-bluff. I must call.

UTG in a 5-handed game, K4o is a weak offsuit hand with poor post-flop playability and high chance of being dominated by better kings. Standard fold to preserve stack and wait for better spots, especially late in the tournament with 40 hands left and deep stacks. No reads from history justify opening this trash hand from early position.

I hold King-Five offsuit (K5o) in UTG+1 (middle position in a 5-handed game). The player UTG (Elon) has folded. K5o is a very weak hand, especially from this position, as it is easily dominated by better Kings and plays poorly post-flop. With aggressive players behind me and a relatively deep stack (71 BB), there is no incentive to open this speculative hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

Folds to the button in a short-handed game means blinds are wide but still have to defend a lot. Even with a weak offsuit Q3, raising is profitable as a steal + we retain position and initiative, and we can comfortably fold if we face a 3-bet. I’ll use a standard small open size to risk less while still pressuring the blinds.
J7o is a weak offsuit hand in the small blind facing a button raise. Out of position with poor playability - easy fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The Button (FiveTwo) has raised to 2.5x (250). In this position, Ace-Eight is a standard defending hand against a wide Button opening range. FiveTwo has shown significant aggression in recent hands (notably Hand #109), so I don't want to 3-bet and face a potential 4-bet with a hand that has mediocre playability post-flop. Calling allows me to realize my equity, close the action pre-flop, and see how the board develops while keeping the pot manageable out of position.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a relatively low board (8-4-3). While my hand is likely the best at the moment, I am out of position against the Button (FiveTwo), who was the pre-flop aggressor. FiveTwo has demonstrated a high level of aggression in recent hands. Checking to the raiser is the standard play here, allowing me to see how he chooses to proceed. This keeps the pot manageable and allows him to continue with bluffs or semi-bluffs (like flush or straight draws) that I currently beat.

BTN vs BB single-raised pot: after Flash checks, I still have an overall range advantage from the preflop raise, but this board hits BB’s defend range reasonably often (many 8x/4x/straight+flush-draw combos). With Q3 I’ve flopped bottom pair, which has some thin value and benefits from protection vs two overcards and random backdoors. A small c-bet accomplishes a lot: it can fold out unpaired overcards, deny equity to hands like A-high/K-high with backdoors, and lets me define the hand. If I get check-raised, this hand is too weak to continue and I can fold comfortably.

I have top pair with the best kicker (Ace) on a board of 8-4-3 with a flush draw. FiveTwo is an aggressive player who frequently bets when checked to. By calling his 200 bet rather than raising, I keep his wide range in, allowing him to continue bluffing on future streets with hands I currently dominate. It also allows me to control the size of the pot while out of position, especially since the board could become more dangerous if a heart or an overcard appears.

I currently hold top pair with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a board of 8-4-3-2. While the deuce of clubs doesn't change much, it does complete a possible straight for a hand like 5-6, though that is only a small part of the Button's range. Given FiveTwo's history of high aggression (as seen in Hand #109 and Hand #74), I want to continue my strategy of checking to him. This allows him to continue bluffing with his wide range of overcards, flush draws, or air. If I bet now, I likely only get called by better hands or draws that have decent equity, while folding out the bluffs that I currently beat. Checking keeps the pot manageable and maximizes my value against an aggressive opponent.

We c-bet small and got called on a flop that connects fairly well with BB’s defend range (8x/4x, heart draws, some straight draws). The 2c doesn’t meaningfully improve our hand (still just bottom pair) and doesn’t change the texture enough to generate a lot of fold equity versus that continuing range—most of what called flop will call again. Betting here mostly builds a pot where we’re often behind and can get put in tough spots by a check-raise or river aggression. With position, checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our (thin) showdown value versus missed draws/A-high, and lets us respond more accurately to river action.

On the river, the board is 3c-8h-4h-2c-Th. I have a pair of Eights with an Ace kicker. The Ten of Hearts is an overcard to my pair and completes a potential heart flush. FiveTwo checked the turn after I checked to him, which suggests he might have been pot-controlling a medium-strength hand or giving up on a bluff/draw. By checking the river, I allow him to check back with hands that have showdown value (like a lower pair) or attempt a bluff with missed straight draws (like 5-6). If I bet, I likely only get called by hands that beat me (like a Ten, a flush, or a slow-played set) and fold out his bluffs. Given his previous aggression, checking to induce a bluff or reach a cheap showdown is the most consistent and safe line.

River completes the front-door heart flush (and adds some higher pairs like Tx), so our pair of 3s has very thin value. After BB called the flop and then checked turn/river, their range still contains a lot of 8x/4x, some slow-played flushes, and some missed straight draws. Betting doesn’t get called by worse often (missed draws mostly fold), while better hands (any 8x+, any heart) can call and flushes can even check-raise. With position and a hand that can beat busted draws, the best EV line is to take the free showdown and avoid turning a marginal made hand into a bluff.

I am in UTG position with Ten-Three offsuit (Th 3d). This is a statistically very weak hand with poor playability and no flush or straight potential. Being first to act pre-flop with 4 players behind me, the likelihood of someone having a better hand is high, and I will be out of position post-flop. There is no strategic merit in entering the pot with this holding. The correct play is to fold immediately.

5-handed from UTG+1/HJ, A5o is an acceptable open: it blocks strong Ax (reducing 3-bet frequency), can make strong top-pair combos, and has wheel/straight potential. With 100bb stacks and relatively standard open sizes in this game, I want to apply pressure and pick up blinds rather than limp and invite multiway play. I’ll use a consistent ~2.5x open to keep my range protected and give myself fold equity versus the blinds and button.

K4 suited on the button facing a 2.5x open from UTG+1. This is a marginal hand but I have position and suited cards give me some playability - potential for flushes and the king has some high card value. Stack is healthy at 19k. Calling in position is reasonable here to see a flop cheaply.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit (K7o). A player in early position (FiveTwo) has raised to 2.5x, and the Button (Claude) has called. K7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially in a multi-way pot. Given that both opponents have shown significant aggression in previous hands and that I would be forced to act first on all subsequent streets, the risk of being dominated or outplayed is too high to justify a call. Folding is the most disciplined and stack-preserving move here.

Preflop in BB with 7s6d offsuit, facing an early position raise from FiveTwo (UTG+1, strong range like 88+, ATs+, KQs+, AJo+ given history of aggressive play) to 250 and a button call from Claude (can be wider but still adds strength). Pot is 650, to call 150 gives ~4.3:1 odds (19% equity needed), but 76o has poor equity (~20-25% multiway OOP) against this range, limited playability offsuit, and I'm OOP postflop against two players with deep stacks. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 111/150). No strong blockers or suited potential to justify speculative call.

Heads-up as the preflop raiser on a fairly dry J-6-3 rainbow board, I still have a meaningful range advantage (overpairs, strong Jx like AJ/KJ, and big broadways). With A5o I don’t have much immediate equity beyond the ace overcard and weak backdoors, so I don’t want to build a big pot—but I do want to realize fold equity and deny two-overcard hands (KQ/QT/KT) from realizing cheaply. A small c-bet accomplishes that while keeping my range protected and my risk low. If I get raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I’ll often slow down on many turns unless I improve or pick up a strong barrel card.

I have Kh4h on Jc3s6h - just K-high with a backdoor heart flush draw. FiveTwo's c-bet is small (200 into 650, ~30% pot). With position, one overcard, and backdoor flush potential, calling this small bet to see a turn is reasonable. I can re-evaluate on the turn - if a heart comes I'll have a real draw, and if I improve I can continue.

Turn pairs the top card (J), which is generally a good barreling card for the preflop raiser: my range contains a lot of strong Jx (AJ/KJ/QJ/JTs) and overpairs, while Claude’s flop-calling range includes many 6x/3x and pocket pairs that now hate life facing another bet. With A5o I have little equity and don’t want to bloat the pot versus a Button range that can contain trips, so I choose a small, range-pressure bet that targets folds from his floats (AQ/KQ/QT) and marginal made hands. If raised, I can comfortably fold; if called, I’ll often slow down on most rivers.

Turn brought the Jh, giving me a flush draw with Kh4h (9 outs). Pot is 1375, I need to call 325 - getting roughly 4:1 pot odds. With ~18% equity to hit the flush and potential additional equity if I hit a King, calling is correct. The bet sizing is small relative to pot.

River brings the A♠ giving me top pair, but with a weak kicker (A5) I’m in a classic thin-value / pot-control spot after betting flop and turn and getting called twice. Claude’s turn-calling range contains a lot of Jx (that now has trips) and some boats (33/66), which crush me, but it also contains plenty of pocket pairs (77–TT/99), some 6x/3x that may look me up versus a small sizing, and occasional floats that improved to a worse ace. Checking invites him to polarize with a bigger bet (value with Jx, occasional bluffs), putting me in a tougher decision. A small block/value bet sets my own price, can still get called by worse made hands, and I can comfortably fold if raised since raises are heavily Jx/boats.

River brought the As, missing my flush draw completely. I have K-high which beats nothing for value. FiveTwo has bet all three streets (200, 325, 500) with small sizing. The Ace on river is a terrible card for me - it likely improves their range and they could easily have Ax, Jx, or a pocket pair. I'm getting about 4.4:1 (500 to win 2200), needing to be good ~23% of the time. While the small sizing could indicate a bluff, K-high is too weak to call here - I lose to any pair and the Ace river hits many hands FiveTwo would have. Folding.

5-handed UTG is still the earliest position and we’ll face frequent 3-bets/plays from the Button/SB/BB. Q5o is too dominated (bad kicker, poor playability) and doesn’t realize equity well out of position in multiway/3-bet pots. With a healthy ~109bb stack, we don’t need to force thin steals here—fold and wait for stronger opens.
A6 suited is a playable hand in UTG+1. Suited aces have good playability with flush potential. I'll open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) as a standard open.

I am on the button with King-Nine offsuit. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game. While I have position, K9o is a marginal hand that is frequently dominated by Claude's opening range (which likely includes K-J, K-Q, A-K, and mid-to-high pairs). With the blinds still to act and a comfortable stack of over 150 big blinds, I see no reason to play a mediocre hand against the table chip leader. I will wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

82o is a complete trash hand with no playability, straight draw potential, or suitedness. Facing an early position raise from Claude (UTG+1 in 5-handed), who has been aggressive but often with value or semi-bluffs in history. Pot odds are roughly 2:1 (calling 200 into 400), but my equity against a typical EP opening range (10-15% hands like pairs, broadways, suited aces) is around 20-25% preflop, and postflop I'll be OOP with a weak hand unlikely to improve meaningfully. No fold equity for a 3-bet squeeze as SB facing EP raise, and stacks are deep enough that villain won't fold often. Preserve stack for better spots; folding is standard and +EV long-term to maximize tournament stack.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o facing a raise to 250 (2.5bb) from Claude in UTG+1. The pot odds are favorable (calling 150 to win 400, requiring ~27% equity), but T8o performs poorly against an early position opening range, which is likely to dominate me (AT, KTs, QTs, JTs, TT+). Furthermore, I would be playing the entire post-flop hand out of position against the chip leader, making it difficult to realize equity and easy to make expensive second-best mistakes. Given the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage, folding is the disciplined play to preserve my stack.
7c 3s is a trash hand in UTG position. No playability - not suited, not connected, low cards. Easy fold.

In a 5-handed game, King-Seven suited is a strong enough hand to open from the Cutoff (UTG+1) after the Under-the-Gun player has folded. I will place a standard 2.5x raise to 250 to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds or play a pot with position.

Hand #113, pre-flop on the button with QsTh (offsuit QTo). Pot is 400, facing a 250 raise from Flash in early/mid position (UTG+1). My stack is 8225 (~82BB), Flash has 15025 (deep). QTo is a playable hand on the button due to position, queen-high blocker, straight potential, and suited? Wait, offsuit but still decent. Defending wide vs single raise is GTO-ish, as pot odds are good (calling 250 into ~650 post-call, ~2.6:1). Folding too tight late in tournament; 3-betting polarizes range and risks OOP post-flop if called/shoved. Calling keeps pot small, realizes equity in position, and exploits if Flash c-bets light. Opponents deep, no immediate fold equity edge without reads. Plan: call, play post-flop dynamically.

I am holding Js 5h in the Small Blind. This is a statistically weak hand (J5 offsuit), especially when facing a raise from early position (Flash) and a call from the button (Elon). Being out of position against two opponents with a hand that has poor playability and is easily dominated makes calling unprofitable, despite the pot odds. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

We’re getting a very good price closing the action: 150 more to win a 650 pot (~23% immediate), and KTo has adequate equity versus an UTG+1 open plus a button flat. A squeeze here is tempting, but KTo is a poor candidate (dominated when called, plays tough OOP, and doesn’t have suited equity), so I prefer to defend and realize equity rather than bloat the pot out of position with a marginal bluff/value hand.

Multiway and out of position on A♣ Q♥ A♠, the preflop raiser has the strongest ace-density and is incentivized to c-bet frequently; my range is at a disadvantage here, so leading doesn’t accomplish much and risks getting raised. With K♥T♦ I have a gutshot to Broadway (J) and some blocker value (Kx), so checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me respond appropriately to sizing (peel small bets, fold to big pressure, and potentially take stabs on favorable turns if checked through).

The flop of Ac-Qh-As is extremely dry and heavily favors my pre-flop opening range, as I have all the strong Ax hands (AK, AQ, AJ, ATs) and QQ in my range. However, in a three-way pot, the utility of a continuation bet decreases because the probability that one of my two opponents has an Ace or a Queen increases. My King-high has some marginal showdown value against total air, and by checking, I can keep the pot small and see how the other players react. If the action checks around, I get a free turn card. If Elon bets behind me, I can safely fold knowing my King-high is likely beaten.

Post-flop on A♣ Q♥ A♠ board with Q♠ T♥ (second pair, queen kicker). Pot 800, checked by BB and preflop raiser Flash, now on button with position. Board is paired aces (dry, few draws: possible gutshots like KT/JT, but minimal). My range as button caller includes broadways, Ax, suited connectors; blocks Q, which is valuable. Flash's check after raising pre suggests weakness (missed flop, AK/AQ not great with paired board, slowplaying AA/set unlikely to check BB too). FiveTwo BB check typical light defend. Betting exploits fold equity against worse (KQ/JQ, floats, bluffs), thin value from Ax (one ace), lower pairs; protects vs turn Q/J/T. Half-pot bet (400-500) balances value/bluff freq, keeps pot manageable deepstacked (~80BB). Checking risks giving free card to draws/improvers, loses EV vs folds. Dynamic post-flop per preflop plan: bet here for max stack growth late tournament.

Elon’s 500 bet into 1300 on A♣ Q♥ A♠ is a spot where at least one of the two opponents (Flash preflop raiser, Elon button flatter) is very likely to have an Ace at some frequency, and our hand’s real equity is mostly just the 4-out gutshot to a J for Broadway. We’re not closing the action (Flash can still raise), and we have poor implied odds because (1) a lot of Ax will slow down on scary runouts, and (2) when someone specifically has AQ we’re drawing dead even if we hit the J. The pot odds (500 to win 1800 ≈ 27.8%) are too demanding for a mostly-4-out draw in a multiway, dominated-range spot. Raising is also unattractive with low fold equity multiway and weak blockers. Best is to preserve stack and fold.

Elon's bet of 500 into a pot of 800 on a board of Ac-Qh-As is a strong move after both the Big Blind and I (the pre-flop aggressor) checked. While it is possible Elon is attempting to steal a dry pot, my hand (Kd-7d) has zero equity on this board and no reliable way to improve. Even if I were to catch a King, I would still lose to any Ace or a Queen. Floating here without a piece of the board or a draw is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding Jack-Four offsuit in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak starting hand, especially from early position in a five-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 150 big blinds, and there is no reason to risk chips on a marginal-to-poor hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

KJo is a strong hand from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game with deep stacks. It's well above average, with good top-pair/top-kicker potential, straight draws, and domination equity against typical calling ranges. Flash folded UTG, so no limpers. Standard play is to raise for value and position, targeting 2.5-3x the BB to build the pot while maintaining fold equity against blinds. Raising to 300 (3x BB) is balanced, avoids min-raise weakness, and sizes well given pot of 150. Will isolate weaker hands or take it down preflop often. Folding is too tight late in tournament; calling/limping is passive and invites multiway pots.

I hold TcQc (Queen-Ten suited) on the Button facing a 3bb open from Elon in UTG+1. With approximately 70bb effective stacks, this hand is a strong candidate for a flat call. It plays very well in position, having the potential to make straights and flushes, and dominates some of the opener's bluffing range while having decent equity against their value range. 3-betting is an option but calling keeps the pot manageable and keeps Elon's range wider, allowing me to use my position post-flop. Folding is too tight for a suited broadway hand on the button.

We’re getting a very good price (250 more to win 750 now, plus potential BB dead money) with a suited, connected hand that realizes equity reasonably well multiway. A squeeze 3-bet with J9s is a bit thin here given our out-of-position disadvantage, Pro’s willingness to continue/4-bet, and Claude’s big stack in the BB who can put us in tough spots. Calling keeps variance controlled while still capturing EV from flops where we make strong draws/two pair/flushes and can win big pots versus one-pair hands.

K3o is weak but I'm getting 5:1 pot odds (200 to win 1000) in the BB with 3 players already in. This is a standard defend - I can see a flop cheaply and fold if I miss. Need to hit top pair or better to continue post-flop.

Multiway (4-handed) and out of position versus the preflop raiser on an A-J-8 two-tone board, our J9 has some showdown value (middle pair) but is dominated by a lot of continuing ranges (Ax, AJ, strong Jx like KJ/QJ, plus diamond draws). Leading doesn’t fold out better hands and mostly gets called/raised by stronger ranges, while also building a pot where we’re often behind. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets us react to sizing—comfortable continuing versus a single small/mid c-bet, but ready to fold to heavy multiway pressure.

I have K3o on A-J-8 with two diamonds. Completely missed this flop - no pair, no draw. With K-high I have showdown value against air but zero reason to bet into 3 opponents on this connected board. Easy check.

Preflop, I raised KJo from UTG+1, got three callers (Pro BTN wide range likely, FiveTwo SB and Claude BB limped-called, weaker ranges). Flop 8c Ad Jd hits me for top pair with K kicker (TP2K), solid showdown value and blocks some Ax/Jx. Board is draw-heavy: diamond flush draw, open-enders like KT/QT/109. Both blinds checked (weakness, unlikely monsters given limp-call pre). Multiway pot (1200), deep stacks, late tournament. As preflop raiser, c-bet range reps Ax/Jx/sets. Betting denies equity to draws (charge 9-10% of stacks minimally), gets value from worse pairs/draws (e.g., QJ, TJ, flush/straight draws, pocket pairs). Size 800 (~2/3 pot) balances value/protection/fold equity without overbetting marginal hand. Check risks button Pro betting Ax/draws behind; bet takes control, builds pot OOP with decent hand. Check-call/raise too passive/speculative. Will fold to raise (protect stack).

On the flop [8c Ad Jd], I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and a backdoor club flush draw. Facing a large continuation bet of 800 (2/3 pot) from the pre-flop raiser into three opponents suggests significant strength, likely a strong Ace or better. The pot odds are roughly 28%, but my direct equity with a gutshot is much lower (approx 8-9% on the turn). Additionally, there are two players yet to act behind me who could check-raise or call. Committing over 10% of my remaining stack on a weak draw in a multi-way pot is not profitable. Folding is the disciplined choice.

Elon’s 800 c-bet into 2000 is a fairly small sizing on a board where he can be betting very wide (Ax, broadway/draws, some air), and our J9s has enough immediate showdown value to continue—especially because Claude can have plenty of worse Jx/diamond floats that we beat. Folding would be too tight versus this sizing. Raising is bad: we’re multiway, out of position, and our pair is dominated by Ax and better Jx, so a check-raise mostly gets action from stronger hands and forces us into a big-pot/stack-off situation we don’t want. Best line is to call and reassess turns; if Claude check-raises big behind, we can comfortably fold and avoid torching chips.
K3o completely missed this A-J-8 two-diamond board. No pair, no diamond draw, no straight draw. Just K-high against a bet and a caller. Easy fold - I have no equity worth calling 800 for.

Turn K♦ is a very poor card for our J9s: it adds another overcard to our pair, improves a lot of Elon’s flop c-bet range (AK/KJ/KQ/KTdd), and brings in/strengthens diamond draw combos. Our hand is now mostly bluff-catcher / thin showdown value with no meaningful draw to improve. Donk-leading doesn’t accomplish much (better hands won’t fold, worse hands often fold, and we risk getting raised off our equity), so the best plan is to keep the pot controlled and let Elon barrel his bluffs at a size we can evaluate. We’ll check with the intention to call small-to-reasonable bets and fold versus heavy pressure.

Turn board Kd completes the diamond flush (Ad Jd Kd) and improves my Kc Jh to two pair (kings and jacks), a very strong hand that beats most one-pair hands like Ax, Jx, 88, and unfinished draws. FiveTwo limp-called pre from SB (wide range including Ax, suited connectors, diamonds, pairs), check-called my flop c-bet on a draw-heavy board (likely Ax, weaker pairs, draws, or floats), then checks again (weakness or trap, but unlikely nuts given history of check-calls). Pot 2800, my stack 7675 (~77BB), effective with FiveTwo 9525. Late tournament (114/150), value bet to extract from Ax/Jx/pairs/draws while denying equity to missed draws; check risks free river card or missed value. Size ~2/3 pot (1800) balances value/protection/fold equity, reps strong range (sets, flushes). Folding out weaker Ax maximizes EV; will reevaluate raise.

We’re facing a very unfavorable turn barrel: after c-betting small multiway, Elon now fires 1800 into ~2800 (pot is 4600 including his bet), on A♦-K♦-J♦-8c. With Js9s we have a weak second pair (bad kicker), no diamond, and essentially no clean improvement (one J left, which is often not even good versus AJ/KJ). This K♦ also smashes a lot of his continuing range (AK, KJ, AQ/AX with diamonds) and gives his diamond holdings strong semibluffs, and given his short remaining stack he’s close to committing—so we should expect heavy river pressure. Calling needs ~28% equity and we won’t realize it profitably; raising is worse because he can jam over the top and we can’t continue. Best play is to preserve chips and fold.

UTG in a 5-handed game requires a tight opening range. Tc3h is a complete trash hand: offsuit, poorly connected, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated by any raising range. Folding preserves my healthy 100BB stack for better opportunities late in the tournament with 35 hands left. No fold equity worth pursuing here.

I hold [9s 2c] in UTG+1 (effectively the cutoff in 5-handed play). This is 9-2 offsuit, statistically one of the worst starting hands in Hold'em. Given my position and the weakness of the hand, there is no strategic merit in entering the pot. The only correct play is to fold immediately.

On the button with Q9o after two folds, this is a profitable steal/iso spot: we have position, reasonable playability, and we pick up 150 dead chips very often. Both blinds have shown willingness to defend/3-bet, so I want an open size that applies pressure but doesn’t bloat the pot too much versus a 3-bet range I can’t continue against comfortably. Raising also keeps my range strong and avoids giving the blinds a free look.

Plan: open, fold to a big 3-bet; continue versus smaller/linear 3-bets only if sizing is favorable and reads warrant (generally still folding Q9o).

I have AKo in the small blind facing a button open of 250. This is a premium hand and a clear 3-bet spot. FiveTwo has been active and is opening from the button with a wide range. I should 3-bet to around 750-800 to isolate and build a pot with my strong hand.

I am holding 7-5 offsuit in the big blind. The button (FiveTwo) opened with a raise, and the small blind (Claude) 3-bet to 800. 7-5o is an extremely weak hand and cannot profitably call a 3-bet, especially out of position against two aggressive opponents. Folding is the only sensible play.

Claude’s small blind 3-bet to 800 is a reasonable sizing that gives me tempting pot odds, but Q9o is still a dominated, low-realization hand versus even a moderately wide SB 3-bet range (lots of Qx/Kx/Ax that have me in bad shape). Calling would put me into too many tough postflop spots where I make second-best top pairs and bleed chips. A 4-bet bluff with Q9o is also unnecessary here given stack depth and tournament context; I can pick better blocker candidates (Ax/Kx suited) and keep my open/steal strategy intact without taking a thin, high-variance line. Best play is to preserve stack and fold.

I am in UTG position with Js3s, a very weak suited hand. In a 5-handed game, this hand is well outside the standard opening range from early position. With 67 big blinds, there is no pressure to make loose moves. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better hand.

5-handed we can open wider, but J4s is still a very dominated suited jack from UTG+1 and we’ll be out of position versus the Button and both blinds. With Claude on the Button and Flash/Elon in the blinds showing plenty of aggression (3-bets/pressure), raising invites a lot of tough defend/3-bet spots where J4s performs poorly, and limping just creates multiway pots with a hand that rarely makes strong top pairs. Preserving EV and avoiding marginal OOP spots is best here.

I have K3o on the button with two folds in front of me. This is a standard button steal opportunity. K3o is playable from the button against just the blinds. I'll make a standard 2.5x open raise to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with K♣ 7♦. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button. While a King-high hand might seem decent, K7 offsuit is quite weak to play out of position against the Button's opening range, especially when the Big Blind (Elon) is still to act and could potentially squeeze or also call. Given my stack size and the stage of the tournament, I should avoid marginal situations where I'll likely be playing a bloated pot out of position. Folding is the most prudent play here.

Facing a button raise to 250 after folds, with blinds posted making pot 400 and to call 150. A4o (Ac4h) is a standard defend from BB vs BTN wide range due to ace blocker, decent equity (~40-45% vs typical BTN open), and pot odds of 150 into 550 effective (3.67:1). OOP but deep stacks allow maneuvering postflop. Folding too tight; 3bet sizing would be to ~700-900 but A4o not ideal 3bet candidate without suitedness or better connectivity. Prefer calling to realize equity and control pot OOP vs likely wide range. Late in tournament but stacks deep (~100BB), no need to tighten excessively.

On this flop of Jh Ah 8s, I hold Ac4h for top pair top kicker (pair of Aces). The board is highly coordinated with a strong heart flush draw, broadway straight draws (e.g., T9, Q10), and gutshots. As OOP after calling a button open preflop, checking is standard with TPTK on such a wet board to pot control, induce bluffs from Claude's wide button range (which includes many draws, weaker Ax, and floats), and realize equity multiway if needed (though heads-up). Betting risks check-raises from sets/draws or getting floated; checking allows opponent to cbet ~50-75% pot wide, which I can call profitably given ~75-85% equity vs. typical cbet range while keeping pot manageable OOP. Deep stacks favor playing postflop cautiously with strong but vulnerable hand. Late tournament but 100+BB effective, no need to force action.

I raised K3o from the button and got called by Elon in the BB. The flop is Jh Ah 8s - I completely whiffed with just king high. This board heavily favors the caller's range (Ax, Jx hands). I have no pair and no draw. C-betting here would be a pure bluff into a board that connects well with his calling range. Checking back to control pot size and see a free card is the prudent play.

On the turn Jd, the board is now Jh Ah 8s Jd, pairing the Jacks, creating two pair possibilities (e.g., Jx), full houses, or straights (e.g., 9T, QT completing gutshots/open-enders). Holding Ac4h for top pair top kicker (TPTK), equity remains strong (~70-80% vs. Claude's check-behind flop range, which is polarized: weak Ax/floats/draws that missed or monsters like sets/AJ). However, OOP first to act on a paired wet board deep-stacked (~100BB effective), betting risks check-raises from improved hands (Jx, two-pair) or draws, bloating pot OOP with vulnerable TPTK. Checking induces bluffs from missed draws (hearts, broadway), weak pairs, or floats in Claude's wide button range; allows pot control; realizes equity by calling stations or trapping value. Preflop dynamics (BTN open vs BB defend) and flop check-check suggest Claude weak/trapping rarely; check exploits this by keeping pot small OOP late in tournament while preserving fold equity post-river if needed. Standard GTO-ish play on dangerous board.

I have K-high on a paired board (Jh Ah 8s Jd). Elon checked again on the turn. With just king high and no draw, I have minimal showdown value but also no reason to build the pot. The paired jack actually makes it less likely he has a jack, but an ace is still very possible in his range. Betting would be a pure bluff and he's shown willingness to check-call postflop. I'll check back and take a free river - I might win at showdown against worse high cards or fold to any aggression.

River 9s completes potential straights (e.g., T7 for 7-8-9-T-J) and a spade flush draw (3 spades on board), making the board extremely dangerous with paired Jacks already. Holding Ac4h for top pair top kicker (Aces), which blocks AA/AJ but is vulnerable to Jx two-pair combos (AJ, J9, J8, JT), sets, straights, and flushes. Claude's button raise preflop followed by check-check on flop (wet with heart flush/broadway draws) and turn (pairs board, helping some Jx) polarizes his range to missed draws/floats/weak Ax or monsters (unlikely given passivity). Deep stacks (~100BB effective for me, deeper for Claude), pot small (550), OOP first to act: betting risks check-raises from nuts/improved draws, builds pot OOP with vulnerable hand late in tournament (116/150 hands). Checking maximizes EV by inducing bluffs from missed draws (spades/hearts/broadway like Q10/KQ/T9), pot-controlling vs. value (call stations thin), and check-folding profitably if bet into (Ac blocks strong Ax, equity ~40-60% vs. polarized betting range). GTO favors check here vs. aggressive button after check-check streets; preserves stack for better spots while realizing equity passively. No fold needed as first to act.

River: Jh Ah 8s Jd 9s. I have K-high with no pair. Elon has checked every street. While his passivity suggests weakness, my K-high has essentially no showdown value against any pair. Bluffing into a player who has check-called multiple streets in history seems -EV with this sizing. The pot is small at 550 and I'm not representing much after checking back flop and turn. Check back and concede the pot if he has anything.

Qs3s is too weak to open from UTG even 5-handed: it’s dominated by better queens and plays poorly versus the likely 3-bet/iso pressure from the deeper stacks behind. Limping is also low-EV. Preserve stack and wait for stronger opening candidates.

A4o in UTG+1 with FiveTwo already folded. This is a marginal hand from early position, but with a healthy stack (18,350) and only 4 players behind, I can open raise. Ace-high has some value and can take down blinds or flop top pair. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am holding pocket sixes on the button, which is a strong starting hand but vulnerable to overcards. Claude has raised to 250 from UTG+1. Calling here is the most standard play; it allows me to play the pot in position and set-mine effectively without over-committing against the current chip leader. If the blinds fold or call, I can evaluate the flop and determine if my pair holds up or if I hit a set.

KQ suited is a premium broadway hand with excellent playability, equity, and fold equity. Facing an early position raise from Claude (aggressive raiser based on history) and a button flat-call from Flash (frequent caller), 3-betting from the SB takes initiative, builds the pot for value against worse hands (e.g., KJo, AQo, suited aces), and can isolate or fold out marginal holdings. Position is imperfect postflop, but stacks are deep (~100BB+), and KQs performs well heads-up or multiway if it holds. Calling risks multiway pots OOP and BB squeeze; folding is too tight. Sizing to ~3.5x the raise (900 total) balances value and protection.

I am in the Big Blind with 7h Jd, a very weak offsuit hand. The action has been heavy: an open from UTG+1, a call from the Button, and a 3-bet squeeze to 900 from the Small Blind. I am facing a bet of 800 more into a pot of 1500. My equity with J7o against a 3-bet range and potential callers behind is extremely low. There is no strategic justification for defending here. This is a standard fold.

Elon 3-bet to 900 from SB after I opened and Flash called. A4o is a weak holding that plays poorly against a 3-bet range - I'm often dominated by better aces and will be out of position in a bloated pot. Even with Flash's call, this is a clear fold. No need to invest 650 more chips with a hand that has poor postflop playability.

I have pocket sixes on the button. Claude opened for 250, and I just called. Elon, in the small blind, has now 3-bet to 900. Claude has folded, so the action is back to me. Calling 650 more into a pot that will be 2150 gives me decent odds (approx 3.3:1) to set-mine, especially since I am in position. Elon's stack is around 100 big blinds, providing enough implied odds if I hit a six. However, if I miss the set, I will likely have to fold to further aggression on most flops. Given the stack depths and position, calling to see a flop is a profitable play.

Heads-up OOP vs Flash on a dry 7d4h2s flop after my preflop 3bet which he called from the button. KQhh gives me the nut flush draw (two overs to the 4h), two live overcards for equity against pairs, and backdoor straight potential. My 3bet range is strong (premiums, broadways), and this board misses much of Flash's calling range (Ax suited, broadways, pairs like 88-JJ that may have folded to 3bet, suited connectors). He can have weaker hearts, gutshots (65, 53, T5), small pairs, or floats. Equity is excellent (~45-60% vs ranges). Donk leading (c-bet OOP) continues aggression, gets value from worse draws/pairs that can't fold, denies equity to floats, builds pot for when I hit. Checking risks giving free cards to his equity hands or check-raise bluffs. Sizing ~55% pot balances value/protection without overcommitting OOP on draw. Deep stacks allow fold equity if raised.

I called the 3-bet pre-flop with pocket sixes primarily to set-mine, as Elon's squeeze from the small blind against an early-position open usually indicates a very strong range. The flop of 7-4-2 rainbow is relatively safe, but I have not improved my hand. Elon has led out with a substantial continuation bet of 1200 into a 2150 pot. While 66 is an overpair to two of the board cards, it is behind all of Elon's likely overpair range (88-AA) and only ahead of his overcard bluffs (AK, AQ). Given that Elon has shown a willingness to barrel multiple streets in previous hands, calling here would likely commit me to a large pot with a very marginal hand on the turn and river. Since my primary goal for calling pre-flop was to hit a set and I missed, the most disciplined play is to fold to this significant aggression and preserve my stack.
K5o from UTG is too weak to open. King-high with a poor kicker plays poorly out of position against 4 opponents. Standard fold from early position.

I have pocket Kings, the second-best starting hand. I'm in an early position and want to build a pot for value while narrowing the field. A standard 3x open is appropriate here to induce action from weaker hands or big cards while protecting my equity.

Hand 118, position button with 8d 3h, a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suited potential. Flash raised to 300 from UTG+1 after Claude folded UTG, indicating strength. Pot is 450, to call 300 is poor odds for this hand. Late in tournament (118/150), 5 players left, stacks deep relative to blinds (my stack 12k+), no need to speculate with unplayable cards. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 7d, a weak off-suit hand. Flash opened from UTG+1 to 300 (3bb). Facing an early position raise while being out of position with a hand that is easily dominated and plays poorly post-flop, the clear decision is to fold. The pot odds do not justify a call given the reverse implied odds and weak equity against a standard opening range.

Facing a 3x open from Flash in early position (5-handed), 6♢4♠ is too low-equity and, more importantly, has terrible playability out of position (dominated, rarely makes strong top pairs, and struggles to realize equity). While the pot odds (200 to win 650 after calling) are tempting, this hand will under-realize badly versus a relatively strong opening range. A 3-bet bluff is also unattractive with this combo (poor blockers, poor postflop prospects when called). Best EV is to let it go and preserve stack.

I am in the UTG position with 2d 8s, which is an extremely weak starting hand. Given the stack sizes and the early position, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot with such a hand. Folding is the standard and correct play here.

J♥ 5♥ is a marginal suited one-gapper at best. From UTG+1 (early position) in a 5-handed game, facing 3 players to act behind including the button (Pro) who can have wide range, and blinds (FiveTwo and Claude) who defend frequently based on history. Pot is 150 (blinds only), limp-call 100 is weak and invites multiway pots where J5s plays poorly postflop. Raising risks domination by better Jx or suited broadways. Deep stacks (120BB effective), but late tournament (hand 119/150), folding preserves stack for premium spots to maximize EV and ladder up. Standard discipline: fold.

I am on the Button with 2s 5c, which is one of the worst starting hands in hold'em. The action has folded to me. While I have position, raising with such a weak hand is generally not profitable unless the blinds are extremely tight, and even then, it's risky against the large stack in the Big Blind. I have a healthy stack of over 65 big blinds, so there is no need to force action with garbage cards. I will fold.

Folds to me in the small blind, so this is a blind-vs-blind spot where we should be opening very wide to pick up the dead money and deny BB’s equity. Q5o is comfortably inside a standard SB open range heads-up, and raising also gives us initiative on most flops. I’ll use a solid pressure size (3x) rather than just completing, and I’m prepared to fold if Claude responds with a large/strong 3-bet.

82o is one of the worst starting hands in poker. Even getting 2:1 pot odds to call 200 into a 400 pot, this hand has very poor playability and will rarely make strong hands. FiveTwo has been active but I have no equity to defend here profitably.

UTG position with 8s3d offsuit is one of the weakest hands possible. It has poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and is easily dominated. With deep stacks and the need to maximize stack late in the tournament (hand 120/150), folding is mandatory to avoid speculative plays from early position where I'll be out of position postflop against strong ranges. Pot has blinds posted (150 total), no action yet, but limping or raising is -EV.

I am in UTG+1 (Hijack) with 3s 6d, which is a very weak unsuited hand. With 65BB, I am the shortest stack but still deep enough that I should play solid ranges. This hand is far below the opening threshold for this position. I fold.

Button vs blinds 5-handed, A4o is strong enough to open: it has an ace blocker, decent top-pair potential, and plays well in position. Limping gives up initiative and invites multiway; raising pressures the blinds’ wide ranges and allows us to win the pot preflop often. If we face a large 3-bet from either blind, this hand is generally a straightforward fold given dominated-ace issues.

8Ts is a marginal hand but playable against a button open. FiveTwo has been active with steals. Getting decent pot odds (200 to win 400) to see a flop, though I'll be out of position. Worth a call to potentially hit a straight draw or pair.

I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit in the Big Blind. The Button (FiveTwo) opened with a min-plus raise to 250, and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to put in 150 chips to call into a pot of 600, giving me 4:1 odds. T9o is a very reasonable hand to defend in a multi-way pot with these odds. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the pot odds are too good to pass up, and the hand has decent playability on many flop textures.

I completely missed this flop with 8d Ts. Board is Js 6h 4s - I have no pair, no real draws (just a runner-runner straight needing both 7 and 9). With two players behind me and zero equity, checking is the only sensible option. No reason to bluff into a multiway pot with nothing.

I defended my big blind with Ten-Nine offsuit and the flop of Jack-Six-Four rainbow has completely missed my hand. I have no pair and no significant draw. Claude has checked from the small blind, and I should also check to see what the pre-flop aggressor, FiveTwo, decides to do. Leading out here would be unnecessary as a bluff given there are two other players in the pot. I will check and likely fold to any significant bet.

Multiway flop (SB+BB) on Js-6h-4s is a relatively good connect for the blinds’ defending ranges (lots of Jx/6x/4x, spade draws, and pairs). With Ad4c we have a thin made hand (bottom pair) but no meaningful backdoor equity, and a small c-bet will often get called by better or invite an uncomfortable check-raise from draws/strong value. Checking back keeps the pot manageable, realizes our showdown equity in position, and lets us respond to turn action more accurately.

Turn brought a King, still no pair for me. However, I now have a spade flush draw with Ts and two spades on board (Js, 4s). That's roughly 9 outs (~19% equity). The flop checked through, so no one showed strength. Betting here as a semi-bluff could work, but check is safer in a multiway pot - I can see a free river card and potentially hit my flush. No need to build the pot without made hand.

On the turn, the board now reads Jack-Six-Four-King. I still have no pair and no draw with my Ten-Nine. The Small Blind (Claude) has checked again. Given that the pre-flop aggressor (FiveTwo) checked back the flop, the hand is likely weak all around, but I have zero showdown value. However, there is no need to turn my hand into a bluff right now into two opponents when I have no equity. I will check and see what the Button does. If any bet is made, I will fold.

Turn goes check-check to us in a 3-way pot. Our hand (Ad4c) is a very thin made hand (bottom pair) on Js-6h-4s-Kh. The King improves a meaningful chunk of the blinds’ ranges (Kx floats/defends, plus they still have plenty of Jx/6x/4x, pairs, and spade draws). Betting here mostly gets called by better and folds out worse hands we already beat, and it also risks getting check-raised off our modest showdown value. Checking back maximizes realization and keeps the pot manageable in position, letting us re-evaluate on the river if they continue to show weakness.

Missed my flush draw - river brought 5h instead of a spade. I have 10-high with no made hand. Everyone checked flop and turn showing weakness, but in a 3-way pot someone likely has at least a small pair. Pot is only 750 - not worth risking chips on a bluff with complete air. Check and hope for a miracle showdown.

The river has completed the board as Jack-Six-Four-King-Five. I am holding Ten-Nine offsuit, which is simply Ten-high with no showdown value. The action has been checked all the way through the flop and turn, and the Small Blind (Claude) has just checked to me on the river. While a bluff might occasionally push off a better hand like Ace-high or a small pair, the pot is relatively small (750) and there are still two opponents in the hand. Turning my hand into a bluff here is risky given that FiveTwo (the pre-flop aggressor) has yet to act. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, or more likely, fold if a significant bet is made behind me.

River goes check-check to us in a 3-way pot on Js-6h-4s-Kh-5h and we hold Ad4c (pair of 4s, A kicker). This is a marginal showdown hand: it beats all missed spade draws and pure overcard floats, but loses to any Jx/Kx, most 6x that improved to two pair with 65, and a lot of 5x/4x two-pair combos (54) that the blinds can easily have.

Betting here is a classic thin/value-block attempt, but multiway it’s hard to get called by worse often enough: most hands worse than a pair (A-high/missed spades) fold, while the hands that continue are frequently better pairs/two pair. Since both opponents checked, we maximize EV by taking the free showdown rather than isolating ourselves versus stronger calling ranges or inviting a check-raise. If we happen to be best, we win the pot as-is; if not, we avoid torching chips on a low-confidence value bet.

I am in UTG position with King-Three offsuit (Kd 3h). This is a statistically weak hand with poor playability, especially from early position in a 5-handed game. With 65 big blinds, I have plenty of chips and should not be opening wide from UTG to avoid bleeding chips or getting into difficult post-flop spots out of position against deeper stacks. The standard and correct play is to fold.

5-handed from UTG+1 we can open fairly wide, but J7o is near the bottom and plays poorly out of position versus multiple opponents. The players behind (Claude/Flash/Elon) have shown willingness to 3-bet, which makes a light open likely to either lose 250 pre or get put in tough postflop spots when called. Limping isn’t attractive either with no initiative and the blinds able to isolate. Best EV is to release and preserve stack for stronger opens/spots.
J3o on the button with only blinds left to act. This is a marginal hand but a good steal spot with position. I have a healthy stack at 17750. Standard 2.5x raise to try to take down the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce offsuit (Q2o). Claude has raised from the Button to 250. While Claude's range on the button is likely wide, Q2o is a very weak hand to defend out of position. Calling would put me in a difficult spot post-flop without the lead or position, and raising as a bluff is unnecessary given my healthy stack and the late stage of the tournament. The most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a more profitable situation.

Facing a button raise to 250 after two folds preflop in the big blind with Qc3c. Button ranges are wide, including many suited connectors and broadway cards that my suited queen has good equity against (~38% vs typical button open). Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 into 400 (2.67:1), and with deep stacks (effective ~11k), implied odds are strong if I hit flush/straight draws or top pair. Out of position but standard BB defense spot. Folding too tight late tournament; 3-betting marginal without blockers. Call to realize equity and control pot size.

Flopped the nut flush draw (Qc highest club) with Qc3c on 9c-2s-6c board, giving me 9 outs to nuts (35-40% equity vs typical button c-bet range). Also backdoor straight possibilities minor. OOP in BB vs button open, heads-up, pot 550, deep effective stacks (~11k). Standard to check first to pot control, induce wide c-bet from Claude's polarized range (high equity realization). If he bets small, raise for value/protection; if large/donky, call and play streets aggressively. Leading risks getting check-raised and playing OOP post-flop awkwardly with draw; checking maximizes fold equity on raise and implied odds if hits. Folding impossible with this strength late tournament.

Flop is 9c 2s 6c - completely missed my J3o. However, I raised preflop and have position. This is a good spot for a continuation bet as the board is dry and doesn't connect well with BB calling ranges. A small c-bet around 1/3 pot can take this down often.

Nut flush draw on a paired flush draw board (9c-2s-6c), giving me 9 immediate outs to the nuts (~35% equity vs Claude's button c-bet range, which includes overpairs, top pair, weaker draws, bluffs, and floats). OOP after defending wide preflop call, but his small 200 bet into 550 pot (~1/3 pot) screams polarization: either very strong (sets, two-pair unlikely) or weak (missed broadway/high cards). Pot odds excellent (750:200 or 3.75:1, need ~21%), plus massive implied odds (~11k effective stacks) if I hit, and fold equity on raise as he can't call wide. Checking/call risks giving free cards to overcards/straights, multiway villain equity. Raising semi-bluffs standard: builds pot when ahead, pressures his wide range (he's folded flops/turns to aggression in history like H9, H11, H38), denies equity to his draws/overs. Size to ~3.5x his bet (700 total) balances value from worse (KdQd type draws, pairs like 77/88/T7) and bluffs while keeping stacks playable postflop. Shoving too strong/overplays equity OOP deep; flat risks check-back turns. Late tourney (121/150), aggressive to maximize stack with premium draw.

Elon check-raised my c-bet from 200 to 700. I have J3o which completely missed this 9c 2s 6c board - no pair, no draws. Against a check-raise, my J-high has virtually no equity. Elon likely has a 9, a 6, a set, or a club draw. Easy fold with complete air facing aggression.

5-handed from UTG we can open wider than full ring, and 9d5d is a playable suited hand that benefits from fold equity plus decent postflop potential (flushes, some straight/2p runouts) when called. Limping invites isolates and multiway pots; folding is fine but too tight given table size and stacks. I’ll take the initiative and open a standard size that pressures blinds while keeping the pot manageable if we face a 3-bet.
7s3d is a junk hand with no playability. Facing a UTG raise with this trash, folding is the clear play.

I am on the Button with Ace-Four offsuit. FiveTwo (UTG) has raised to 250. Although FiveTwo has been active and raising frequently, an UTG raise in a five-handed game (essentially the Cutoff position) still represents a range that generally dominates A4o. Calling leaves me vulnerable to being dominated if an Ace hits or being squeezed by the blinds, particularly the short-stacked Pro in the Big Blind. Given the tournament stage (Hand 122 of 150) and my healthy stack size, there is no need to play a marginal hand out of position or in a bloated pot against an early position raiser.

Qs3h is a complete trash hand with no high card strength, suitedness, or playability. Facing a UTG raise from FiveTwo in a 5-handed game, who often raises but from early position likely has a strong range. Calling 200 into a 400 pot OOP would be a mistake as postflop playability is poor and I'll be out of position. Folding preserves my above-average stack (12.5k) for better spots with 28 hands left in the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with 6d 7h, facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG. The Small Blind has folded. Pot odds are 150 to call into a pot of 400, requiring about 27% equity. While my raw equity against a typical UTG opening range is around 30-32%, my realization of equity will be poor playing out of position with a weak offsuit connector against a strong range. 6d 7h is generally a fold versus an Early Position open due to reverse implied odds and difficulty in realizing equity. Folding preserves my stack of 6475 (approx 65 BBs) for better spots.
32 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker, and I'm in UTG position with 4 players behind me. Clear fold.

I am in UTG+1 with Jack-Seven offsuit (J7o). Claude has already folded from UTG. J7o is a weak hand that does not meet the standards for an open-raise from this position in a 5-handed game. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a more profitable situation.

On the button with two folds ahead (Claude UTG and Flash UTG+1), I am first in with Ah6d. Ace-high hand with position, good blocker to top pair, and decent playability postflop. Blinds are loose/passive based on history (e.g., FiveTwo defends wide, Pro limps/calls often). Raising steals blinds frequently or builds pot in position. Standard open-raise size 2.5BB to 250. Pot 150, to call 100, but raising for fold equity and initiative. Deep stacks allow flexibility. Folding too tight late tournament; goal to accumulate chips.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd Td (suited connectors/broadway), facing a Button raise from Elon to 250. My stack is roughly 64 BBs. JTs is a strong hand that plays well post-flop but plays poorly out of position if the pot gets too large pre-flop. A 3-bet is a viable option to take the initiative, but calling is a lower-variance standard play that keeps the Button's range wide and offers good pot odds (calling 200 into a pot of 400+). I will call to see a flop, wary of my positional disadvantage.

KQo is a strong blind-defense hand versus a Button open, and the Small Blind cold-call adds dead money plus caps their range toward weaker hands. Squeezing here maximizes fold equity, often wins the pot preflop, and when called we play with initiative and good broadway coverage. With stacks deep enough, I’ll 3-bet to a larger sizing due to the caller to isolate and deny odds.

Preflop on the button with Ah6o after opening to 250. Pro flatted from SB, showing looseness. FiveTwo 3-bet to 1100 from BB, which is sizable but they defend/3-bet wide based on history (frequent aggression, calls with marginal hands). Pot 1600, calling 850 gives ~1.9:1 odds. Ah6o has decent blocker to AA/AK, live cards, and plays well postflop in position against both opponents. Deep stacks (mine 12k+), late in tournament (hand 123/150), position allows realizing equity effectively – can play flops profitably, especially vs BB range which includes bluffs/broadways. Folding ace-high OOP would be too tight; 4-betting light risky without read on fold equity. Calling keeps pot manageable, initiative somewhat, and exploits position to maximize stack growth.

I initially called the 250 raise, but the Big Blind (FiveTwo) has squeezed to 1100 and the initial raiser (Elon) has called. While JTs is a hand that plays well post-flop, playing a large 3-bet pot out of position against two opponents is highly disadvantageous. My hand is frequently dominated by the Big Blind's squeezing range (which likely includes JJ+, ATs+, KJs+, AQo+), and my equity realization will be poor from the Small Blind. The price of calling 850 more chips is tempting (getting roughly 3:1), but the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a negative expected value spot. Preserving the 6225 stack is preferable to bleeding chips in a marginal spot.

As the preflop aggressor in a 3-bet pot, this K♦-T♠-4♦ flop is very good for my range (lots of strong Kx/overpairs) and I’ve connected with top pair (K) plus a strong kicker (Q). Elon’s call range contains many worse hands that can continue (KJ/KQ/KT, TT-55, diamond draws, QJ/J9 type floats). I want value and to deny equity to overcards/flush+gutters, while keeping worse hands in and not bloating the pot unnecessarily with a non-nut one-pair hand. A small-to-medium c-bet accomplishes this efficiently.

Heads-up on the flop vs FiveTwo (BB 3-bettor) with position. Board Kd Ts 4d hits their 3-bet range hard (kings, broadways, Ax diamonds). Ah6d is ace-high with Ad blocker but no pair/draw, backdoor diamond flush/possible str8 outs. Pot 3300, donk bet 850 (~25% pot) is small, consistent with value or probe; FiveTwo aggressive history but folds to resistance often (e.g., Hand 2 river fold, Hand 31). Pot odds 3.8:1 direct (need ~21% equity), better implied with position/IP realization. Equity vs polarized range ~30-40% (blocks AK/AA, live A). Calling keeps pot small, exploits position to fold weak turns or barrel if improves/fold equity later. Raising risks vs value (sets up jam turns), folding too tight late (hand 123/150, stacks deep, accumulate chips). Plan: call, c/c weak turns, bet missed or raise strong.

Turn brings the 3rd diamond, which is a meaningful range-shift: a chunk of Elon’s flop continuing range (A♦x♦, Q♦J♦, J♦9♦, etc.) just got there, and betting KQ for “thin value/protection” risks getting called mostly by better (flushes/sets/two pair) while folding out many worse one-pair hands that now hate the texture.

With SPR ~2, pot control matters a lot with a non-nut one-pair hand. Checking keeps Elon’s bluffs and worse hands in (including hands with a single diamond that may stab), and my Q♦ provides a useful blocker to many Q-high flush combinations, improving my check/call prospects versus reasonable sizing.

Heads-up on turn vs FiveTwo (aggressive BB 3-bettor) with position. Board Ts4d Kd8d is draw-heavy with flush completed on turn diamond; villain's flop c-bet range (value: Kx, sets, two-pair; bluffs: suited connectors/Ax duds) polarized, and check suggests weakness (missed draw, air, or trap with monster). Ah6d is ace-high bluff-catcher with Ad blocker but no pair/draw, ~25-35% equity vs range. Pot 4150, deep effective stacks (~10k), late tournament (123/150). Betting as bluff risks villain's calling station tendencies (history of calling big rivers/stacks off); thin value unlikely. Checking controls pot, takes free river card (realizes equity passively, exploits position), avoids bloating pot OOP-ish (villain first but checked), sets up check-raise or hero-call river if needed. Consistent with flop call plan: c/c weak boards, accumulate chips without variance. Fold equity low on scary board; prefer showdown value.

River is a relative blank after the 3rd diamond hit on the turn and Elon checked back. That turn check-back makes it less likely he has a flush (many flushes value-bet turn at this SPR), while his range still contains plenty of worse Kx (KJ/KT/K9s), some Tx, and occasional underpairs that may bluff-catch small. With K♥Q♦ we have top pair/top kicker and the Q♦ is a useful blocker to some Q-high flushes, so a thin value bet is preferred over checking and letting him realize a free showdown with hands that would have paid something. I’ll keep the sizing small to get called by worse Kx and avoid bloating the pot on a 3-diamond board; if raised, I can comfortably fold this one-pair hand.

River facing 1250 bet into 5400 pot (pot odds ~4:1, need ~19% equity). Ah6d is ace-high bluff-catcher with Ad blocker on diamond flush board. FiveTwo's range from BB 3-bet pre, flop lead on KdTs4d (hits value), check turn after 8d flush completion (weakness/polarization), river lead on blank 3s suggests thin value (weak Kx, pairs), nuts (flushes, sets), or bluffs (missed diamond draws like J9d/QJd, str8 draws, air). History shows FiveTwo aggressive wide but folds to resistance sometimes; checked turn likely caps strength. Equity vs polarized betting range ~30-40% (beats bluffs/missed draws, loses to value). Late tournament (123/150), deep stacks, calling realizes EV without bloating pot further. Folding ace-high too tight vs aggro range; consistent with prior calls for position/equity. Call to accumulate chips exploiting wide lines.
I am UTG with Q5s. In a 5-handed game, this hand is too weak to open-raise from the first position. Folding is the standard play here to conserve my stack and wait for better opportunities.

5-handed late in tournament with deep stacks (~90BB). ATo offsuit is a standard opening range from UTG+1: strong ace blocker, decent playability postflop, dominates weaker aces and broadways. Players have been aggressive historically, so raising builds the pot and takes initiative. Standard size 2.5x BB to 250, avoiding min-raise which invites multiway pots.

I am holding 8h 2c on the Button, which is statistically one of the weakest hands in poker. Facing a raise to 250 from Elon in UTG+1, I have no equity to call and no blockers or playability to justify a 3-bet bluff. The standard and only reasonable play is to fold immediately to preserve my stack of 62.25 BBs.

Elon has opened to 2.5x from early position and we’re in the small blind with Q7o, which is heavily dominated by his continuing range (better queens, broadways, suited connectors that outplay us). Calling creates an awkward, low-realization spot OOP with the BB still behind, and turning this hand into a 3-bet bluff is too loose versus an EP open given our poor blockers and playability. Best EV is to fold and preserve stack for better spots.

Q5o in the BB facing a UTG+1 raise. Getting 2.67:1 pot odds (call 150 to win 400), but Q5o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Elon has shown aggression throughout the tournament.
