This hand has poor playability post-flop - I'll often be dominated when I hit a queen, and five-high is rarely useful. Standard fold.

UTG with 5d3s, a trash hand with no connectivity, suitedness, or high card strength. Early position requires a tight range. Pot is 150 from blinds, no value in calling or raising. Folding preserves my deep stack (94BB) for better spots in the late stages of the tournament (hand 125/150). Opponent tendencies from history don't justify speculative play here.

I hold a premium hand (AKo) in UTG+1 (effectively Hijack 5-handed). With 62 big blinds, I am the shortest stack but still deep enough to play standard power poker. My goal is to build a pot for value and deny equity to random hands in the blinds. A standard open raise to 2.5bb (250) is appropriate here, consistent with table dynamics and allowing me to play comfortably against the deeper stacks behind me.

Pro is opening from UTG+1 (5-handed), which is still a relatively strong range. 32s is at the very bottom of defendable hands: it has poor high-card value, frequent domination when we make a small pair, and no useful blockers for a profitable 3-bet bluff. Flat-calling also invites a squeeze from the blinds and puts us in marginal multiway spots. Best EV is to just let it go and preserve stack for better steal/3-bet opportunities.

Ac7h in SB facing UTG+1 open to 250. A7o is marginal but playable. Getting 2:1 odds to call 200 into 400 pot. Not strong enough to 3-bet against early position raise, but Ace-high has enough playability to see a flop. Calling is the balanced play here.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 4d. Pro raised to 250 from UTG+1, and Claude called from the Small Blind. Although I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call (150 more to win a total pot of 600), 84o is a very weak hand that performs poorly multi-way and out of position. Given that I have a healthy stack and we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, there is no need to defend with such a weak range against an early-position raiser. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I have Ac7h on Jh Kc 4h board. Completely missed - just ace high with a weak flush draw (7h). The board hits UTG+1's raising range well (KJ, KQ, AK, JJ, etc.). With no made hand and only a weak draw, checking is clearly correct. No reason to bluff into a player who likely connected with this board.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AhKd) on a King-high board with a flush draw (Jh Kc 4h). This is a strong value hand. The board texture is somewhat wet (flush draw, straight connectors), so checking back gives a free card to draws which isn't ideal. I want to extract value from worse Kings (KQ, KT), Jacks (QJ, JTs), and flush draws. My Ah blocks the nut flush draw, but there are plenty of other draws. A continuation bet is standard here to build the pot and deny equity. A sizing of around 40% of the pot (250 into 600) is sufficient to get value from weaker hands and charge draws without overcommitting if I get check-raised.

Pro bet small (250 into 850) which is a 30% pot bet. I have Ac7h giving me a flush draw with the 7h on a two-heart board. Getting 4.4:1 odds to call 250. With ~35% equity to hit the flush by river, this is an easy call. The small bet sizing often indicates a range bet or marginal hand. Calling to chase the flush draw with implied odds.

Turn brought 7c, giving me middle pair of 7s with ace kicker. I also picked up the nut flush draw in clubs (Ac with Kc 7c on board). I have a decent made hand + strong draw combo. Pro's small flop bet (250 into 500) often indicates a range bet or marginal hand rather than a strong holding. With my improved equity and position to act first, I should lead out for value/protection. Betting ~40% pot to charge draws and get value from worse while building pot for when I hit flush.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) on a J-K-4-7 board. After check-calling the flop, the opponent (Claude) leads out (donk bets) on the turn for 450 into a pot of 1100. This small lead often indicates a blocking bet with a marginal hand (like a weaker King, a Jack) or a semi-bluff with a draw. Raising here would likely fold out worse hands that I dominate and isolate me against better hands like sets or two pair. Since I am in position, calling is superior as it keeps the opponent's range wide, keeps bluffs in play, and controls the pot size with a one-pair hand. My Ah also blocks the nut flush draw, slightly reducing the combos of strong draws he might be aggressive with.

River brought 5h completing the heart flush. I have Ac7h - just a pair of 7s with ace kicker. I don't have a flush (only 4 hearts total). My pair of 7s is a weak showdown hand on a K-J high board. Betting for value would only get called by better hands (Kx, Jx, flushes, two pair). Checking is correct - I can potentially win at showdown against missed draws or worse pairs, but I shouldn't build the pot with this marginal holding.

On the river, the board is [Jh Kc 4h 7c 5h], completing the heart flush draw. I hold [Ah Kd] (Top Pair, Top Kicker) and the Ace of Hearts, which blocks the nut flush. Claude checks to me after leading the turn. His line (check-call flop, donk-lead turn, check river) is often indicative of a marginal made hand (like a weak King or Jack) or a missed straight draw, though the flush completion is concerning. Betting here is thin value; worse hands like KQ or KJ might fold given the scary board, and better hands (flushes, two pair, sets) will call or check-raise. Since I have good showdown value but am vulnerable to the completed flush, the prudent play is to check back and realize my equity without risking more chips.

I am in UTG position holding Queen-Four offsuit (Qc 4d). This is a very weak hand with poor playability due to being unconnected and unsuited with a low kicker. Given my position and the fact that there are 4 players left to act behind me, opening this hand would be a losing play in the long run. My stack is healthy at around 72 big blinds, so there is no pressure to make desperate moves. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

5-handed from UTG+1, 8♠5♣ offsuit is too weak to profitably limp or open-raise. Limping invites isolation from the Button/SB with position, and raising builds a pot with a hand that will be dominated and realize equity poorly. Preserve stack and wait for better spots.

I have 9c6h on the button with it folded to me. This is a good spot to steal the blinds. While 96o isn't a premium hand, being on the button with only the blinds left to act makes this a standard steal attempt. I'll raise to 250 (2.5x) to try to take down the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s5d. Claude has raised to 250 from the Button. While Button raises are often wide, 9-5 offsuit is too weak to defend out of position, especially with another player (Elon) still to act behind me in the Big Blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

KsJs is a premium suited broadway hand with excellent playability, high equity against a button raising range which is likely wide given the position and folds before. Facing an isolation raise from Claude on the button after two folds, there's good fold equity on a 3-bet. Effective stacks are deep (93BB), but 3-betting takes initiative, builds the pot for value, and denies equity to worse hands like Ax suited or broadway offsuit that Claude might fold. Calling keeps the pot small but leaves me OOP post-flop acting first. Raising to 3x the initial raise (750 total) is standard sizing here for value and semi-bluff potential.

I have 9c6h - a weak hand that I opened from the button as a steal attempt. Elon 3-bet to 750, and I need to call 500 more. With 96o, I have very poor playability against a 3-bet range. Even with position, this hand doesn't have the equity or playability to continue profitably. Easy fold.

5-handed UTG, 9♠6♣ offsuit is too weak to open profitably from the worst position: it’s dominated often, realizes equity poorly, and we face a decent amount of isolation/3-betting from the remaining aggressive stacks (Flash/Elon/Claude). Limp-calling is also a low-EV line with this hand. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better spots.
72 offsuit in early position - this is the worst starting hand in poker. Easy fold.

I am on the button with Q9o. FiveTwo and Claude have folded, leaving only the blinds to act. In a 5-handed game, opening around 40-50% of hands from the button is standard strategy. Q9o is a solid hand to open for a steal or to play in position post-flop. I will raise to 2.5x the big blind to apply pressure on Elon and Pro.

Pocket eights is a premium hand, roughly top 6% of hands, strong enough to 3-bet facing a button open after two folds. Flash's button range is wide (likely 30-50% including suited connectors, Ax, broadway, pairs). Early folders suggest isolation opportunity. 3-betting builds the pot, denies equity to BB, and takes initiative OOP. Calling risks multiway pot which is bad for set-mining OOP. Size to ~3.2x the open (800 total) is standard for deep stacks, balancing value and fold equity. My stack ~97BB allows flexibility post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind facing a button raise to 250 and a 3-bet from the Small Blind to 800. My hand is Ten-Five suited (Tc 5c). This is a very weak hand to be calling a 3-bet with, especially cold. The pot odds are roughly 38%, but I am out of position against the aggressor, and the original raiser (Flash) is still to act behind me and could 4-bet. Elon's 3-betting range from the Small Blind against a Button open is likely strong, dominating my hand. Folding is the only standard and profitable play here to preserve my stack of ~72 big blinds.

I opened from the button with Q9o, a standard range-merging steal attempt in a 5-handed game. Elon has 3-bet from the small blind. While I would have position post-flop, Q9o is quite weak against a 3-betting range from the blinds, which often includes hands that dominate me like AQ, KQ, or pocket pairs. Given the tournament stage and my healthy stack, I prefer to fold and look for a better spot rather than calling 5.5 extra big blinds with a dominated marginal hand.
KJs is a strong hand in UTG position. Standard open raise to 250 (2.5x BB) is appropriate. This hand has good playability with both straight and flush potential.

Claude has raised from UTG, which typically indicates a strong range. My hand, King of Clubs and Four of Spades, is quite weak and does not play well post-flop, especially out of position against an UTG opener. With several players still to act behind me, the most prudent move is to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand.

AKo is a premium hand that plays well multiway or heads-up, and being on the button gives me position on Claude's UTG raise and anyone else who calls. Claude's UTG open range is strong but AK dominates much of it (e.g., AQ, AJ, KQ, pairs below KK). SB and BB stacks are deep enough, but I want to charge them and thin the field. 3-betting to 800 (about 3.2x the raise) builds the pot for value while maintaining fold equity. Folding or calling is too weak; this is a clear 3-bet spot late in the tournament to maximize stack growth.

I hold Queen of Diamonds and Trey of Spades in the Small Blind. The action facing me is a raise from UTG (Claude) to 250 and a 3-bet from the Button (Elon) to 800. My hand is extremely weak (unsuited, disconnected, low kicker). Facing significant strength from two opponents and being out of position, I have no equity to justify calling a 3-bet. Pot odds are insufficient for a hand this weak. The correct play is to fold immediately.

We’re in the big blind facing an UTG open and a Button 3-bet. Js3c is far too weak to continue: it’s dominated by virtually all of both players’ continuing ranges, plays very poorly out of position in a bloated pot, and we can’t reliably realize the ~37% raw pot odds needed (700 to win ~1900 after calling) because our equity vs a strong 3-bet range is much lower and we’ll frequently face tough postflop decisions or additional pressure (UTG can still 4-bet/call behind). Preserving stack and avoiding negative-EV defense is best here.

KJs is a strong hand that plays well postflop with straight and flush potential. Facing a button 3-bet to 800, I need to call 550 into a pot of 1750 (after my call) - about 3.2:1 odds. KJs has good equity against a button 3-betting range and playability. Folding is too tight, 4-betting turns this into a bluff. Calling and playing postflop is the standard approach.

Completely whiffed this flop with KJs. Board is Ac 5s 2c - no pair, no flush draw (wrong suit), no straight draw. Elon 3-bet from the button and this Ace-high board connects well with his 3-betting range. Betting would be a pure bluff with almost no equity. Check and see what he does - if he bets I'll likely fold.

Flop Ac5s2c hits my AK top pair top kicker perfectly, giving me the nuts or close to it against much of Claude's UTG calling range (e.g., AQ/AJ, pocket pairs). The board has flush draw (clubs) and gutshot straight draws (43,64), so betting protects against those while extracting value from worse aces, sets, or draws. Claude's check suggests weakness or a trap, but with position and initiative from my 3-bet, a pot-sized bet (half-pot to 2/3 works, but ~1100 denies equity and builds pot late in tournament). Check risks giving free cards to draws; folding impossible. Bet sizing to ~1100 (about 2/3 pot) balances value/protection/fold equity.

Completely missed this flop with KJs. The board is Ac 5s 2c - I have no pair, no flush draw (wrong suits), and no straight draw. Elon 3-bet preflop and this Ace-high board heavily favors his 3-betting range which contains many Ax hands. He's betting 1100 into ~1750, showing strength. I have almost no equity here - just hoping for runner-runner or K/J that may not even be good. Easy fold.

I am UTG with Jack of Diamonds and Seven of Clubs (J7o). This is a weak offsuit hand and does not meet the requirements for an opening range from an early position, even in a five-handed game. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are still 21 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no reason to risk chips on a suboptimal hand in the worst position at the table. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop, UTG+1 in 5-handed game with A2o (offsuit ace-deuce). This is a weak hand with poor playability, dominated by any other ace, and not suited or connected. Standard play is to fold from early position to avoid tough spots postflop. Stacks are deep (110BB effective), tournament nearing end (hand 129/150), so preserve stack for premium hands rather than speculative calls or raises. No reads justify entering with this trash.

I am on the Button with 4h 8s, which is an extremely weak unsuited hand (8-high). Although I have position, the players in the blinds (FiveTwo and Claude) have large stacks (over 130BB and 150BB respectively) compared to my 71BB stack, making them likely to defend or apply pressure. Attempting to steal with such poor holdings is negative EV. The standard play is to fold.

Blind-vs-blind with the button folded, ranges are very wide and initiative matters a lot. QTo is comfortably ahead of a BB random holding, has decent playability, and raising pressures Claude to fold a meaningful chunk while also letting us realize equity better when called. I prefer a slightly larger than min-raise sizing to reduce his immediate pot odds and simplify postflop.

T8 suited is a solid hand to defend from the BB against a SB raise. I'm getting 2:1 pot odds (200 to win 400), and suited connectors play well postflop with flush and straight potential. FiveTwo has been active, so calling is the standard play here. No need to 3-bet but definitely worth continuing.

This is a dry K-high board (K♣4♦3♠) where I retain a significant range advantage after raising blind-vs-blind. Claude’s BB defend is wide and contains a lot of hands that simply miss (random broadways, suited connectors that didn’t connect, etc.). With Q♦T♥ I have little showdown value but do have some equity (two overs to the low cards + backdoor diamond potential), so a small c-bet is a good way to fold out his air/weak floats cheaply while keeping my risk low versus his Kx/sets. I’ll use a small 1/3-pot sizing to apply pressure efficiently and not bloat the pot when called.

I completely missed this flop with Tc8c. Board is 4d Kc 3s - I have no pair, only a backdoor flush draw (one club on board), and no straight draw. My hand has virtually no equity here. Even though FiveTwo's c-bet is small (200 into 600), floating with ten-high and minimal draw potential is just burning chips. Easy fold.

UTG position in a 5-handed game with As7c offsuit. While short-handed play loosens opening ranges, A7o is still marginal from early position due to poor post-flop playability, weak kicker, and vulnerability to domination by stronger aces. Preflop pot is 150 with 100 to call (standard blinds), but limping is weak and raising risks tough spots OOP with a speculative hand. Late in tournament (hand 130/150), prioritize strong hands to maximize stack without unnecessary variance. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Cutoff (UTG+1 in a 5-handed game) with Jack-Five offsuit (5h Js). This is a very weak hand with poor playability and equity. Although I have a reasonable stack depth of roughly 71 big blinds, there is no strategic justification for opening the pot with this holding from this position. The standard and correct play is to fold immediately.

Folded to the button 5-handed, we should open a very wide range to pressure the blinds. 9♠6♣ is not a premium, but it’s within a reasonable BTN steal/open, especially with a small sizing that risks less and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: take it down pre often; if called, play mostly as a low-equity one-pair/straight-draw type hand; versus a large 3-bet from the blinds, comfortably fold.

98o in the SB facing a button min-raise to 200. Getting good odds (350:150) to call with a hand that has decent playability and straight potential. Not strong enough to 3-bet but too good to fold at this price against a wide button opening range.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. FiveTwo raised to 200 from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. It is 100 more for me to call into a pot of 500. While the pot odds are 5-to-1, Ten-Deuce offsuit is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability. I will be out of position against two opponents for the rest of the hand, making it difficult to realize any equity. Even if I hit a Ten, I am likely outkicked by their ranges. Given I have a healthy stack and want to maximize my position in the final 20 hands of the tournament, I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

I completely missed this flop with 98o. Board is Kd Tc 4c - I have no pair, no flush draw (only one club), and at best a weak gutshot possibility. Against a button opener who could have connected with this K-high board, betting as a bluff would be risky. Checking to see a free turn is the best line here.

On K♦T♣4♣ this board connects pretty well with a SB flatting range (Kx, Tx, pocket pairs, QJ/J9, club draws), and 9♠6♣ has essentially no immediate equity (no pair, no real straight draw—only weak backdoors). A small c-bet would get floated too often, leaving us in awkward turn/river spots versus a sticky check-call range. Checking back keeps the pot controlled, realizes our small backdoor equity, and lets us take a delayed stab on favorable turns (7/8/club/A) or when Claude shows repeated weakness.

I still have nothing - 98o with no pair, no draw on Kd Tc 4c 2h. FiveTwo checked behind on the flop showing potential weakness, but I have zero equity besides maybe catching a 9 or 8. Bluffing here is risky since FiveTwo's flop check could mean they're trapping with a K or have a mid-strength hand. Best to check and see a free river.

Claude’s line (SB flat pre, check flop, check turn) is heavily weighted toward medium/weak one-pair and some give-ups. After checking back flop, my range still credibly contains plenty of Kx that wants to start getting value/protection on the turn, so a delayed c-bet is believable. With 9♠6♣ I have essentially no showdown value and very little river equity, so checking again just surrenders too often. I’ll apply pressure with a medium sizing that can fold out his air/A-high and some pocket pairs, and if called I’ll mostly shut down unless a strong scare card arrives and his river action stays passive.

I have 98o with no pair, no flush draw (only 8c), and no straight draw on Kd Tc 4c 2h. FiveTwo checked flop but is now betting 325 into 500 on the turn. I need 28% equity to call but I only have about 13% (6 outs to hit a 9 or 8, which would still be weak pairs). No reason to continue with complete air and insufficient odds.

I am in UTG position with 2c 5c, which is a very weak hand (low suited gapper). At this stack depth (71 BB) and with four players left to act, opening this hand is loose and unprofitable in the long run. The standard play is to fold and wait for a better starting hand or a better position to make a move, especially in the late stage of the tournament (Hand 131/150).

We’re 5-handed but still in an early seat (UTG+1) with a very weak offsuit hand that plays poorly postflop (dominated when we make a pair, few strong runouts, and hard to realize equity out of position versus the blinds). With stacks still relatively deep, there’s no need to invest 100 as a limp or open-raise this combo; we preserve chips for better steal and 3-bet spots.
Ah9h on the button is a solid hand to raise for value. Suited ace with decent kicker, in position, with 2 players already folded. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am in the Small Blind holding King-Jack offsuit (KJo). Claude has opened with a 2.5x raise from the button. KJo is a strong hand against a wide button opening range. While I could 3-bet to take the initiative, calling is a solid play that allows me to see a flop and manage the pot size while out of position. I am getting 3:1 odds on the call (200 more to win a total pot that will be 600 if the BB folds, or 750 if the BB calls). I will call and evaluate the flop.

Preflop in BB facing button raise to 250 after two folds, with SB calling 200. Pot is 600, calling 150 more for total 250 committed. Excellent pot odds (~4:1 immediate, plus implied with deep stacks). Ac3o has decent equity against Claude's wide button raising range (steal-heavy based on history) and Flash's calling range from SB (speculative hands). Multiway pot possible, but Ace blocker helps vs AA/AK ranges, and 3h adds some flush potential. Deep stacks (100+BB effective) allow for postflop playability; can check-fold weak flops or continue profitably with top pair/top kicker scenarios. Folding too tight late in tournament; 3-betting marginal with offsuit weak kicker. Call to realize equity and control pot size OOP.

The flop of 8c-6h-Th is fairly coordinated and likely hits the ranges of both the pre-flop raiser (Claude) and the big blind (Elon), who defended. I have two overcards (King and Jack) which provide some equity, but no made hand or significant draw on this texture. Being out of position against two opponents, the best play is to check and see how the action develops. If there is a small bet, I may consider calling to see a turn card, but I will likely fold to significant aggression.

Flop of 8c-6h-Th completely misses my Ac3h. I have ace-high with negligible equity multiway against Claude's wide button opening range (which includes many overcards, pairs, and draws here) and Flash's speculative calling range. No pair, no straight draw, only weak backdoor flush/straight potential. Pot is 750, stacks deep (~100BB+), but betting would be a pure bluff without fold equity justification OOP after SB checks. Checking realizes equity thin (Claude may fire continuation bets we can fold to) while controlling pot size and avoiding bloating with air. Folding irrelevant since to-call is 0. Late tournament: preserve stack for better spots with 20 hands left.

I have Ah9h on 8c6hTh - nut flush draw with 9 clean outs plus ace overcard. Both opponents checked, giving me a great semi-bluff opportunity. Betting for value and to build the pot with my strong draw. ~60% pot bet should generate fold equity while building pot for when I hit.

On a coordinated board of 8c-6h-Th, my King-Jack offsuit has missed completely. While I have two overcards, this board texture hits both the button's opening range and the big blind's defending range very well. Claude's bet of 450 into two opponents suggests a made hand or a strong draw. With Elon still to act behind me and being out of position, calling with just King-high is too risky given the likelihood that one of my opponents has connected with the board. I will fold and preserve my stack.

Facing a 450 bet into 750 on a flop of 8c6hTh, pot now 1200 with ~27% equity needed to call. Ac3h is pure ace-high with zero made hand, no straight draw, and only backdoor flush/straight potential. Claude's button c-bet range is wide but strong enough on this coordinated board (possible pairs, two overs, flush/straight draws) that my equity is crushed OOP after checking to him. Flash folded, so heads-up now. No fold equity for a raise without position or leverage. Late tournament (131/150), deep stacks (~100BB), but preserve for better spots rather than spew chips calling air or bluff-raising into aggressor. Standard fold to realize 0 equity correctly.

5-handed but still first to act, 9♣5♣ is near the bottom of a profitable UTG opening range. It plays poorly out of position versus an aggressive lineup that has shown a lot of 3-betting (especially from the Button/SB) and will often put us in tough, low-realization spots. With deep stacks and no need to gamble, I’ll pass and preserve chips for higher-EV opens.

8Tc in UTG+1 is a marginal hand. With 152 BBs effective, I have flexibility. This hand has some straight potential and playability. I'll make a standard open raise to 250 to try to take down the blinds or play in position against callers. Folding is also reasonable but I've been opening fairly wide and getting good results.

Claude has raised to 250 from UTG+1 in a five-handed game. My hand, Queen-Trey offsuit on the Button, is far too weak to defend against an early-position open, even with the positional advantage. I will fold and wait for a more profitable situation.

Hand #132 pre-flop, Small Blind with Kc2d offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and dominated by any raising range containing a King. Claude raised to 250 from UTG+1 after FiveTwo folded, indicating a strong range (top ~15-20% likely, pairs 66+, broadway, suited aces, etc.). Calling 200 OOP gives poor implied odds due to reverse position and hand's lack of equity. Raising as a bluff is suboptimal with poor blockers, no fold equity against an early position raiser, and deep stacks where villain can continue profitably. Pot 400 post-call wouldn't justify it. Folding preserves stack late in tournament (132/150 hands, 107BB effective), waiting for better spots to maximize EV. History shows Claude plays aggressively post-flop when raising early, no need to speculate.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 2s, facing a raise to 250 from Claude in UTG+1. The pot is currently 400, and I need to call 150. The pot odds are approximately 27% (150/550). However, 6h 2s is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker, with very poor playability and equity well below the required threshold against a reasonable opening range. Being out of position against the chip leader with such a weak hand makes folding the clear correct decision to preserve my stack of ~70 big blinds.
5d9c is a weak offsuit hand in UTG position. This is not a playable hand from early position - folding is the clear play.

I am holding Ten-Five suited in UTG+1 after the UTG player has folded. While it's a suited hand, Ten-Five is far too weak to open from this position, especially with several aggressive players and a short stack (Pro) still behind me. My stack is healthy and there's no reason to take a marginal risk this late in the tournament (Hand 133 of 150).

Folded to me on the button in a 5-handed game with ATo, a strong hand for this spot. Excellent position, ace blocker, good playability postflop. Standard raise to isolate blinds or steal the pot preflop. Late in tournament (hand 133/150), stealing blinds helps chip accumulation. Raising to 2.5x BB (250 total) is appropriate size: builds pot without overcommitting, min-raise is 200 but larger sizes perform better vs loose blinds. FiveTwo (BB) has been aggressive in past hands but equity holds up.

I hold 9s8h in the Small Blind facing a Button raise to 250 from Elon. 89o is a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position, and I still have the Big Blind behind me who covers me and could squeeze. Given the stack depth and late stage of the tournament, avoiding marginal out-of-position spots against button aggression is prudent. The required equity to call is roughly 33%, but realizing that equity from the SB is difficult.

Button open from Elon is likely wide-handed. With 4♣4♥ in the big blind, calling is high chip-EV: we’re deep enough to realize strong implied odds when we flop a set, and we avoid inflating the pot out of position with a hand that doesn’t play well in 3-bet pots and can get forced off its equity versus a 4-bet. We defend our blind at a good price and keep weaker hands in his range.

Heads-up BB vs BTN on A♠-2♠-8♦, our 4♣4♥ is an underpair with no meaningful draws (no spade, only very thin runner-runner straight possibilities). This board heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range (lots of Ax), so leading doesn’t generate enough folds and just bloats an out-of-position pot with a marginal hand. Best is to check our range, realize what little showdown value we have, and be prepared to mostly fold versus normal c-bets unless sizing is very small and we have a specific read.

Flop As2s8d is a good spot for a continuation bet as the preflop raiser from the button. My AhTd gives me ace-high with the nut flush blocker (Ah blocks As trips or better) and a backdoor diamond flush draw via Td. The board is dry overall: ace-high with a spade flush draw but no straight draws really connect well. FiveTwo's BB defending range is wide and he checked, which is weak. My button opening range hits this board well (Ax, broadways, some pairs), so c-betting for thin value against worse Ax, bluffs/draws, and protection vs spade draws is standard. Late in tournament (133/150), pot control isn't priority; building stack with position. Bet ~55% pot (300 into 550) balances value/protection without overbetting deep stacks. FiveTwo check-calls small bets often from history.

We’re BB vs BTN on A♠-2♠-8♦ holding 4♣4♥. This flop is very favorable for Elon’s range (lots of Ax and strong continues), while our hand is just an underpair with no spade and essentially no playable backdoor equity. Versus a 300 c-bet into a pot that includes his bet (we’d need to continue ~26% equity), our realistic path to winning is mostly only by spiking a set (too infrequent) and we’ll often face additional barrels that force us to fold after investing more. Check-raising is also poor here with no blockers and low fold equity. Best chip-EV is to release and keep our stack intact for better spots.

I am in the UTG position with 7c 9s. This is a weak, off-suit hand that does not play well in multi-way pots or against early position raises. Given the current tournament stage (Hand 134/150) and my stack size, there is no strategic reason to open this hand from UTG. Folding is the standard and correct play to preserve my stack.

3c6s is a trash hand from UTG+1 position in a 5-handed game. Offsuit, low cards with a gap, minimal playability. Pot is 150 from blinds (SB 50 + BB 100), facing a standard BB of 100 to call. Early position with 4 players behind (Pro button 6975, SB FiveTwo 13525, BB Claude 15250). Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament (hand 134/150). No need to speculate with marginal equity.

I am on the Button with [Ks Qd], which is a premium offsuit hand, typically top 10-15% of hands. With the action folding to me, this is a clear opening opportunity to steal the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds. A standard open size in this tournament has been 2.5bb (250 chips). This size balances fold equity with pot control. I do not want to limp and give the blinds a free look, nor do I want to fold such a strong hand in late position.

We’re in the small blind facing a BTN open. 85o has poor playability and no suit, offers little postflop equity realization out of position, and it’s also a bad 3-bet candidate (no blockers, weak when called). Even with a decent price to complete, the big blind can come along or squeeze, and we’ll be forced to navigate multiway/OOP spots with a dominated hand. Best EV and lowest variance line is to let it go and wait for a better defend/3-bet hand.

I have Ah7d in the BB facing a standard button raise to 250. A7o is a decent defending hand - getting 2.67:1 pot odds (call 150 into 400). Against a button open, this is a standard defend. 3-betting would be too aggressive with this hand. Calling to see a flop.

Board is Kh Kc Ts - very dry and heavily favors the preflop raiser. I have A7o which is just ace high with no draws. This board smashes Pro's button opening range (any K, any T, pocket pairs). I should check and evaluate. If Pro bets, I'll likely fold as I have minimal showdown value against any reasonable betting range.

I have flopped trips with a strong kicker (Kings with a Queen). The board is Kh Kc Ts. This is an extremely dry and strong board for my range, but also for my specific hand. I am effectively 'way ahead'. Betting now likely folds out all of the opponent's air, which constitutes a large portion of his Big Blind defense range. By checking back, I accomplish two things: 1) I disguise the strength of my hand, potentially inducing bluffs on later streets, and 2) I allow the opponent to catch up and make a second-best hand (like hitting a pair of Tens or a straight draw) that can pay off a value bet on the turn or river. Since I block the straight draw (holding a Queen blocks QJ) and the top pair (holding a King), it is less likely he has a hand strong enough to call a bet. Therefore, checking is the best option to maximize expectation over the whole hand.

Board is KK TT - double paired. Pro checked behind on the flop which signals weakness (likely no K or T). With this board, if neither of us has a K or T, we're both playing the board with our kicker. I have Ace high which is the best possible kicker - my hand is effectively KK TT A. Pro's flop check suggests they don't have trips/full house. I should bet small for thin value with my ace kicker. If raised, I fold easily.

I have turned a Full House (Kings full of Tens) on a double-paired board of [Kh Kc Ts Td]. This is a virtual nut hand, only losing to Quad Tens (Pocket Tens), and chopping with any other King (since the best five cards are K-K-K-T-T). My hand beats any Ten (Tens full of Kings) and all other holdings. The opponent's lead of 175 into a pot of ~550 is small, often indicative of a blocker bet with a Ten, a chop-seeking bet with a King, or a cheap bluff. Raising here would likely fold out all bluffs and weaker hands (like Tx) that I want to keep in the pot, while only getting called by hands that chop with me. Calling allows the opponent to continue bluffing or value-betting a worse hand (Tx) on the river, maximizing my expected value.

Board is KK TT 9 - I'm playing the board with my Ace kicker (KKTT A). Pro checked flop and called a small turn bet, suggesting no K or T. The river 9h is somewhat dangerous as 99 would now be a full house. With Ace high kicker, I have good showdown value but betting accomplishes little - worse hands fold, better hands (including 99 which just made a boat) call/raise. Checking to show down my best possible kicker is optimal.

I have reached the river with a powerful Full House (Kings full of Tens) on a board of [Kh Kc Ts Td 9h]. My hand is effectively the nuts, losing only to Quad Tens (unlikely) and chopping with any other King. Claude's line—checking the flop and betting small on the turn—suggests a marginal hand like a Ten (Tx), a weak King trying to set a cheap price, or a missed straight draw. By checking the river, he has capped his range. Given my passive play (checking back the flop and just calling the turn), my hand is under-represented. A bet here is mandatory to extract value from a Ten (which makes a lower Full House) or to get a chop-call from a weak King. A bet of 450 (half pot) is large enough to get value but small enough to ensure a call from a Ten.

Pro bet 450 into 900 pot on river - small sizing. Their line (checked flop, called small turn bet, now betting river) is unusual. The 9h completes very few hands except 99 which is now a full house. However, getting 4:1 odds (call 450 to win 1800), I only need to be right ~20% of the time. With Ace kicker on this KKTT9 board, I beat all unpaired hands and any pocket pair below 9. Pro's small bet sizing doesn't scream strength - 99 would likely bet larger for value. This feels like thin value or a bluff. Calling.

UTG position with 6h4s offsuit, a complete trash hand with no playability, low cards, no connectivity, no suits. Early position requires tight range; folding is mandatory to avoid spew. Pot is small blinds, no value in playing this.

I am holding King-Four offsuit (K4o) in UTG+1 (early position). This is a statistically weak hand that plays poorly out of position and is often dominated by better Kings or Aces. With 79 big blinds, I have no pressure to make loose moves to survive. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve chips for better opportunities.

5-handed and folded to us on the button, Ts8s is well within a profitable steal/opening range. We have good playability postflop (suited, connected enough) and position versus both blinds. Raising also realizes fold equity and denies the blinds a cheap realization. I’ll use a standard ~2.5x sizing to pressure both blinds without bloating the pot unnecessarily.

Holding 5h4c offsuit in the small blind facing a button raise to 250. This is a weak offsuit hand that plays poorly out of position. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds, 54o doesn't have the playability to justify calling here - I'll be out of position the entire hand with a marginal holding. Folding is the disciplined play.

I am in the big blind holding Jack-Deuce offsuit (Js 2d). The action folded to the button (FiveTwo), who raised to 250. While I am getting decent pot odds to call the additional 150, J2o is a very weak hand with poor post-flop playability, especially out of position. Given that we are nearing the end of the tournament (Hand 135 of 150) and I have a healthy stack, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand and risk losing more chips in a disadvantageous spot. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am holding 3c 2h, which is arguably the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em (7-2 offsuit is often cited, but 3-2 offsuit is statistically very weak with extremely limited straight potential and no high card value). Being Under the Gun (UTG), I am in the worst position at the table to play such a weak hand. With a healthy stack of 79 big blinds and only 14 hands remaining in the tournament, there is zero incentive to get involved with garbage cards. The only strategically sound decision is to fold immediately.

5-handed and first in (Pro folded), A4o is comfortably within a short-handed opening range: it blocks strong Ax holdings, has decent equity when called, and benefits a lot from taking initiative and picking up blinds/antes. I’ll open rather than limp to avoid multiway spots with a weak kicker. Sizing to 250 (2.5x) pressures the blinds and keeps the pot manageable so I can fold cleanly if I face a big 3-bet from the more aggressive players.
36 offsuit is a very weak hand. Facing a UTG+1 open, even from the button this hand has poor playability and will rarely make strong hands. Easy fold.

I am in the Small Blind with 8s 2c, which is a very weak starting hand. FiveTwo has raised to 250 from UTG+1. Given the strength of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a player who opened from an early position, folding is the most sensible play to preserve my stack.

Facing a raise to 250 from FiveTwo in UTG+1 after folds from Pro (UTG), Claude (Button), and Flash (SB). Pot is 400, and I need to call 150 more in the BB with 9d8h (offsuit connectors). This hand has excellent playability, good implied odds with deep stacks (~100BB effective), and favorable pot odds (~2.67:1 direct, better implied). FiveTwo has shown aggression from early position in history (e.g., frequent raises and value-betting flops), suggesting a somewhat wide range including hands like suited connectors, broadway, and pairs where 98o has ~35-40% equity OOP. Folding wastes BB equity late in tournament (15 hands left, 5 players); 3-betting risks playing OOP with marginal hand; calling allows set-mining, straight potential, and controlling pot size postflop. Plan to play aggressively if hits strong draws or improves.

Heads-up on a paired Ace-high flop (Ad Ac Js) after calling a UTG+1 raise preflop with 98o offsuit from BB. This board smashes FiveTwo's early position opening range (heavy on Ax, pairs, broadway), giving them top-heavy equity while my hand has zero showdown value, no draws (no flush, no straight draw beyond speculative gutshots unlikely to realize profitably), and poor playability OOP. Previous plan relied on hitting equity (~35-40% preflop assumed), but complete miss means checking to induce bluffs or realize equity cheaply is suboptimal given strength disparity. Betting risks isolating worse vs polarized range or getting raised off equity entirely; check-fold maximizes EV by conceding dead money (pot 550 for my 150 investment, +EV fold vs expected bet size). Late tournament (15 hands left), preserve stack for better spots vs aggressive FiveTwo history.

We flop trips (Ah with AdAc), but with a very weak kicker (4). Versus the BB’s defend range, a large portion of his continuing hands that put money in (most Ax) have us outkicked, while the hands we beat (random broadways/pairs/Jx without an ace) often won’t pay much. In position, checking back keeps the pot manageable, avoids getting check-raised into a tough spot by better Ax, and also keeps his air in so he can stab turn/river. We don’t need much protection on this paired board, since very few turns meaningfully outdraw trips.

Turn board Js Ad Ac 3s, holding 9d8h (98o offsuit). No pair, no draws (no flush draw, no straight draw/gutshot), pure air with negligible equity against FiveTwo's UTG+1 raising range (Ax, KK-QQ, sets likely after checking paired Aces flop). Preflop call was for playability/implied odds, but flop complete whiff; villain's flop check behind suggests possible medium strength (KK-JJ, missed Ax?) or trap, but turn 3s (spade flush draw possible, blank for most ranges) doesn't improve me. OOP heads-up pot 550 (~5.5BB), deep stacks (~100BB), but spewing by betting bluff risks villain's calling range (Ax, two-pair+) or raise; check maximizes EV by realizing fold equity postflop while preserving stack late tournament (15 hands left, 5 players). If villain bets, fold (as per flop plan); if check, see free river or small bet cheap. FiveTwo aggressive history (frequent raises/value bets), but flop check tempers strength; no profitable bluff spot.

Elon has checked twice on a very static paired board, which caps him toward a lot of Jx, pocket pairs, and some air that will often just check river again if I keep checking. Even though I have trips, my kicker is very weak, so I don’t want to bloat the pot versus his Ax (which frequently has me outkicked). A small turn bet gets thin value from Jx/PPs and charges the new spade-draw possibilities while keeping the pot controlled if he continues with a better ace.

Facing a small turn bet of 200 into 750 from FiveTwo's UTG+1 range after checking a paired Ace flop (Js Ad Ac), now board Js Ad Ac 3s. Holding 9d8h for pure air—no pair, no flush draw (8h no spades/diamonds), no straight draw, <15% equity vs villain's value-heavy range (Ax+, KK+, sets, two-pair). Preflop call was speculative for playability/implied odds, but complete miss on flop/turn; villain's flop check (possible trap/medium strength like QQ-JJ or missed draw) followed by small turn bet suggests thin value or probe, but calling OOP commits to river with no showdown and poor reverse implied odds. Pot odds ~3.75:1 insufficient for equity needed (~20%+), risking stack spew late tournament (hand 136/150, 5 players, ~100BB deep). Folding preserves stack for better spots vs aggressive FiveTwo history; no profitable bluff-raise given strength disparity and position.

5-handed this is effectively short-handed; JTo is good enough to open from UTG with a positional disadvantage but solid playability and blockers to strong broadways. The blinds have been active with 3-bets at times, so I’ll use a standard small open to keep risk controlled while still applying pressure to the blinds and picking up dead money frequently. If facing a larger 3-bet from the tougher/aggressive seats (Button/SB), I can comfortably fold.

AhTh is a strong suited ace. FiveTwo opened to 250 from UTG. I have position on them and a hand that plays well postflop with flush potential and decent high card strength. 3-betting to around 750 is standard here to build the pot and potentially take it down preflop or play heads up with initiative.

In this hand, I am on the button with Ace-Three of clubs. While A3s is a decent speculative hand, the action ahead of me is very strong: FiveTwo opened from UTG, and Claude 3-bet from UTG+1. Facing a raise and a 3-bet from early positions significantly narrows the range of hands my opponents are likely to hold, typically consisting of premium pairs (JJ-AA) and high-equity broadways (AK, AQ). Cold-calling a 3-bet with a weak suited Ace is generally a losing play as I am likely dominated or facing a large pair. Folding preserves my stack for more favorable situations where I have the initiative.

Preflop from SB facing UTG raise to 2.5BB and 3bet to 7.5BB from UTG+1. My hand 5c9h is offsuit, unconnected, poor playability, no blockers to strong ranges. Both opponents likely have premium hands (AA-JJ, AK, etc.). Pot odds ~1.6:1 not enough for speculative call OOP. Deep stacks but no fold equity postflop. Easy fold to preserve stack late in tournament (hand 137/150).

I am in the Big Blind holding Qs4h, which is a very weak off-suit hand. The action started with an UTG open to 250, followed immediately by a 3-bet to 750 from UTG+1. This sequence indicates significant strength from two early-position players. Facing a 3-bet cold, out of position, with a trash hand, I have no reason to get involved. The pot odds are insufficient to justify a call with this holding against such strong ranges. Preserving my 78 BB stack is the priority here.

Claude’s 3-bet from UTG+1 is a strong, positionally advantaged range, and with JTo we’re dominated by a big chunk of his value and semi-value (AJ/KJ/QJ, AT/KT/QT, big pairs). Even though the price is ~30% (500 to win 1650), our equity realization is poor out of position in a 3-bet pot and we’ll often be forced into tough, -EV continues on many flops. This hand is much better as an open than as a defend; I’ll avoid bloating the pot and preserve stack for higher-EV spots.
5s 3d is a very weak hand - low offsuit cards with poor playability. From UTG position with 4 players behind me, this is a clear fold.

QJo is a standard opening hand in a 5-handed game from UTG+1. With Claude folding, I am the first to enter the pot. A raise to 3x the big blind is a standard play to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds or see a flop in position against the blinds.

6d2c is one of the worst possible starting hands, with no high card value, no suitedness, no connectivity for straights or flushes. Facing a raise to 300 (3x BB) from Flash in early position (UTG+1), this is a clear fold. I'm on the button with deep stacks, but calling would commit chips to a hand with zero post-flop playability, poor implied odds, and no fold equity if I raise. Preserving stack for premium hands in the late stages of the tournament (hand 138/150) to maximize EV.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Four offsuit (Th 4c). An early position player (Flash) has raised to 300. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly, especially out of position against a strong range. The pot odds do not justify a call, and a 3-bet bluff is ill-advised given the hand strength and position. The standard and correct play is to fold.

Flash is opening from UTG+1 in a 5-handed game, which is typically a reasonably strong range. T6o is highly dominated, plays poorly out of position, and won’t realize equity well even getting ~31% pot odds (200 to win 650 after calling). A bluff 3-bet isn’t attractive this deep versus an early open. Best is to preserve chips and wait for better defend candidates.

J9o is a marginal hand that is generally a fold from Under the Gun (UTG) in a 5-handed game. It lacks the high-card strength and connectivity to be a profitable open-raise from this position, especially given the aggressive tendencies of the players behind me. With a comfortable stack and only 11 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to take unnecessary risks with weak hands out of position.

UTG+1 with A♥T♠ suited, a strong hand warranting a raise in early position. Standard open-raise size of 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot, deny equity to weaker hands, and take initiative. Deep stacks (~100BB) allow for post-flop play if called. Folding Flash UTG suggests no strong hand there, blinds may fold or defend wide.

Current hand is 139/150. I am on the Button with a stack of 77.5 BBs (7750). My cards are 4s Tc, which is a very weak off-suit hand. Elon opened from UTG+1 to 300 (3BB). Given the hand strength, calling is -EV due to poor equity and playability, even in position. 3-betting is not viable as a bluff here against an early position opener. The standard play is to fold.

Elon is opening from early position in a 5-handed game to 3bb, and we’re in the small blind with 3♠6♥ offsuit—one of the weakest holdings. Calling puts us out of position versus the raiser with poor playability and invites the big blind to come along, creating a multiway pot where we’ll realize equity very poorly. A 3-bet bluff here is also unattractive because our hand has almost no equity when called and Elon has shown willingness to continue/4-bet in bigger spots. Best EV is to conserve chips and fold.
5c7d is a weak offsuit hand. Facing an EP raise from Elon, I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds but will be out of position postflop with a hand that doesn't play well. Easy fold.

UTG with 7s4h offsuit is a complete trash hand with no playability, connectivity, or high card strength. Equity is abysmal multiway, and raising UTG lacks fold equity against deep-stacked opponents. With only 10 hands left, preserve stack for premium spots rather than spew chips in a terrible position. Standard fold.

I am in early/middle position with 2d 8s, which is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With players left to act behind me, including the button, and no chips invested yet, the only correct play is to fold immediately to conserve my stack.

We’re 3-handed with position, but 62o is at the absolute bottom of a profitable button strategy. Both blinds have shown willingness to defend/3-bet, which reduces immediate fold equity and makes this hand’s poor playability (dominated lows, weak top pairs, no straight potential) a consistent leak. Limping for 100 also isn’t attractive because our equity realization is poor and we invite the SB to isolate. Best EV is to pass and keep our stack for better steal spots.

Folded to me in the SB with T5o. This is a marginal hand, but heads-up against just the BB, I should either raise to try to steal or fold - completing is generally a weak play. T5o has some playability and I can try to take it down preflop with a standard raise.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit (A8o). The action folded to the Small Blind (Claude), who raised to 250. In a blind-versus-blind situation, A8o is a very strong hand. Claude has been active, and his range for opening from the Small Blind is likely very wide. While calling is a viable option to see a flop in position, I will put in a 3-bet to 750. This allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot immediately, and charge his weaker hands if he decides to call.

Facing a 3-bet from Flash with T5o. This is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position against a 3-bet range. I'm getting roughly 2:1 on a call but T5o has poor equity against a 3-betting range and will be difficult to play profitably postflop OOP. Clear fold.

I am in UTG position with a very weak hand, 4c 2c. Being the short stack with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I must be selective with my spots. Opening from early position with a low suited one-gapper is too risky and has negative expected value. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

5-handed and after UTG folds, we’re effectively in a relatively late position with a playable suited king. Ks5s has decent blocker value (reduces Kx continues), can flop strong top pairs and has some straight/flush potential. Limping is too passive and invites iso from the button/SB; folding is slightly too tight given short-handed dynamics and our stack depth. I’ll open-raise to a standard 2.5x to apply pressure and keep initiative while not bloating the pot excessively versus likely 3-bets behind.

As8o on the button facing a UTG+1 open from FiveTwo. This is a solid spot to 3-bet for value and isolation. I have position, a decent ace-high hand, and can put pressure on the blinds while potentially getting heads up against FiveTwo with position. A raise to 750 is standard sizing here.

I am holding 4d 3d in the small blind. There has been an open-raise from FiveTwo (UTG+1) to 250, followed by a 3-bet from Claude (Button) to 750. Although 43s has some speculative value, calling a 3-bet out of position against two opponents who have shown strength is a recipe for losing chips. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and a healthy stack, there is no reason to risk chips on such a weak hand in this configuration.

T8o is a marginal hand at best. Facing a 3-bet from Claude on the button after FiveTwo's UTG+1 open to 250. I'm OOP in BB, and FiveTwo still to act, so potential multiway pot. Pot odds ~2.8:1 but postflop playability poor with offsuit connectors, dominated equity vs likely strong ranges. Near end (hand 141/150), better to fold weak hands and wait for premium spots to maximize stack. Standard fold.

Claude’s button 3-bet versus our UTG+1 open can be quite wide 5-handed, but K5s is a poor flat OOP (dominated when we make top pair, hard to realize equity). It plays better as a blocker-based 4-bet bluff: Ks removes some of his strongest continues (AK/KQ), and a 4-bet creates immediate fold equity while setting a low SPR if called, letting us apply pressure with initiative. If he 5-bet jams, we simply fold; if he calls, we can c-bet/jam favorable textures and give up on high K boards when needed.

FiveTwo 4-bet to 2000 after my 3-bet. A8o is a marginal 3-bet hand that plays poorly against a 4-bet range. FiveTwo's 4-bet range is typically strong hands like AA, KK, QQ, JJ, AK, AQ. Against this range, A8o is often dominated and has poor equity (25-30%). I need to call 1250 into 2900 but this is not enough pot odds given how badly I'm doing against their range. Clear fold.

5-handed but still UTG: J3o is too weak, heavily dominated when it makes top pair, and has poor straight/flush potential. With multiple opponents who have shown willingness to 3-bet and apply pressure, opening or limping this hand will bleed EV and create tough postflop spots deep-stacked. Best is to preserve stack and wait for better holdings/positions.
Q5o is a weak hand from UTG+1. This is an easy fold - not playable from early position with 4 players still to act behind me.

I am on the button with T6o. The action has folded to me. While I could attempt to steal the blinds, T6o is a relatively weak hand even from the button. Elon (SB) and Pro (BB) have shown they can be defensive or aggressive. Given that we are late in the tournament (Hand 142 of 150) and my stack is healthy, I will avoid unnecessary risks with marginal hands and simply fold.

Preflop in SB vs BB only after folds. K6s suited is a strong hand for SB steal range: king blocker, suited connectivity, good playability postflop. Raising builds pot OOP with initiative, steals blinds often (Pro has defended but folded many times to aggression). Standard 2.5x raise size (250 total) balances steal/value. Deep stacks (107BB), 9 hands left: apply pressure to grow stack without showdown risks. Limp weak, fold too tight.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit (T4o). The Small Blind (Elon) has raised to 250 (2.5x BB). While this is a blind-versus-blind scenario where ranges are typically wider, T4o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Even though the pot odds are roughly 30%, realizing that equity will be difficult against an aggressive opponent like Elon without hitting the board hard. Defending here would likely lead to bleeding chips or facing difficult decisions on later streets. Given my stack depth and the stage of the tournament (Hand 142/150), preserving chips by folding this trash hand is the prudent play.
T3 suited in UTG is too weak to open, even at a 5-handed table. The hand lacks sufficient strength and playability from early position. Easy fold.

We are currently on hand 143 of 150, sitting with a healthy stack of 13,275 chips. In this 5-handed format, Jack-Ten offsuit is a marginal but playable hand from my current position (UTG+1/Hijack). Since the player under the gun folded, I have an opportunity to take the initiative. Opening for a standard 2.5x raise (250 chips) allows me to potentially take down the blinds and antes or play a pot in position against the blinds. If I face a significant 3-bet from the aggressive big blind (FiveTwo) or the button (Elon), I can safely fold without sacrificing too much of my stack as we approach the end of the tournament.

Hand #143 pre-flop on the button with 6s2d, a complete trash hand with no playability, suitedness, or high card strength. Flash raised to 250 from early position (UTG+1 after one fold), indicating strength. Pot 400, to-call 250 (2.5bb), but 62o has zero equity against a raising range and poor post-flop potential. Late in tournament (hand 143/150), preserve stack for better spots with 5 players remaining. Standard fold to avoid spew.

I am in the Small Blind with 7c 8d (78 offsuit). An Early Position player (Flash) has raised to 250 (2.5BB). Against an EP opening range, 78o is significantly behind. Additionally, I would be playing the entire hand out of position, making it difficult to realize equity. Calling requires 200 chips into a pot of 400, offering 3:1 odds, but the reverse implied odds and positional disadvantage make this a clear fold, especially given my healthy stack depth (76BB) and the late stage of the tournament (Hand 143/150).

Flash opens to 2.5x and it’s folded to us in the BB getting a good price (150 to win 400 ~ 27% immediate). 8♠5♠ is a marginal but playable suited hand that realizes equity reasonably well in a heads-up pot, and I don’t love a 3-bet bluff here since we’re OOP, have weak blockers, and would be turning a hand with decent playability into a high-variance spot. Defend by calling and play postflop with suited potential/board coverage.

Flop A♥-2♠-3♥ is much better for the preflop raiser’s range (lots of Ax), and our hand (8♠5♠) has only a gutshot to the 4 plus some backdoor spade potential. Donk-betting here doesn’t generate much fold equity versus Flash’s likely c-bet continues (Ax, some broadways w/ backdoors), and it also denies us the chance to realize equity cheaply or to use a check-raise selectively when we pick up additional equity (a 4, a spade, or favorable turn cards). Best is to check and respond based on sizing—continue versus small bets with our gutshot/backdoors, and fold versus large pressure without improvement.

I was the pre-flop aggressor with Jack-Ten offsuit and was called by FiveTwo in the Big Blind. The flop comes Ace-high with a low wheel-draw texture (A-2-3) and two hearts. This board favors my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser, as I am much more likely to hold strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) than the Big Blind. FiveTwo has checked to me. I will put out a standard continuation bet of about 40% of the pot (225 chips) to represent the Ace and take the pot down immediately. If FiveTwo calls or raises, I can comfortably fold or check down the rest of the hand, as I have no real showdown value or draws, but the situational advantage dictates a lead here.

Flash’s c-bet is small (225 into 775 ~29% pot), giving us strong immediate odds: 225 to win 1000 (~22.5% needed). With 8♠5♠ on A♥-2♠-3♥ we have a clean gutshot to the 4 (4 outs ~16%) plus some runner-runner equity via spades and occasional overcards being relevant if Flash is c-betting wide. Raising doesn’t credibly fold out Ax and just bloats the pot OOP with a weak hand, so the best EV line is to continue versus this sizing and realize our equity, then proceed aggressively only on improving turns (4, spade, some wheel-completing/pressure cards).

Turn 9♦ doesn’t improve us: our only real equity is still the 4-out gutshot to a 4 for the wheel (and the backdoor spade flush is now dead). This board still heavily favors Flash’s preflop raising range (lots of Ax), and if we lead here we’ll frequently get called by Ax / heart-draws and have almost no equity to continue. The best EV is to check, keep the pot controlled, and respond based on sizing (fold to meaningful pressure; occasionally continue only versus very small bets given our thin draw).

On the turn, the 9 of diamonds is largely a brick that doesn't significantly change the board texture. Having already represent the Ace with a continuation bet on the flop and been called by FiveTwo, my Jack-high has very little showdown value, but a second barrel here is unlikely to force a fold from any Ace or even a stubborn pocket pair. Given FiveTwo's willingness to call on the flop, checking behind allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my minimal equity, and potentially see a cheap showdown or evaluate the river action in position. With only a few hands remaining in the tournament, preserving my stack is a priority over a high-risk, low-equity bluff.

River (A♥-2♠-3♥-9♦-3♦) we have 8♠5♠ with no showdown value, so checking just concedes the pot whenever Flash checks back (and we can’t profitably bluff-catch if he bets). The turn check from the preflop raiser caps Flash’s range toward a lot of give-ups and some pot-control Ax, while the river pairing the 3 is a strong range-advantage card for the BB (we have far more 3x/boats than Flash). This is a good spot to run a polarized bluff representing trips/boats. I’ll use a near-pot sizing to pressure weak Ax and all missed heart/backdoor holdings, while keeping risk controlled versus a snap-call range of stronger Ax/boats.

On the river, the board pairs the 3, and FiveTwo leads for 900 into a 1,000-chip pot. This is a very strong lead after the turn went check-check. My Jack-high has absolutely no showdown value and cannot win by calling. FiveTwo's range for calling the flop on an Ace-high board includes weak Aces, pairs that have now improved to two pair or trips, and various draws. Given the size of the bet and the fact that there are only a few hands remaining in the tournament, a bluff-raise is unnecessarily risky. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better spots in the final few hands.

I am UTG with King-Deuce offsuit (Ks 2h). This is a very weak starting hand, especially from early position in a 5-handed game. Given the table dynamics and the fact that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 144 of 150), there is no reason to risk chips with a marginal hand that plays poorly post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Hand #144, UTG+1 position with Jc9h offsuit. This is a speculative hand with poor playability out of position. Standard play is to fold weak offsuit broadways from early position to avoid difficult postflop spots OOP with a short-handed table but deep stacks. No reads or history justify playing it. With tournament nearing end (144/150), preserve stack for better spots. Pot is blinds only (150), to limp 100 but limping OOP bad. Fold to wait for stronger hands.

I am on the Button with 3s 9s, which is a very weak starting hand (bottom 15%). Although I have the positional advantage, the opponents in the blinds (FiveTwo and Claude) have significantly larger stacks and have shown capability to play back (e.g., Claude 3-betting light in Hand 141, FiveTwo playing aggressively). With 76 BBs, I am the shortest stack but not in immediate danger, so there is no need to force a steal with such low equity cards. A standard Button opening range is around 40-50% of hands; 93s is well outside this range. The prudent play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

Folded to us in the SB, so this is a heads-up blind-vs-blind spot where we should attack very wide. Td3d is weak but has decent playability (suited, can make top pair + backdoors) and benefits a lot from taking the pot down preflop. With stacks deep enough, a raise also denies BB a free realization of equity. I’ll size a bit larger than min to apply pressure and reduce BB’s pot-odds to defend with trash.

Q4o is a weak hand facing a raise from SB. FiveTwo has been active throughout the tournament. While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds, Q4o plays poorly out of position and doesn't have good implied odds. Better to fold and wait for a better spot with only 6 hands left.

UTG with 3c9s in a 5-handed late-stage tournament (hand 145/150). This is a trash hand: low, offsuit, disconnected, poor post-flop playability. Facing deep stacks except Pro at 76BB, no position advantage. Pot has blinds (150), to call 100, but raising or calling commits chips to a weak range. Standard fold to wait for premium hands to maximize stack.

I am in UTG+1 (MP) with [5d Th], which is a very weak unsuited hand. With 5 hands left in the tournament and being the short stack (though still deep at 76BB), there is no incentive to open loose with trash hands. I fold.

Button with AhAd in a 5-handed game after UTG/UTG+1 fold. This is the top of range and wants to build a pot in position versus blinds who will defend widely. Open-raising also denies equity to random hands and sets up favorable SPR for value betting on later streets. I’ll use a standard, non-suspicious sizing that keeps weaker hands in while still generating fold equity.

I have ATs in the small blind facing a button raise from FiveTwo to 300. ATs is a strong hand - suited broadway with an ace. Against a button open which should be wide, this is an excellent 3-betting hand for value and to take the initiative. I'll 3-bet to around 900-950 to put pressure on both FiveTwo and the BB.

I am in the Big Blind holding 4d 7c, which is a very weak hand. FiveTwo opened from the Button and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. Given that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against a 3-bettor and the original raiser with a sub-par hand, there is no reason to commit any chips. Folding is the only viable play here to preserve my stack as we approach the end of the tournament.

We have AhAd vs a small blind 3-bet after opening the button. This is the top of range and wants to play for stacks against Claude’s likely wide 3-bet range (especially in 5-handed). Flat-calling would under-realize value and allow more awkward SPR/runouts; a 4-bet builds the pot, denies equity to hands that would otherwise realize well, and sets up an easy shove on many flops. In position, I’ll use a solid, not-oversized 4-bet sizing that keeps dominated hands and bluffs in while still pressuring his range.

FiveTwo 4-bet to 2500 after my 3-bet. ATs is a decent hand but facing a 4-bet, I'm likely dominated by AK/AQ or crushed by big pairs. Even if FiveTwo is capable of light 4-betting, calling 1600 more out of position with ATs puts me in a tough spot postflop. 5-bet jamming would be too loose. With only 5 hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack, folding is prudent here.

I am holding 8h 2d, which is statistically one of the worst starting hands in poker. Being Under the Gun (UTG), I am in the worst position to play such a weak hand, with four players left to act behind me. With a healthy stack of 76 BBs and only a few hands left in the tournament (146/150), there is no incentive to play this trash hand. The correct and only standard play here is to fold immediately.

4♣5♦ is too weak and dominated to invest from UTG+1, especially with three players behind who have shown willingness to 3-bet/iso-raise. Limping invites pressure and puts us in tough, low-EV spots out of position; opening needs an unrealistic fold rate to show immediate profit. With a healthy stack lead late in the session, preserving chips and picking better steal/3-bet spots is higher EV.
A9o on the button with folds to me is a clear open. This is a standard situation to raise and try to steal the blinds or play in position if called. Standard 2.5x open to 250.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited, a hand with good post-flop playability. Claude, on the button, has raised to 2.5x the big blind. With pot odds of approximately 3:1 and a hand that flops well, calling to see a flop is a standard play, although it puts me out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my stack size and the stage of the tournament, I want to see a flop with this speculatively strong hand without committing too many chips to a 3-bet bluff against an active opponent.

Hand 146 of 150, very late in tournament, need to maximize stack. In BB with AhKc, premium hand AKo. Button Claude raised to 250 (loose button range), SB Flash called 200 (loose calling range). OOP but AK dominates much of their ranges, great equity multiway or heads-up. 3-bet for value, to isolate vs Claude's wide range, charge Flash's weak holdings, and take fold equity. Sizing to 1000 total (4x the raise, considering dead money and pot ~600; effective ~107BB deep, standard aggressive size). Builds pot with strong hand, positions me well.

Facing a 3-bet to 1000 with A9o after I opened. Flash is still in the pot and could call behind. A9o doesn't play well multiway against a 3-bet range - it's a marginal hand that often gets dominated by better aces. The pot odds require me to put in 750 to win ~2250, but my equity isn't great here, especially if Flash comes along. This is a standard fold spot.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Eight suited. Claude opened from the button, and I initially called. However, Elon in the Big Blind has now executed a significant squeeze to 1,000 chips. With Claude (the original raiser) folding, the action is back to me. Although T8s has some post-flop potential, calling a 4x raise out of position against a range that likely consists of high pairs and strong broadways is mathematically and strategically disadvantageous. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, preserving my stack and avoiding high-variance spots with marginal hands is the priority. Folding here allows me to maintain a healthy stack for more favorable opportunities.

5-handed from UTG, JTs suited is firmly in my open-raising range: it plays well postflop, can make strong disguised hands, and benefits a lot from taking initiative. With deep stacks, I want to build pots when I have a good, connected suited hand while also applying pressure to the blinds and avoiding a passive limp that invites multiway play and squeezes. I’ll use a standard 2.5bb open to 250 to keep my range consistent with prior sizing and to deny some equity to the blinds.

A6o facing a UTG raise. This is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against an early position raiser. With 3 hands left and a decent stack of 12,475, no need to take unnecessary risks. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am on the button with 6-4 offsuit. FiveTwo, the current chip leader, has opened with a raise from UTG. While FiveTwo has been playing aggressively in recent hands, my hand is extremely weak and does not justify a call or a 3-bet bluff, especially with only four hands remaining in the tournament. Preserving my stack to maintain a strong position is more important than contesting a pot with a bottom-tier hand against an active opponent.

Late in the tournament (hand 147/150), stacks are deep except Pro in BB (7500). Holding A9o in SB facing UTG raise to 250 from FiveTwo (15850 stack), who has been an aggressive raiser historically. A9o blocks AA/AK, dominates parts of UTG range like AJo+, KQo, pairs. Two folds create squeeze opportunity with fold equity, but BB still to act. 3-betting OOP is standard for value/thin value and to deny equity to calling range. Size to 900 (3.6x original raise) balances fold equity and gets value from worse aces, broadways. Calling OOP is poor due to position; folding too tight with strong ace.

I am in the Big Blind holding 5c 2c, a very weak suited hand. The action involves an opening raise from UTG (FiveTwo) and a 3-bet from the Small Blind (Elon) to 900. Cold calling a 3-bet with 5-high is not a viable strategy, especially as the original raiser (UTG) is still left to act and could 4-bet. My hand has very poor equity against the strong ranges implied by a UTG open and an SB 3-bet. With only a few hands left in the tournament and a healthy stack of 75bb, there is no reason to risk chips here. The decision is a clear fold.

Elon’s SB 3-bet to 900 is a fairly standard size and we’re deep enough (and importantly in position postflop) to realize the equity of JThh well. JTs suited plays great as a flat versus a blind 3-bet: it can flop strong draws and nutted straights/flushes, and it has good implied odds when we make big hands. A 4-bet here would mostly bloat the pot against a range that can continue strongly and risks getting forced into tough spots or folding out the weaker hands we want to keep in. With BB already folded, I’m happy to take position and see a flop.

Late in tournament (hand 147/150), I 3-bet pre-flop with A9o from SB as a thin value/semi-bluff exploiting FiveTwo's UTG range and position. FiveTwo's call suggests strength: KK, AKo/s, QQ/JJ, maybe sets/pairs. Flop 8h Kd Ks is extremely dry and heavily favors villain's calling range (pairs KK for quads possible with KhKc, AK for TP/TK, QQ/JJ crushed). My A9s is pure air: ace-high no pair/draw, As doesn't hit flush draw (only Ks spade). OOP first to act into 1900 pot with 10k stack, betting risks stacking off to better ace/pairs or getting check-raised. Checking maximizes fold equity if villain checks back (possible with KK slowplaying or weak), but prepared to fold to bet as villain likely bets value. Avoids spew, preserves stack for last hands.

Elon 3-bet pre and now checks on a very static paired board (K K 8). My hand (JhTh) has essentially no showdown value here and only backdoor equity, so checking back gives up too much EV and lets him realize with hands like AQ/QJ/TT-99. A small position stab pressures his air and medium pairs, and it’s also a good range bet size on paired boards where neither side wants to inflate too much. If he check-raises, I can comfortably fold; if he calls, I can barrel selectively on good turns (hearts/Q/9/A) that add equity and apply pressure.

Late in tournament (147/150), holding A9o which missed completely on dry K-K-8 rainbow flop. My preflop 3bet was thin value/semi-bluff, but villain's call and flop bet into checked pot (650 into 1900) screams strength: KK (quads possible), AK (TPTK), QQ/JJ/T T, sets. As blocks AA/AKs but provides no showdown value or draw OOP. Pot odds ~4:1 but calling commits ~10% stack OOP multiway potential with turn/river facing further aggression, no fold equity on raise due to board texture and villain range. Folding preserves 10475 stack for final hands, avoiding spew against polarized range where villain rarely bluffs here.
UTG with T4 suited is too weak to open from early position. Only 3 hands left, no need to get spewy with marginal holdings. Standard fold.

With only three hands remaining in the tournament, I need to protect my stack and look for high-probability situations. Holding Eight of Diamonds and Six of Hearts in UTG+1 is a very weak position to open, especially following a fold from UTG. I have a healthy stack and there is no reason to risk chips on a sub-optimal hand at this stage.

Jc3s is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against calling ranges, and dominated often. Position on button offers steal opportunity with folds ahead, pot odds for call are decent (150 pot, call 100), but calling is spewy with trash and leads to difficult postflop spots multiway or OOP. Raising risks ~250+ to win 150 if blinds fold, but Pro and FiveTwo defend frequently based on history (e.g., FiveTwo deep and aggressive, Pro calls stations). Late in tournament (hand 148/150), deep stacks (~100BB+), but no need to gamble with marginal spot; preserve for premium hands in final hands. Best EV is fold.

I am in the Small Blind with J4o. The action has folded to me. The Big Blind (FiveTwo) is the chip leader with 17,000 and has been playing very aggressively in recent hands (e.g., Hand 147, 145). My hand is very weak, and playing it out of position against an aggressive big stack is a losing proposition. Attempting to steal with a raise is risky given the likelihood of being called or 3-bet by the big stack. Completing the blind offers good pot odds but leaves me with a weak hand out of position. Folding is the most prudent action to preserve chips for the final two hands.

I am currently in second place in the tournament with only two hands remaining (Hand 149 and 150). My stack is 12,450, while Claude is right behind me with 12,375. Holding King-Five offsuit in the Under-The-Gun (UTG) position is quite weak, even in a five-handed game. Opening here invites unnecessary risk; a loss in this pot could easily drop me to third or fourth place. Since maximizing the stack and maintaining rank are the priorities with the tournament ending, I will play it safe and fold this marginal hand to avoid potential chip loss before the final hand.

Js6h is a weak offsuit hand with poor postflop playability and high chance of domination. In UTG+1 position with multiple players behind, including deep stacks like FiveTwo (171BB) and Claude (124BB), folding is standard to preserve chips for better opportunities on the second-to-last hand. No steal incentive here with healthy stacks and early position.

Current hand is 149/150. I am on the Button with [Qd 7h]. Action folded to me. Q7o is a weak offsuit hand. While being on the button offers a stealing opportunity, the blinds (FiveTwo and Claude) are deep-stacked and have shown aggression previously.
