FiveTwo in the Small Blind specifically has a large chip lead and may defend widely or 3-bet. The risk of opening a dominated hand like Q7o outweighs the small reward of stealing the blinds at this late stage of the tournament. Preserving the stack for a better spot in the final hand is prudent.

Folded to us in the small blind, so this is a great spot to attack the big blind with a wide raising range. K9 suited is comfortably ahead of Claude’s random BB checking range and plays well postflop (good top-pair potential, suited equity, and some straight potential). Completing gives up initiative and lets BB realize equity too easily; raising wins the pot immediately a meaningful amount and, when called, we can c-bet favorable boards and control pot size when needed from out of position. I’ll use a standard SB open size to pressure the BB without bloating variance.

64 offsuit is a very weak hand. FiveTwo raised from SB, showing aggression. While pot odds are 200 to win 400 (2:1), this hand plays poorly postflop with no real equity or playability. With only 2 hands left in the tournament and a decent stack, no need to defend with garbage.

QTo offsuit is a marginal hand at best, and from UTG in a 5-handed game, it falls outside of a standard opening range. Typical UTG opens include high pairs, AKo/AQs, broadway suited, etc. Raising risks playing out of position post-flop with a weak hand, and limping is not standard. Folding preserves stack for better spots, even on the final hand. Opponents have deep stacks except Pro, but no need to get involved with this hand early.

This is the final hand (150/150). I am the short stack with 7450 chips. My hand is Queen-Six suited (Q6s), which is a marginal hand, especially from early/middle position (UTG+1/Cutoff). Given the stack sizes, the larger stacks behind me (FiveTwo, Claude, Flash) can easily apply pressure or 3-bet, forcing me to fold and lose chips unnecessarily. Since the goal is to maximize stack size and there are no future hands to play for, preserving my current stack by avoiding a negative or neutral EV spot with a weak hand is the optimal strategy. The reward of stealing the blinds (150) does not outweigh the risk of losing chips in the final hand.

Folds to us on the Button with a significant chip lead. Even though 4c2c is very weak, this is a profitable spot to apply pressure and steal the blinds with position, especially 5-handed where Button opens are wide. A small open also keeps risk controlled; if we face a 3-bet, we can comfortably fold and preserve stack.

Final hand of the tournament. QTs is a solid hand in the small blind against a button open. FiveTwo has been active throughout. This is a good 3-bet candidate - suited broadway cards with straight potential. I'll apply pressure with a 3-bet to around 750, which puts FiveTwo in a tough spot with a likely wide button range.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently in 2nd place with 12,350 chips, while the chip leader, FiveTwo, has 17,000. Claude, the small blind, has 11,625. In this specific scenario, FiveTwo (Button) opened for 250 and Claude (SB) 3-bet to 750. I am holding KQo in the Big Blind. While KQo is a strong hand, it is frequently dominated by the 3-betting ranges of a small blind (typically AK, AQ, JJ, QQ, KK, AA). Calling here would put me out of position for the remainder of the hand against two opponents, one of whom has already shown aggression. Furthermore, if I call, the original raiser (FiveTwo) still has the opportunity to 4-bet, which would force me to fold and lose more chips. Since the goal is to maximize the final stack size and I am currently in a strong position, taking a high-variance risk with a hand that is likely behind a 3-betting range is not optimal. Folding preserves my stack and secures my 2nd place finish.

Claude’s small blind 3-bet to 750 is a spot where his range is still meaningfully stronger than my Button open range. Even though the price is tempting (500 to win 1100 now), 4c2c has very poor playability vs a condensed/stronger range and suffers heavy reverse implied odds (often making dominated low pairs/weak flushes). With a big stack and no need to take thin, high-variance defenses, I stick to the plan: open wide, but comfortably release the very bottom vs 3-bets.
